docs.title: Monetary-Financial Model — Documentation
docs.text:
The Monetary-Financial Model is an interactive forecasting tool that runs entirely in your browser. It shows quarterly forecasts for about fifty economies — growth, inflation, interest rates, public finances, and more — and lets you bend them: click on a chart where you think inflation will be, and every other chart updates so the whole forecast stays consistent with the estimated economics. Thirty-three of the economies are linked through trade and financial channels, so pushing the United States moves the euro area, Japan, and China too.

Three rules govern everything on the screen:

  1. Data are data. Solid history is observed. Where a country's latest quarter has not been published yet, the model fills the gap using everything already observed elsewhere — and those filled-in points are always drawn in amber, never disguised as data.
  2. The model is estimated once, then frozen. Within the year, new data releases shift the starting point of the forecast (the panel currently starts projections at 2026 Q1) but never silently change the model itself. Re-estimation happens once a year, on a complete final year of data. Each quarterly update is archived as a vintage, and the Monitor page shows exactly what each data release did to the forecasts.
  3. Judgment is visible. If you move a forecast away from what the model says — a scenario, a long-run target, a policy counterfactual — the chart says so.

Under the hood it is a Bayesian Vector Autoregression (BVAR) per economy, coupled into a Global VAR (GVAR) with a fully endogenous United States, plus DSGE impulse responses for policy counterfactuals. The deployed tool is served at the /bvar-scenario path of the academic website, hosted on GitHub Pages (the matyasfarkas/starter-hugo-academic Hugo Academic site). The build/deploy chain is documented in Build & CI.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• User Manual (PDF) — the manual is in two parts. Part I, "Using the tool", is written for someone with no econometrics background: each section says what you can do, how to do it (step by step, naming the exact buttons), and what you will find on the screen. If you have five minutes, read the quick-start section and skip the rest until you need it. Part II, "How the machinery works", has the full methodology — BVAR estimation, COVID-as-missing data augmentation, the GVAR coupling and closed-form solve, the fiscal accounting, long-run targets, vintages, and reproducibility.
  • User Guide — a short web quick-start: your first scenario in five minutes, and where to go next. Defers to Part I of the manual for everything else.

Empirical results are in

An out-of-sample horse-race (56 origins, 2010Q1–2024Q4, × 9 models, CRPS-primary scoring + 90% Model Confidence Set) and the endogenous-US Tier-A spillover analysis have run. The headline: AR(p)/DFM lead the point race, but the EM-Bayesian GVAR is inside the Model Confidence Set on the nominal block (inflation at every horizon, the short rate at h ≤ 4), decisively beats the textbook OLS classical GVAR, and the endogenous-US variant weakly dominates the frozen anchor. Read the numbers and the honest caveats (over-confident draw densities; provisional spillover magnitudes) in Empirical Evaluation.

docs.title: Methodology and data
docs.text:
• Fiscal Block Methodology — how the estimated fiscal drivers relate to the CBO and IMF fiscal-policy frameworks, the exact debt-dynamics recursion (snowball + SFA closure + primary balance), and the known limitations (including the China nominal-GDP inflation double-count).
  • Data & sources — per-source-family documentation (FRED / Eurostat / OECD-QNA / IMF-WEO), the 2025 estimation vintage versus the frozen-model quarterly refresh, and the full country × variable coverage matrix are in the User Manual (PDF) (Part II, §Data Sources), generated from app/public/data/coverage_manifest.json. Where a series lags its country's release calendar, the gap to the common 2026 Q1 origin is filled by the cross-country model and flagged as a nowcast in the manifest (imputedThrough) — the same cells the app draws in amber.
  • Empirical Evaluation — the out-of-sample forecast horse-race (9-model density race, CRPS + Model Confidence Set), the endogenous-US Tier-A spillover analysis, and the honest caveats. Implemented in a separate evaluation repository.
  • Paper Outline (PDF) — the working outline of the accompanying paper.

docs.title: For developers and maintainers
docs.text:
• Architecture (for AI / developers) — dense codebase map of the compute engine, GVAR topology, fiscal block, data conventions, and tests. Also documents the drag-perf / UX architecture (§5a/§8b): the heavy ~4.5 s GVAR solve runs off the live drag path — zero solves during a drag, exactly one cheap "direct" solve on pointer-up, and the full cross-country fixed point with uncertainty bands only on the Solve full GVAR button; the optimistic conditioned dot (conditionedDotY.ts) snaps to the click instantly; and per-chart React.memo repaints one panel, not all ~35.
  • Pipeline — the offline build → estimate → check → port → couple → test → deploy pipeline that produces the shipped data.
  • Modules & API — module-level reference for the compute and data layers.
  • Data Schemas — the JSON contracts the browser app reads.
  • Build & CI — the green gate, the test taxonomy, and the deploy chain.
  • Maintainer's Guide — operations, adding a country, and re-estimating models. Quarterly vintage shifts run through app/scripts/auto_update.mjs (--check, --shift --asof), which archives the old vintage, imputes ragged edges cross-block, rebuilds provenance, and writes the release-impact report behind the /monitor page.

docs.title: Project history
docs.text: • Changelog — release notes and notable changes.

docs.title: Runbook: adding a country / updating a country's data and model
docs.text:
This is a human, step-by-step guide for the data-and-estimation side of the tool. It covers three jobs:

  • A. Update an existing country (refresh its data, re-estimate, redeploy). Worked example: the US.
  • B. Add a brand-new country from scratch (standalone single-country model, optional GVAR link).
  • C. Add / refresh a 4-variable fiscal block (US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN today).

The frontend is generic: it reads n (the number of variables) and every index from the model JSON, so once a country's JSON files are in app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/ and the country is registered in the single registry app/src/lib/data/countries.ts, the charts, conditioning, uncertainty, and the long-run anchor (LRP / entropic tilt) all work automatically — no extra data files are needed for any of them. The only hard part is the data and the estimation. (For the code-side architecture, see ARCHITECTURE_FOR_AI.md.) The deployed tool is live at the /bvar-scenario path of the academic website, hosted on GitHub Pages (the matyasfarkas/starter-hugo-academic Hugo Academic site).

Long-run anchor (LRP / entropic tilt). Once a model ships, the user can set a long-run target and tilt the forecast toward it: the Compute button is enabled whenever an enabled anchor exists (auto-selecting the Quick mode), and the tilt moves the unconditional central path (the weighted mean from reduceCloud), not just the fan. The tilt moment is the H-horizon forecast, not the steady state. For a GVAR bloc the anchor is marginal (per-bloc), with a disclaimer shown in the UI. This is entirely frontend — adding a country needs no LRP-specific files.

Current topology (so the numbers below have context). The coupled GVAR has 33 blocs, and the US is now a FULLY ENDOGENOUS coupled bloc, not the frozen anchor. Its spec.json carries the three ROW*_US stars (ROWDEM_US/ROWINF_US/ROWFIN_US, block:'row') built from a US trade-weights row, so it both drives Rest-of-World and receives RoW→US feedback (n=41: 30 domestic + 4 global + 4 fiscal + 3 stars). The set is: the US + euro area as one aggregate bloc (EA) + 31 Rest-of-World blocs (JP UK CA CN CH KR MX AU TR SE PL NO DK IN BR RU ID SA AR ZA TH MY CL CO CR CZ HU IL IS NZ TW). With the US endogenous the coupling is the augmented (stars ++ US-global) closed-form operator of dimension 2060, with spectral radius rho(M) = 0.924 < 1 (stable; verified off the shipped coupling_default.json.gz header — rhoSpectral = 0.924041). The engine detects endogeneity from the data: US_ENDOGENOUS = !!usIdx.row && !!WEIGHTS24.weights.US in gvarSolve.ts, so COUPLED_COUNTRIES = ['US', ...rows]. The 17 euro members (DE FR IT ES NL AT BE FI GR IE LU PT SI SK EE LT LV) are standalone single-country models (domestic + 4 global commons + trade-weighted foreign stars, estimated in separation via MAXIT=1) — they are not in the coupled GVAR and have no weights row. Reason: the fully-disaggregated euro GVAR was non-convergent (rho>1); aggregating the euro area restores stationarity, which is why EA is one bloc and the members are standalone. (50 country directories ship in all: 33 coupled GVAR blocs + 17 standalone euro members.)

Registry / selector. The country dropdown is driven by app/src/lib/data/countries.ts (one entry per economy, { code, name, group }). Adding a country = add an entry there + drop its data files; no dropdown edit is needed. The selector groups entries into "GVAR" / "Other countries" optgroups (driven by isGvarCountry from gvarLoader in controls/TopBar.tsx; plain names, no "- GVAR" suffix). group is only a coarse tag (core/oecd/em); GVAR membership is data-driven — a bloc is GVAR-linked iff its spec.json carries block:'row' vars AND it has a row in app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_24bloc.json (the filename is historical — the file now holds the 33-bloc weights, including the US row). The legacy app/public/data/meta/countries.json is display metadata only.

docs.title: What a country needs (the contract)
docs.text:
The app loads these files from app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/ (<CC> = 2-letter code, e.g. US, EA):

File Required What it is spec.json yes { n, variables:[{id,name,transformation,isFinancial,prior,block}] } — block ∈ domestic/row/global/fiscal; row+global are the GVAR foreign/commons blocs, fiscal tags the 4 fiscal-accounting vars; a standalone non-fiscal bloc is all domestic companion_mean.json yes estimated VAR coefficients { betahat, sigmahat, n, lags } baseline.json yes unconditional forecast in growth/display space (mean + q16/q84/q05/q95) baseline_levels.json yes unconditional forecast in level space historical.json / historical_levels.json yes last ~40 quarters (growth / level) transformations.json yes ['log'\|'lin', ...] per variable companion_draws.json optional posterior draws → enables Full uncertainty bands fiscal_meta.json 6 fiscal countries fiscal-block indices {gdp,deflator,revenue,primaryExp,effRate,sfa} + d0 + sfaDefault. Present for US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN

transformation: 'log' = the series is stored as 100·log(level) and shown as 4·Δ (q/q annualized growth) — use it for real GDP, prices, indices. 'lin' = passed through as-is — use it for rates, ratios, unemployment. isFinancial just groups the variable under the "financial" header.

Fiscal block (the 6 fiscal countries) — now 4 variables. US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN carry four estimated fiscal vars (block:'fiscal', all lin):

  1. FREV_GDP — revenue / GDP × 100 (% of GDP, 4-quarter trailing ratio)
  2. FPEXP_GDP — primary expenditure (= total exp − interest) / GDP × 100 (% of GDP)
  3. EFFI — effective interest rate = net interest / lagged gross debt × 100 × 4 (% per annum). This reverts the earlier debt-service swap (DSVC) back to the rate — older builds estimated DSVC; the current builds estimate EFFI.
  4. SFA — stock-flow adjustment, a new estimated lin BVAR variable = the EXACT one-step residual that closes the app's debt recursion (see below).

fiscal_meta.json idx is {gdp, deflator, revenue, primaryExp, effRate, sfa} — there is no debtService key any more. In fiscalAccounting.ts computeFiscalBlock reads idx.effRate as the rate driver, and — because idx.sfa is set — reads the per-quarter SFA forecast off the level path (levelPaths[t][idx.sfa]), so the debt/GDP projection now carries the deficit–debt discrepancy forward with its own dynamics and bands instead of being defaulted to 0. (fiscalAccounting.ts still contains a dormant idx.debtService branch for backward compat; the shipped metas leave it unset.)

The debt recursion (per quarter, computeFiscalBlock): 

pb_t  = FREV_GDP_t − FPEXP_GDP_t                                  (% of GDP, annual)
gNomQ = 100·Δlog(realGDP) + 100·Δlog(deflator)                   (% per quarter, off the level path)
d_t   = d_{t-1}·(1 + EFFI_t/400)/(1 + gNomQ_t/100) − pb_t/4 + sfa_t/4     (debt/GDP, %)
Debt-to-GDP is DERIVED (never an estimated variable); d0/sfaDefault seed and fall-back it.

SFA = the EXACT app residual (data-build only — see §C). For every non-COVID quarter the build rearranges that same recursion onto the observed gross-debt/GDP path: 

snowball_t = ((1 + EFFI_t/400)/(1 + gNomQ_t/100) − 1)·d_{t-1}
SFA_t      = 4·(d_obs_t − d_{t-1} − snowball_t + pb_t/4)          (% of GDP, annual)
so rolling the recursion forward with this SFA reproduces the observed debt path to machine precision (closure checked < 1e-6pp). The COVID quarters are set to NaN (NOT interpolated — an interpolated value cannot equal the residual) so the COVID-as-missing Gibbs imputes them with the rest of the block. SFA captures the deficit–debt-discrepancy / gross-debt-perimeter seam (intragovernmental holdings, federal-flows-vs-gross-debt). Deployed sfaDefault (≈ non-COVID SFA mean, % GDP): US 1.7518, EA 0.325, JP 2.233, UK 3.1487, CA 3.191, CN 4.017 (verified in each bloc's shipped fiscal_meta.json). The SFA estimation-sample length (number of non-COVID quarters with an SFA observation) is US 41, EA 21, JP 21, UK 23, CA 23, CN 18 — these are SFA-column sample lengths, not the bloc's spec variable count n (which is US 41, EA 24, JP 23, UK 25, CA 25, CN 20). Do not conflate the two.

A checkable walkthrough lives in docs/fiscal_accounting_walkthrough_US_UK.xlsx (built by pipeline/build_fiscal_walkthrough_xlsx.py) alongside the source docs/Fiscal_Accounting_US_UK_NSA.xlsx.

The estimation tooling lives in pipeline/:

  • build_country_data.py — pulls FRED-based countries (EA/UK/JP/CA …) → raw CSV/.mat.
  • build_oecd_eurostat.py / build_oecd_batch.py — pull the OECD (EU/EFTA + others) economies.
  • build_us_auto.py — hands-off US builder (pulls everything from FRED + builds the fiscal drivers).
  • build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py (cc ∈ us/ea/jp/uk/ca/cn) — the 4-var fiscal-block builder: writes {FREV_GDP, FPEXP_GDP, EFFI, SFA} into the bloc's country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json (the exact-residual SFA + COVID-NaN recipe) and rewrites fiscal_meta/<CC>.json (see §C). Data build only — no estimation, no deploy.
  • export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m — the GVAR-EM iterative estimator (despite the 24 in the name it produces the 33-bloc coupled set with the US ENDOGENOUS and the euro area as one EA aggregate). Set env US_ENDOGENOUS=1 (requires a US weights row). It runs a parfor Jacobi sweep over the blocs (MAXIT iterations to the Monte-Carlo noise floor, TOL=0.03), each bloc estimated by COVID-as-missing Gibbs (cgl_bvarV5). MAXIT=1 = single-country (separated) mode for standalone blocs / euro members.
  • export_bvar_us_covid.m / export_bvar_oecd_covid.m / export_bvar_country.m — single-country exporters (US-only, OECD, generic).
  • country_specs.ts (variable lists for spec.json) / app/src/lib/data/countries.ts (the country registry / dropdown).

API key: the FRED pullers read FRED_API_KEY from the environment. Always pass your own: FRED_API_KEY=<your_key> python3 pipeline/.... Do not commit a key (app/.env.local is the place for local secrets and is git-ignored). Get a free key at https://fred.stlouisfed.org/docs/api/api_key.html.

Network: the FRED/IMF pulls need outbound HTTPS. If you run inside a sandbox that blocks the network, disable it for the pull step.

docs.title: A. Update an existing country (worked example: the US)
docs.text: The US is fully automated end-to-end — this is also the test case for this runbook. Because the US is now an endogenous GVAR bloc, a US data refresh is a GVAR re-estimation (§B6 path), not a single-country export. The single-country export_bvar_us_covid.m still exists for an isolated US-only model, but the deployed US is produced by the GVAR-EM loop.

docs.title: A1. Rebuild the US dataset (macro + fiscal)
docs.text:
cd "<repo root>"
FRED_API_KEY=<your_key> python3 pipeline/build_us_auto.py    # macro + fiscal drivers, COVID handled
python3 pipeline/build_fiscal_us_4var.py                     # SFA → exact app residual, COVID-NaN

build_us_auto.py pulls every US series on the auto-detected quarterly grid (currently to 2025Q4) and assembles the raw matrix. build_fiscal_us_4var.py then overwrites only the SFA column in pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_US_glob.json with the exact one-step residual of the app recursion (reading FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/EFFI + the real-GDP & deflator level columns the app reads, plus the observed debt/GDP from pipeline/fiscal_raw/us_grid.json), NaN-ing the COVID window (2020Q2–2021Q4), runs the < 1e-6pp closure check, and rewrites pipeline/fiscal_meta/US.json (idx{gdp,deflator,revenue,primaryExp,effRate,sfa}, d0, sfaDefault = non-COVID SFA mean).

docs.title: A2. Re-estimate the coupled GVAR (the US is endogenous)
docs.text:
# In MATLAB, with the repo's BVAR/ on path (the .m adds it):
US_ENDOGENOUS=1 matlab -batch "run('pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"

This estimates all 33 blocs (adding/refreshing the US changes everyone's trade-weighted RoW, so the whole set is re-estimated), with the US folded into the convergence loop with its ROW*_US stars. It saves the workspace to /tmp/gvar24_workspace.mat and writes the full JSON contract per bloc to /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/. Confirm the printed per-iteration max star change falls below TOL (convergence) and the printed GDP1q central forecasts are sane.

For an isolated US-only refresh (no GVAR), export_bvar_us_covid.m (cgl_bvarV5, sur=0, empirical-Bayes lambda/mu, 1000 draws / 500 burn-in) still writes a standalone US export to /tmp/us_export/. But the deployed US is the endogenous GVAR bloc, so prefer the loop above.

docs.title: A3. Sanity-check, then port over the live data
docs.text:
Inspect /tmp/gvar24_export/ first (never overwrite live data blind). Then port every bloc (6-sigfig compaction is the deployed convention) and refresh the fiscal meta:

cd "<repo root>"
python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py                 # all blocs → app/public/data/bvar/<CC> (6 sig-figs)
cp pipeline/fiscal_meta/US.json app/public/data/bvar/US/fiscal_meta.json

docs.title: A4. Regenerate the coupling artifact
docs.text:
Any change to a deployed bloc invalidates the precomputed coupling (it is keyed by modelSig):

cd app
npx vite-node scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs    # → public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz

docs.title: A5. Run the green gate
docs.text:
cd app
npx tsc --noEmit
npx vitest run                         # all must pass; watch the GVAR + scaling canaries
BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario npm run build

If n changed, the canary test scenario-scale-convention.test.ts may carry a fixture pinned to the old layout — regenerate it (the pipeline has _verify/gt.m, which resolves columns by variable id, so it is layout-robust) and re-run. Everything else is index-agnostic.

docs.title: A6. Deploy
docs.text:
cd "<repo root>"
git add app/public/data/bvar
git commit -m "Update US + coupled GVAR (data through <quarter>)"
git push bvarscenario main              # host auto-builds on push

The push to bvarscenario (matyasfarkas/BVARScenarioTool) triggers a GitHub Actions run that builds the static app (BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario) and copies app/out/* into the matyasfarkas/starter-hugo-academic website repo's static/bvar-scenario/. That repo is a Hugo Academic site published to GitHub Pages, which rebuilds and serves it at the /bvar-scenario path. (The Action itself has no Hugo step.) Hugo is pinned to 0.122.0 in the website repo (0.123+ removed getCSV and broke the wowchemy/academic theme — do not bump it).

Done = the gate is green and the push succeeded. Open the live site (the /bvar-scenario path of the academic site on GitHub Pages) and confirm the US baseline, a quick conditioning scenario, and a "Solve full GVAR" run look sane.

docs.title: B. Add a brand-new country
docs.text: Use an existing FRED country (EA/UK/JP/CA) as the template.

docs.title: B1. Choose the variables and find FRED IDs
docs.text: Decide the variable block (a typical small open economy uses ~12–16: real GDP, consumption, investment, government spending, exports, imports, industrial production, unemployment, CPI/HICP, a short rate, a 10Y yield, the nominal effective exchange rate). For each, find the FRED series ID and tag it log (levels/indices) or lin (rates/ratios). Prefer quarterly, seasonally adjusted, long-history series. If a country's data is not cleanly on FRED (e.g. China), source it from IMF IFS/WEO or OECD and assemble the same raw matrix by hand — but use real observed data, never synthetic placeholders.

docs.title: B2. Add the country to the data builder
docs.text:
Add a block to COUNTRIES in pipeline/build_country_data.py (mirror the EA entry):

"XX": {
  "name": "Country Name",
  "start": "YYYY-01-01",
  "domestic": [
    ("FRED_ID", "GDP_XX", "XX Real GDP", "log", False),
    ... one tuple per variable: (fred_id, internal_name, display_name, 'log'|'lin', is_financial)
  ],
},

Then run FRED_API_KEY=<key> python3 pipeline/build_country_data.py to pull the raw data.

docs.title: B3. Reflect the spec + register the country
docs.text:
Add the matching variable list to pipeline/country_specs.ts (used to emit spec.json) and add a one-line entry to the registry app/src/lib/data/countries.ts:

{ code: 'XX', name: 'Country Name', group: 'oecd' },   // group: 'core' | 'oecd' | 'em'

That single entry is what puts the country in the dropdown (in the "Other countries" optgroup until it becomes a GVAR bloc) — the engine reads the real n from companion_mean.json, so no size metadata is needed here. (The legacy app/public/data/meta/countries.json is display-only and not required.)

docs.title: B4. Estimate + export
docs.text:
Estimate with the COVID-as-missing Gibbs sampler (cgl_bvarV5) and emit the full JSON contract (§"What a country needs"). For a standalone single-country bloc the cleanest template is pipeline/export_bvar_oecd_covid.m / export_bvar_country.m; for a GVAR bloc use export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m (§B6). The exporter produces companion_mean, baseline (growth) and baseline_levels, historical and historical_levels, transformations, and companion_draws. Standard config: the 2020 COVID quarters are NaN'd (window self-calibrated to the 2020Q2 GDP trough, searched within 2019Q4–2021Q1) and DK-imputed; lambda/mu are empirical-Bayes (MH on the completed-data marginal likelihood); 'sur',0,'noc',1,'Ndraws',1000,'Ndrawsdiscard',500.

If the estimator returns a singular/NaN SIGMA, the data is the cause: a NaN in a discontinued series, or near-collinear national-accounts identities. Drop the offending leading-NaN rows and/or fix a multicollinear variable. With COVID imputed, the empirical-Bayes lambda optimum is well-behaved (estimated ~0.24–0.56), so a fixed lamfix is no longer needed; if you do pin one, keep noc=1 or sur=1 free or lambda degenerates to NaN.

docs.title: B5. Place files, regenerate coupling (if GVAR), gate, deploy
docs.text:
cp /tmp/<XX>_export/*.json app/public/data/bvar/XX/      # or: python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py
cd app
# (if XX is a GVAR bloc, regenerate the artifact — see B6.4)
# npx vite-node scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs
npx tsc --noEmit && npx vitest run && BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario npm run build
cd .. && git add app/public/data/bvar/XX app/src/lib/data/countries.ts pipeline/*.py pipeline/country_specs.ts
git commit -m "Add XX country model" && git push bvarscenario main

docs.title: B6. (Optional) GVAR integration
docs.text:
To link the new country into the 33-bloc coupled GVAR (a Rest-of-World bloc rather than a standalone single-country model). Caveat: keep the euro area aggregated — adding the euro members as their own coupled blocs makes the system non-stationary, which is why the 17 euro members ship as standalone models (no weights row) and EA is one bloc. Verify the spectral radius stays < 1 after re-estimation (it is 0.924 for the current 33-bloc / dim-2060 endogenous-US set).

  1. Add the bloc to the trade-weights file. Add XX (with its ISO-3 code) to the bloc list in app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_24bloc.json (regenerated by pipeline/build_trade_weights_full.py / pipeline/build_trade_weights.py from IMF IMTS; the US own-reporter row is built by pipeline/build_us_weights_row.py). Every bloc's row is renormalized over the active set, so adding a country changes everyone's trade weights.
  2. Give the bloc its foreign + global variables. Its spec.json must carry the 3 Rest-of-World stars (ROWDEM_XX/ROWINF_XX/ROWFIN_XX, block:'row') and the 4 shared global commons (GLB_OIL/GLB_USLR/GLB_USBAA/GLB_USEQ, block:'global'). The estimator export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m builds the stars from partners' completed series and iterates the foreign components to a cross-country fixed point — re-estimating every bloc (adding a country changes everyone's trade-weighted RoW). The raw inputs are the pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<XX>_glob.json files (domestic + the 4 globals; the stars are appended at export time, not stored in the glob). Run with US_ENDOGENOUS=1 so the US stays a coupled bloc.
  3. No frontend code edits are needed for topology. The GVAR engine self-configures from the deployed spec.json files + weights_24bloc.json (configureGVARTopology/deriveBlocIdx/US_ENDOGENOUS in gvarSolve.ts): a bloc becomes active automatically once it ships block:'row' vars and has a weights row. Do not hand-edit IDX/W/COUPLED_COUNTRIES.
  4. Regenerate the precomputed coupling. After re-estimating + porting, rebuild the coupling artifact: 
    cd app && npx vite-node scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs
    It builds the augmented-state coupling M in JS (DK-smoother probes), inverts (I − M) via a MATLAB BLAS batch (app/scripts/invert_coupling.m), and writes the gzipped artifact app/public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz directly ({modelSig, pinStructure:'', dim, rhoSpectral, M, invImM}, 6-sig-fig). The plain .json is removed/skipped because at this bloc count it would exceed GitHub's 100 MB per-file limit (gzip brings the current dim-2060 artifact to ~30 MB); the loader decompresses it in-browser via the native DecompressionStream (falling back to a plain .json only if the .gz is absent). The browser solves x = (I − M)⁻¹d from this artifact; without it the in-browser inverse (~19 min at 24 blocs; far worse at the augmented endogenous dim) is infeasible. The artifact is keyed to the data by modelSig, so it must be regenerated whenever any deployed bloc changes.
  5. Add tests. Extend the GVAR canaries in app/src/__tests__/compute/ (e.g. gvar-closed-form-routing.test.ts, gvar-coupling-artifact.test.ts, gvar-derived-topology.test.ts, and the endogenous-US / closed-form stress tests) to cover the new bloc.

Two-tier solve UX, drag off the heavy path (FYI, no per-country work). On the live ~50-bloc US-endogenous panel every mean solve is multi-second (~4.5 s warm / ~13 s cold), so no GVAR solve fires during a drag: the drag path is a cheap conditioning preview (the conditioned dot snaps to the cursor instantly via conditionedDotY.ts), exactly one cheap DIRECT first-order solve (solveGVARDirect, affine per-probe kernels, numerically exact) fires on pointer-up, and the complete fixed point + uncertainty bands run only on the "Solve full GVAR" button / top-bar Compute. A "computing…" badge and a direct/full mode pill are shown; the ≈95 MB coupling artifact is stripped from direct-mode worker posts; and per-chart React.memo means one conditioning edit repaints one panel, not all ~35. New blocs inherit this automatically.

docs.title: C. Add / refresh a 4-variable fiscal block
docs.text: The 6 fiscal countries (US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN) carry {FREV_GDP, FPEXP_GDP, EFFI, SFA} (block:'fiscal', all lin). The dedicated builders are pipeline/build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py. They are data-build only (no estimation, no deploy, idempotent).

docs.title: C1. What the builder does
docs.text:
Each build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py:

  1. Computes the four columns from the country's raw fiscal source (e.g. pipeline/fiscal_raw/<cc>_grid.json
  2. the macro glob), with the same scaling as the legacy build_fiscal_drivers.py columns: FREV_GDP = 100·rev4/ngdp4, FPEXP_GDP = 100·(exp−interest)4/ngdp4, EFFI = 100·4·interest_t/gross_debt_{t-1} (% p.a.).
  3. Computes SFA = the exact one-step residual that closes the app recursion on the OBSERVED debt/GDP path (reading EFFI, pb = FREV − FPEXP, and gNomQ = 100·Δlog(realGDP) + 100·Δlog(deflator) off the SAME glob columns the app reads), then NaN-s the COVID window so the COVID-as-missing Gibbs imputes those quarters (do NOT interpolate). It runs a closure check (< 1e-6pp) and aborts if the residual does not reproduce the observed debt path.
  4. Edits in place pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json — replacing the prior fiscal columns at their positions and appending the 4th (SFA) column with block:'fiscal' tags — and rewrites pipeline/fiscal_meta/<CC>.json so idx = {gdp, deflator, revenue, primaryExp, effRate, sfa} (NOT debtService), with d0 (last observed debt/GDP) and sfaDefault (non-COVID SFA mean).

docs.title: C2. Run it
docs.text:
cd "<repo root>"
python3 pipeline/build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py     # cc ∈ us ea jp uk ca cn ; edits glob + fiscal_meta in place

(The US builder, build_fiscal_us_4var.py, overwrites only the SFA column and leaves FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/EFFI byte-identical; the others — ca/cn/ea/jp/uk — additionally revert DSVC → EFFI and append SFA.)

docs.title: C3. Re-estimate, port, deploy
docs.text: The fiscal columns are part of the bloc's domestic/fiscal block, so they are estimated by the same GVAR-EM loop (or single-country exporter). Continue with §A2–A6 (re-estimate via export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m, port with port_gvar_exports.py, copy the refreshed pipeline/fiscal_meta/<CC>.json to app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/fiscal_meta.json, regenerate the coupling artifact, gate, deploy). The shipped fiscal_meta.json is what the frontend reads — porting the bloc JSON alone is not enough; the meta must be copied too.

docs.title: C4. MANDATORY RULE — regenerate fiscal_meta indices after ANY variable-order change
docs.text:
RULE (must run after any re-estimation or port that can change a fiscal bloc's variable order or count): run node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs.

fiscal_meta.json stores the fiscal-driver columns by POSITION (idx = {gdp, deflator, revenue, primaryExp, effRate, sfa}), and the debt recursion in fiscalAccounting.ts reads the level path at exactly those positional indices. Any change to a bloc's variable order or count silently re-points those indices at the WRONG columns. This is not hypothetical: the 33-bloc GVAR-EM refit inserted variables ahead of the fiscal block in every spec except US, which re-pointed (e.g.) EA's effRate onto the equity index (~col 537) and made the EA/UK debt-to-GDP path explode. The porter does not regenerate the meta, so the stale indices ship unless you fix them.

pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs re-resolves the fiscal indices by variable id (the canonical, order-independent source) from the deployed spec.json and rewrites fiscal_meta.json for all six fiscal blocs (US EA UK CA JP CN), preserving the model parameters d0/sfaDefault (verified — it only rewrites meta.idx). It is idempotent.

cd "<repo root>"
node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs
# prints per bloc:  US: ok {…}   |   EA: FIXED {old} -> {new}   …   N fiscal_meta.json files corrected.

Run it after every port that touches a fiscal bloc (it is cheap and idempotent — when nothing moved it prints ok for all six and changes nothing). The fiscal conditioning/back-cast canaries (fiscalAccounting.test.ts, fiscal-backcast.test.ts, fiscal-conditioning-integration.test.ts) are the backstop, but the explicit re-index is the fix — a green build with stale indices is still wrong.

docs.title: Quick reference — files touched when adding/updating a country
docs.text: Concern File(s) Data pull pipeline/build_country_data.py (FRED) / build_oecd_eurostat.py (OECD) / build_us_auto.py (US) Spec definition pipeline/country_specs.ts Fiscal block (US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN) pipeline/build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py → pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json + pipeline/fiscal_meta/<CC>.json (idx{gdp,deflator,revenue,primaryExp,effRate,sfa}); ship as app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/fiscal_meta.json Fiscal meta re-index (RULE) pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs — re-resolve fiscal indices BY ID from the deployed spec; run after ANY variable-order change (§C4) Estimate + export JSON pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m (GVAR-EM, US_ENDOGENOUS=1, 33 blocs) / export_bvar_us_covid.m / export_bvar_oecd_covid.m / export_bvar_country.m Port exports → live data pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py (6-sig-fig) → app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/*.json Country registry (dropdown) app/src/lib/data/countries.ts (selector groups via isGvarCountry in app/src/components/controls/TopBar.tsx) GVAR weights / RoW (optional) app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_24bloc.json (33-bloc weights incl. US row; name is historical), pipeline/build_trade_weights_full.py, pipeline/build_us_weights_row.py, pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m GVAR coupling regen (optional) app/scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs → app/public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz (gzipped, dim-2060 augmented endogenous-US, ρ≈0.924) Topology (auto, no edit) app/src/lib/compute/gvarSolve.ts self-configures from spec + weights (US_ENDOGENOUS, configureGVARTopology) Fiscal recursion (read-only) app/src/lib/compute/fiscalAccounting.ts (computeFiscalBlock, SFA via idx.sfa) Tests app/src/__tests__/compute/ (canaries + GVAR closed-form / coupling / topology / endogenous-US stress)

docs.title: Codebase Guide (for AI agents and new developers)
docs.text:
This document is a dense, accurate map of the Monetary-Financial Model (a global projection and scenario tool, built on an EM-Bayesian Global VAR engine) for an automated agent or a developer who needs to make a correct change quickly. It explains where things are, why they are shaped the way they are, and the conventions you must not break. Read it before editing compute or pipeline code.

Companion docs: README.md (overview), USER_GUIDE.md (UI), fiscal-block-methodology.md (fiscal math), ADDING_A_COUNTRY.md (human runbook for data/model updates).

docs.title: 1. What the app is
docs.text:
A static, client-side web app: a Next.js (App Router) frontend that does all econometrics in the browser in TypeScript. There is no required backend for the core features — the pre-estimated models are static JSON under app/public/data/ and every Kalman filter / smoother / matrix op runs on the user's machine. A FastAPI backend exists only for computing DSGE IRFs from custom policy rules and is not needed for normal use.

The economic content is a three-layer methodology:

  1. Conditioning — impose user paths on any BVAR variable; solve the conditional forecast with a Durbin–Koopman (DK) simulation smoother on the BVAR companion form.
  2. Counterfactual — overlay a DSGE-model policy counterfactual (MMB IRFs + a policy rule, or the Barnichon–Mesters optimal-policy QP).
  3. Uncertainty — posterior-draw fan charts.

On top of these sit three extensions: a 6-country fiscal block (US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN — derived debt/GDP), a 33-bloc GVAR layer with a fully endogenous US (cross-country spillovers across an endogenous US bloc + an EA aggregate + 31 Rest-of-World blocs, ~90% of global demand), and a long-run anchor (entropic tilt of the predictive density toward a user-set long-run target — longRunTilt.ts).

🔴 TOPOLOGY CHANGE — the US is now an ENDOGENOUS coupled bloc, NOT the frozen anchor. The deployed US/spec.json carries n=41: its domestic + fiscal vars plus 3 ROW*_US trade-weighted stars (cols 38–40, block:'row') plus the 4 global commons it determines. With a US row in weights_24bloc.json, configureGVARTopology sets US_ENDOGENOUS = true: the US now drives RoW AND receives RoW→US feedback through its own stars. The frozen-anchor code paths still exist (gated on !US_ENDOGENOUS) and stay byte-identical for legacy/US-less-stars data, but the live deployment runs the endogenous path. The closed form is solved on an augmented state (stars ++ US-global deltas, dim=2060, ρ(M)=0.924041 in the live artifact). See §5/§5a.

The app ships ~50 economies in one registry (app/src/lib/data/countries.ts): 6 core majors, 34 OECD members, 10 non-OECD EM. 33 of them are GVAR-coupled — the endogenous US bloc + an EA aggregate bloc + 31 RoW blocs listed in weights_24bloc.json (the filename is stale; it now holds 33 blocs, US first, with a US weights row that activates the endogenous coupling). Everything else is a standalone single-country model. In particular, the 17 euro-area members (DE FR IT ES NL AT BE FI GR IE LU PT SI SK EE LT LV) are standalone (domestic + 4 global commons + trade-weighted foreign stars, estimated in separation), not in the coupled GVAR and with no weights row. The reason: the fully-disaggregated euro GVAR was non-convergent (ρ(M)=1.089>1); aggregating the euro area into one EA bloc restores stationarity (ρ(M)=0.726) — this is why EA is a single bloc and the members are standalone.

docs.title: 2. Repository layout
docs.text:
app/                         Next.js frontend (the product)
  src/
    app/page.tsx             Top-level layout; loads BVAR + GVAR data; mounts the panels
    components/
      charts/ChartGrid.tsx   THE central component: charts, conditioning, fiscal block, band compute
      controls/              TopBar (country selector — grouped "GVAR"/"Other countries" optgroups
                             driven by isGvarCountry, plain names), ControlPanel, ScenarioBar,
                             SliderInput, ExperimentalToggle
    lib/
      compute/               All econometrics (see §4–§8)
      data/                  loader.ts (single-country), gvarLoader.ts (loads all GVAR blocs +
                             the precomputed coupling), countries.ts (the ~50-economy registry)
    store/useStore.ts        Zustand store: scenarios, gvarPins, flags
    __tests__/               Vitest suites (see §11)
  public/data/
    bvar/<CC>/                       Per-country model JSON (see §10)
    bvar/gvar/weights_24bloc.json    33-bloc list + row-normalized trade weights (topology source;
                                     filename is stale — it holds 33 blocs, not 24)
    bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz  GZIPPED precomputed cross-country coupling (M, (I−M)⁻¹, ρ) —
                                     interactivity enabler; decompressed in-browser via DecompressionStream
    dsge/                            MMB model catalog, rules, IRFs
    meta/countries.json              Legacy country metadata (display only; countries.ts is the source)
pipeline/                    Data + estimation (Python pull, MATLAB estimate, export to JSON)
docs/                        This guide + methodology + runbooks
backend/                     FastAPI (optional, custom DSGE rules only)

docs.title: 3. The render/compute flow (one pass)
docs.text:
ChartGrid is the hub. Per render it computes:

  • condForecast = useConditionalForecast(...) — single-country conditional forecast (mean + level deviation + scenario levels) via the DK smoother.
  • gvarFc = useGvarForecast(...) — for any active GVAR bloc (the endogenous US + EA + the 31 RoW blocs), the cross-country propagated forecast (solved in a Web Worker). null for a non-GVAR (standalone) country. Under the endogenous-US topology it returns a two-tier result: a fast mode:'direct' first-order preview on every pin edit, and the complete mode:'full' fixed point on the explicit "Solve full GVAR" button (solveFullToken) — see §5a/§8.
  • effective = (isGvar && gvarFc && !hasFiscalTarget) ? {...condForecast, ...gvarFc fields} : condForecast — the path actually drawn. This is the single source of truth for "the displayed scenario line".
  • fiscalBlock (the 6 fiscal countries US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN) — derived PB / debt-service / debt-to-GDP from effective.scenarioLevels by the accounting identity.

Refs gvarBandRef and fiscalMeanRef snapshot effective and fiscalBlock so the band-computation effect (which fires only on Compute, deps [uncertaintyComputing]) reads fresh values without re-subscribing.

docs.title: 3a. Per-chart render memoization (so one edit repaints ≈1 of ~35 panels)
docs.text:
A single conditioning edit changes scenarios, re-running the monolithic charts useMemo → every chart object gets a fresh identity. Absent memoization, Plotly would repaint all ~35 panels for one dragged point. Two levers (in ChartGrid.tsx) collapse that to ≈1 panel:

  • reconcileCharts(next, prev, onRebuilt?) (ChartGrid.tsx:205) reuses the PRIOR render's chart object reference for any panel whose freshly-built content is deep-equal (chartDeepEqual, :173), keyed by chartVar.bvarIdx. It returns the prior array outright when every element kept its reference (stable outer identity → no [charts] effect churn). The data is byte-identical — this only preserves reference identity where the content did not move.
  • MemoDraggableChart = memo(DraggableChart) (:2523) skips the re-render entirely when a panel's chart reference and the grid's useCallback'd handler props are all ===.

So a conditioning edit (or a GVAR spillover) repaints only the conditioned panel + any partner panels that actually moved. Pinned by chartgrid-render-memo.test.ts via the exported chartRebuildCounter / draggableChartRenderCounter instrumentation (test-only, ~zero production cost). This is the render optimization; the compute optimization (keeping the heavy GVAR solve off the drag path) is §5a/§8b.

docs.title: 4. The compute engine (app/src/lib/compute/)
docs.text: File Role matrix.ts Flat Float64Array row-major matrices + Cholesky/solve/Toeplitz. All linear algebra. kalman.ts DK Kalman filter + simulation smoother. conditionalForecast (conditional MEAN, no shock), bvarForecastDraw (per-draw unconditional, no shock), dkSimulationDraw (adds innovation). affineBlocKernels = the GVAR perf kernel: conditionalForecast is affine in the pinned VALUES for a fixed pin MASK, so it computes the time-invariant filter/smoother factors ONCE (one filter+smoother pass on the probe baseline) and makes each per-period unit-pin probe a cheap delta forward/backward matvec recursion — replaces (#cols·H) full smoother passes with O(#cols·H) matvec backward passes, byte-equal arithmetic. This is the engine behind the endogenous-US kernel cache (see §5a). useConditionalForecast.ts React hook: builds companion form, runs conditionalForecast, returns { forecasts, levelDeviation, scenarioLevels, counterfactualLevels, ... }. Handles counterfactual modes + fiscal constraints. taylor-rule.ts The 9 preset rules + custom Taylor rule. barnichon-mesters.ts B&M (2023) optimal-policy QP. fiscalAccounting.ts computeFiscalBlock — the debt/GDP accounting recursion, 6 countries (US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN; see §7). longRunTilt.ts After-estimation entropic tilt (PLR) toward a user long-run target — per-draw weights, no re-estimation (see §8a). gvarDragState.ts Module-level signal bus (drag begin/end + compute-in-flight flags) off the zustand store, so a drag does not re-render the grid. useSyncExternalStore lets only the badge subscribe; useGvarForecast defers the heavy solve to drag-END (see §5a/§8b). ../conditionedDotY.ts The optimistic conditioned-dot display↔stored inverse pair (conditionedDotDisplayY / conditioningDeviationFromDisplayY) so a pinned marker snaps to the click instantly (see §8b). Note: lives at app/src/lib/conditionedDotY.ts (not under compute/).

docs.title: 5. The GVAR engine (app/src/lib/compute/ + lib/data/gvarLoader.ts)
docs.text:
33-bloc GVAR (~90% of global demand). The US is a fully endogenous coupled bloc (n=41): it carries its domestic + fiscal vars, the 4 global commons as its OWN endogenous variables (the US determines oil / US10Y / USBAA / USeq), AND 3 ROW*_US trade-weighted stars (cols 38–40, block:'row') from a US trade-weights row — so the US drives RoW through the globals/its linked series AND receives RoW→US feedback through its own stars, iterating in the cross-country fixed point exactly like a RoW bloc. The other 32 blocs are an EA aggregate plus 31 Rest-of-World blocs (JP, UK, CA, CN, CH, KR, MX, AU, TR, SE, PL, NO, DK, IN, BR, RU, ID, SA, AR, ZA, TH, MY + the 9 newer non-euro economies CL, CO, CR, CZ, HU, IL, IS, NZ, TW). EA and all 31 RoW blocs each carry two kinds of foreign variable:

  • 3 trade-weighted stars (block:'row'): ROWDEM/ROWINF/ROWFIN = trade-weighted partner real-GDP growth / inflation / short rate, renormalized over partners present each quarter.
  • 4 global commons (block:'global', the SAME 4 ids in every bloc, US-determined): GLB_OIL (WTI), GLB_USLR (US 10Y), GLB_USBAA (US BAA → credit spread), GLB_USEQ (US equity). All 4 globals are isFinancial:true in every bloc (verified across US/EA/JP/CN/…), so the COVID-as-missing window keeps them OBSERVED (not macro-masked / imputed) — a coherence fix that applies in particular to GLB_OIL and GLB_USEQ, which were previously mis-tagged: oil and US equity are market data and must condition the COVID imputation, not be holes in it.

How the globals propagate (applyGlobalPins in gvarSolve.ts). Each RoW bloc PINS its 4 global columns to its own baseline + the US scenario DEVIATION of the matching global (matched by id, 1:1): pin = cBaseLvl[col] + (usScenLvl[usCol] − usBaseLvl[usCol]). So a shock to US oil / US financial conditions moves the SAME common delta into every RoW bloc, and each bloc's domestic vars respond via its own estimated coefficients. deriveBlocIdx exposes the global columns as IDX[c].glob (id → column); a bloc with no globals gets glob = {} and applyGlobalPins is a no-op (the legacy globals-free path stays byte-identical).

Frozen-anchor vs endogenous-US treatment of the US global delta. In the frozen-anchor path (!US_ENDOGENOUS) the US is solved FIRST and frozen, so the US scenario deviation is a constant: the global pin is a US→RoW exogenous forcing that enters the per-bloc forcing/baseline d, NOT the RoW↔RoW coupling M (the globals are held fixed across the per-period M probes). Under the endogenous-US path (the live deployment), the US scenario deviation of each global is itself part of the fixed point (the US responds to RoW feedback), so the US-global delta becomes an augmented state variable and its coupling enters M via the buildLinearSystemEndo channels (2)+(3) — see §5a. Indirect effects (globals move a bloc's macro → its stars → other blocs) always flow through the existing star coupling M and are not duplicated.

🔴 DESIGN DECISION — pin the US DELTA, not the US LEVEL (load-bearing). Each RoW bloc estimated its OWN baseline path for the globals (weakly-exogenous regressors), and those baseline LEVELS differ from the US's by tens of 100·log units on oil. Pinning the US level would inject that entire baseline gap as a spurious delta even at ZERO scenario (every country would jump the moment it was selected). Pinning the US delta on each bloc's own baseline gives exactly zero spillover at zero scenario AND an identical common shock under conditioning — mirroring how the star pins use baseRoW + partnerDelta, not the partner level. The first (wrong) implementation pinned the level; it was caught by the CN-deflation and zero-scenario tests. Verified on the real GVAR data: a US GLB_OIL pin yields an identical oil delta across all RoW blocs to ≤1e-9, and a zero scenario yields zero RoW spillover.

The scenario deviation is a linear fixed point x = M·x + d (baseline is data-driven, computed once). The engine solves it in closed form as x = (I − M)⁻¹·d. Under the live endogenous-US topology the state is augmented (RoW+US stars ++ the US-global deltas), so dim = 2060 and ρ(M) = 0.924041 < 1 in the deployed artifact (see §5a for the channel construction; the exact value moves slightly with each refit, what is load-bearing is < 1). M and its inverse come from the precomputed coupling artifact (bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz, GZIPPED and decompressed in-browser via the native DecompressionStream; a plain .json fallback is tried for environments without it) — building/inverting M at this scale is many minutes in-browser, so the artifact is mandatory for interactivity, not optional. The shipped artifact carries modelSig (33-bloc, US:41), pinStructure: '' (the empty/baseline structure), dim, rhoSpectral, M, AND the BLAS-computed invImM. Gauss–Seidel (solveGVAR, which now iterates the US too when endogenous) is kept as a cross-check; closed-form ≡ iterative to ~1e-6 over the full conditioning battery. (Historical note: the pre-endogenous frozen-anchor coupling had dim≈1920, ρ(M)≈0.726 — those figures no longer describe the deployed artifact.)

Topology is self-configured, not hardcoded. configureGVARTopology(specs) (in gvarSolve.ts) reads each deployed spec.json + weights_24bloc.json (the filename is stale — it now lists all 33 blocs) and, for every candidate bloc, derives its gdp/price/rate columns (deriveBlocIdx, mirroring the estimator's findcol prefix order), its RoW-star indices, and its global columns (IDX[c].glob). A RoW bloc is active iff its spec carries block:'row' vars AND it has a weights row. The US is endogenous iff its OWN spec carries the 3 ROW*_US stars AND weights_24bloc.json has a US row — this sets the module flag US_ENDOGENOUS. Two derived sets are mutated in place: - ROW_COUNTRIES = every bloc except the US (the weight-row + global-pin recipients; the US is the global SOURCE, never a global RECEIVER). - COUPLED_COUNTRIES = the blocs that iterate in the fixed point: ROW_COUNTRIES when the US is frozen, or ['US', ...ROW_COUNTRIES] (US first) when US_ENDOGENOUS.

IDX, W, ROW_COUNTRIES, and COUPLED_COUNTRIES are mutated in place at load (importers hold live references); W is keyed for every COUPLED bloc (the US too, when endogenous), renormalised over the active panel. gvarLoader re-configures from whatever blocs are actually deployed. Never hardcode bloc indices or a bloc subset — read IDX/ROW_COUNTRIES/COUPLED_COUNTRIES at runtime.

File Role gvarSolve.ts The engine. configureGVARTopology/deriveBlocIdx (self-config from spec+weights; sets US_ENDOGENOUS), IDX/W/ROW_COUNTRIES/COUPLED_COUNTRIES (live, mutated in place), solveGVARClosedForm (the production entry — routes to the endogenous augmented closed form when US_ENDOGENOUS, else the frozen-anchor (I−M)⁻¹d, both with the amortized coupling cache), solveGVARClosedFormEndo/buildLinearSystemEndo/blocKernels (the augmented-state endogenous path, §5a), solveGVARDirect (fast first-order preview from prewarmed kernels), prewarmGVARKernels/prewarmGVARKernelsChunk (off-critical-path kernel-cache warm), solveGVAR (Gauss–Seidel cross-check; iterates the US when endogenous), buildGVARCouplingMEndo/buildGVARCouplingEndo (export-time M / coupling builds), seedGVARCouplingCache/hydrateGVARCoupling (consume the precomputed artifact), solveViewedScenLvl (viewed-bloc-only; US short-circuits the sweep ONLY when frozen). buildScenarioPins.ts Converts own-pins into the conditional-path matrices the solve consumes. runGvarSolve.ts Pure mean solve wrapper (worker + tests); routes through the closed-form solve. runGvarBandSolve.ts Pure per-draw band solve (experimental). Re-solves the GVAR per posterior draw → conditional cloud. mapGvarSolved.ts Maps a solved level path into display space (same forecasts/levelDeviation/scenarioLevels shape as useConditionalForecast). gvarWorker.ts / gvarMessages.ts Web-Worker glue + message types. useGvarForecast.ts React hook: debounced, stale-guarded worker solve; main-thread fallback. gvarLoader.ts Loads every deployed GVAR bloc's raw JSON (GVAR_BLOC_CANDIDATES, 404-tolerant) + the coupling artifact; re-runs configureGVARTopology.

Special-case handling baked into the topology config: CN's GDP is real (since the 2026-06 surgical CN-only re-estimate — NOMINAL_GDP_BLOCS is now empty) → its DEM contribution reads gdp directly, not the deflated gdp − price; rate-less blocs (CH/NO) are dropped from partners' FIN; hyperinflation blocs (AR) are dropped from partners' INF and FIN (kept in DEM). These mirror the estimator (export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m) exactly. W is renormalized over the active bloc set at config time; the weights provenance is app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_24bloc.json.

Variable-coherence audit (verified). All 1048 variables across the 50 blocs were audited for coherent transformation / prior / isFinancial tagging, and the per-economy variable table (each bloc's variables with their transformation · prior · financial flag) was added to the LaTeX manual (docs/user_manual.tex). Two coherence fixes shipped with the WN-prior refit: the 4 globals are now isFinancial:true everywhere (above), and IP_CA (Canada industrial production) was rebuilt as a log LEVEL — chained from the discontinued FRED level index plus growth through 2025Q4 — rather than a growth/diff series, so it lives in the same 100·log level space as the other log variables (verified: IP_CA transformation:'log' in CA/spec.json).

docs.title: 5·EM. How the coupled blocs are estimated — the GVAR-EM loop (offline)
docs.text:
The coupling above operates on blocs that are already estimated. They come out of the offline GVAR-EM iterative estimator pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m (the M-step/E-step loop; the runbook to run it is §12a). The fixed point is in the completed star variables, not in the scenario deltas — distinct from the solve-time x=Mx+d of §5. Algorithm (verified against the script):

GVAR-EM (export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m), env knobs MAXIT (default 12), TOL (default 0.03):

  load every bloc's raw panel  DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json   (≤ vintage; 100·log / lin via transformData)
  mask COVID:  NaN the macro columns over 2020Q2..Q4 (financials + ROWFIN stay OBSERVED)   # COVID-as-missing
  initialise each bloc's completed panel D.<CC>.comp (seed interpolation)
  repeat up to MAXIT times:
    # --- E-step: rebuild the trade-weighted stars from the current completed partner panels ---
    for each COUPLED bloc c:
      ROWDEM/ROWINF/ROWFIN_c  =  Σ_j  w_{c,j} · partner_j's (deflated) gdp-growth / inflation / rate
                                  read from D.<j>.comp, renormalised over partners present each quarter
                                  (CN gdp deflated by gdp−price; CH/NO dropped from FIN; AR dropped from INF/FIN)
    # --- M-step: re-estimate every bloc in PARALLEL on its [domestic | 3 stars] panel ---
    parfor each bloc c:                                                  # parpool('local')
      pos = find(prior=='WN')                                           # §6a — WN cols (fiscal ratios), [] otherwise
      [Xcond, BETA, SU, Dforecast] = cgl_bvarV5([dom | stars], lags=3, pos, nDraws, nBurn, MCMCconst, H,
                                                'mcmc',1,'MNpsi',0,'sur',0,'noc',1, 'Ndraws',1000,'Ndrawsdiscard',500)
      #  cgl_bvarV5 = GLP/Minnesota csminwel mode (bvarGLPmf) + Durbin–Koopman simulation smoother:
      #  the DK smoother fills the masked COVID + missing-star cells given the OBSERVED columns (per-cell, not per-row)
      D.<c>.comp <- Xcond                                              # the DK-completed panel
    until  max | Δ(stars) | < TOL   or   MAXIT reached                  # MC noise floor ~0.13 ⇒ a few iters suffice

  # MAXIT=1  ⇒  no star re-convergence = single-country / "separated" estimation (the (iv-b)/single-BVAR ablation)
  export per bloc:  spec.json, companion_mean.json, companion_draws.json, baseline(_levels).json, historical(_levels).json

The "E-step" is the Durbin–Koopman completion of the foreign/star (and COVID-masked) cells; the "M-step" is the Bayesian re-estimation of each bloc; iterating to a fixed point in the completed stars is the EM analogy. Set US_ENDOGENOUS=1 to fold the US into the parfor set (it then builds its own ROW*_US stars from its partners and feeds the global commons — §5/§5a). This is the estimator the empirical horse-race (§14) drives at every out-of-sample origin.

docs.title: 5a. Endogenous-US augmented closed form + the affine-kernel perf path
docs.text:
When US_ENDOGENOUS, the US both (i) receives partner spillovers through its own ROW*_US stars and (ii) drives every RoW bloc's globals (which the RoW blocs pin to own-base + the US-global DELTA). With the US frozen that delta was a constant in d; now it is part of the fixed point, so the compact state is AUGMENTED (buildLinearSystemEndo):

x = [ star pin-delta s_{c,v,t}  for every COUPLED bloc c (US included), v∈{DEM,INF,FIN}, t ]
    ++ [ US global-column delta gΔ_{p,t}  for each US global column p, t ]

with three coupling families assembled into M from the constant per-period smoother kernels: 1. star ← star — receiver c's star v at t ← partner j's linked-col response from j's star pin (vp,tp); D[v] = identity (rate) or 4·Δ (gdp/price). The US is BOTH partner and receiver. 2. gΔ ← US star — the US global col p at t = the US free-var response to its OWN star pins (a definition row). 3. star ← gΔ — a RoW partner j's domestic vars respond to its global pin (Kg[j][p]), feeding the stars of the receivers that have j as a partner (weight w_rj; US excluded — it has no global pins).

d then carries only the exogenous own-pin contributions (each bloc's own scenario pins moving its linked series at zero partner/global delta) plus the US's own-pin global delta; the US-as-partner term is in M (1), the US-global term in M (2)+(3). solveGVARClosedFormEndo solves x = (I−M)⁻¹d — using the hydrated artifact inverse when present, otherwise an inverse-free Neumann fixed-point x ← d + Mx (the in-JS O(dim³) inverse at dim≈2060 is ~46 min, NEVER built live; the Neumann path converges at ρ(M)≈0.924 in a few-hundred cheap matvecs) — then reconstructs each bloc's full level with one final conditionalForecast.

Affine-kernel perf (blocKernels + affineBlocKernels). A bloc's response kernels — K[v][tp] (star-pin IRFs) and Kg[p][tp] (global-pin IRFs) — depend ONLY on that bloc's params + its OWN pin STRUCTURE (which cells are pinned), not on partner data, the global topology, or pin VALUES. So: - All (3 stars + #globals)·H per-period kernels for a bloc come from one filter+smoother pass on the probe baseline plus cheap per-cell matvec recursions (affineBlocKernels in kalman.ts) instead of one full conditionalForecast per (var, tp) — numerically identical (<1e-9), ~10–20× faster on the n=41/lags=3/H=20 US bloc. - The kernels are cached (_blocKernelCache, keyed by modelSig + bloc + own-pin-cells). In the dominant flow (the user pins ONLY the US) all 32 RoW blocs keep an empty own-pin structure ⇒ their kernels are reused ⇒ only the US re-probes. prewarmGVARKernels/prewarmGVARKernelsChunk warm the empty-structure kernels off the critical path (in the worker right after load); prewarmGVARKernels also caches the empty-structure assembled M so the first US-pin solve reuses it.

Two-tier UX (solveGVARDirect + the "Solve full GVAR" button). The full fixed point pays smoother reconstructions and an (I−M) solve. To stay reactive on a drag, solveGVARDirect returns a first-order preview: x_direct = d (the US scenario's one-hop spillover, no RoW↔RoW or RoW→US feedback) mapped to each bloc's level delta straight from the prewarmed kernels (pure matvecs, no Kalman pass, sub-50 ms) — it UNDER-states the response (omits multiplier feedback). runGvarSolve.ts routes mode:'direct' → solveGVARDirect (live preview on pin edits) and mode:'full' → solveGVARClosedForm (the complete fixed point, on the explicit button). The slow path is a structure-change direct solve (placing/moving a US pin re-probes the US bloc, several seconds): the hook (useGvarForecast.ts) defers it to drag-end so it fires once per gesture, shows a "computing…" badge, and the worker strips the ~95 MB coupling artifact (withoutCoupling) from direct-mode posts (solveGVARDirect never uses it). Value-only re-drags (same structure ⇒ kernel-cache hits) and full solves dispatch live. ChartGrid.tsx shows the mode badge ('DIRECT preview' amber / 'full solve' blue) and the ▶ Solve full GVAR button whenever the active result is a direct preview.

docs.title: 6. Data-space conventions (CRITICAL — most bugs live here)
docs.text:
• A variable's transformation is 'log' or 'lin' (in spec.json).
  • log variables are stored as 100·log(level). The chart displays q/q annualized growth = 4·Δ(100·log level). Historical growth in historical.json is already this 4·Δ form.
  • lin variables (rates, ratios, unemployment) are passed through as-is (level = display).
  • Conditioning is WYSIWYG: a directly-pinned quarter shows exactly baseline + your deviation.
  • Posterior draws are flat arrays with shape beta_shape=[k, nv, nDraws] (k=n·lags+1, nv=n) and sigma_shape=[n, n, nDraws]. Index: beta[draw*k*nv + j*k + i], sigma[draw*n*n + j*n + i]. These are Float64Array after loadCompanionDraws (converted once for clone/index speed).
  • GVAR RoW pins exchange paths in 100·log level space (avoids a differencing bug): for a log RoW var the pin delta is 4·Δ(level-gap), for a lin rate it is the level-gap.

When in doubt, the canary tests (§11) encode the exact conventions — run them.

docs.title: 6a. The per-variable prior convention (RW vs WN) — prior in spec.json
docs.text:
Every variable in spec.json carries a prior field — 'RW' (random walk, the default) or 'WN' (white noise / stationary). It selects the prior mean on that variable's OWN first lag in the GLP/Minnesota prior:

prior Own-first-lag prior mean Behaviour Used for 'RW' (default) 1 the variable is a priori a random walk (persistent, drifts at the long end) every macro/financial level, all RoW stars, EFFI 'WN' (stationary) 0 the variable is a priori white noise → its forecast mean-reverts to the in-sample mean the fiscal GDP-ratios FREV_GDP, FPEXP_GDP, SFA

The mechanism (verified — BVAR/MFBVAR/GLPreplicationWeb/bvarGLPmf.m). The Minnesota prior-mean matrix b is built with a diagonal of diagb over the own-first-lag block; WN columns get that diagonal entry zeroed:

% bvarGLPmf.m, lines 192–195
b=zeros(k,n);
diagb=ones(n,1);
diagb(pos)=0;   % Set to zero the prior mean on the first own lag for variables selected in the vector pos
b(2:n+1,:)=diag(diagb);

pos is the vector of WN column indices. It is the 3rd positional argument of the single-bloc estimator cgl_bvarV5.m (named stationary there; pos=stationary at line 19) and is threaded straight into the bvarGLPmf call ('pos', pos) and every downstream logMLVAR_* / BVAR call.

Where it flows (spec.json → estimator pos → forecast). 1. The estimators read the per-variable prior from the spec and build the pos index set: - pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m: statCols = find(Spec.Prior(keep)=="WN")' (on the kept domestic block — stars are appended AFTER and are always RW), passed as cgl_bvarV5(x, lags, Dc.statCols, …). - pipeline/export_bvar_euro_fsap.m: statCols = find(Prior=="WN")', passed as cgl_bvarV5(x, lags, statCols, …). - Both use a robust priorsfrom(vars) helper that tolerates MATLAB's jsondecode dropping a prior field that is present on only some variables (cell-array vs struct-array shapes); a missing/empty prior defaults to 'RW' (the historical behaviour) — so the WN prior is purely additive and a no-op (byte-identical) for the 44 non-fiscal blocs (their pos is empty). 2. The estimator bakes the prior into the exported coefficients — the WN mean-reversion lives in the posterior betahat/sigmahat (and the draws) written to companion_mean.json / companion_draws.json. 3. The TypeScript runtime never reads .prior (grep: no .prior access anywhere in app/src). The forecast uses the estimated coefficients only — the prior has already done its work at estimation time. prior in the deployed spec.json is therefore estimation provenance / documentation, not a runtime switch. (Consequence: changing a prior requires a re-estimation + re-export, not just a spec edit.)

Result (verified on the US bloc). Under the old all-RW prior the fiscal ratios drifted at the long end (US revenue stuck near ~19%); with the WN prior FREV_GDP mean-reverts toward its in-sample mean — the deployed US/baseline_levels.json shows FREV_GDP ≈ 18.06 at h=0 → ≈ 17.4 by h=8 → ≈ 17.9 at the long horizon, instead of drifting up. EFFI (the effective interest rate) deliberately stays RW (it is persistent). Committed 6f0161a / 22250be / e38673a. The prior=='WN' assignment is consistent across all 6 fiscal blocs (verified: FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/SFA = WN, EFFI = RW in US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN spec.json).

docs.title: 7. The fiscal block (fiscalAccounting.ts) — 6 countries (US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN)
docs.text:
Stock-flow-consistent, CBO/IMF-style. The BVAR estimates 4 fiscal drivers — FREV_GDP (revenue/GDP·100), FPEXP_GDP (primary-exp/GDP·100), EFFI (the effective interest rate = net interest / lagged gross debt ·100, annualised) and SFA (the stock-flow adjustment, % of GDP); reporting variables (debt service, debt/GDP, deficit, GFN, snowball) are derived. The debtService (DSVC) parametrisation was reverted — the endogenous interest variable is back to EFFI, and SFA is now the new estimated BVAR variable.

Stationary (WN) prior on the GDP-ratios (see §6a). The three GDP-ratio drivers FREV_GDP, FPEXP_GDP, SFA are estimated with the prior:'WN' stationary mean (own-first-lag prior mean = 0) so they mean-revert to their in-sample means instead of drifting at the long end (the old all-RW prior left US revenue stuck near ~19%; it now reverts toward ~17.4%). EFFI stays prior:'RW' — the effective rate is genuinely persistent. This is purely an estimation-prior choice baked into the exported coefficients; fiscalAccounting.ts is unchanged by it.

  • pb = revenue − primaryExp (primary balance, + = surplus).
  • SFA is a NEW estimated BVAR variable = the EXACT residual of the app's debt recursion — the deficit–debt discrepancy (observed dDebt − (deficit + interest − growth)), driven by the federal-flows-vs-gross-debt perimeter seam / intragovernmental holdings. It is computed so the recursion closes to machine precision; COVID quarters are set NaN (the COVID-as-missing Gibbs imputes them). The debt/GDP projection now carries the FORECAST SFA forward (its own dynamics + bands) instead of defaulting to 0.
  • Recursion (per quarter): d_t = d_{t-1}·(1 + EFFI/400)/(1 + gNomQ/100) − pb/4 + sfa/4, with pb = FREV − FPEXP and gNomQ = 100·log(realGDP)-diff + 100·log(deflator)-diff (q/q nominal, read off the level path). Debt service (interest, % GDP) = EFFI·d_{t-1}/100. Debt-to-GDP is derived by rolling this budget identity forward from d0 (debt is not a BVAR variable).
  • fiscal_meta.json carries { d0, sfaDefault, idx:{ gdp, deflator, revenue, primaryExp, effRate, sfa } }. debtService is NO LONGER in the deployed idx — computeFiscalBlock therefore takes the effRate branch (interest = effRate·d_{t-1}/100) and reads the per-quarter SFA from idx.sfa (the estimated SFA column). Deployed indices (read from the live files, NEVER hardcode — these are positional and DRIFT on any variable-order change, see the gotcha below): US {0,7,33,34,35,36}, EA {0,6,17,18,19,20}, JP {0,7,16,17,18,19}, UK {0,8,18,19,20,21}, CA {0,8,18,19,20,21}, CN {0,1,13,14,15,16} for {gdp,deflator,revenue,primaryExp,effRate,sfa}. SFA means (sfaDefault, % GDP): US 1.75, EA 0.33, JP 2.23, UK 3.15, CA 3.19, CN 4.02. Variable counts n: US 41, EA 24, JP 23, UK 25, CA 25, CN 20.

🔴 STALE-POSITIONAL-INDEX gotcha (flagged-for-process, not a code bug). The porter (pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py) does not regenerate fiscal_meta.json, and these indices are positional. The 33-bloc GVAR-EM refit inserted variables ahead of the fiscal block in every spec except US, so the old EA {0,6,14,15,16,17} / JP {0,7,14,15,16,17} / UK,CA {0,8,16,17,18,19} / CN {0,1,11,12,13,14} silently pointed at the WRONG columns — EA's effRate landed on the equity index (col ~537), so d·(1+effRate/400) compounded at ~134%/q and debt/GDP exploded. After any re-port you must run node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs (re-resolves the six indices by variable id from the deployed spec.json, preserves d0/sfaDefault, idempotent). The fiscal-meta-indices.test.ts canary asserts every deployed bloc's derived debt path stays bounded — it is what catches a stale port. - The fiscalAccounting.ts debtService/dsvc plumbing (runFiscalRecursion, computeFiscalBlock, requiredPrimaryBalance) is dormant but retained: it activates only when a model ships an idx.debtService, which the current deployment does not. With idx.sfa set and idx.debtService unset, computeFiscalBlock reads levelPaths[t][idx.sfa] for the per-quarter SFA and uses the effective-rate interest form. - There is a checkable Excel walkthrough docs/fiscal_accounting_walkthrough_US_UK.xlsx (and the user's source docs/Fiscal_Accounting_US_UK_NSA.xlsx).

See fiscal-block-methodology.md for the full derivation.

docs.title: 8. Uncertainty bands (two modes — know the difference)
docs.text:
The band-computation effect lives in ChartGrid.tsx (deps [uncertaintyComputing], fires on Compute). Quick=50 draws, Full=200.

  • DEFAULT (model-specific density, fast, on by default). For every posterior draw, take the unconditional forecast (bvarForecastDraw, full forecast width) and parallel-shift it by the same mean scenario delta sourced from gvarBandRef.current.effective.forecasts (the GVAR-propagated path for any active GVAR bloc, else the single-country conditional). The band keeps full width (does not collapse when one point is pinned) and centres on the displayed line. The derived-fiscal charts (the 6 fiscal countries) get a matching parallel-shift band (per-draw unconditional computeFiscalBlock, shifted by mean scenario−baseline PB/debt via fiscalMeanRef).
  • EXPERIMENTAL (per-draw iterative GVAR density, opt-in). Gated behind the store flag experimentalFeatures (lower-left ExperimentalToggle). Re-solves the conditional cross-country system per draw (runGvarBandSolve). This is the conditional cloud — it pinches at pins (and collapses when lightly conditioned) and is slower. The request builder iterates the active GVAR bloc set (not a hardcoded subset), so every bloc's own-pins are carried; solveViewedScenLvl skips the cross-country sweep for a US-viewed bloc only when the US is frozen (!US_ENDOGENOUS) — under the endogenous-US topology the US depends on the fixed point like any bloc, so it goes through the full solve.

Why two modes: the per-draw conditional re-solve over-determines the conditional mean when only a point or two is pinned, so it compresses too much; the default parallel-shift preserves the model's own forecast width while still centring on the scenario. The default is the right answer for almost everyone; the experimental mode is there for users who want the pinching behaviour.

docs.title: 8a. Long-run anchor (PLR) — longRunTilt.ts
docs.text:
A user can pin a long-run target for a variable or a spread h'·y. Instead of re-estimating, longRunTilt.ts entropically tilts the existing posterior draws (KL-min / exponential-tilting importance reweighting) so the weighted-mean long-run moment hits the target. The tilt now moves the UNCONDITIONAL central path (the weighted mean from reduceCloud), not just the fan — so both the baseline and the scenario fans are pulled toward the target. The long-run moment defaults to the long-horizon forecast E[y_{T+H}] at the ≈10-year (40-quarter) horizon (reuses the bvarForecastDraw companion iteration, no innovation), not the steady state — the level VARs are near-unit-root so (I − ΣB_l)⁻¹c diverges. This is the user-facing, after-estimation analogue of GLP (2019) "Prior for the Long Run".

UI/Compute (ControlPanel.tsx): the Compute button is enabled whenever an enabled anchor exists, and applying the tilt auto-turns-on Quick uncertainty if it is off; the target input sits below the variable dropdown; the panel includes a "what it tilts toward" explanation and an ESS badge (low ESS ⇒ target far from the data — relax it or use one anchor). The panel is no longer gated to non-GVAR models: it now renders for GVAR blocs too, with a "GVAR bloc — marginal anchor" disclaimer — for a GVAR bloc it reweights only that bloc's own draws, anchoring the bloc's marginal long-run forecast, not the joint cross-country solution (partners + US-determined globals still pull the displayed path), so the realised long-run value may differ from the target. A fully joint re-anchoring is a planned extension.

docs.title: 8b. Drag-perf UX — the heavy GVAR solve is OFF the live drag path (DEPLOYED)
docs.text:
The dominant interaction is dragging a pin on a GVAR bloc. The full cross-country fixed point is multi-second (§5a), so the live drag path must do zero heavy compute. Four pieces make the drag feel instant while the correct answer reconciles on release:

  • Zero solves during a drag; one cheap solve on pointer-up. The drag handler signals beginGvarDrag() / endGvarDrag() through the gvarDragState.ts bus. While isGvarDragging(), useGvarForecast withholds every GVAR mean solve (value-only AND structure-change — each is ~4.5 s warm / ~13 s cold on the live 50-bloc panel, verified at useGvarForecast.ts:279) and just marks one as owed. On onGvarDragEnd (pointer-up) it dispatches exactly one direct solve, deferred ~100 ms (coalesces back-to-back gestures + waits for the final store write). The full fixed point + uncertainty bands run only on the explicit Compute / "Solve full GVAR" token (solveFullToken). The mousemove path is itself rAF-coalesced to one store write per frame (ChartGrid.tsx:2388), so a drag is ~one cheap condForecast per frame, not a solve.
  • The dragged bloc's line stays live via condForecast fallback. While dragging, ChartGrid's effective memo (subscribed to the drag flag via subscribeGvarDragging) swaps the viewed bloc's line from the frozen GVAR path to the single-country DK-smoother condForecast (~1–5 ms), then flips back to the propagated GVAR path on release. (gvar-drag-line-band.test.ts.)
  • Optimistic conditioned-point dot (conditionedDotY.ts). The pinned marker, the line at the pin, and the collapsed band there snap to the just-clicked value instantly — they cannot wait for the ~700 ms solve. At active+conditioned quarters ChartGrid overrides the scenario y with conditionedDotDisplayY(...) (ChartGrid.tsx:1656), reconstructed from the conditioning PATH the click wrote (the exact inverse of the display→stored map conditioningDeviationFromDisplayY used by both the drag and the right-click). So the dot round-trips to the cursor for every variable type (level-display oil/FX/equity/house via logToIndexLevel; growth-displayed log / lin direct). The smoothed line + bands reconcile after the solve. (conditioned-dot-optimistic.test.ts.)
  • Right-click plants the bullet at the cursor. A right-click on a forecast chart enables and pins the quarter at the cursor's display y, using the same conditioningDeviationFromDisplayY mapping (ChartGrid.tsx:2484, :1300) — so a planted bullet and a dragged one are identical. (right-click-plant-at-cursor.test.ts.)

A "computing…" badge (driven by setGvarComputing / subscribeGvarComputing on the same bus, so only the badge re-renders) shows while the release solve is in flight; ChartGrid shows the mode badge ('DIRECT preview' amber / 'full solve' blue) and a ▶ Solve full GVAR button whenever the active result is a direct preview (§5a).

docs.title: 9. State (store/useStore.ts, Zustand)
docs.text:
• scenarios[] + activeScenarioId — each scenario holds its own conditioning paths, conditioned quarters, counterfactual settings, pbCond/debtCond fiscal targets, cachedBands.
  • gvarPins[scenarioId][country] — own conditioning per country. Persists across setCountry (so conditioning the US then switching to EA still shows the spillover). It is not wiped in the setCountry reducer — do not "fix" that.
  • uncertaintyMode / uncertaintyComputing / uncertaintyComputed — drive the band effect.
  • experimentalFeatures (default false) — gates the per-draw iterative band.

docs.title: 10. The per-country data contract (public/data/bvar/<CC>/)
docs.text:
Each country directory provides (loaded by loader.ts / gvarLoader.ts):

File Contents spec.json { n, variables:[{id,name,transformation,isFinancial,prior,block}] }. The engine reads n from here / companion_mean.json — index-agnostic. transformation = 'log'/'lin' (§6); prior = 'RW'/'WN' (§6a — estimation provenance, NOT read at runtime); isFinancial = kept observed through the COVID-as-missing window (vs macro-masked); block = 'domestic'/'row'/'global'/'fiscal' (§5/§10). companion_mean.json { betahat[k][n], sigmahat[n][n], n, lags } (constant row FIRST — matches kalman.ts). companion_draws.json { beta, beta_shape, sigma, sigma_shape, n_draws } flat posterior draws (lazy-loaded; large). baseline.json { mean[H][n], q16/q84/q05/q95 } in display/growth space. baseline_levels.json { mean[H][n], q16/q84/q05/q95/q50 } in level space (feeds the fiscal block, GVAR, and the level-display index/FX/equity uncertainty fans — see displayUnits.ts). historical.json / historical_levels.json last ~40 quarters, growth / level. transformations.json ['log'|'lin', ...] per variable. fiscal_meta.json The 6 fiscal countries (US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN) — { d0, sfaDefault, idx:{ gdp, deflator, revenue, primaryExp, effRate, sfa } } (debtService dropped, sfa added); see §7.

Every GVAR bloc tags each variable with block in spec.json: 'row' = the 3 trade-weighted stars (per-bloc), 'global' = the 4 shared commons (GLB_OIL/GLB_USLR/GLB_USBAA/GLB_USEQ, identical across blocs, US-determined), 'fiscal' = the 4 fiscal drivers (the 6 fiscal blocs), 'domestic' = everything else. block:'row' is what marks a bloc as GVAR-active (§5). The US spec.json now also carries block:'row' stars (ROWDEM_US/ROWINF_US/ROWFIN_US, cols 38–40) and the 4 globals — this is what makes the US endogenous (§5/§5a). Standalone (non-GVAR) economies carry only 'domestic' (+ 'fiscal' for a fiscal bloc).

docs.title: 11. Tests (app/src/__tests__/, Vitest)
docs.text:
The suite is SPLIT into fast and heavy halves — both gate the deploy (see BUILD_AND_CI.md for the authoritative taxonomy):

  • npm test runs the fast suite — vitest run minus seven --exclude globs (the heavy solver/perf patterns). The three deploy-blocking canaries (scenario-scale-convention, gvar-convergence, gvar-coupling-artifact) are all here.
  • npm run test:heavy runs exactly those excluded patterns, serialised to one fork (--poolOptions.forks.maxForks=1): gvar-us-endogenous, gvar-direct-channel, gvar-band-solve, gvar-closed-form-*, gvar-band-closedform-*, affine-blockernels-parity, gvar-kernel-affine-independence — the multi-second dim-2060 endogenous solves + the affine-kernel parity gates.
  • npm run test:all (= npx vitest run) sweeps everything in one pass (slower).

The canary tests pin the conventions — treat a change in them as a red flag:

  • scenario-scale-convention.test.ts — the 100·log / 4·diff display convention.
  • conditioning-wysiwyg.test.ts — pinned quarter == baseline + deviation exactly.
  • counterfactual-recovers-scenario.test.ts — counterfactual round-trip.
  • fiscalAccounting.test.ts / fiscal-backcast.test.ts — the debt recursion vs MATLAB.
