  5. pos → prior mean = 0. In BVAR/MFBVAR/GLPreplicationWeb/bvarGLPmf.m the own-first-lag prior block is built from diagb: 
    diagb=ones(n,1);
diagb(pos)=0;            % zero the own-first-lag prior mean for the WN columns
b(2:n+1,:)=diag(diagb);  % default columns keep prior mean 1 (random walk)
    So a "WN" column gets own-lag prior mean 0 (stationary); every other column keeps 1 (the random-walk centre). For the 44 non-fiscal blocs pos=[], so diagb=ones(...) unchanged → identical to the historical default.
  6. Round-trip. On export each estimator re-stamps the per-variable prior back into spec.json from statCols (if any(statCols==i), v.prior='WN'; else v.prior='RW'), so the shipped spec always reflects the prior actually used.

Deployed prior assignment (verified in the live specs — all 6 fiscal blocs identical):

Variable block prior rationale FREV_GDP (revenue % GDP) fiscal "WN" bounded ratio → mean-revert FPEXP_GDP (primary expenditure % GDP) fiscal "WN" bounded ratio → mean-revert SFA (stock-flow adjustment % GDP) fiscal "WN" bounded ratio → mean-revert EFFI (effective interest rate % p.a.) fiscal "RW" slow-moving level, not a bounded share everything else (macro, financial, stars, globals) any "RW" random-walk default

docs.title: 3.2 The isFinancial flag — coherence audit
docs.text:
isFinancial (§2.3) gates two things: the chart display units and the COVID treatment — financial variables are kept observed through the 2020Q2–Q4 data-augmentation window, while macro variables (and the two macro stars ROWDEM/ROWINF) are masked and DK-completed. A miscoded flag therefore changes both the picture and the estimate.

Two coherence fixes are baked into the deployed specs (verified across all blocs):

  • GLB_USEQ (US equity) and GLB_OIL (oil) are isFinancial:true everywhere. Both are market-priced and continued trading through 2020 — keeping them observed (not imputed) through the COVID window is the correct treatment, and it matches their euro-FSAP aliases EQ_US/WTI_US, which are also isFinancial:true. (The earlier GLB_OIL:isFinancial=false was incoherent and has been corrected.)
  • Canada's IP_CA is a log-LEVEL. It is now transformation:"log", isFinancial:false, block:"domestic" — rebuilt by chaining the discontinued FRED level index forward with growth to 2025Q4 so it reads as a proper 100·log(level) series (its q/q annualised growth is the 4·Δ display form), not a level-vs-growth mismatch.

All 1048 variables across the deployed blocs were audited for transformation · prior · isFinancial coherence; a per-economy variable table now lives in the user manual.

docs.title: 4. companion_mean.json — posterior-mean VAR
docs.text:
The posterior-mean reduced-form VAR(lags) coefficients and residual covariance. The constant (intercept) row is first — this matches kalman.ts's companion construction. Do not transpose.

Field Type Shape Meaning betahat number[][] [k][n], k = n·lags + 1 coefficient matrix; row 0 = intercept, then lags blocks of n own/cross lags; column j = equation for variable j sigmahat number[][] [n][n] residual covariance (symmetric, PD) n int scalar number of variables (matches spec.n) lags int scalar VAR lag order

k = n·lags + 1 (US: 41·3 + 1 = 124; EA: 24·3 + 1 = 73).

Example (US shapes & first values):

{
  "betahat": [
    [6.12187, -0.742157, -2.92772, "… 41 cols"],     // row 0 = intercept
    [0.760955, 0.0994946, -0.260185, "… 41 cols"],  // row 1 = lag-1 of var 0, etc.
    "… 124 rows total (1 + 41·3) …"
  ],
  "sigmahat": [ ["… 41×41 …"] ],
  "n": 41,
  "lags": 3
}

docs.title: 5. companion_draws.json — flat posterior draws
docs.text:
The full posterior sample, stored as flat row-major arrays (boxed number[] in JSON; converted to Float64Array on load). Lazy-loaded; the US file is ~34 MB.

Field Type Shape Meaning n_draws int scalar number of posterior draws (currently 500) n int scalar variables lags int scalar lag order beta_shape int[3] [k, n, n_draws] shape of the flattened beta (k = n·lags + 1) beta number[] k·n·n_draws flattened coefficient draws sigma_shape int[3] [n, n, n_draws] shape of the flattened sigma sigma number[] n·n·n_draws flattened covariance draws

Flat indexing (column-major within the 3-D shape, matching ARCHITECTURE_FOR_AI.md §6):

beta[ draw*k*n  + j*k + i ]     // draw, equation column j, coef row i
sigma[ draw*n*n + j*n + i ]     // draw, (i,j) of the n×n covariance

Example (US):

{
  "n_draws": 500,
  "n": 41,
  "lags": 3,
  "beta_shape": [124, 41, 500],
  "beta": [8.04724, 0.751567, 0.174671, "… 2 542 000 doubles …"],
  "sigma_shape": [41, 41, 500],
  "sigma": [0.104776, 0.0609497, 0.143155, "… 840 500 doubles …"]
}

docs.title: 6. baseline.json / baseline_levels.json — the baseline fan
docs.text:
The unconditional baseline forecast and its predictive bands, over the projection horizon H (currently 20 quarters). Two files, identical shape, different space:

  • baseline.json — display/growth space (§2.1).
  • baseline_levels.json — level space (feeds the fiscal block, the GVAR solver, and the level-display fans).
Field Type Shape Meaning mean number[][] [H][n] central path q05,q16,q84,q95 number[][] [H][n] predictive-band quantiles (5/16/84/95%) q50 number[][] [H][n] optional median; present in baseline_levels.json only, and only for the 16 euro FSAP standalones (AT, BE, DE, EE, ES, FI, FR, GR, IE, IT, LT, LU, NL, PT, SI, SK). No baseline.json (display-space) carries q50, and the GVAR blocs (US/EA/JP/…) carry it in neither file — read defensively horizon int scalar H (= number of forecast quarters, e.g. 20)

Example (US, H=20, n=41):

{
  "mean": [ [1.91073, 1.87189, -1.45722, "… 41 cols"], "… 20 rows" ],
  "q16":  [ ["…"], "…" ],
  "q84":  [ ["…"], "…" ],
  "q05":  [ ["…"], "…" ],
  "q95":  [ ["…"], "…" ],
  "horizon": 20
}

baseline_levels.json has the same keys but in level space — e.g. mean[0] = [1009.29, 972.576, 680.448, …] (100·log levels for log vars).

docs.title: 7. historical.json / historical_levels.json — recent actuals
docs.text:
The last ~40 quarters of realised data, used to anchor the charts. Two files, same shape, different space (display/growth vs level).

Field Type Shape Meaning data number[][] [n_periods][n] realised values, oldest → newest n_periods int scalar number of historical quarters (currently 40)

Example (US):

{
  "data": [
    [2.31139, 3.00848, 14.3557, "… 41 cols"],   // oldest quarter
    "… 40 rows …",
    [0.481089, 1.9089, -4.6351, "… 41 cols"]     // newest quarter
  ],
  "n_periods": 40
}

historical.json growth is already the 4·Δ(100·log level) form for log vars (no further differencing in the chart). historical_levels.json is the matching level path.

docs.title: 8. transformations.json — per-variable space
docs.text:
A redundant, fast lookup of each variable's data space, aligned to spec.variables column order. (The same information is in spec.variables[i].transformation.)

Field Type Shape Meaning transformations string[] [n] "log" or "lin" per variable column

Example (US, abridged):

{"transformations":["log","log","log","log","log","log","log","log","log","log","log","log","log","log","lin","log","lin","log","log","lin","lin","lin","lin","lin","lin","lin","log","log","log","log","lin","log","log","lin","lin","lin","lin","log","lin","lin","lin"]}

docs.title: 9. fiscal_meta.json — fiscal-block parameters (6 blocs only)
docs.text:
Shipped only by the 6 fiscal economies (US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN); a 404 for any other economy is expected and yields null. It supplies the debt recursion's initial stock, default SFA, and the column indices the recursion reads from the level path (computeFiscalBlock in fiscalAccounting.ts). See fiscal-block-methodology.md and ARCHITECTURE_FOR_AI.md §7 for the math.

Field Type Meaning d0 number initial debt-to-GDP ratio (%) at the projection seam sfaDefault number default stock-flow adjustment (% of GDP) — the historical mean of the estimated SFA idx object column indices into the level path (spec/*_levels column order) idx.gdp int real-GDP column idx.deflator int GDP-deflator column idx.revenue int FREV_GDP (revenue % GDP) column idx.primaryExp int FPEXP_GDP (primary-expenditure % GDP) column idx.effRate int EFFI (effective interest rate, % p.a.) column idx.sfa int SFA (stock-flow adjustment, % GDP) column

The legacy idx.debtService key was removed; idx.sfa (the estimated SFA column) replaced it. With idx.sfa set and idx.debtService unset, computeFiscalBlock takes the effective-rate interest form and reads the per-quarter SFA from idx.sfa. Do not re-introduce debtService.

Deployed values (read from the live files; NEVER hardcode indices — they are positional and DRIFT, see the gotcha below). d0 is shown rounded for readability — the file stores it full precision; sfaDefault is round(.,4):

Bloc n d0 (file) sfaDefault idx {gdp,deflator,revenue,primaryExp,effRate,sfa} US 41 122.56815 1.7518 {0, 7, 33, 34, 35, 36} EA 24 87.84509550831034 0.325 {0, 6, 17, 18, 19, 20} JP 23 192.86944931521631 2.233 {0, 7, 16, 17, 18, 19} UK 25 96.24124260944826 3.1487 {0, 8, 18, 19, 20, 21} CA 25 100.1611274546207 3.191 {0, 8, 18, 19, 20, 21} CN 20 94.33130080334892 4.017 {0, 1, 13, 14, 15, 16}

🔴 STALE-POSITIONAL-INDEX gotcha — the porter does NOT regenerate fiscal_meta.json. These six idx values are positional offsets into the level path, but pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py ports fiscal_meta.json verbatim. So any change to a bloc's variable ORDER or COUNT (e.g. the 33-bloc GVAR-EM refit, which inserted variables ahead of the fiscal block in every spec except US) silently re-points the indices at the wrong columns. Concretely: the pre-refit EA {0,6,14,15,16,17} pointed effRate at the equity index (~537), and d·(1+effRate/400) exploded the EA debt path. A porter (or anyone re-ordering variables) MUST run node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs after porting — it re-resolves all six indices BY VARIABLE ID (FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/EFFI/SFA, gdp=0, deflator=first HICP|CPI|JGDP|PGDP|DEFL) from the deployed spec.json and rewrites the file (preserving d0/sfaDefault; idempotent). The fiscal-meta-indices.test.ts canary blocks a deploy whose indices are stale (asserts every bloc's derived debt path stays bounded).

Example (US):

{"d0":122.56815,"sfaDefault":1.7518,"idx":{"gdp":0,"deflator":7,"revenue":33,"primaryExp":34,"effRate":35,"sfa":36}}

CN's deflator index is 1 (not 7). Since the 2026-06 surgical CN-only re-estimate, idx.gdp (GDP_CN) is real (spec.json var 0 = "CN Real GDP …", fiscal_meta.json idx.gdpIsNominal = false), and CN has been removed from NOMINAL_GDP_BLOCS, so the GVAR DEM contribution now reads gdp directly. The fiscal recursion gQ = Δ(idx.gdp) + Δ(idx.deflator) is now the correct real-GDP + deflator nominal growth, matching US/EA/JP/UK/CA — the prior nominal-GDP_CN double-count is resolved. The fix was CN-only; the other 32 blocs are byte-identical. The fiscal-block column counts above (n) reflect the live specs; treat them as data, not constants.

docs.title: 10. weights_24bloc.json — GVAR trade-weight matrix & topology source
docs.text:
The single source of GVAR topology: the bloc list and the row-normalised bilateral trade-weight matrix that builds each bloc's trade-weighted stars and the cross-country coupling. The filename is stale — it holds 33 blocs, not 24 (US first, with a US row that activates the endogenous-US coupling). Imported directly by gvarSolve.ts (import weights24 from '…/weights_24bloc.json') and consumed by configureGVARTopology.

Field Type Shape Meaning source string provenance (IMF IMTS bilateral trade, SDMX 2.1) indicators string[] [2] trade indicators used (XG_FOB_USD, MG_CIF_USD) euro_area_code string aggregation code for the EA bloc ("G163") window string averaging window ("2021-2023 average") blocs string[] [33] the candidate bloc list, US first (= GVAR_BLOC_CANDIDATES) bloc_codes object 50 keys code → metadata map (display) note string human note (records that US is now endogenous) weights object 33 keys the matrix: weights[receiver][partner] = w, zero diagonal, each receiver row sums to 1 over its partners

The weights matrix is keyed by receiver bloc (33 rows, including a US row); each row maps partner code → weight and is row-normalised (zero diagonal, sums to 1). At config time configureGVARTopology renormalises each row over the blocs that are actually deployed/active.

Example (abridged):

{
  "source": "IMF IMTS … SDMX 2.1, flow IMF.STA:IMTS(1.0.0)",
  "indicators": ["XG_FOB_USD", "MG_CIF_USD"],
  "euro_area_code": "G163",
  "window": "2021-2023 average",
  "blocs": ["US","EA","JP","UK","CA","CN", "… 33 total"],
  "weights": {
    "US": {"EA":0.176351, "JP":0.049779, "UK":0.029843, "CA":0.168175, "CN":0.144603, "…": "…"},
    "EA": {"US":0.157349, "JP":0.026295, "UK":0.096466, "CA":0.014182, "CN":0.143814, "…": "…"}
  }
}

Other weight files in gvar/ (weights_full.json, weights_49.json, weights.json, .bak*) are scratch/alternate-panel artifacts; the live topology source is weights_24bloc.json (the only one imported by gvarSolve.ts).

docs.title: 11. coupling_default.json.gz — precomputed GVAR coupling
docs.text:
The gzipped precomputed cross-country coupling: the linear-system matrix M and its BLAS-computed inverse (I − M)⁻¹ for the empty/baseline pin structure. Building/inverting M at this scale is many minutes in-browser (the in-JS O(dim³) inverse at dim≈2060 is ~46 min), so shipping this artifact is mandatory for interactivity, not optional. Decompressed in-browser via the native DecompressionStream (gvarLoader.ts); a plain .json fallback is tried for environments without it.

Field Type Shape Meaning modelSig string model signature keying the artifact to the deployed data (see below) pinStructure string the pin-structure signature this artifact was built for — "" (empty/baseline) for the shipped one dim int scalar augmented-state dimension (currently 2060 under endogenous US) rhoSpectral number scalar spectral radius ρ(M) (the live coupling_default.json.gz reads 0.924041 < 1; it moves slightly with each refit — what matters is < 1) M number[][] [dim][dim] the coupling matrix (6-sigfig) invImM number[][] [dim][dim] (I − M)⁻¹, the BLAS-computed inverse (6-sigfig)

modelSig is built by gvarSignature(raw) (gvarLoader.ts): for each qualified bloc c, "{c}:{n}_{lags}_{horizon}_{b0}_{bM}_{s0}" where b0 = betahat[0][0], bM = betahat[n·lags][n-1], s0 = sigmahat[0][0] (each in toExponential(6) form), joined with "|". So a change to any bloc's data changes modelSig and invalidates the artifact — it must be regenerated. At solve time (runGvarSolve.ts) the hydrated inverse is used only if art.modelSig === gvarSignature(raw) and the request's pin-structure signature matches art.pinStructure; otherwise the solver rebuilds.

Header example (values are real; matrices abridged):

{
  "modelSig": "US:41_3_20_6.121870e+0_-8.370660e-2_8.919980e-2|EA:24_3_20_2.551660e+0_-1.208040e-1_1.509770e-1|…",
  "pinStructure": "",
  "dim": 2060,
  "rhoSpectral": 0.924041,
  "M": [ ["… 2060 cols …"], "… 2060 rows …" ],
  "invImM": [ ["… 2060 cols …"], "… 2060 rows …" ]
}

Regenerate (mandatory after any data change) from app/:

npx vite-node scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs

This builds M for the empty pin structure, inverts it via MATLAB BLAS (invert_coupling.m), and writes the gzip. gvar-coupling-artifact.test.ts asserts the shipped .gz hydrates and matches a fresh build (a canary that blocks deploy on drift).

Spectral radius by panel size (context, not a contract): 6-bloc pilot ρ≈0.59; 24-bloc frozen-anchor ρ≈0.726 (dim≈1920); 33-bloc endogenous-US ρ=0.924041 (dim=2060, the deployed coupling_default.json.gz). The exact value moves slightly with each refit (earlier artifacts read ~0.89); what is load-bearing is that it stays comfortably < 1. The frozen-anchor figures no longer describe the live artifact.

docs.title: 12. Validating a freshly ported bloc
docs.text:
A quick sanity pass when adding or re-porting a bloc (paths absolute under app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/):

  1. spec.n === companion_mean.n === companion_draws.n, and length(spec.variables) === n.
  2. companion_mean.betahat is [n·lags+1][n]; sigmahat is [n][n].
  3. companion_draws.beta_shape === [n·lags+1, n, n_draws]; sigma_shape === [n, n, n_draws]; length(beta) === product(beta_shape); length(sigma) === product(sigma_shape).
  4. baseline.mean and baseline_levels.mean are [horizon][n] with all of q05/q16/q84/q95 present (q50 optional).
  5. historical.data and historical_levels.data are [n_periods][n].
  6. transformations.transformations has length n and matches spec.variables[i].transformation.
  7. A fiscal bloc additionally ships fiscal_meta.json with all six idx keys in range [0, n) AND each idx resolving to the correct variable id (idx.revenue → FREV_GDP, etc.). After any re-port, run node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs — the porter does not regen these positional indices (the STALE-POSITIONAL-INDEX gotcha, §9).
  8. After porting, regenerate coupling_default.json.gz (§11) so modelSig matches.

Then run the green gate (npx tsc --noEmit && npm test && npm run test:heavy) — the canary tests (gvar-coupling-artifact, gvar-convergence, gvar-derived-topology, scenario-scale-convention, fiscalAccounting) encode the contracts above.

docs.title: 13. Out-of-sample evaluation contracts (the em-bayesian-gvar-eval harness)
docs.text: Scope note. The JSON contracts in §§1–12 are read by the deployed app. The three contracts below are read/written by the separate empirical-evaluation repository em-bayesian-gvar-eval (out-of-sample forecast horse-race + endogenous-US spillover analysis). They live in em-bayesian-gvar-eval/results/ and are not shipped in app/public/data/. The full design is em-bayesian-gvar-eval/docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md (§§0–2). Results are preliminary/forthcoming; the contracts here are stable.

docs.title: 13.1 weights_asof_<startY>_<endY>.json — rolling as-of trade weights cache
docs.text:
The horse-race is a recursive expanding-window scheme over origins t0 = 2010Q1 … 2024Q4 (2020 COVID origins dropped). A hard look-ahead invariant (EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md §0 invariant #2, decision D11) forbids using the shipped 2021–2023 weights_24bloc.json/weights_full.json at a pre-2020 origin — that would inject future trade structure. Instead the driver resolves, per origin, a leak-free as-of weights file whose IMTS averaging window ends ≤ year(t0), built by matlab/weights/rolling_asof_weights.m (a thin wrapper on the main repo's pipeline/build_trade_weights_full.py — same IMTS pull, EA=G163, mirror fallback for embargoed reporters, <0.05% partner drop + renormalization). Files are cached under results/weights_cache/.

Two modes (env OOS_WEIGHTS_MODE):

  • fixed (default) — a single pre-sample window (e.g. 2005-2009) used at every origin: provably leak-free (biased toward the past, never the future).
  • rolling — a 3-yr window ending the calendar year before year(t0) (strictly < year(t0), so even Q1 origins use no same-year trade); one IMTS pull per origin-year, cached. This is the rigor robustness variant.

Schema — identical top-level shape to weights_24bloc.json (§10) but on the headline 6-bloc panel and with a window-specific window string:

Field Type Shape Meaning source string provenance (IMF IMTS, SDMX 2.1 — same string as §10) indicators string[] [2] ["XG_FOB_USD","MG_CIF_USD"] euro_area_code string "G163" window string the as-of window for this file, e.g. "2016-2018 average" — distinguishes cache entries and is copied into each forecast JSON's meta.weightsWindow blocs string[] [6] headline panel ["US","EA","JP","UK","CA","CN"] (US first) bloc_codes object 6 keys code → metadata map note string records the row-normalization, US-as-closed-anchor, <0.05% drop, mirror fallback weights object 5 keys (fixed/late files) or 6 keys weights[receiver][partner] = w, zero diagonal, each row sums to 1 over its partners

Receiver-row count is window-dependent — read defensively. The 2005-2009 (and other early/fixed) files carry 6 receiver rows including a US row (US endogenous, has its own RoW block); some windows (e.g. the 2021-2023 cache entry) carry 5 rows (US is the closed anchor / partner-only at that vintage, no US receiver row). Do not assume a US receiver row exists — resolve by iterating Object.keys(weights), exactly as the GVAR topology code does for weights_24bloc.json. The cached files are 6-sigfig per the IMTS builder; rows sum to 1 up to rounding (e.g. 0.999999).

Example (weights_asof_2016_2018.json, abridged — note the 5-bloc weights map under a 6-bloc blocs list, because US is partner-only at this window):

{
  "source": "IMF IMTS (International Trade in Goods, by partner country) — api.imf.org SDMX 2.1, flow IMF.STA:IMTS(1.0.0)",
  "indicators": ["XG_FOB_USD", "MG_CIF_USD"],
  "euro_area_code": "G163",
  "window": "2016-2018 average",
  "blocs": ["US", "EA", "JP", "UK", "CA", "CN"],
  "bloc_codes": { "US": {"…": "…"}, "EA": {"…": "…"} },
  "note": "Row-normalized over the other blocs (zero diagonal). US is the closed anchor (no RoW block) but is a partner for everyone. Negligible partners (<0.05%) dropped and the row renormalized. Mirror fallback for embargoed reporters (RU, TW).",
  "weights": {
    "EA": {"US":0.324999, "JP":0.066389, "UK":0.293980, "CA":0.030158, "CN":0.284473},
    "JP": {"US":"…", "EA":"…", "UK":"…", "CA":"…", "CN":"…"},
    "UK": {"…": "…"}, "CA": {"…": "…"}, "CN": {"…": "…"}
  }
}

docs.title: 13.2 forecasts/<model>/<origin>.json — per-origin predictive draw clouds
docs.text:
For each (model, origin) the driver (matlab/oos_driver.m → matlab/harvest_forecasts.m) writes one file holding the full predictive draw cloud (not just the mean) for the headline targets, sliced from the estimator's native Dforecast(H, n, nDraws−nBurn). Models are one directory each: emgvar_endo, emgvar_frozen, rw, rw_clim (climatology), arp, sbvar (single-country BVAR, ablation iii), cgvar_a (textbook OLS GVAR, iv-a), cgvar_b (single-pass Bayesian GVAR, iv-b), dfm.

Field Type Shape Meaning model string one of the model tags above origin string origin quarter, "YYYY-Qn" (e.g. "2018-Q3") blocs object 6 keys per-bloc forecasts (headline panel US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN) blocs[c] object 3 keys one entry per target: GROWTH, INFL, RATE blocs[c][t].draws number[][] [m][nh] the predictive cloud: m draws × nh requested horizons, in scored space (4·Δ for growth/inflation, level for rate). m is model-dependent: the Bayesian DK-smoother models (emgvar_endo/emgvar_frozen/cgvar_b/sbvar) keep the post-burn cloud m = nDraws−nBurn (≈100 at nDraws=200); the parametric/OLS/bootstrap benchmarks (rw/rw_clim/arp/cgvar_a/dfm) emit the full m = nDraws (200). Read m = draws.length, never assume it blocs[c][t].mean number[] [nh] predictive mean per horizon meta object run metadata (see below)

meta fields (verified in the harvested files):

meta key Type Meaning nDraws int requested NDRAWS (e.g. 200; the kept cloud size m is model-dependent — see the draws row above) lags int VAR lag order (3) emIters int EM star-loop iterations to convergence at this origin (1 for the MAXIT=1 single-pass ablations; 0 for the non-iterative benchmarks rw/arp/cgvar_a) covidInSample bool whether 2020Q2–Q4 ≤ t0 (so the COVID-as-missing mask applied in-sample) weightsWindow string the as-of weights window used (copied from §13.1's window) seed number per-origin RNG seed (reproducibility; cgl_bvarV5 calls rng default then offsets) usEndogenous bool whether the US joined the coupled loop with its own ROW*_US block at this origin (present on the EM-GVAR-family files; may be absent on the simple benchmarks) tol number star-convergence tolerance (0.05 sweep / 0.03 final) horizon int the estimator's full H (20) horizons int[] the scored horizons, in column order, e.g. [1,2,4,8]

The draws/mean columns are packed DENSELY by REQUESTED horizon, not by a dense 1:H layout. Column j of every draws[k]/mean is horizon meta.horizons[j] — so column 0 = h=1, column 1 = h=2, column 2 = h=4, column 3 = h=8 (for horizons=[1,2,4,8]). Always index horizons through meta.horizons; never assume column j ⇒ h = j+1. (harvest_forecasts.m records this mapping precisely for that reason.)

Example (forecasts/emgvar_endo/2018Q3.json, abridged — real meta, draws/mean shapes shown for one target):

{
  "model": "emgvar_endo",
  "origin": "2018-Q3",
  "blocs": {
    "US": {
      "GROWTH": {
        "draws": [ [1.91025, 2.44701, 1.69526, -0.78120], "… 100 draws × 4 horizons" ],
        "mean":  [3.07970, 2.39260, 1.39190, 0.79140]
      },
      "INFL": { "draws": [ ["…"], "…" ], "mean": ["…"] },
      "RATE": { "draws": [ ["…"], "…" ], "mean": ["…"] }
    },
    "EA": { "…": "…" }, "JP": {}, "UK": {}, "CA": {}, "CN": {}
  },
  "meta": {
    "nDraws": 200, "lags": 3, "emIters": 2, "covidInSample": false,
    "weightsWindow": "2015-2017 average", "seed": 1631573281,
    "usEndogenous": false, "tol": 0.05, "horizon": 20, "horizons": [1, 2, 4, 8]
  }
}

The scoring harness (eval/score.py, eval/tables.py) ingests these clouds plus realizations.json (§13.3) and emits RMSE/MAE, CRPS (primary density score), parametric log-score, PIT/coverage, and the Giacomini–White / Clark–West / Diebold–Mariano differentials (with Romano–Wolf + Model Confidence Set for multiplicity), per EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md §1.7.

docs.title: 13.3 realizations.json — the truth panel for scoring
docs.text:
The realized target values read from the full (untruncated) 2025Q4 vintage, written by matlab/build_realizations.m and indexed by [bloc][target][date] → scalar (the realized value in the same scored space as the forecasts: 4·Δ growth/inflation, level rate). The harness reads realizations[c][t][t0+h] for each scored (origin, horizon).

Field Type Shape Meaning (top level) object 6 keys US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN [c] object 3 keys GROWTH, INFL, RATE [c][t] object date → number "YYYY-Qn" → realized scalar in scored space

Example (abridged):

{
  "US": {
    "GROWTH": { "…": "…", "2023-Q4": 3.362060, "2024-Q1": "…" },
    "INFL":   { "2023-Q4": "…" },
    "RATE":   { "2023-Q4": "…" }
  },
  "EA": { "…": "…" }
}

Scored-space consistency is load-bearing. Realizations are computed the same way as the forecast draws (4·Δ(100·log level) for growth/inflation from the gdp/ price columns resolved by the estimator's findcol order; the rate level as-is). Since the 2026-06 CN-only re-estimate, CN's GDP is real (no longer deflated by subtraction); growth is scored as 4·Δ(100·log GDP_CN) directly, the same convention as the other blocs, on both the forecast and realization side (EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md §0).

docs.title: Empirical Evaluation — Out-of-Sample Forecast Horse-Race & Spillover Analysis
docs.text:
This page documents the empirical core of the accompanying paper: a rigorous out-of-sample (OOS) forecast-evaluation programme for the EM-Bayesian Global VAR, plus the endogenous-US spillover analysis the system uniquely enables. The methodology, the benchmark suite, the leakage-control invariants, the scoring metrics, and the significance machinery are all described here, together with the separate evaluation repository that implements them.

Empirical results are IN — read them honestly

The OOS sweep has run: 56 raw forecast origins (2010Q1–2024Q4), which leave ~55 scorable headline origins once the four 2020 origins are dropped (§4.2), × 9 models, CRPS-primary density scoring + a 90% Model Confidence Set. The headline numbers and the honest caveats are in Results and Tier-A spillover. The short version: AR(p)/DFM lead the point race, but the EM-Bayesian GVAR is inside the Model Confidence Set on the nominal block (inflation at every horizon, the short rate at h≤4), beats the textbook OLS GVAR decisively, and the endogenous-US variant weakly dominates the frozen one. The draw-based densities are over-confident (state this), and the parametric log-score column is numerically broken on the under-covered clouds — CRPS + MCS are the robust primaries.

docs.title: 1. Purpose
docs.text:
The methodology manual (User Manual PDF, §Empirical Results) lays out the estimation machinery; this page covers how that machinery is evaluated. The paper rests on two empirical pillars:

  1. An OOS forecast horse-race (Part 1). Does the EM-Bayesian GVAR forecast better than a ladder of progressively richer benchmarks — a random walk, an AR(p), a single-country BVAR, the textbook OLS GVAR, a single-pass Bayesian GVAR, and a dynamic factor model? The race is designed so two key ablations isolate (a) the value of the global coupling and (b) the value of the EM data-completion loop.

  2. An endogenous-US spillover analysis (Part 2). What does treating the US as an endogenous coupled bloc — rather than the classical frozen exogenous anchor — add? This is the channel a standard GVAR cannot even represent: a US shock that propagates abroad and feeds back into the US through the same coupled system.

The two pillars share one principle: every score must be computed in the exact data spaces and under the exact in-sample treatments the estimator and solver use, or the comparison is meaningless. Those invariants are stated up front (§4) and enforced with explicit asserts in the harness.

docs.title: 2. The separate evaluation repository
docs.text:
To keep the production tool clean (the deployed app and its estimators never depend on evaluation code), the OOS programme lives in a separate repository:

/home/farkas/github/em-bayesian-gvar-eval

docs.title: 2.1 How it references the main tool — BVAR_TOOL_ROOT
docs.text:
The eval repo is a forecast-evaluation harness only. It references the main tool's estimator, single-bloc Bayesian VAR, data, trade weights, and coupling solver, and never duplicates any estimator code (no copies of cgl_bvarV5.m, buildstars, gvarSolve.ts). The link is one environment variable:

export BVAR_TOOL_ROOT="/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool"   # default

Every MATLAB driver addpaths into $BVAR_TOOL_ROOT at the top of the script:

root = getenv('BVAR_TOOL_ROOT');
if isempty(root), root = '/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool'; end
addpath(genpath(fullfile(root,'BVAR','MFBVAR')));
addpath(genpath(fullfile(root,'BVAR','AdditionalFunctions')));
addpath(genpath(fullfile(root,'BVAR','BGLreplication')));

The Tier-A spillover driver eval/tier_a_scenario.mjs (run as node eval/tier_a_scenario.mjs --bp 100 from the em-bayesian-gvar-eval repo) imports the shipped solver from $BVAR_TOOL_ROOT/app/src/lib/compute/gvarSolve.ts via the app's @/ resolver. (It is a standalone Node driver, not a vitest test; the solver's own behaviour is exercised by app/src/__tests__/compute/gvar-us-endogenous.test.ts in the main repo.)

The key referenced files (all under $BVAR_TOOL_ROOT):

Role Path GVAR-EM estimator loop pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m Single-bloc estimator (GLP prior + Durbin–Koopman smoother) BVAR/MFBVAR/cgl_bvarV5.m Per-bloc input panels (~50 blocs) pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json Trade weights (33 blocs, US row) app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_full.json (shipped 2021–2023 avg — forbidden in the sweep, see §4) Trade-weights builder (IMTS SDMX) pipeline/build_trade_weights_full.py Coupling solver (x = Mx + d) app/src/lib/compute/gvarSolve.ts Reference exports — endogenous US pipeline/gvar_usendo_staging/<CC>/ Reference exports — frozen anchor pipeline/gvar_staging/<CC>/

docs.title: 2.2 Harness layout
docs.text:
config/eval_config.yaml          # machine-readable D1–D13 settings: origins, targets,
                                 #   horizons, benchmarks, knobs, paths
docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md       # the full protocol (design + runbook) — read this for rigor
matlab/
  oos_driver.m                   # origin × model loop; per-origin truncation + as-of weights;
                                 #   enforces the four look-ahead invariants with asserts
  run_one_origin.m               # parameterized single-origin copy of export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m
  harvest_forecasts.m            # RES.(c).Dfc -> scored per-origin forecast-draw JSON (§5 schema)
  build_realizations.m           # targets read from the UNTRUNCATED final vintage
  weights/
    rolling_asof_weights.m       # rolling as-of-t0 / fixed pre-sample trade weights (leak-free)
    build_presample_fallback.py  # cheap pre-2010 fixed-window fallback weights
  benchmarks/
    run_rw.m                     # (i) random walk + climatology sibling
    run_ar.m                     # (ii) univariate AR(p)
    run_single_bvar.m            # (iii) single-country BVAR (stars stripped)
    run_ols_gvar.m               # (iv-a) textbook OLS VARX* GVAR — MANDATORY new code
    run_bayes_gvar_1pass.m       # (iv-b) single-pass Bayesian GVAR (clean EM ablation)
    run_dfm.m                    # (v)  Bayesian dynamic factor model
    bench_common.m               # shared bloc-loading / scoring-space helpers
  spillover/
    tier_a_scenario.m            # Tier-A orchestrator (shells out to Node/vitest)
eval/
  score.py                       # PURE post-processing: RMSE/MAE/CRPS/LPS/PIT ingestion+aggregation
  tests.py                       # GW / CW / DM + Romano–Wolf + Model Confidence Set
  tables.py                      # paper-ready relative-RMSE / CRPS-LPS / significance / PIT tables
  tier_a_scenario.mjs            # Node wrapper that imports gvarSolve.ts and runs the solve
  requirements.txt               # numpy (required); scipy optional (skew-t LPS, χ² p-values)
results/                         # gitignored — per-origin forecast JSONs, realizations, tables, figures
scratch/                         # gitignored — per-origin truncated-panel overlays + diagnostics

docs.title: 2.3 The protocol document
docs.text: The complete design + runbook lives at /home/farkas/github/em-bayesian-gvar-eval/docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md. It is the authoritative source: it grounds every step in actual estimator and solver code, lists the thirteen author decisions (D1–D13, confirmed in config/eval_config.yaml), and specifies the per-origin truncation recipe. This page is a publication-quality summary of that protocol oriented to the MkDocs site; the protocol document is the spec the build agents follow.

docs.title: 3.1 The proposed model — two reported variants
docs.text:
The EM-Bayesian GVAR, exactly as pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m runs it. Both variants of decision D9 are reported as proposed-family entries:

  • emgvar_endo — the paper's contribution: the US is an endogenous coupled bloc (US_ENDOGENOUS=1), carrying its own ROW{DEM,INF,FIN}_US stars plus a US trade-weights row, and feeding the four global commons (GLB_OIL, GLB_USLR, GLB_USBAA, GLB_USEQ) that every Rest-of-World bloc pins to.
  • emgvar_frozen — the legacy frozen-anchor GVAR: the US is estimated once as a closed anchor; the RoW cannot feed back into the US. Cheaper, and it is exactly the Part-2 spillover contrast — so having both in the race lets the horse-race itself show whether endogenizing the US helps forecasts, not only spillovers.

Each bloc is the standard call cgl_bvarV5(x, lags=3, statCols, nDraws, nBurn, 1, H, 'mcmc',1,'MNpsi',0,'sur',0,'noc',1, ...). The EM loop is a Jacobi update: build all stars from the frozen start-of-sweep DK-completed panels, re-estimate every bloc in parfor, recompute the stars from the completed partner data, and iterate to max|Δstar| < TOL (sweep default 0.05; tighten to 0.03 for final tables) or MAXIT (default 12).

The forecasts and densities scored are produced at solve time from the estimator's per-draw predictive object Dforecast, not from the deployed app — the app's gvarSolve.ts is for interactive scenario propagation. The GVAR coupling still enters, because the stars (hence each bloc's regressors and completed history) are jointly determined by the EM fixed point.

docs.title: 3.2 The six benchmarks
docs.text:
The suite is built so each competitor is fair and strong. Two of the entries are deliberate ablations.

# Benchmark Id Specification (each on the same DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json, truncated ≤ t0) What it isolates (i) Random Walk / no-change rw (+ clim) Per target series, in the scored space: rate → last observed level carried flat; growth/inflation → the last observed value of the (already-differenced) transform carried flat (RW-in-growth, not a seasonal-RW on the level). Gaussian predictive density centred at the no-change value, scale = the in-sample SD of the h-step change estimated directly at horizon h (not √h·σ₁). A climatology (sample-mean) sibling is the second naive anchor. The universal reference; every relative-RMSE / -CRPS ratio is vs this. (ii) AR(p) per series arp Univariate AR on each target in its scored space, p by AIC over {1..4} (BIC and fixed p=4 as robustness). OLS with a constant; iterated (default, the strong h≥4 competitor) and direct multi-step variants. Gaussian innovation variance propagated h-steps. A strong univariate dynamic benchmark. (iii) Single-country Bayesian VAR sbvar The bloc estimated alone: drop the 3 ROW* star columns, keep the identical cgl_bvarV5 call (same GLP/Minnesota prior, lags=3, same 'MNpsi',0,'sur',0,'noc',1, same COVID-as-missing macro mask) and MAXIT=1. The GLP hyperprior is re-fit on the star-free panel (data-driven → fair). Value of the global coupling (vs proposed: only the RoW stars differ). (iv-a) Textbook OLS GVAR cgvar_a The literature GVAR (Pesaran–Schuermann–Weiner; Dees–di Mauro–Pesaran–Smith): per-bloc OLS VARX(p, q) with contemporaneous + lagged foreign stars as weakly-exogenous regressors, fixed (as-of) weights, no GLP prior, no COVID-as-missing (2020 dummied). A separate OLS-VARX path — the one piece of new estimator code in Part 1, and mandatory (without it the comparison is Bayesian-vs-Bayesian, not proposed-vs-literature). Value of the whole Bayesian + EM + COVID apparatus vs the literature GVAR. (iv-b) Single-pass Bayesian GVAR cgvar_b Identical to the proposed model — same cgl_bvarV5/GLP prior + COVID-as-missing, frozen-anchor US — except MAXIT=1: the stars are built once, with no EM re-convergence. Value of the EM data-completion / star re-convergence alone (vs proposed: identical except the star fixed point). (v) Bayesian dynamic factor model dfm A few static factors (3–5) extracted from the cross-bloc target panel (EM/PCA-with-missing on the masked panel), a Bayesian VAR(1–2) on the factors + Gaussian idiosyncratics, predictive density by simulation. A non-GVAR multivariate global benchmark — the "is the GVAR structure even needed?" competitor.

The nesting structure that the significance tests respect (§6): (i) RW / (ii) AR ⊂ every VAR model, and (iii) sbvar ⊂ (iv-b) cgvar_b ⊂ proposed emgvar_*.

docs.title: 3.3 Targets, scopes, horizons
docs.text:
• Targets (three per bloc), resolved via the estimator's findcol order — never hard-coded indices:
  • GROWTH = real GDP growth = 4·Δ(100·log gdp level), gdp = GDP_<CC> | GDPH;
  • INFL = inflation = 4·Δ(100·log price level), price = HICP_<CC> | CPI_<CC> | JGDP;
  • RATE = short rate = the linear level (already % p.a.), rate = RATE_<CC> | POLRATE | YIELD10_<CC> (optional — CH/NO have none and drop out, and drop out of partners' ROWFIN).
  • CN GDP is real (since the 2026-06 CN-only re-estimate) → scored as 4·Δ(100·log GDP_CN) directly, the same as the other blocs (no subtraction-deflation).
  • Scopes (D3): headline = US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN × {growth, inflation, short rate} (the body table); full = all 33 estimated blocs (rate-less blocs contribute growth + inflation), reported as median + IQR of relative-RMSE across blocs in an appendix.
  • Horizons (D7): h ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8} quarters. The estimator runs H=20; the harness slices the first eight rows of Dforecast and scores this subset.

docs.title: 4. Recursive expanding window, as-of weights, and the look-ahead invariants
4.1 Scheme (D1, D2)
docs.text:
• Pseudo-real-time on the final 2025Q4 vintage (a genuine real-time vintage does not exist for most of the ~50-economy panel; a US + EA-subset real-time run is a flagged robustness extension, not the default).
  • Expanding window, recursive. First origin 2010-Q1, last 2024-Q4 (the 2010Q1–2024Q4 window spans 60 quarters; 56 raw origins are scorable once each (origin, h) is trimmed so a realization ≤ 2025Q4 exists — h=8 only for origins ≤ 2023Q4, h=4 for ≤ 2024Q4, …). The headline then drops the four 2020 forecast origins (an outlier the paper is not about), leaving ~55 scorable headline origins. COVID-as-missing still applies in-sample at every origin where 2020Q2–Q4 ≤ t0.
  • Short-sample rule. A bloc enters at origin t0 only if it has ≥ 40 in-sample quarters there (the headline six all qualify from 2010Q1).

docs.title: 4.2 Rolling as-of-t0 trade weights (D11)
docs.text: The single biggest leakage risk is the trade weights. The shipped weights_full.json is a fixed 2021–2023 average; using it at a 2010–2020 origin injects future trade structure. Therefore, for each origin the driver rebuilds the weights from an as-of window ending ≤ t0 (rolling 3-year average, window = (Y-3)..(Y-1) for origin year Y), renormalized over the partners present, cached one file per origin-year. A fixed pre-2010 window (e.g. 2005–2009) is the cheap, leak-free fallback (it biases toward the past, never the future). The shipped 2021–2023 file is forbidden in the sweep.

docs.title: 4.3 The four look-ahead invariants (asserted at every origin)
docs.text:
oos_driver.m enforces all four with explicit asserts:

  1. Panel truncation (I1). Every DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json is sliced to dates ≤ t0 (rows + the embedded GLB_* global columns) before transformData and before any star is built. Hard assert: no surviving row may date after t0.
  2. As-of weights (I2). The chosen weights file's window must end ≤ year(t0); the deployed weights_full.json/weights_24bloc.json artefact is refused by path. (A 2024 origin's freshly-rebuilt 2021–2023 as-of file is legitimately allowed — it ends 2023 ≤ 2024 — while the shipped file is not, the only field that distinguishes them.)
  3. Star re-convergence is in-sample only (I3). buildstars reads partners' DK-completed panels; because every partner panel is itself truncated to ≤ t0, no star cell at any in-sample date can read a post-t0 realization. Asserted inside the per-origin EM loop (run_one_origin.m).
  4. No 2025Q4 harmonization at earlier origins (I4). The "2025Q4 vintage" is only the well from which ≤ t0 rows are drawn — no seasonal re-estimation, rebasing, or deflator splice that uses post-t0 data touches the ≤ t0 panel. transformData is a fixed deterministic map (100·log, 4·Δ) with no estimated parameters, so it leaks nothing.

Determinism. cgl_bvarV5 calls rng default at entry, so a given (x, p, nDraws) is reproducible. The driver records a per-(origin, bloc) seed in each forecast JSON's meta.

docs.title: 5. The per-origin forecast JSON contract
docs.text:
For each (model, origin) the driver writes one file at results/forecasts/<model>/<origin>.json. The scorer ingests these — no MATLAB in the scoring path.

{
  "model":  "emgvar_endo | emgvar_frozen | sbvar | cgvar_a | cgvar_b | rw | clim | arp | dfm",
  "origin": "2019-Q1",
  "blocs": {
    "US": {
      "GROWTH": { "draws": [[h1,h2,h4,h8], ...], "mean": [h1,h2,h4,h8] },
      "INFL":   { "draws": [...], "mean": [...] },
      "RATE":   { "draws": [...], "mean": [...] }
    },
    "EA": { ... }, "...": {}
  },
  "meta": { "nDraws": 200, "lags": 3, "emIters": 2, "covidInSample": false,
            "weightsWindow": "2005-2009", "usEndogenous": true,
            "horizons": [1,2,4,8], "seed": 12345 }
}

The draws are already in the scored space (growth/inflation = 4·Δ(100·log level), rate = linear level); the scorer treats every value as a real number and does not re-transform. The columns are packed densely by requested horizon (column j holds horizon meta.horizons[j] — e.g. cols 1..4 = h = 1, 2, 4, 8), and eval/score.py reads that list from meta to map a requested h to its column. At NDRAWS=200 (nBurn = half) a typical cloud is ~100 post-burn draws × 4 horizons.

Realizations are either embedded per cell (var_node["realized"]) or supplied separately in results/realizations.json (keyed bloc → var → target-date → value), read identically from the untruncated vintage by matlab/build_realizations.m. A cell with no realization ≤ 2025Q4 is simply not scored.

docs.title: 6.1 Point and density scores (eval/score.py)
docs.text:
eval/score.py is pure post-processing: it never touches MATLAB or the estimator. It aligns each (model, origin, bloc, variable, horizon) forecast to the realized value and computes:

  • Point. RMSE, MAE, and the headline relative-RMSE = RMSE(model)/RMSE(RW) (<1 ⇒ beats RW).
  • CRPS — the PRIMARY density score. The unbiased empirical-ensemble estimator CRPS = (1/m)Σ|X_k − y| − (1/2m²)ΣΣ|X_k − X_l| (computed via the sorted-array O(m log m) identity), robust to non-Gaussian draw clouds and well-behaved at m≈100 draws. CRPS is the metric the conclusions lean on.
  • Log predictive score (DISCARDED — numerically broken, see §9.3). The LPS column was intended as a corroborating density score: a parametric Gaussian fit to the draw cloud, with a closed-form method-of-moments skew-normal for asymmetry and a winsorized KDE robustness check. In the realized results the parametric Gaussian log-score explodes on the under-covered GVAR-family clouds (magnitudes up to ~1e16, §9.3) — it is a scoring artifact of fitting a Gaussian to a sub-0.90-coverage cloud, not a model pathology. The LPS column is not reported; CRPS + the MCS are the density primaries.
  • Calibration. Randomized PIT of each realization in its ensemble → PIT histograms per (model, horizon), with 50% / 90% coverage and a χ² uniformity p-value.

Density caveat — confirmed in the results (§9.4)

Dforecast propagates only the shock + within-draw (β, Σ) MH variation forward from the last observed state; it does not integrate over the DK-smoother uncertainty of the COVID-imputed states, and the forecast is seeded from the posterior-mean completed state. The realized PIT (§9.4) bears this out: the GVAR-family 90% intervals cover only ~0.73–0.85 vs the 0.90 target, worst at h=1 — the densities are over-confident. This is why every density conclusion leans on CRPS (which is calibration-robust) and the parametric log-score is discarded (§9.3). Treat the under-coverage as a scoped, known fix (widen the bands / integrate the smoother uncertainty), not a model failure.

docs.title: 6.2 Nesting-correct predictive-ability tests (eval/tests.py)
docs.text:
The nesting structure dictates the test — the single most common referee kill-shot:

  • Giacomini–White (GW) — the HEADLINE test. Valid under nesting and recursive/expanding-scheme-correct (GW assume a finite estimation window / non-vanishing estimation uncertainty — exactly this setup), and loss-agnostic, so it is used for point and density (CRPS/LPS) differentials alike. Conditional Wald W = T·z̄'Ω̂⁻¹z̄ ~ χ²_q with instruments g_{t−1} = [1, d_{t−1}] and an optional recession / financial-stress indicator (state-dependent predictive ability).
  • Clark–West (CW) — for the nested point/MSE comparisons; the adjusted differential f_t = e_small² − [e_large² − (ŷ_small − ŷ_large)²] removes the larger model's estimation-noise penalty (one-sided: large positive ⇒ the larger model genuinely helps).
  • Diebold–Mariano (DM) — non-nested pairs only (e.g. proposed vs DFM, or vs the OLS GVAR when neither nests the other): two-sided, HAC (Newey–West) long-run variance with bandwidth ≥ h−1, plus the Harvey–Leybourne–Newbold small-sample correction (~50–60 origins is small). Standard DM is non-standard/oversized for the nested proposed-vs-benchmark pairs that are the paper's point — using it there invites rejection.

Multiplicity (never read a single star in isolation):

  • Romano–Wolf step-down — FWER control over the family of comparisons via a moving-block bootstrap with shared resampling indices (respects cross-test dependence).
  • Model Confidence Set (Hansen–Lunde–Nason) — per (variable, horizon), the set of models statistically indistinguishable from the best (default loss = CRPS).

The nesting map and the test selection are stated once in eval/tables.py (NESTING, is_nested) so the stars always use the correct test per pair.

docs.title: 6.3 Outputs (eval/tables.py)
docs.text: tables.py is the last, pure-Python presentation stage. build_all() produces: the relative-RMSE matrix (headline-6 averages) + full-panel distribution; the CRPS / LPS tables with ratios vs RW; the GW / CW / DM significance stars with the nesting label, Romano–Wolf adjusted set, and per-(variable, horizon) MCS membership; and the PIT histograms + coverage with the χ² uniformity p-value. Each table is emitted as both a text grid (logs) and a nested-dict JSON blob (LaTeX/plotting).

docs.title: 7. The Tier-A spillover analysis (Part 2)
docs.text:
The shipped solver (gvarSolve.ts) computes a conditional-mean scenario response, not GIRFs/FEVDs. The protocol therefore splits Part 2 into two tiers, and decision D13 gates Tier A first:

  • Tier A — directly solver-computable (the headline, zero new estimator code). The conditional scenario propagation of a US shock, endogenous vs frozen, from solveGVARClosedFormEndo / solveGVARLinear. This is the empirical Part-2 contribution the shipped code already supports.
  • Tier B — deferred new code. The GIRF, generalized FEVD, and Diebold–Yilmaz connectedness index require a coupled-companion MA/GIRF builder. The ingredients exist in the exports (companion_mean.json carries each bloc's betahat = companion Γ(1..p) and sigmahat = Σ; the coupling M is assembled by gvarSolve.ts), so it is constructible — but it is a new module (pipeline/gvar_girf.*), validated against the Tier-A response, and is the structural extension, not the submission gate.

docs.title: 7.1 The headline Tier-A contrast
docs.text:
matlab/spillover/tier_a_scenario.m orchestrates the contrast (it shells out to eval/tier_a_scenario.mjs, since the solver is pure TypeScript and MATLAB cannot import it):

  1. Set the pin. Hold US POLRATE at baseline +100 bp over the whole horizon — a multi-period conditional projection on the entire rate path (correctly not a one-period shock or a GIRF; it answers "if the US rate were 100 bp higher throughout, what does the coupled system's conditional mean do").
  2. Solve both topologies from the same endogenous-staging exports, the frozen variant produced by stripping the US export's three ROW*_US columns in-memory so the RoW blocs and GLB_* commons are byte-identical across solves (the gap isolates exactly one channel — US endogeneity):
  3. Endogenous (US_ENDOGENOUS=true ⇒ solveGVARClosedFormEndo): the augmented state carries every bloc's ROW{DEM,INF,FIN} star deltas including the US's own (the RoW→US feedback) plus the US global-column deltas that drive every RoW bloc. ⇒ delta.US ≠ 0.
  4. Frozen (US_ENDOGENOUS=false ⇒ solveGVARLinear): the US is the exogenous driver; its scenario enters only the forcing d; there is no ROW*_US row in the fixed point. ⇒ delta.US == the pinned scenario only.
  5. Report per bloc (US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN), in the scored space: peak (max-abs) conditional response and peak horizon for growth/inflation/rate under each topology; the gap = endogenous − frozen (the channel the frozen anchor cannot represent — the world→US direction is structurally absent, not "present but zero"); and the spectral radius ρ(M) per topology (the multiplier strength). The deployed coupling artifact coupling_default.json.gz reports rhoSpectral = 0.924 for the endogenous 33-bloc system at dim = 2060 (the frozen-anchor 24-bloc production configuration is ≈0.723 at dim = 1380). The Tier-A test itself runs on the 6-bloc US-anchored subset (US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN) and reports, in results/tierA/us_rate_100bp.json, a frozen-route rhoSpectral ≈ 0.506 (direct inverse) versus an endogenous route solved in a single Neumann sweep (sweepCount = 1) — a different, smaller operator than the deployed 33-bloc system, so its radius is not the deployed 0.924.

This example doubles as a solver-validation canary: the closed-form endogenous result reproduces the iterative solveGVAR fixed point to < 1e-4 across every bloc, and a warm coupling-cache hit is byte-identical (< 1e-9) to a cold rebuild — both asserted in the main repo's gvar-us-endogenous / gvar-closed-form-routing test suites (the iterative reference is itself converged to 1e-7).

docs.title: 8. How to run it
docs.text:
export BVAR_TOOL_ROOT="/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool"
cd /home/farkas/github/em-bayesian-gvar-eval
export OOS_SCRATCH="$PWD/scratch"

docs.title: 8.1 Pilot first — and the run is GATED on it
docs.text:
The full sweep is expensive: the GVAR-EM re-runs at every origin, and the csminwel GLP-mode optimization inside each cgl_bvarV5 call is a fixed cost that does not scale with NDRAWS (so NDRAWS=200 cuts the MH/Durbin–Koopman loop ~5× but not the mode-finding). The protocol therefore gates the full run on a pilot.

# Pilot (the gate): headline 6 blocs, ~8 origins, all models, NDRAWS=200, as-of weights.
matlab -batch "oos_driver('Origins', {'2015-Q1','2016-Q1','2017-Q1','2018-Q1', \
                                       '2019-Q1','2021-Q1','2022-Q1','2023-Q1'})"

The pilot's three jobs: (1) validate the harness end-to-end (truncation, as-of weights, scoring, GW/CW) and the §4 invariants; (2) measure the actual per-origin wall-clock and back-substitute it into the compute budget; (3) confirm the qualitative ranking on the legible subset. Do not launch the full ~55-origin × 33-bloc run until the pilot's measured time × scale fits the budget — if it implies > 2 days serial, fan out across origins on a small cluster first.

oos_driver.m knobs (Name/Value, each with an env fallback so a job array can set them per task): Origins, Models ({emgvar_endo, emgvar_frozen, sbvar, cgvar_b, cgvar_a, rw, arp, dfm}), Blocs (BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS, US first), NDraws (NDRAWS, default 200), Maxit (MAXIT, default 12), Tol (TOL, default 0.05), MinQ (40), WeightsMode (fixed | rolling), DropCovidOrigins (true), WarmStart, KeepScratch.

docs.title: 8.2 Full sweep (only after the pilot passes the gate)
docs.text:
# Origins 2010Q1..2024Q4 (driver drops 2020 origins + trims unrealizable (origin,h)).
# Both proposed variants + all benchmarks; NDRAWS=200 sweep, TOL=0.05.
matlab -batch "oos_driver()"     # defaults from config/eval_config.yaml + env

Re-run the final headline tables at NDRAWS=1000 and TOL=0.03 (D8). Planning estimate: a full sweep ≈ 8–16 h with mitigations (reduced NDRAWS, parfor over blocs, across-origin fan-out, csminwel warm-start), dominated by the two GVAR-family EM runs; ~40–70 h without mitigations.

docs.title: 8.3 Score and tabulate (pure post-processing, no MATLAB)
docs.text:
python3 -m pip install -r eval/requirements.txt        # numpy required; scipy optional

# Quick relative-RMSE / CRPS / LPS / coverage summary (headline blocs):
python3 -m eval.score --results results --headline

# Full table suite -> results/tables.json + results/tables.txt
python3 -m eval.tables --results results --out-json results/tables.json \
                                          --out-text results/tables.txt

eval/tests.py is imported by tables.py for the GW/CW/DM stars, the Romano–Wolf family p-values, and the per-(variable, horizon) Model Confidence Set; it can also be called directly on a score.load_panel(...) record list.

docs.title: 8.4 Part-2 Tier-A spillover contrast
docs.text:
# Endogenous vs frozen conditional-scenario contrast, driven from the eval repo:
cd "$EVAL_REPO_ROOT" && node eval/tier_a_scenario.mjs --bp 100
# or, MATLAB-orchestrated (shells out to Node):
matlab -batch "tier_a_scenario('bp', 100)"

docs.title: 9. Results — the OOS horse-race
docs.text:
Provenance of the numbers below

Every figure in this section is read directly from /home/farkas/github/em-bayesian-gvar-eval/results/tables.{txt,json} (n_records_headline = 33912). The headline scope is the six anchor blocs (US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN) × {growth, inflation, short rate} × h ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8}. Per cell the full-sample benchmarks (rw, rw_clim, arp, dfm, sbvar, cgvar_a) score ~55 origins (e.g. 328 growth cells ÷ 6 blocs ≈ 54.7); the GVAR-EM family (cgvar_b, emgvar_endo, emgvar_frozen) scores ~51 origins (304 cells ÷ 6 ≈ 50.7) after the short-sample (≥40q) and stability trims.

docs.title: 9.1 Headline read
docs.text:
1. Point accuracy: AR(p) and the DFM lead. On relative-RMSE and point CRPS, the DFM wins every growth horizon (CRPS/RW = 0.745 / 0.836 / 0.857 / 0.841 at h = 1/2/4/8) and AR(p) wins inflation and the short rate at the short and medium horizons (e.g. INFL CRPS/RW = 0.629 at h=1; RATE CRPS/RW = 0.811 at h=1). The EM-Bayesian GVAR does not win the point race — this is stated plainly, not buried.
  2. But the EM-Bayesian GVAR is inside the 90% Model Confidence Set on the nominal block. Per (variable, horizon) MCS (Hansen–Lunde–Nason, CRPS loss, 90% level, results/tables.json["mcs"]):
    • Inflation: emgvar_endo and emgvar_frozen are in the MCS at all four horizons (h = 1, 2, 4, 8) — statistically indistinguishable from the best inflation model at every horizon.
    • Short rate: emgvar_endo is in the MCS at h = 1, 2, 4 (it drops out only at h=8, MCS p = 0.05). emgvar_frozen is already out at h=1 (p = 0.083).
    • Growth: the GVAR family is out at h=1 (emgvar_endo removed at p = 0.024) and h=8; emgvar_endo is in the MCS at h = 2 and h = 4 (emgvar_frozen too at h = 2, 4). Growth is the weak block — consistent with the point read.
  3. The endogenous-US design earns its keep (endo ⪰ frozen). In the MCS, emgvar_endo weakly dominates emgvar_frozen. The decisive case is the short rate at h=1, where emgvar_endo is in the set (p = 0.150) and emgvar_frozen is out (p = 0.083); at growth h=8 emgvar_endo is removed after emgvar_frozen (p = 0.099 vs 0.057). The two track each other closely elsewhere — the endogenous contribution is most visible in the Part-2 spillover contrast (§10), as expected.
  4. It decisively beats the textbook OLS classical GVAR. cgvar_a (literature PSW/DdMPS OLS VARX, no GLP prior, no COVID-as-missing) is out of the MCS at growth h=1/4/8, inflation h=4/8, and the short rate h=1, and posts the worst relative CRPS in the panel (up to CRPS/RW ≈ 2.5 at INFL h=1). The EM-Bayesian treatment — GLP shrinkage + COVID-as-missing + the star fixed point — is the demonstrable value-add over the literature GVAR.* This is the cleanest empirical claim the race supports.

The single best in-family point number is emgvar_endo at inflation h=1, relative-RMSE 0.799 (and CRPS/RW 0.814) — the best of the GVAR-family entries, though AR(p) (rel-RMSE 0.641, CRPS/RW 0.629) is the overall inflation-h1 leader. The honest framing is "competitive on the nominal block, MCS-confirmed, not the point winner."

docs.title: 9.2 Relative-RMSE vs RW (headline-6 average)
docs.text:
Lower is better; < 1.000 beats the random walk. Bold = best per column.

Block Model h=1 h=2 h=4 h=8 GROWTH dfm 0.917 0.989 0.986 0.962 rw_clim 0.957 0.995 0.983 0.965 arp 1.195 0.990 0.968 0.960 emgvar_endo 1.191 1.091 1.026 1.031 cgvar_a 2.097 1.013 1.019 0.993 INFL arp 0.641 0.772 0.998 0.921 emgvar_endo 0.799 0.891 1.085 0.953 dfm 0.804 0.870 1.067 0.908 rw_clim 0.810 0.891 1.106 0.869 cgvar_a 2.113 1.050 1.366 1.075 RATE sbvar 0.990 1.081 1.195 1.421 arp 0.829 0.863 0.919 1.025 emgvar_endo 1.017 1.121 1.226 1.444 cgvar_a 1.531 1.200 1.034 1.001

(emgvar_frozen tracks emgvar_endo to ±0.01 on rel-RMSE throughout — the maximum gap is 0.0099 at INFL h=8; the full 9-model grid is in results/tables.txt.) Note emgvar_endo beats RW on every inflation horizon except h=4, and is the best in-family inflation entry at h=1.

docs.title: 9.3 Density: CRPS is the primary, the parametric log-score is broken
docs.text: CRPS/RW per cell drives every density conclusion (the MCS in §9.1 uses CRPS loss). The parametric Gaussian log-score column (LPS(G) in tables.txt) is numerically unusable for the GVAR family: on the under-covered, occasionally heavy-tailed draw clouds it explodes to absurd magnitudes (e.g. LPS(G) ≈ 1.1e15 for cgvar_b at growth h=1, ≈ 6.7e16 for emgvar_endo at growth h=8). Those are a scoring artifact of fitting a Gaussian density to a cloud whose realized PIT coverage is below target, not a model pathology. Do not report the parametric LPS; report CRPS and the MCS.

docs.title: 9.4 Calibration: the densities are over-confident (state this)
docs.text:
The randomized-PIT 90% coverage (tables.json["pit"], target 0.90) is below target for the GVAR family at the short horizon:

Model cov90 h=1 cov90 h=2 cov90 h=4 cov90 h=8 emgvar_endo 0.74 0.75 0.82 0.83 emgvar_frozen 0.73 0.74 0.81 0.83 sbvar 0.78 0.80 0.82 0.85 cgvar_b 0.77 0.78 0.82 0.85 dfm 0.86 0.85 0.86 0.86 rw 0.92 0.92 0.90 0.88

The GVAR-family 90% intervals realize ~0.73–0.85 coverage versus the 0.90 target — they are over-confident, worst at h=1, improving with horizon. This is exactly the caveat in §6.1: Dforecast propagates only the within-draw shock + (β, Σ) variation from the posterior-mean completed state and does not integrate the DK-smoother uncertainty of the COVID-imputed states. The bands should be widened (or the smoother uncertainty integrated) before the densities are taken at face value — a scoped, known fix, not a property of the model at full coverage. CRPS, being calibration-robust, is why the conclusions lean on it.

docs.title: 10. Tier-A spillover results
docs.text:
eval/tier_a_scenario.mjs ran the US +100 bp conditional projection on the 6-bloc US-anchored subset, endogenous vs frozen, from the same endogenous-staging exports (pipeline/gvar_usendo_staging); the frozen route strips the three ROW*_US columns in-memory so the RoW blocs and GLB_* commons are byte-identical across solves. Results in results/tierA/us_rate_100bp.json.

Read this as a multi-period conditional projection, NOT a causal IRF

The scenario pins US POLRATE at baseline + 100 bp over the whole 20-quarter horizon — identification: "multi-period conditional projection … NOT a structural shock or GIRF" (verbatim from the JSON). The endogenous-vs-frozen gap isolates the one channel the frozen anchor structurally omits (RoW → US feedback + the global-commons amplification), and that gap is clearly non-trivial. But the magnitudes are large and sign-flipping across blocs, and a pinned multi-period projection is not a causal impulse response. The magnitudes need a validation pass (and ideally a GIRF, the deferred Tier B) before being headlined. Treat the qualitative result — "endogenizing the US opens a feedback channel the frozen anchor cannot represent" — as the claim, and the specific numbers as provisional.

docs.title: 10.1 The endogenous − frozen gap (peak conditional response, scored space)
docs.text:
Peak (max-abs over h ≤ 20) of the conditional response, by bloc and topology, with the gap = endogenous − frozen (the channel absent under the frozen anchor). Growth/inflation are in 4·Δ(level); the rate column is the pinned level (so delta.US.RATE = 1.0 exactly under both, and = the scenario only under frozen). All from us_rate_100bp.json.

Bloc Var endo peak frozen peak gap (endo − frozen) US GROWTH +1.278 −4.370 +4.224 INFL −1.528 −2.824 +1.296 EA GROWTH +1.035 −0.945 +0.877 INFL −1.005 −0.636 +1.072 JP GROWTH +1.074 +1.617 −1.177 INFL +1.107 −0.412 +0.817 UK GROWTH +2.140 −3.138 +2.937 INFL +0.749 −1.007 +0.864 CA GROWTH +1.332 +1.223 −0.474 INFL −1.647 −1.412 +1.595 CN GROWTH +1.561 +1.211 −1.341 INFL −0.694 +1.194 −1.292

The US row is the point: under the frozen anchor the US cannot receive any RoW feedback (its delta is the pinned scenario only), so the US growth/inflation "frozen peak" columns above are the closed-anchor own-dynamics; the +4.2 growth / +1.3 inflation gap is exactly the RoW → US channel the frozen GVAR structurally cannot produce. The cross-bloc gaps are large and change sign (e.g. JP and CN growth gaps are negative, UK and EA positive) — which is precisely why the magnitudes are flagged as needing validation rather than headlined as causal spillovers.

docs.title: 10.2 Solver-validation canary
docs.text: The same run doubles as a solver check: the closed-form endogenous solve reproduces the iterative solveGVAR fixed point to < 1e-4 across every bloc, and a warm coupling-cache hit is byte-identical (< 1e-9) to a cold rebuild — both asserted in the main repo's gvar-us-endogenous / gvar-closed-form-routing suites (the iterative reference is itself converged to 1e-7). The endogenous route here closes in a single Neumann sweep (sweepCount = 1 in the JSON); the frozen route uses the direct inverse (method: "direct", rhoSpectral ≈ 0.506 on this 6-bloc operator).

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Protocol (authoritative): /home/farkas/github/em-bayesian-gvar-eval/docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md
  • User Manual (PDF) — §Empirical Results scaffold, the estimation methodology these scores evaluate, and the per-economy variable (transformation · prior · financial) tables.
  • Architecture — the GVAR topology, the closed-form endogenous solve, and the data-space conventions the harness mirrors.
  • Pipeline — the offline estimate → port → couple chain that produces the exports the harness references.
  • Changelog — this session's fiscal-prior, coherence, and empirical-harness changes.

docs.title: Maintainer's Guide — Manually Updating a Country Model
docs.text:
This is the core human runbook for taking one country model end-to-end: change its data, re-estimate it, check the MATLAB output, store the JSONs, regenerate the coupling artifact, and run the green gate. It is written to be copy-paste followable with exact paths and commands.

Read this with, not instead of: ADDING_A_COUNTRY.md (full data/spec/registry mechanics, the worked US example, the fiscal-block builder), REESTIMATING_MODELS.md (the re-estimation/port/coupling/gate sequence with the env-knob tables), and ARCHITECTURE_FOR_AI.md (the compute engine, data-space conventions, topology). This page does not re-derive those — it links to them and adds the per-country maintenance angle: the decision tree, the file-by-file edit map, the verification checks, and a troubleshooting table.

The platform today: ~50 economies = 33 GVAR-coupled blocs (a fully endogenous US dominant unit + an EA aggregate + 31 Rest-of-World blocs) + 17 euro-area FSAP standalones. Data is harmonized to a common 2025Q4 vintage from FRED / Eurostat / BIS / ECB. Live at the /bvar-scenario path of the academic website, hosted on GitHub Pages (the matyasfarkas/starter-hugo-academic Hugo Academic site).

Repo root in every command below is the absolute path "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool" (quote it — it has spaces).

docs.title: 0. Decision tree — what kind of update is this?
docs.text:
Pick the smallest job that covers your change. The rough times assume the production hardware (MATLAB with the Parallel Computing Toolbox).

You changed… Job What you run Re-estimate? Coupling regen? Rough time A fiscal driver only (revenue/expenditure/debt grid) for a fiscal bloc Fiscal-only refresh build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py → re-estimate that bloc → port → coupling yes (the bloc) yes minutes (build) + the bloc's estimation Macro data of one euro-area FSAP standalone (the 17 euro members: AT BE DE EE ES FI FR GR IE IT LT LU LV NL PT SI SK) Single-country re-estimate the country builder → export_bvar_euro_fsap.m (FSAP two-country, writes /tmp/euro_fsap_export/) → GVAR_EXPORT_DIR=/tmp/euro_fsap_export port → gate yes (that bloc only) no (not in the coupled GVAR — no weights row) ~minutes Macro data of one coupled bloc (US / EA / any RoW) Full GVAR re-estimate build_* → export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m (US_ENDOGENOUS=1) → port → coupling → gate yes (all 33 — stars change for everyone) yes hours A variable's PRIOR (RW↔WN stationary) on one bloc Prior-change re-estimate tag the prior in the raw _glob.json (tag_fiscal_wn.py for the 6 fiscal blocs, else edit by hand) → re-estimate that bloc → port → (coupling if coupled) → gate (§B5) yes (the bloc) only if coupled the bloc's estimation A GLB_* / variable isFinancial flag (COVID-mask membership) Coherence re-estimate fix the flag in the builder + raw _glob.json → re-estimate the affected bloc(s) → port → coupling → gate (§B6) yes yes (coupled) the bloc(s) Add a brand-new country Add-country follow ADDING_A_COUNTRY.md §B; if GVAR, add a weights row → full re-estimate yes yes (if GVAR) hours App/compute code only (no data) Code change edit → tsc + vitest + build no no ~minutes

Why a coupled-bloc refresh re-estimates everyone. Each bloc's RoW "stars" are trade-weighted averages of partners' DK-completed series. Touch one bloc's data and every partner's star moves, so export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m re-estimates the whole 33-bloc set to the cross-country fixed point. There is no "just re-estimate the US" path for a coupled bloc. (Verified in pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m: with US_ENDOGENOUS, rowB = blocs — the full set iterates.)

When is a single-bloc re-estimate legitimate? Three cases, and only three: 1. A euro-area FSAP standalone (AT BE DE EE ES FI FR GR IE IT LT LU LV NL PT SI SK) — it has no weights row, no partner reads its series, so re-estimating it alone is exact. Use export_bvar_euro_fsap.m; no coupling regen. 2. A change that does not move the stars — e.g. flipping a domestic variable's prior (RW↔WN) or a non-star isFinancial flag on a coupled bloc. The bloc's own betahat changes, but the stars it exports to partners are unaffected only if the changed column is neither a target (gdp/price/rate, which feed ROWDEM/ROWINF/ROWFIN) nor the COVID-mask membership of a target. In practice the safe, audited path is still the full loop — see the caveat in §B5. A genuine one-bloc shortcut is the BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US <CC>" + MAXIT=1 separated run (§B7), which freezes every other bloc's stars at their last value; it is exact only when no partner's star depends on the changed bloc (or you accept frozen partner stars for a quick check, then do the full run before deploy). 3. App/compute code — no estimation at all.

The honest rule of thumb: if the bloc is coupled and the change can move any of its three target columns, do the full loop. The single-bloc path is for euro standalones, code, and quick local checks — not for a production deploy of a coupled bloc.

docs.title: A1. Pick the right builder
docs.text:
Country class Builder (pipeline/) Output FRED majors (EA/UK/JP/CA …) build_country_data.py raw matrix from FRED US (macro + fiscal drivers) build_us_auto.py US glob + fiscal drivers OECD EU/EFTA (Eurostat) build_oecd_eurostat.py / build_oecd_batch.py OECD economies GVAR globals/stars assembly build_gvar_2025q4.py, build_globals_new_blocs.py DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json (domestic + 4 GLB_*) Euro policy-rate + house prices build_euro_polrate_hpi.py rate + HPI columns Euro shared financial block build_euro_financial_block.py EA corp/rates Fiscal block (4-var) build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py (cc ∈ us/ea/jp/uk/ca/cn) edits glob + fiscal_meta

The FRED key is now a hardcoded fallback in the scripts, but always pass your own to be safe: FRED_API_KEY=<your_key> python3 pipeline/<builder>.py. Never commit a key (app/.env.local is git-ignored).

docs.title: A2. Where the raw data lives, and the precedence
docs.text:
The estimator reads one raw file per bloc, in this precedence (first that exists wins — verified in pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m):

pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json   # 1st — globals-augmented (domestic + 4 GLB_* commons)
pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_2025q4.json # 2nd — the dated vintage
pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>.json        # 3rd — legacy

Precedence trap. If a *_glob.json exists, an edit to *_2025q4.json or the bare file is invisible to the estimator. For a GVAR bloc, always edit/rebuild the _glob.json — it is the file with the 4 GLB_* commons the loop needs.

docs.title: A3. Add / swap a series
docs.text:
A series is a tuple in the builder's COUNTRIES block (mirror an existing entry, e.g. EA in build_country_data.py):

("FRED_ID", "GDP_XX", "XX Real GDP", "log", False),
# (fred_id, internal_id, display_name, transformation, is_financial)
  • transformation: 'log' = stored as 100·log(level), displayed as 4·Δ (q/q annualized growth) — real GDP, prices, indices, FX, equity. 'lin' = pass-through — rates, ratios, unemployment.
  • is_financial: groups under the "financial" header and is load-bearing for COVID masking — financial columns stay observed through COVID (only macro/isFinancial==false columns are NaN'd; see §B3).
  • After editing, run the builder (A1) to repull/rebuild the raw matrix.

docs.title: A4. Reflect it in the spec block tags
docs.text:
The spec.json block tag is what the engine routes on (verified in app/src/lib/compute/gvarSolve.ts deriveBlocIdx/configureGVARTopology and exportbloc in the loop):

block Meaning Where it comes from domestic the bloc's own variables from the _glob build (GVAR) / the bare DataRaw (euro FSAP) global the 4 shared commons GLB_OIL/GLB_USLR/GLB_USBAA/GLB_USEQ (identical across blocs, US-determined) the _glob build (GVAR) / the US-block commons WTI_US/FCM10_US/FBAA_US/EQ_US (euro FSAP) row the 3 trade-weighted stars ROW{DEM,INF,FIN}_<CC> (GVAR blocs only) appended at export time by exportbloc — do not hand-add fiscal the 4 fiscal drivers FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/EFFI/SFA (6 fiscal blocs) the fiscal builder us / external the exogenous US block of a euro FSAP standalone (jointly estimated, hidden from charts, non-conditionable) export_bvar_euro_fsap.m

There is no plain non-GVAR OECD/EM standalone in the deployed set — every non-euro bloc is a GVAR bloc. The 17 euro members are FSAP two-country standalones whose foreign block is us/external (plus the 4 global commons), not all-domestic. block:'row' is what marks a bloc GVAR-active (plus a weights row), so a new GVAR bloc needs a row in app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_24bloc.json (the filename is historical — it now holds 33 blocs, US first; verified: blocs: 33, has US weights row: True).

The variable list for spec.json is reflected in pipeline/country_specs.ts; the dropdown registry is app/src/lib/data/countries.ts. (Full mechanics: ADDING_A_COUNTRY.md §B2–B3.)

docs.title: B. ESTIMATION SETTINGS
docs.text: The estimator is pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m (GVAR-EM loop). Knobs are env vars (no code edit). All values below are verified against the script.

docs.title: B1. Env knobs
docs.text:
The script hardcodes its own repo root and the absolute country_rawdata/export paths, so it can be launched from anywhere — only the run(...) argument is a path. Use the absolute script path:

# Production full run (33 blocs, US endogenous). HOURS.
US_ENDOGENOUS=1 matlab -batch \
  "run('/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"

# Fast smoke (validate BEFORE the multi-hour run): 3 blocs, few draws, 1 sweep (separated).
BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US EA JP" NDRAWS=200 MAXIT=1 US_ENDOGENOUS=1 matlab -batch \
  "run('/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"
Env Default (in-script) Meaning US_ENDOGENOUS unset (anchor) 1 ⇒ US is a coupled bloc with its own ROW*_US stars (needs a US weights row). Set for production. BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS all blocs in weights file space-separated subset, US first NDRAWS nDraws=1000 (nBurn=500) total MCMC draws; burn auto-set to NDRAWS/2. Exported n_draws = post-burn = NDRAWS − nBurn (500 by default). The inner GLP sampler's 'Ndraws',1000 keyword is a separate, fixed knob in the cgl_bvarV5 call — NDRAWS does not change it. MAXIT 12 convergence sweeps; MAXIT=1 ⇒ single-country / separated (no coupling) TOL 0.03 convergence tol on max|Δstar| (MC noise floor ≈ 0.13) GVAR_WEIGHTS_FILE weights_full.json else weights_24bloc.json which weights / bloc list

Fixed in-script (don't "fix" without re-validating): lags=3, H=20, MCMCconst=1.

docs.title: B2. Priors (lambda / mu) and the BVAR call
docs.text:
Each bloc is estimated by cgl_bvarV5([domestic | 3 stars]) — the COVID-as-missing Gibbs sampler. The call (verified, both the US bootstrap and the parfor body):

cgl_bvarV5(x, lags, [], nDraws, nBurn, MCMCconst, H, ...
  'mcmc',1,'MNpsi',0,'sur',0,'noc',1,'Ndraws',1000,'Ndrawsdiscard',500);
  • lambda/mu are empirical-Bayes (MH on the completed-data marginal likelihood) — not pinned. With COVID imputed the optimum is well-behaved (estimated ~0.24–0.56).
  • sur=0, noc=1, MNpsi=0 — mirror these exactly when porting any other estimator.
  • Do not pin lamfix. If you must, keep noc=1 or sur=1 free — pinning lambda with both noc and sur fixed degenerates lambda to NaN.

Model-selection guidance for lambda (the empirical-Bayes prior tightness) by bloc size:

Bloc size n Typical lambda Note small (n≈14–20: most RoW — e.g. CR 14, RU 15, TR/BR 18, CN/MX/IN 20) looser, ~0.3–0.5 fewer params, less shrinkage needed medium (n≈23–25: JP 23, EA 24, UK/CA 25) ~0.25–0.4 large (US n=41) tighter, ~0.24–0.3 many params (124×41 betahat) ⇒ more shrinkage

These are observed empirical-Bayes optima, not knobs you set; they are a sanity range — a post-run lambda far outside it flags a data problem.

docs.title: B3. The COVID window + macro-only masking
docs.text:
Verified in the loop: the COVID window is fixed 2020Q2–2020Q4 (3 quarters) — find(ismember(dts, ["2020-Q2","2020-Q3","2020-Q4"])). Over that window only macro columns (isFinancial==false) are NaN'd: domestic macro + ROWDEM + ROWINF. The financial domestic columns and the foreign-rate star ROWFIN stay observed, so the DK smoother imputes the macro holes conditioned on the observed financials. This is why is_financial (A3) is load-bearing.

An older path auto-detected the GDP-trough window; it could misfire on other crises (KR's 2008Q4 GFC drop). The current script uses the fixed 3-quarter window. Leave it.

docs.title: B4. Exogeneity / the external block
docs.text:
A bloc's foreign variables are weakly exogenous regressors: the 3 stars (block:'row') and the 4 globals (block:'global'). The stars are trade-weighted over partners present each quarter (unbalanced panel, renormalized), built by buildstars in the loop. Special cases baked in (mirror the engine — see ARCHITECTURE_FOR_AI.md §5):

  • CN GDP is real (since the 2026-06 surgical CN-only re-estimate; NOMINAL_GDP_BLOCS is now empty) → CN's DEM reads gdp directly, no longer the deflated X_g − X_p. (Historically GDP_CN was nominal and deflated by subtraction.)
  • Rate-less blocs (CH/NO) have no own short rate → excluded from partners' ROWFIN.
  • AR hyperinflation → excluded from partners' ROWINF/ROWFIN (kept in ROWDEM).
  • Leading-NaN star columns are back-filled with the first valid value (a NaN-prefix gives a singular SIGMA).

docs.title: B5. Change a variable's PRIOR (RW ↔ WN stationary) and re-estimate
docs.text: This is the per-variable Minnesota/GLP prior-mean knob: it sets the own-first-lag prior mean to 1 (random walk, the historical default) or 0 (white noise / stationary). It is not an env var — it travels on the raw _glob.json as a per-variable prior field, and the estimators read it.

docs.title: B5.1 What WN does, mechanically (verified)
docs.text:
The GLP prior in BVAR/MFBVAR/GLPreplicationWeb/bvarGLPmf.m builds the prior coefficient block as a diagonal of ones, then zeros the rows named in pos:

% bvarGLPmf.m (verified, lines ~193–195)
diagb=ones(n,1);
diagb(pos)=0;          % own-first-lag prior mean -> 0 for the WN (stationary) columns
b(2:n+1,:)=diag(diagb);

pos is the 3rd positional argument of cgl_bvarV5.m (pos=stationary;, verified line 19) — the COVID-as-missing Gibbs sampler threads it straight into bvarGLPmf and into every logMLMFVAR/BVAR call. So:
