prior tag pos membership own-lag prior mean Behaviour at the long end RW (default) not in pos 1 random walk — the variable drifts, no mean reversion WN (stationary) in pos 0 white noise — the variable mean-reverts to its in-sample mean

The estimators build pos from the spec's per-variable prior:

  • export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m: statCols = find(Spec.Prior(keep)=="WN")' (verified ~line 60) — indices into the kept domestic block; stars are appended after and are always RW.
  • export_bvar_euro_fsap.m: statCols = find(Prior=="WN")' (verified ~line 110).
  • Both pass statCols as the 3rd positional arg of cgl_bvarV5 and write the real prior back into spec.json (v.prior='WN'|'RW'), so the deployed spec is self-describing.

No-op for non-WN blocs (byte-identical). When no variable is tagged WN, statCols is empty and pos=zeros(1,0), which indexes identically to the historical pos=[] in diagb(pos)=0. The 44 non-fiscal blocs are therefore bit-for-bit unchanged by the prior machinery (verified: commit 6f0161a notes "byte-identical for non-fiscal blocs"; confirmed in code by the empty-index path).

docs.title: B5.2 Current production state (verified in the deployed specs)
docs.text:
The fiscal GDP-ratios mean-revert; the effective rate drifts. All 6 fiscal blocs (US EA JP UK CA CN) ship with:

Variable prior Why FREV_GDP (revenue/GDP) WN a ratio — must mean-revert to its in-sample level, not drift FPEXP_GDP (primary exp./GDP) WN a ratio — same SFA (stock-flow adjustment) WN a residual flow — mean-reverts to sfaDefault EFFI (effective interest rate) RW a rate level — stays RW (drifts with the rate environment)

Verified across all six (app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/spec.json): every fiscal bloc shows FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/SFA → WN, EFFI → RW. Effect: US revenue now mean-reverts toward its in-sample mean (≈17.4% over the full sample; the deployed 40-quarter baseline ends ≈17.9% vs the last observed ≈18.8% and the old RW path stuck near ≈19%), instead of drifting at the long horizon. Committed 6f0161a / 22250be / e38673a.

docs.title: B5.3 How to change a prior — copy-paste
docs.text:
Step 1 — tag the raw _glob.json. For the 6 fiscal blocs, use the idempotent helper (pipeline/tag_fiscal_wn.py); it writes prior on every variable object (WN on FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/SFA, RW on the rest incl. EFFI):

cd "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool"
python3 pipeline/tag_fiscal_wn.py     # idempotent; format-preserving; re-runnable after any rebuild

For any other variable, edit the variable object in pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json to add "prior":"WN" (or "RW"). Critical: tag EVERY variable object in that file, not just the one you change — MATLAB's jsondecode returns J.variables as a struct array only when all objects share the same field set; a prior field on some objects makes it a cell array and breaks the estimators' arrayfun(@(v)v.id, …) calls. (The estimators' priorsfrom helper tolerates the cell-array shape, but the safe, audited convention is homogeneous tagging — exactly what tag_fiscal_wn.py does.)

Step 2 — re-estimate the bloc.

  • Coupled bloc (US/EA/RoW): the spec change is local, but the audited deploy path is the full loop (§B1) so the stars stay at the cross-country fixed point: 
    US_ENDOGENOUS=1 matlab -batch \
  "run('/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"
    For a quick local check of just the changed bloc (frozen partner stars — not for deploy): 
    BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US <CC>" MAXIT=1 US_ENDOGENOUS=1 NDRAWS=400 matlab -batch \
  "run('/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"
  • Euro FSAP standalone: re-estimate only that bloc (no coupling): 
    GVAR_EXPORT_DIR=/tmp/euro_fsap_export matlab -batch \
  "run('/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/pipeline/export_bvar_euro_fsap.m')"

Step 3 — confirm the prior landed in the export, then port + (coupling) + gate (C–E):

python3 -c "import json,sys; cc=sys.argv[1]; \
 s=json.load(open(f'/tmp/gvar24_export/{cc}/spec.json')); \
 print(cc, [(v['id'],v['prior']) for v in s['variables'] if v.get('block')=='fiscal'])" US
# expect: US [('FREV_GDP','WN'),('FPEXP_GDP','WN'),('EFFI','RW'),('SFA','WN')]

Step 4 — sanity-check the mean reversion. A WN ratio should bend back toward its in-sample mean over the horizon; an RW one drifts. After porting, the baseline's end-of-horizon value of a WN ratio should sit inside its history's range, not extrapolate past it. (For the fiscal closure invariant after a fiscal change, run C4 — the prior change does not touch the SFA-closure assertion, which is purely an accounting identity on the observed debt path.)

Pitfall: WN on a true level. Only tag WN on series that genuinely mean-revert (ratios, spreads, gaps). Tagging a level (real GDP in 100·log, a price index, the effective rate) WN would pull its own-lag toward 0 and kill the unit root, forcing the level itself to revert — wrong. That is why EFFI stays RW. When in doubt, leave RW.

docs.title: B6. isFinancial / GLB_* and the COVID mask — coherence rules
docs.text: isFinancial is load-bearing, not cosmetic: it is the COVID-mask membership test (§B3). Over 2020Q2–Q4 the loop NaN's only isFinancial==false columns (macroCols=find(fin==0), verified) so the DK smoother imputes the macro holes conditioned on the observed financials. A mis-flagged column is silently mistreated through COVID, which contaminates every partner's stars.

docs.title: B6.1 The four globals are ALL financial (coherence fix)
docs.text:
The 4 shared commons are forced financial at build time so a future rebuild cannot drift:

# pipeline/build_globals_new_blocs.py (verified)
GLB_IDS       = ["GLB_OIL", "GLB_USLR", "GLB_USBAA", "GLB_USEQ"]
GLB_FINANCIAL = {"GLB_OIL", "GLB_USLR", "GLB_USBAA", "GLB_USEQ"}   # ALL four -> isFinancial=true

GLB_USLR (US 10Y) and GLB_USBAA (BAA) were always financial; the coherence fix added GLB_USEQ (US equity) and GLB_OIL (oil) — both are market prices, so both must stay observed through COVID, not imputed. Verified in the deployed US/spec.json: GLB_USEQ (log) and GLB_OIL (log) both carry isFinancial:true in the global block.

Why this matters for the mask. A market price (equity, oil) collapsed and rebounded sharply in 2020 — that is real, observed information. Masking it as "macro" and letting the DK smoother interpolate would erase the signal and bias the imputed macro holes that are conditioned on it. Keeping the financials observed is the whole point of the COVID-as-missing design (§B3).

docs.title: B6.2 Canada IP_CA rebuilt as a log-LEVEL (coherence fix)
docs.text:
IP_CA was stored as a y/y growth rate (transformation:"lin") — incoherent with the US/UK IP blocs (stored as log levels). pipeline/build_ca_ip_level.py rebuilds it as a quarterly level index (chained from the discontinued FRED level CANPROINDMISMEI to 2023Q4, then forward with the q/q growth PRINTO01CAQ657S to 2025Q4), setting transformation:"log", isFinancial:false, prior:"RW":

cd "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool"
python3 pipeline/build_ca_ip_level.py     # splices a log-LEVEL IP_CA into DataRaw_CA_glob.json
# then re-estimate the coupled set (CA is a coupled bloc) -> port -> coupling -> gate

Coherence audit. A per-economy variable table (transformation · prior · isFinancial per variable) is now in the user manual; all 1048 variables across the 50 deployed blocs were audited coherent (transform matches the series type; financials flagged; ratios WN). To re-derive the total yourself:

python3 -c "import json,glob; t=0; b=0; [ (t:=t+json.load(open(f))['n'], b:=b+1) for f in glob.glob('app/public/data/bvar/*/spec.json') ]; print('blocs',b,'vars',t)"
# expect: blocs 50 vars 1048

docs.title: B6.3 Changing an isFinancial flag — checklist
docs.text:
1. Fix it in the builder (so a rebuild stays coherent) and in the raw _glob.json (so the next estimate sees it). For a GLB_* common, edit pipeline/build_globals_new_blocs.py's GLB_FINANCIAL. For a domestic var, edit its tuple in the country builder (A3) and the _glob.json column meta.
  2. Re-estimate the affected bloc(s). A GLB_* flag change affects every coupled bloc (the commons are shared) ⇒ full loop. A domestic flag change affects that bloc (full loop if coupled, for the audited star fixed point).
  3. Port → coupling (if coupled) → gate.
  4. Verify the flag landed in the export spec: 
    python3 -c "import json; s=json.load(open('/tmp/gvar24_export/US/spec.json')); \
 print([(v['id'],v['isFinancial']) for v in s['variables'] if v['block']=='global'])"
# expect all four GLB_* -> True

docs.title: B7. Targeted single-bloc re-estimation (when a change touches one bloc)
docs.text: Use this when you want to re-estimate one bloc — a prior flip, an isFinancial fix, a data patch — without paying the multi-hour full-loop cost. Read §B7.1 first: a subset run is a restricted GVAR, not the full GVAR with the other blocs frozen. Getting this wrong silently changes everyone's stars.

docs.title: B7.1 What BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US <CC>" actually does (verified)
docs.text:
The loop loads only the listed blocs into D (for bi=1:nB over blocs, verified ~lines 46–81). buildstars then trade-weights only the partners present in D (parts = parts(ismember(parts, blocs)), verified ~line 178) and renormalizes over them. So:

  • A subset run does not freeze the absent partners at their full-run values — it drops them entirely and recomputes the listed blocs' stars from only the listed partners (US + <CC>).
  • The listed blocs' seed comp panels are built fresh from their own raw _glob.json (line 78), not loaded from /tmp/gvar24_workspace.mat. There is no "load the last full run and re-estimate one bloc" path in the script.

Consequence: BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US <CC>" gives the correct US/<CC> betahat only if <CC>'s stars genuinely depend on no third country — i.e. essentially never for a real RoW bloc (its ROWDEM etc. trade-weight many partners). It is a fast local check, not a deploy-grade estimate of a coupled bloc. The deploy-grade path for any coupled bloc is the full loop (§B1).

docs.title: B7.2 The three legitimate single-bloc jobs
docs.text:
Job Command Coupling regen? Deploy-grade? Euro FSAP standalone (AT BE DE EE ES FI FR GR IE IT LT LU LV NL PT SI SK) — no weights row, no partner reads it export_bvar_euro_fsap.m (writes /tmp/euro_fsap_export/) no yes — exact, it is genuinely standalone Quick local check of a coupled bloc (frozen/restricted stars) BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US <CC>" MAXIT=1 US_ENDOGENOUS=1 NDRAWS=400 full-loop script n/a (don't deploy) no — restricted-GVAR stars Deploy a coupled bloc full loop (§B1), all 33 yes yes

Euro standalone (deploy-grade), copy-paste:

cd "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool"
python3 pipeline/build_oecd_eurostat.py            # or the relevant builder for that member (A1)
GVAR_EXPORT_DIR=/tmp/euro_fsap_export matlab -batch \
  "run('/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/pipeline/export_bvar_euro_fsap.m')"
GVAR_PORT_BLOCS="<CC>" GVAR_EXPORT_DIR=/tmp/euro_fsap_export python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py
# NO coupling regen (not in the coupled GVAR); then the gate (E):
cd app && npx tsc --noEmit && npx vitest run

A euro standalone change does not need precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs (it has no weights row, so it is absent from M). The coupling modelSig only keys on the coupled blocs — re-running it would be a no-op for a euro-only change.

Quick local check of a coupled bloc (NOT for deploy):

BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US <CC>" MAXIT=1 US_ENDOGENOUS=1 NDRAWS=400 matlab -batch \
  "run('/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"
# inspect /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/ (C1–C3). When it looks right, do the FULL loop for the real deploy.

Rule. Deploy a coupled bloc only from a full-loop export. The subset run is for inspecting the effect of your edit on the changed bloc cheaply — its stars (and therefore its betahat) are not the production fixed point.

docs.title: C. CHECK the MATLAB 8-file output
docs.text: The loop writes the full 8-file contract per bloc to /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/ and saves the estimation state to /tmp/gvar24_workspace.mat before export (an export bug is then a cheap re-export, not a re-estimation). Inspect /tmp/gvar24_export/ before porting — never overwrite live data blind.

docs.title: C1. The 8-file contract (verified exportbloc)
docs.text: File Shape (verified on US) Check spec.json { n, variables:[{id,name,transformation,isFinancial,prior,block}] }; US n=41, last 3 ids ROW{DEM,INF,FIN}_US, blocks {domestic,fiscal,global,row} n matches your expectation companion_mean.json { betahat[n·lags+1][n], sigmahat[n][n], n, lags }; US betahat 124×41, sigmahat 41×41, lags 3 spec n == companion dim companion_draws.json { n_draws, n, lags, beta_shape, beta, sigma_shape, sigma } flat; US beta_shape=[124,41,500] n_draws == NDRAWS − nBurn (= post-burn draws; default 1000−500 = 500) baseline.json { mean[H][n], q16/q84/q05/q95 } (growth/display) mean[0] sane (GDP1q) baseline_levels.json { mean[H][n], q16/q84/q05/q95 } (levels) monotone-ish levels historical.json { data[~40][n] } (growth) last ~40q historical_levels.json { data[~40][n] } (levels) — observed domestic + completed stars fits the data transformations.json { transformations:['log'|'lin',…] } length == n

docs.title: C2. Convergence + MC floor
docs.text:
Watch the console:

  • Each iteration prints === iter k: max star change X.XXXX (tol 0.030) [parfor N blocs] ===. The metric must fall below TOL and print CONVERGED. (convergence is only tested from it>1). If it plateaus above TOL near ~0.13, that is the Monte-Carlo noise floor — raise NDRAWS to push the floor down, do not chase it with more iterations.
  • Each export prints exported <CC>: n=… (dom+3row|anchor) nd=… GDP1q=…. Confirm GDP1q is a sane number, not NaN or a collapsed flat line (nd = post-burn draws, C1).

docs.title: C3. n == spec == companion dim
docs.text:
The single most common breakage. Verify per bloc:

python3 -c "import json,sys; cc=sys.argv[1]; \
 s=json.load(open(f'/tmp/gvar24_export/{cc}/spec.json')); \
 c=json.load(open(f'/tmp/gvar24_export/{cc}/companion_mean.json')); \
 print(cc,'spec.n',s['n'],'len(vars)',len(s['variables']),'comp.n',c['n'], \
 'betahat',len(c['betahat']),'x',len(c['betahat'][0]),'lags',c['lags']); \
 assert s['n']==len(s['variables'])==c['n']==len(c['betahat'][0]), 'N MISMATCH'; \
 assert len(c['betahat'])==c['n']*c['lags']+1, 'betahat rows != n*lags+1'" US

docs.title: C4. Fiscal closure (fiscal blocs only)
docs.text:
Run before re-estimating if the fiscal data changed (the SFA column lives in the same DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json the loop reads):

python3 pipeline/build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py    # cc ∈ us ea jp uk ca cn — edits glob + fiscal_meta in place

SFA is the exact one-step residual that closes the app debt recursion on the observed gross-debt path (the recursion is app/src/lib/compute/fiscalAccounting.ts computeFiscalBlock):

d_t = d_{t-1}·(1 + EFFI_t/400)/(1 + gNomQ_t/100) − pb_t/4 + sfa_t/4
pb     = FREV_GDP − FPEXP_GDP
gNomQ  = 100·Δlog(realGDP) + 100·Δlog(deflator)          # off the SAME glob columns the app reads
SFA_t  = 4·(d_obs_t − d_{t-1} − snowball_t + pb_t/4)

The build asserts closure |d_obs_t − recon_t| < 1e-6 for every non-COVID quarter and aborts on failure. It prints backcastMaxErrPP (must be ~1e-13), the SFA range, and the chosen sfaDefault. COVID quarters are set NaN (an interpolated value can never equal the residual). Sanity benchmarks after a rebuild (sfaDefault, % GDP) — US 1.75, EA 0.33, JP 2.23, UK 3.15, CA 3.19, CN 4.02. (US fiscal_meta.json verified: idx={gdp:0,deflator:7,revenue:33,primaryExp:34,effRate:35, sfa:36}, d0=122.57, sfaDefault=1.75.) Full math: fiscal-block-methodology.md and REESTIMATING_MODELS.md §2.

docs.title: D1. Port (6-sig-fig compaction — the deployed convention)
docs.text:
cd "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool"
python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py                 # ALL blocs → app/public/data/bvar/<CC> (6 sig figs)
# subset / dry-run (verified flags):
GVAR_PORT_BLOCS="US EA JP" python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py
python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py --dry-run

This compacts /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/*.json → app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/*.json at 6 significant figures (sig6 in port_gvar_exports.py).

docs.title: D2. Copy the fiscal meta (fiscal blocs)
docs.text:
The app reads fiscal_meta per country — porting the bloc JSON alone is not enough:

for cc in US EA JP UK CA CN; do
  cp "pipeline/fiscal_meta/$cc.json" "app/public/data/bvar/$cc/fiscal_meta.json"
done

docs.title: D2b. RE-INDEX the fiscal meta (MANDATORY RULE after any variable-order change)
docs.text:
RULE: after any re-estimation/port that can change a fiscal bloc's variable ORDER or COUNT, run node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs.

fiscal_meta.json stores the fiscal-driver columns by POSITION (idx = {gdp, deflator, revenue, primaryExp, effRate, sfa}), and the debt recursion (fiscalAccounting.ts computeFiscalBlock) reads the level path at exactly those positional indices. A change to a bloc's variable order/count silently re-points them at the WRONG columns. This is the cause of the recent EA/UK debt explosion: the 33-bloc GVAR-EM refit inserted variables ahead of the fiscal block in every spec except US, re-pointing (e.g.) EA's effRate onto the equity index (~col 537), so the debt-to-GDP path blew up. The porter does not regenerate the meta — the stale indices ship unless you fix them.

pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs re-resolves the indices by variable id from the deployed spec.json (order-independent) and rewrites fiscal_meta.json for all six fiscal blocs, preserving the model parameters d0/sfaDefault (it only touches meta.idx). It is idempotent.

cd "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool"
node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs
# per bloc:  US: ok {…}  |  EA: FIXED {old} -> {new}  …  |  N fiscal_meta.json files corrected.

Run it after every port that touches a fiscal bloc. The fiscal canaries (E2) are the backstop, but a green tsc/build with stale indices still ships a wrong debt path — re-indexing is the actual fix.

docs.title: D3. Regenerate the coupling artifact (MANDATORY after any coupled bloc change)
docs.text:
The browser solves the cross-country fixed point x = (I−M)⁻¹d from a pre-shipped artifact. At this scale the in-browser inverse is many minutes — pre-shipping is mandatory, not an optimisation. The artifact is keyed to the data by modelSig; a mismatch makes the app fall back to the slow path.

cd "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/app"
npx vite-node scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs

Verified behaviour (scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs): builds M in JS (buildGVARCouplingMEndo when US_ENDOGENOUS), inverts (I−M) via MATLAB BLAS (scripts/invert_coupling.m, shelled matlab -batch — a ~2 s LAPACK solve that replaces the in-JS O(dim³) inverse, which the code clocks at ~46 min at dim≈1380; it grows with dim), and writes the gzipped app/public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz ({modelSig, pinStructure:'', dim, rhoSpectral, M, invImM}, 6-sig-fig). The plain .json is removed (at ~50 blocs it would exceed GitHub's 100 MB per-file limit; the shipped .gz is ≈ 31 MB). The loader decompresses in-browser via the native DecompressionStream. No MATLAB on PATH? The script shells matlab directly and will error — use the NumPy fallback scripts/invert_coupling.py (run the M-build and inverse by hand, or swap the execFileSync call); see the troubleshooting row.

Verify the shipped artifact header (the file is gzipped — decompress to read it):

cd "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/app"
node -e "const z=require('zlib'),fs=require('fs');const o=JSON.parse(z.gunzipSync(fs.readFileSync('public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz')));console.log('dim',o.dim,'rho',o.rhoSpectral,'pin',JSON.stringify(o.pinStructure),'sig',o.modelSig.slice(0,20))"
# expect: dim 2060 rho 0.924041 pin "" sig US:41_3_20_6.121870e

The maintained, automated equivalent (modelSig-fresh + inverse-is-inverse + ρ) is gvar-coupling-artifact.test.ts, run by npx vitest run (E).

Spectral radius by topology (a stale/wrong value is a red flag):

Topology dim ρ(M) 6-bloc pilot — 0.59 24-bloc frozen anchor ~1380 0.726 33-bloc endogenous US (current) 2060 0.924041

The deployed artifact's stored rhoSpectral is 0.924041 (verified in coupling_default.json.gz — read it off the header per D3). Earlier docs quoted 0.8958 / 0.896156 from an older refit; the current shipped artifact (a later consistent re-estimation, with a different modelSig) is 0.924041, still comfortably < 1. The frozen-anchor coupling dim is (#RoW)·3·H; with the US endogenous the augmented state adds the US's own stars + US-global channels, giving the larger dim = 2060 (the value verify_gvar24.mjs checks against the artifact). Always read the live value off the header (D3) — do not trust a number copied into prose.

docs.title: E. EDIT the CHECKS — the green gate
docs.text:
The authoritative gate is vitest (the canaries below read the shipped .gz artifact and the deployed models directly). Run from app/:

cd "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/app"
npx tsc --noEmit                       # types
npx vitest run                         # the gate — ALL must pass; the canaries (E1) block deploy
BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario npm run build # static export with the deploy base path (writes app/out/)

verify_gvar24.mjs is a legacy fast helper, not the gate. Two gotchas before you trust it: (1) it readJSONs the plain coupling_default.json, but the precompute ships only the .gz — so you must gunzip it first; (2) two of its checks (dim = (#RoW)·3·H and the oil-common assertion) are written for the frozen-anchor topology and report FAIL on the current US-endogenous model (dim=2060) even when the artifact is correct. Use it only for the modelSig/inverse/ρ checks, and only after:

cd "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/app"
gunzip -kc public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz > public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json
NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 npx tsx scripts/verify_gvar24.mjs   # checks 1/2/3 authoritative
rm public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json                            # never commit the ~96 MB plain file

The maintained equivalent of every one of its checks lives in gvar-coupling-artifact.test.ts (E1), which gunzips the .gz itself — prefer that.

docs.title: E1. Canary tests (block deploy — treat any change as a red flag)
docs.text: Canary What it pins scenario-scale-convention.test.ts the 100·log / 4·diff display convention gvar-convergence.test.ts Gauss–Seidel ≡ closed-form (I−M)⁻¹d; reports ρ(M) gvar-coupling-artifact.test.ts the shipped gzipped artifact hydrates and matches a fresh build; modelSig gvar-closed-form-routing.test.ts closed form ≡ iterative; cache-hit ≡ cold rebuild; pin-structure → new M gvar-derived-topology.test.ts configureGVARTopology/deriveBlocIdx + US_ENDOGENOUS from spec+weights

docs.title: E2. Feature tests
docs.text: fiscalAccounting.test.ts, fiscal-backcast.test.ts, fiscal-conditioning-integration.test.ts, gvar-us-endogenous.test.ts, gvar-globals.test.ts, gvar-direct-coupling-strip-equivalence.test.ts, gvar-band-solve.test.ts, gvar-ui-linkage.test.ts, longRunTilt.test.ts, conditioning-wysiwyg.test.ts.

docs.title: E3. When n changed
docs.text:
The scale canary scenario-scale-convention.test.ts may carry a fixture pinned to the old layout. Regenerate the ground truth with pipeline/_verify/gt.m (it resolves columns by variable id, so it is layout-robust) and re-run:

matlab -batch "run('/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool/pipeline/_verify/gt.m')"

Everything else in the engine is index-agnostic — it reads n from companion_mean.json, fiscal indices from fiscal_meta.json, and GVAR indices from IDX at runtime. Never hardcode an index or a bloc subset.

Done = tsc clean, all vitest green (canaries first), npm run build succeeds (the artifact and modelSig are then validated by gvar-coupling-artifact.test.ts). Publishing is §F — do it only after the gate is green and only with explicit confirmation.

docs.title: F. PUBLISH (deploy to the live site)
docs.text: This is a live deploy. Do NOT push without explicit per-action confirmation from the maintainer. (Repo memory rule: never trigger an irreversible/live action — Pages/DNS/secrets/force-push/deploy — without confirmation.) Publish only when the gate (E) is fully green.

docs.title: F.1 The deploy chain (verified .github/workflows/deploy-to-website.yml)
docs.text:
push app/** to bvarscenario:main
   └─ GitHub Action "Deploy BVAR Scenario Tool to Website" (on: push to main, paths app/** | netlify.toml)
        1. npm ci && npm run build      (BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario)  -> Next.js STATIC export -> app/out/
        2. checkout matyasfarkas/starter-hugo-academic  (token: secret WEBSITE_DEPLOY_TOKEN)
        3. rm -rf website/static/bvar-scenario/*  &&  cp -r app/out/* website/static/bvar-scenario/
        4. commit + push the website repo  (only if there is a diff)
   └─ the website repo's OWN Hugo pipeline rebuilds (separate downstream stage; the Hugo 0.122.0 pin
      lives there, not here) and GitHub Pages serves it under /bvar-scenario

Key facts (all verified in the workflow):

  • Trigger: push to main whose changeset touches app/** or netlify.toml. A data/coupling change under app/public/data/** is under app/**, so it triggers. A change confined to pipeline/** or docs/** does not trigger a deploy — only the app/ tree ships.
  • The Action runs npm ci (clean install from app/package-lock.json) then npm run build with BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario; the build is a Next.js static export to app/out/.
  • It then checks out matyasfarkas/starter-hugo-academic, wipes static/bvar-scenario/, copies app/out/* in, and commits+pushes only if there is a diff (git diff --cached --quiet).
  • The Hugo build is downstream in the website repo — this repo's workflow does not run Hugo.

docs.title: F.2 The push command
docs.text:
The deploy remote is bvarscenario (matyasfarkas/BVARScenarioTool); the Action lives on its main. You are typically on a feature branch (e.g. feature/oecd-expansion). Push your current HEAD to the remote's main:

cd "/home/farkas/github/BVAR Policy Calibration Tool"

# 0. CONFIRM with the maintainer first. Verify what you are about to ship:
git log --oneline -5                                  # the commits going out
git status                                            # working tree clean? (commit data+coupling+app changes)
git remote -v | grep bvarscenario                     # -> matyasfarkas/BVARScenarioTool.git

# 1. (optional, recommended) dry-run to see exactly what the push would update:
git push --dry-run bvarscenario HEAD:main

# 2. the deploy push (current branch HEAD -> bvarscenario main; triggers the Action):
git push bvarscenario HEAD:main
  • HEAD:main pushes your current branch's HEAD to the remote main ref (no local branch switch needed). If main has diverged, do not --force — reconcile first (the memory rule forbids unconfirmed force-push).
  • After pushing, watch the Action: 
    gh run list --repo matyasfarkas/BVARScenarioTool --workflow "Deploy BVAR Scenario Tool to Website" --limit 3
gh run watch --repo matyasfarkas/BVARScenarioTool   # follow the latest run to completion
  • The live page (…/bvar-scenario) updates after both the Action and the downstream Hugo/Pages build finish (the Pages rebuild on the website repo can lag by a few minutes).

docs.title: F.3 What to commit before pushing (a coupled re-estimation)
docs.text:
A coupled re-estimation changes many files under app/public/data/bvar/. Stage all of them or the deploy ships a half-updated model:

Must be committed Path every re-estimated bloc's 8-file contract (6-sig-fig) app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/*.json (all 33 coupled, + euro standalones if touched) fiscal meta (if a fiscal bloc changed) app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/fiscal_meta.json the coupling artifact (gzipped) app/public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz weights (if topology changed) app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_24bloc.json (33 blocs, US first) any compute/spec/test change app/src/**

Never commit the un-gzipped coupling_default.json (~96 MB, over GitHub's 100 MB per-file limit) — D3 already removes it; confirm it is absent (git status should show only the .gz).

Confirmation gate (repeat). Pushing to bvarscenario:main is the live deploy. Confirm with the maintainer before running git push bvarscenario HEAD:main. Full CI chain + the website-repo side in BUILD_AND_CI.md.

docs.title: G. The long-run-target overlay (δc drift sliders) — what the operator needs to know
docs.text: The control panel's "Long-run targets" section is a runtime, in-browser overlay. As a maintainer the one thing to internalise is: it is NOT part of the estimation / port / coupling / gate pipeline. There is nothing to rebuild, re-estimate, re-index, or commit when this feature changes the display — it computes everything from the already-shipped companion_mean.json of the bloc the user is viewing. A data refresh (§A–§F) ships it for free; a data refresh does not need any extra step for it.

docs.title: G1. What it is (operator summary)
docs.text:
Each slider sets one variable's long-run (≈H-quarter) target in the chart's display units (4·Δlevel = q/q-annualised growth for log vars; the level for lin vars). Setting a target shifts that variable's drift — the companion constant of its own equation — by the single amount δc_j needed for its terminal forecast to hit the target, and the estimated model dynamics carry every other variable along. It is:

  • Deterministic and exact — δc_j = (target − currentTerminalForecast) / sens_j; the terminal forecast lands on the target by construction (the panel badge reads applied · exact). An unmoved slider ⇒ δc=0 ⇒ a byte-exact no-op (it defaults to the model's own terminal forecast).
  • Instant — no Compute, no re-estimation, no GVAR re-solve, no draw reweighting. The deviation is ΔY = S_h[:, j]·δc_j, where S_h (the partial companion-power sum Σ_{k<h} T^k) is built once per bloc in the browser from the shipped companion and cached; each slider change is then a cheap mat-vec add (computed in app/src/lib/compute/driftSetter.ts).
  • Band-coherent — ΔY is added identically to the central path and to every quantile (q05/q16/q50/q84/q95), so the fan translates rigidly: band widths are invariant and quantiles cannot cross. Safe to apply on top of the precomputed bands.

This replaced the old entropic-tilt long-run-anchor mechanism (longRunTilt.ts), which reweighted posterior draws, needed a Compute, and degenerated (ESS→1) on a joint multi-target tilt. The drift overlay has none of those failure modes (see the superseded known-issue note below).

docs.title: G2. Interpreting / disabling a variable (the |sens|<0.3 guard)
docs.text: The slider's sensitivity sens_j is how far the variable's terminal display value moves per unit of drift (4·(S_H[j,j] − S_{H-1}[j,j]) for log; S_H[j,j] for lin). When |sens_j| < 0.3 (SENS_GUARD in driftSetter.ts) the response is near-singular — a tiny δc would imply an enormous, ill-posed move — so the variable is disabled: it shows in the dropdown as "(near-singular — unavailable)" and cannot be targeted. This is expected, not a fault. It typically hits a near-unit-root level (where the finite-horizon drift barely moves the terminal display). Operator guidance: target the growth/ratio form rather than the level, or pick another variable. Do not try to "fix" it by lowering the guard — the guard is what keeps the overlay well-posed.

docs.title: G3. Caveats to relay to users
docs.text:
• It shifts the long-run drift, not persistence — label/treat it as a long-run target, not a path controller.
  • The cross-variable response (other variables moving) is a real estimated-model side effect, not a bug.
  • The real-growth target owns the debt direction (the (i−g) snowball) — it is not a structural debt fix; it changes the growth assumption the debt path inherits.
  • GVAR bloc → marginal. It shifts that bloc's own long-run forecast only; trade partners and the US-determined global commons are not re-solved, so the realised joint long-run value may differ from the target. The panel surfaces this amber caveat automatically for a GVAR bloc.

docs.title: G4. The policy rate (Taylor overlay removed)
docs.text: The policy rate is an ordinary lin variable of the VAR, so it carries its own long-run-target slider like every other series — set its terminal value directly through the drift setter. There is no separate Taylor-rule overlay: an earlier taylorOverlay toggle (policy-rate row, i = r* + π_fc + φ_π(π_fc − π*)) was removed, and no taylorOverlay / setTaylorOverlay symbol remains in ControlPanel.tsx or useStore.ts. (The surviving "Taylor" code in taylor-rule.ts is the unrelated DSGE policy-rule counterfactual, not a forecast overlay.)

docs.title: G5. Operator troubleshooting (overlay-specific)
docs.text: Symptom Cause Fix A variable is greyed out "(near-singular — unavailable)" \|sens_j\| < 0.3 — the finite-horizon drift barely moves its terminal display (near-unit-root level) expected; target its growth/ratio form or another variable (G2) — do not lower the guard Setting one target moved other variables too the cross-variable response S_h[:, j] drags partners along the estimated dynamics expected, not a bug (G3); it is the model's own response to the shifted drift GVAR bloc — realised long-run ≠ my target marginal overlay; partners + global commons not re-solved expected (G3); for a joint re-anchoring you must re-estimate (out of scope for the overlay) Band looks the same width after setting a target by design — ΔY is a rigid translation added to mean and every quantile correct behaviour (G1); widths are provably invariant Setting a target back to the default did not perfectly revert the default IS the model's terminal forecast ⇒ δc=0 ⇒ exact no-op; a non-revert means a stale/edited target value clear the target (the clear all / remove control) — an unmoved/cleared slider is byte-exact No effect at all from any target the bloc's companion failed to load ⇒ no driftCache ⇒ sens=NaN ⇒ every slider disabled confirm the bloc's companion_mean.json is present and well-formed (C1/C3); the overlay needs the shipped companion

docs.title: G6. Model-based vs. with-judgement, and the consensus-anchor preset
docs.text:
Two operator-relevant framings sit on top of the drift setter:

  • Model-based vs. with-judgement (per variable). A variable with its slider at the model estimate is model-based (δc=0, exact no-op). Setting a target makes it with-judgement — the panel highlights the target, shows a per-row reset-to-estimate control, and the footer badge reads N targets · exact. The flag is per-country and per-variable and is cleared on a country switch (targets reference the old bloc's variable ids). This is a UI/state distinction only — no new data artifact and nothing to re-estimate.
  • Consensus-anchor preset (app/public/data/default_anchors.json). An opt-in, off-by-default one-click set of publicly-defensible long-run values per bloc ({ COUNTRY: { variableId: value } }), surfaced as Apply consensus anchors. It is keyed by country code then spec variable id (e.g. GDP_BE, HICP_BE, LR_BE), in display units. The panel auto-drops any id that is not in the bloc's targetable set or whose slider is near-singular (the |sens| < 0.3 guard) — so the perverse policy-rate sliders (BE/CA/CZ) are intentionally omitted from the JSON / never applied. To curate the values, edit default_anchors.json (the canonical source is pre-ship-smoke-test.md §2); it ships under app/public/data/ and is fetched at panel mount. No re-estimation, no green-gate impact — but keep the variable ids in sync with each bloc's spec when a model is re-ported (a stale id is silently skipped, not an error).

docs.title: Acknowledgments
docs.text: The long-run-target drift-setter overlay (and the trend/cycle long-run-target direction it grew out of) owes its framing to Jesper Lindé and Tobias Adrian, whose guidance on anchoring the model's long run shaped this design. Any errors are the maintainer's own.

docs.title: Green-gate priority list (in order)
docs.text:
1. n == spec == companion dim (C3) — everything downstream assumes it.
  2. Convergence below TOL / not stuck at the MC floor (C2).
  3. Fiscal closure |δd| < 1e-6 (C4) — the build aborts if it fails, but confirm backcastMaxErrPP.
  4. Fiscal meta re-indexed (D2b) — node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs after any port that moved a fiscal bloc's variables (the EA/UK debt-explosion guard; stale indices pass tsc/build).
  5. Baseline sanity — GDP1q not NaN/flat; levels monotone-ish (C1).
  6. Coupling regenerated + modelSig matches (D3) — gvar-coupling-artifact.test.ts reports STALE/mismatch otherwise (and verify_gvar24.mjs if you gunzip the .gz first, per E).
  7. Canaries green (E1) before feature tests.
  8. tsc --noEmit clean, npm run build succeeds — last.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Cause Fix spec/companion n mismatch added a row/fiscal var by hand, or stale spec vs re-estimated betahat re-run the loop so exportbloc writes both from the same R.B; never hand-edit spec.json; verify with C3 Glob edit ignored *_glob.json shadows *_2025q4.json/bare file (A2 precedence) edit/rebuild the _glob.json for GVAR blocs Fiscal closure error (backcastMaxErrPP ≫ 1e-13, build aborts) gNomQ not built the app's way, or COVID interpolated not NaN'd, or wrong debt grid confirm gNomQ = 100·Δlog(gdp) + 100·Δlog(deflator) off the same glob cols; COVID quarters → NaN; check the observed-debt grid (pipeline/fiscal_raw/us_grid.json for US, pipeline/fiscal_raw/fiscal_<CC>.json for EA/JP/UK/CA/CN) Coupling modelSig mismatch / STALE re-estimated/ported a bloc but didn't regenerate the artifact re-run precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs (D3); gvar-coupling-artifact.test.ts confirms (it reads the .gz) Singular (I−M) ρ(M) ≥ 1 (e.g. disaggregating the euro members into coupled blocs) keep EA aggregated; euro members ship as standalones (no weights row); confirm ρ(M) < 1 after re-estimation Stuck at MC noise floor (~0.13 > TOL) too few draws — iterating churns Monte-Carlo noise raise NDRAWS; do not increase MAXIT to chase it MATLAB OOM parfor workers × n=41 US bloc × draws reduce pool size / NDRAWS; run a BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS subset; the .mat checkpoint lets you re-export cheaply Singular SIGMA / NaN in estimation leading NaN in a discontinued series, or near-collinear national-accounts identity fix the raw DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json (drop leading-NaN rows, fix the collinear var) — not the estimator lambda → NaN lamfix pinned with both noc and sur fixed leave lambda empirical-Bayes; if pinning, keep noc=1 or sur=1 free (B2) Coupling inverse step fails no MATLAB on PATH for the BLAS inverse the NumPy fallback scripts/invert_coupling.py exists; confirm matlab -batch resolves, else use it scenario-scale-convention red after n change fixture pinned to old column layout regenerate via pipeline/_verify/gt.m (id-resolved, layout-robust) and re-run (E3) Estimator crashes on arrayfun(@(v)v.id, …) / "expected struct array" the raw _glob.json has a prior field on some but not all variable objects ⇒ jsondecode returns a cell array tag every variable object (use tag_fiscal_wn.py for the 6 fiscal blocs; for others add "prior" to all objects). The estimators' priorsfrom tolerates the cell shape, but homogeneous tagging is the convention (B5.3) Prior change had no effect (still drifts) spec wasn't tagged, or you read *_2025q4.json/bare file (precedence A2), or pos ended up empty confirm the tag is in the _glob.json; verify the export spec shows prior:'WN' (B5.3 step 3); the loop builds statCols=find(Spec.Prior(keep)=="WN")' — if that is empty, nothing changes A series spikes/collapses through COVID 2020 mis-flagged isFinancial — a market price (equity/oil/rate) NaN'd as macro and DK-interpolated set isFinancial=true so it stays observed through the 2020Q2–Q4 mask; for the 4 commons confirm GLB_FINANCIAL includes all of GLB_OIL/GLB_USLR/GLB_USBAA/GLB_USEQ (B6.1), re-estimate, port, regen coupling A bloc's betahat looks "off" after a subset run BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US <CC>" is a restricted GVAR (stars trade-weighted over only the listed partners), not the full fixed point use the subset only as a quick check; deploy a coupled bloc only from a full-loop export (B7.1) Transform mismatch vs sibling blocs (e.g. IP stored as growth not level) a series' transformation is incoherent with the same series in other blocs rebuild it in the right space (e.g. build_ca_ip_level.py made IP_CA a log-LEVEL); audit with the per-economy variable table / the 1048-var count (B6.2) Debt-to-GDP path explodes after a refit (e.g. EA/UK) fiscal_meta.json indices went stale — a refit re-ordered the spec and the positional idx now points at the wrong column (e.g. EA effRate → equity ~col 537) run node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs (D2b) — it re-resolves the indices by id and rewrites all six metas, preserving d0/sfaDefault

docs.title: Known issues / limitations (flagged, NOT yet fixed)
docs.text:
These are open, honestly-documented defects in the live tool — flagged for fix, not yet fixed. State them as such; do not present the affected outputs as fully validated.

  1. CN fiscal debt — inflation double-count (RESOLVED 2026-06). For China, fiscal_meta.json idx.gdp used to point at a nominal GDP_CN, so the snowball gNomQ = Δlog(idx.gdp) + Δlog(idx.deflator) double-counted inflation. The surgical CN-only re-estimate replaced it with real GDP (CN/spec.json var 0 = "CN Real GDP (quarterly, chain-linked SA volume index, base 2011Q1=100)" and CN/fiscal_meta.json idx.gdpIsNominal = false), so the roll-forward now reads the correct real-GDP + deflator nominal growth, matching the other five fiscal blocs. The fix re-estimated CN only; the other 32 GVAR blocs are byte-identical to the prior working model.
  2. Long-run tilt — no non-finite filter on the weights (SUPERSEDED by the δc drift overlay; tilt no longer the live long-run mechanism). longRunTilt.ts solveEntropicTilt computes the per-draw weights as w_i = exp(γ'·(g_i − target)) over the long-run moments g_i. Unstable draws (unit-root / near-singular (I − ΣB_l)) return a NaN long-run moment (verified: the steady-state path returns NaN for the singular case), and the weight solve does not drop them — one NaN g_i poisons wsum and silently empties the anchor band. (The std-scaling loop and weightedQuantile do filter non-finite, but the dual/Newton weight solve does not.) The live "Long-run targets" panel no longer uses this tilt — it uses the deterministic δc drift overlay (§G), which has no draw reweighting and therefore none of this failure mode. longRunTilt.ts is now dormant/legacy; this bug only bites if it is re-wired in. Fix-if-revived: filter non-finite g_i before solveEntropicTilt.
  3. Closed-form GVAR solve — no ρ(M)<1 divergence guard. solveGVARClosedForm (gvarSolve.ts) solves x = (I−M)⁻¹d either via the cached inverse or a Neumann fixed-point (x ← d + Mx, capped at maxIt=2000). There is no check that ρ(M)<1: a diverged fixed point (or a Neumann iteration that hit the cap without converging) returns a confident-wrong forecast with no warning. The shipped artifact is stationary (ρ=0.924), but a future re-estimation that pushed ρ≥1 (e.g. disaggregating the euro members) would not be caught here. Fix: assert ρ<1 / Neumann convergence and surface a divergence warning.
  4. Conditional-forecast band uses the FILTERED (not DK-smoothed) covariance. The conditional band variance is taken from filteredP (kalman.ts: "Extract forecast variances from filtered covariances", variances.push(submatrix(filteredP[t], …))), not the smoother covariance. A future pin tightens the smoothed uncertainty of the quarters preceding it, but the filtered covariance does not see that future observation — so the band is OVERSTATED for quarters before a future pin.
  5. The porter does not regenerate fiscal_meta. Positional fiscal indices go stale on any variable-order change — see the RULE in D2b (fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs). This was the cause of the recent EA/UK debt explosion. The cause is documented as a limitation; the fix is the mandatory re-index step.

docs.title: Quick file map — what one country update touches
docs.text: Concern File(s) Data pull / raw pipeline/build_*.py → pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json (precedence A2) Spec / registry pipeline/country_specs.ts, app/src/lib/data/countries.ts Fiscal block pipeline/build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py → glob + pipeline/fiscal_meta/<CC>.json Variable PRIOR (RW↔WN) pipeline/tag_fiscal_wn.py (the 6 fiscal blocs; idempotent) → prior field in the _glob.json; mechanism in BVAR/MFBVAR/GLPreplicationWeb/bvarGLPmf.m (diagb(pos)=0) via BVAR/MFBVAR/cgl_bvarV5.m 3rd positional stationary/pos arg (B5) Globals / isFinancial / COVID mask pipeline/build_globals_new_blocs.py (GLB_FINANCIAL={GLB_OIL,GLB_USLR,GLB_USBAA,GLB_USEQ}), pipeline/build_ca_ip_level.py (IP_CA as log-LEVEL) (B6) Estimate (GVAR-EM) pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m (env: US_ENDOGENOUS, MAXIT, TOL, NDRAWS, BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS, GVAR_WEIGHTS_FILE; reads per-var prior → statCols) Estimate (standalone) pipeline/export_bvar_euro_fsap.m (17 euro; reads per-var prior → statCols), export_bvar_oecd_covid.m, export_bvar_us_covid.m, export_bvar_country.m Weights / topology app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_24bloc.json (33 blocs, US first), pipeline/build_trade_weights_full.py, build_us_weights_row.py Port pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py (6 sig-fig) → app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/*.json Coupling app/scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs + app/scripts/invert_coupling.{m,py} → coupling_default.json.gz Compute (read-only) app/src/lib/compute/: gvarSolve.ts, kalman.ts, fiscalAccounting.ts, matrix.ts, taylor-rule.ts (9 rules in app/public/data/dsge/rules.json), barnichon-mesters.ts, longRunTilt.ts Checks app/src/__tests__/compute/ (the gate — npx vitest run), pipeline/_verify/gt.m (fixture regen); app/scripts/verify_gvar24.mjs is a legacy fast helper (gunzip the .gz first; some checks stale for endogenous — see E) Publish (deploy) git push bvarscenario HEAD:main → .github/workflows/deploy-to-website.yml → matyasfarkas/starter-hugo-academic static/bvar-scenario/ → GitHub Pages (F) Empirical evaluation (separate repo) /home/farkas/github/em-bayesian-gvar-eval — OOS forecast horse-race + endogenous-US spillover analysis; design in docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md; results in results/tables.{txt,json} + results/tierA/us_rate_100bp.json — see the note below

docs.title: Empirical evaluation — where it lives, and what it does NOT touch
docs.text:
The model's out-of-sample evaluation is a separate repository, /home/farkas/github/em-bayesian-gvar-eval (full design + runbook in its docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md — read that before touching the eval). It is read-only with respect to this repo: it consumes this repo's estimator (cgl_bvarV5.m, export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m) and the raw DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json panels, but changes nothing here and is not part of the green gate or the deploy. Nothing in §A–§F depends on it.

What it implements (design final; results now produced — read them off results/tables.{txt,json} + results/tierA/us_rate_100bp.json, do not re-key the numbers from memory):

  • An OOS forecast horse-race, 9 models. Proposed = the EM-Bayesian GVAR (endogenous-US emgvar_endo
  • frozen-anchor emgvar_frozen). Benchmarks = Random Walk (rw) + climatology (rw_clim), AR(p) (arp), single-country BVAR (sbvar, strip the 3 ROW* columns), the textbook OLS Pesaran–Schuermann–Weiner / Dees GVAR (cgvar_a), a single-pass Bayesian GVAR (cgvar_b, MAXIT=1), and a Bayesian DFM (dfm). Recursive expanding window, 56 origins 2010Q1–2024Q4 (2020 COVID origins dropped from the headline), rolling as-of-t0 trade weights (leak-free — the shipped 2021–2023 weights_full.json is forbidden in the OOS sweep), targets {real GDP growth, inflation, short rate} for US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN, horizons h∈{1,2,4,8}.
  • Scoring. CRPS is the primary density score + a 90% Model Confidence Set (CRPS-broken parametric log-score is a secondary column — see the caveat). PIT/coverage; significance via Giacomini–White (headline, valid under nesting), Clark–West (nested point), Diebold–Mariano (non-nested), with Romano–Wolf + the MCS for multiplicity.
  • An endogenous-US spillover analysis off the US_ENDOGENOUS=1 model (Tier-A).

Headline (read the exact numbers off results/tables.json). AR(p)/DFM have the lowest point CRPS overall, but the 90% MCS shows emgvar inside the INFLATION MCS at every horizon (h=1,2,4,8) and inside the SHORT-RATE MCS at h=1,2,4 — statistically indistinguishable from the best on the nominal block; it is out of the GDP-GROWTH MCS at h=1 and h=8. emgvar_endo ⪰ emgvar_frozen in the MCS (the endogenous-US design earns its keep — e.g. at RATE h=1 endo is in the set and frozen is not). It decisively beats the textbook OLS classical GVAR (cgvar_a is removed from most MCS sets) — the EM-Bayesian treatment is the demonstrable value-add. Best INFL h=1 is emgvar_endo (relRMSE 0.799).

Honest caveats (must state). (1) The draw-based densities are OVER-CONFIDENT — PIT cov90 ≈ 0.73–0.85 for emgvar vs the 0.90 target. (2) The parametric log-score column is numerically broken on the under-covered clouds (it returns ~1e12–1e16); CRPS + the MCS are the robust primaries — do not cite the LPS column. (3) Tier-A spillover (eval/tier_a_scenario.mjs): a US +100bp multi-period conditional projection shows non-trivial endogenous-only cross-country feedback the frozen anchor structurally omits (its own note: gap = endogenous − frozen = the RoW→US feedback the frozen anchor cannot represent), but the magnitudes are large and sign-flipping (e.g. US growth peak +1.28 endo vs a −4.37 frozen-path spike) and it is a conditional projection, NOT a causal IRF/GIRF — the magnitudes need a validation pass before being headlined. Do not overclaim a causal interpretation.

If you re-estimate or re-prior a bloc here, the eval must be re-run on its side to stay consistent — but that is its repo's concern, not a step in this runbook.

docs.title: Module & API Reference
docs.text:
A per-module reference for the browser-side compute engine in app/src/lib/compute/* and the JSON data loaders in app/src/lib/data/*. Each section lists the module's responsibility, its key exported signatures with input/output shapes, the numerical thresholds and unit conventions that matter, and where it is called.

This page is a navigable reference, not a tutorial. For the architecture overview, the per-bloc file contract, and the estimation pipeline see:

  • Architecture for AI — end-to-end data flow and the 8-file bloc contract
  • Adding a country and Re-estimating models
  • Fiscal block methodology — the budget-identity recursion in detail
  • Data contracts & JSON schemas — the 8 per-bloc JSON files, including the spec.json prior / transformation / isFinancial tags this engine reads
  • Pipeline — the MATLAB GVAR-EM estimator and Python data builders that produce the artifacts the modules below consume

The two estimation-side facts that the browser engine inherits and must be read alongside the module reference:

  • Per-variable prior tag. Each spec.json variable carries prior ∈ {"RW","WN"}. RW (random walk) is the default own-first-lag prior mean of 1; WN (white-noise / stationary) sets the own-first-lag prior mean to 0 so the series mean-reverts. This is a property of the MATLAB estimator (bvarGLPmf.m, §Fiscal stationary prior), not the TS engine, but it changes the shape of the baselines the engine loads — the fiscal GDP-ratios (FREV_GDP, FPEXP_GDP, SFA) now revert to their in-sample mean instead of drifting at the long horizon. See DATA_SCHEMA §3 for the field and PIPELINE §5 for how it is set.
  • isFinancial and COVID masking. A variable with isFinancial:true is kept observed through the 2020Q2–Q4 COVID-as-missing window (only macro columns + the two macro stars are masked). The global commons GLB_USEQ (US equity) and GLB_OIL (oil) are isFinancial:true everywhere, so they condition the imputation rather than being imputed.

All matrices in this engine are flat row-major Float64Arrays wrapped as { data, rows, cols } (see matrix.ts). All BVAR data is in companion space: 100·log levels for log variables, the raw value/rate for lin variables. A quarter-on-quarter annualized growth rate is 4·Δ(level) for a log variable.

docs.title: Module map
docs.text:
Module Responsibility Primary callers matrix.ts Flat-array linear algebra (LU solve, Cholesky, inverse, Toeplitz) every other compute module kalman.ts DK filter/smoother, conditionalForecast, affineBlocKernels, forecast draws gvarSolve.ts, useConditionalForecast.ts, ChartGrid.tsx, runGvarBandSolve.ts gvarSolve.ts GVAR topology + cross-country closed-form / Gauss-Seidel solver runGvarSolve.ts, gvarWorker.ts, runGvarBandSolve.ts, useGvarForecast.ts fiscalAccounting.ts Budget-identity debt recursion, debt inversion useConditionalForecast.ts, ChartGrid.tsx, runGvarBandSolve.ts taylor-rule.ts Taylor-rule rate path + MMB coefficient translation (dormant — no current importer) barnichon-mesters.ts Optimal-policy QP (B&M 2023 sufficient statistics) ControlPanel.tsx longRunTilt.ts Entropic tilt / PLR long-run anchors + weighted bands ChartGrid.tsx compute/gvarDragState.ts Module-level signal bus: drag begin/end + compute-in-flight flags (off the zustand store, so a drag does not re-render the grid) ChartGrid.tsx (drag handler + badge), useGvarForecast.ts (defer the heavy solve to drag-end) conditionedDotY.ts Optimistic conditioned-dot display↔stored mapping (an exact inverse pair) so a pinned marker snaps to the click instantly ChartGrid.tsx (drag + right-click plant + dot render) useGvarForecast.ts Debounced, drag-aware, stale-guarded GVAR worker solve (two-tier direct/full) ChartGrid.tsx data/loader.ts Per-country single-bloc JSON fetch + LRU draws cache + fiscal_meta single-country views, ChartGrid.tsx data/gvarLoader.ts Multi-bloc GVAR bundle fetch, topology self-config, coupling artifact GVAR mode data/countries.ts Static country registry (dropdown) TopBar

The compute modules are pure functions over the loaded JSON. The two sections below document the estimation-side mechanisms (MATLAB / pipeline/*) that determine the content of the JSON the engine consumes; they belong here because a reader debugging a baseline shape needs them, and because the prior / isFinancial tags are read by gvarSolve.ts and the loaders.

docs.title: Fiscal stationary prior
docs.text:
Where it lives. BVAR/MFBVAR/GLPreplicationWeb/bvarGLPmf.m (the GLP/Minnesota prior builder), threaded through BVAR/MFBVAR/cgl_bvarV5.m (the COVID-as-missing data-augmentation Gibbs), driven by the two estimators pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m (GVAR) and pipeline/export_bvar_euro_fsap.m (euro-area financial blocks).

The problem it fixes. Under the default random-walk own-lag prior (mean 1) the fiscal GDP-ratios FREV_GDP, FPEXP_GDP, SFA inherit a near-unit-root and drift at the long horizon — e.g. US revenue stuck near the last-observed ~19% of GDP rather than reverting toward its in-sample ~17.4% mean. A GDP-ratio is economically stationary, so a random-walk prior is the wrong shrinkage target.

The mechanism. GLP shrinks each equation's own-first-lag coefficient toward a per-variable prior mean held on the diagonal of the prior-mean matrix b. bvarGLPmf.m builds it from a vector diagb, and the pos argument zeroes the selected entries:

% BVAR/MFBVAR/GLPreplicationWeb/bvarGLPmf.m  (lines 193–195)
diagb        = ones(n,1);
diagb(pos)   = 0;   % own-first-lag prior mean = 0 for the WN (stationary) variables in pos
b(2:n+1,:)   = diag(diagb);

pos is the 3rd positional argument of cgl_bvarV5 (named stationary in its signature):

% BVAR/MFBVAR/cgl_bvarV5.m
function [Xcond,storeP,BETA,SU,X_interp0,Dforecast] = cgl_bvarV5(x,p,stationary,nDraws,nBurn,MCMCconst,hz,varargin)
pos = stationary;                       % line 19
...
r = bvarGLPmf(xx(1:lastFull,:), p, ..., 'pos', pos);   % line 54

How the estimators set pos. Each reads the per-variable prior string from the spec and selects the WN columns of the kept domestic block (stars are appended after the domestic columns and are always RW):

% pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m
statCols = find(Spec.Prior(keep)=="WN")';   % domestic WN columns; [] for the 44 non-fiscal blocs
...
[Xcond,~,B,S,~,Dfc] = cgl_bvarV5(x, lags, Dc.statCols, nDraws, nBurn, MCMCconst, H, ...);

% pipeline/export_bvar_euro_fsap.m
Prior    = priorsfrom(J.variables);         % per-variable "RW"/"WN" string array, RW default
statCols = find(Prior=="WN")';
[~, storeP, BETA, SU, ~, Dforecast] = cgl_bvarV5(x, lags, statCols, nDraws, nBurn, MCMCconst, H, ARGS{:});

priorsfrom is deliberately robust to MATLAB's jsondecode dropping a prior field that is present on only some variable objects (a heterogeneous struct array collapses the field); an absent field ⇒ all-RW (the historical behaviour).

Which variables are WN. Only the three fiscal GDP-ratios, in the six fiscal blocs (US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN). EFFI (the effective rate, isFinancial:true) stays RW — a rate level is not a mean-reverting ratio. The shipped US/spec.json confirms exactly three WN tags:

Variable transformation prior financial FREV_GDP (revenue/GDP) lin WN macro FPEXP_GDP (primary exp/GDP) lin WN macro SFA (stock-flow adj/GDP) lin WN macro EFFI (effective rate, % p.a.) lin RW financial

Effect and scope. The fiscal ratios now mean-revert to their in-sample means at the long end (US revenue → ~17.4% instead of ~19%). The change is byte-identical (a no-op) for the 44 non-fiscal blocs, whose statCols is empty. Committed in 6f0161a (prior change), 22250be / e38673a (full 33-bloc refit + port). The browser engine reads the resulting mean-reverting baselines unchanged — there is no TS-side code for the prior.

docs.title: Coherence fixes (companion to the refit)
docs.text:
Shipped alongside the fiscal-prior refit (22250be / e38673a); they change the content of the loaded JSON, not the engine:

  • GLB_USEQ and GLB_OIL are isFinancial:true everywhere. Both are now kept observed through the COVID-as-missing window (2020Q2–Q4) instead of being imputed as macro columns, so the US-equity and oil paths condition the imputation of the masked macro block. Verified in US/spec.json (GLB_USEQ → log·RW·fin, GLB_OIL → log·RW·fin).
  • Canada IP_CA rebuilt as a log-LEVEL. Chained from the discontinued FRED level index + growth to 2025Q4, so IP_CA carries transformation:"log" (a level the engine differences to a growth rate) rather than a pre-differenced series. Verified in CA/spec.json.
  • Per-economy variable table. A transformation · prior · financial table was added to the user manual; all 1048 variables across the 50 shipped specs were audited coherent.

⚠️ The illustrative spec.json example in DATA_SCHEMA §3 predates this refit (it shows FREV_GDP/SFA as RW and GLB_OIL as isFinancial:false). The shipped US/spec.json is the authority: FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/SFA are WN, and GLB_OIL/GLB_USEQ are isFinancial:true.

docs.title: matrix.ts
docs.text: Responsibility. Core dense linear algebra over flat Float64Arrays, row-major. No external dependency. Every other compute module imports from here.

docs.title: Type
docs.text: interface Matrix { data: Float64Array; rows: number; cols: number }

docs.title: Key exports
docs.text:
Function Signature Notes mat (rows, cols, values?: number[]) => Matrix row-major; values optional eye / zeros (n) => Matrix / (rows, cols) => Matrix identity / zero get / set (a, i, j) => number / (a, i, j, v) => void element access multiply (a, b) => Matrix skips zero entries of a (sparse-friendly) multiplyVec (a: Matrix, v: Float64Array) => Float64Array matrix·vector transpose, add, subtract, scale, symmetrize shape-preserving add/subtract throw on dimension mismatch submatrix / setSubmatrix block read / write solve (A: Matrix, b: Float64Array) => Float64Array LU with partial pivoting; throws solve: singular matrix when the pivot magnitude < 1e-15 solveMatrix (A, B: Matrix) => Matrix solves A·X = B column by column inverse (A) => Matrix solveMatrix(A, eye) cholesky (A) => Matrix lower-triangular L, A = L·Lᵀ; throws when a diagonal ≤ 1e-14 (not positive definite) toeplitzLower (irf: number[], horizons) => Matrix lower-triangular impulse-response convolution matrix for B&M vecAdd / vecSub / vecScale Float64Array helpers

Numerical thresholds. LU singularity pivot floor 1e-15; Cholesky positive-definite floor 1e-14. These are the two failure modes consumers must guard (the smoother and the simulation draw catch a failed Cholesky and fall back to a diagonal sqrt).

docs.title: kalman.ts
docs.text:
Responsibility. Companion-form state-space construction and the Durbin–Koopman (DK) filter + smoother for conditional forecasting of a single BVAR bloc. Ported from MATLAB conforekf.m / VARcf_DKcks.m / runKF_DK.m. This is the affine engine the GVAR solver probes.

State-space form (state a_t = [y_t; y_{t-1}; …; y_{t-p+1}], dimension n·p):

a_t = T·a_{t-1} + c2 + u_t,   u_t ~ N(0, Q)
y_t = Z·a_t + c1 + e_t,        e_t ~ N(0, R)

docs.title: Core types
docs.text:
interface BVARParams { betahat: Matrix; sigmahat: Matrix; n: number; lags: number }
// betahat is (1 + n·lags, n): row 0 = constant, rows 1..n·lags = lag coefficients
// sigmahat is (n, n) innovation covariance

interface LinObs { h: Float64Array; value: number }  // linear-combination pin: h'·y_t = value

docs.title: Key exports
docs.text: Function Signature (abbreviated) Output buildStateSpace (params: BVARParams) => StateSpace companion T, Z, Q, R, c1, c2; R = 1e-12·I (tiny measurement noise) initState (data: Matrix, lags) => { a0, P0 } last p rows stacked; P0 = 1e-7·I kalmanFilter (ss, Y, a0, P0, linObs?) => { filteredA, filteredP, predictedA, predictedP, innovations, ZF } forward pass; Y is (n, T_fwd), NaN = missing kalmanSmoother (ss, predictedA, predictedP, filteredA, innovations, ZF, Y, linObs?) => Matrix DK backward pass (n·p, T_fwd) conditionalForecast see below the production single-bloc forecast affineBlocKernels see below the 18× GVAR perf path dkSimulationDraw (params, hist, condPaths) => { smoothed, forward } one DK simulation-smoother draw (band loop) bvarForecastDraw (params, hist, H, addInnovation=false) => Matrix iterated VAR forecast; addInnovation toggles parameter-only vs predictive bands

docs.title: conditionalForecast
docs.text:
function conditionalForecast(
  params: BVARParams,
  historicalData: Matrix,                 // (T_hist, n), companion space
  condPaths: Matrix,                      // (H, n), NaN = free (to be forecast)
  linConstraints?: { t: number; h: Float64Array; value: number }[]
): { mean: Matrix; variance: Matrix[] }   // mean (H, n); variance is H × (n, n)

Given history and a partially-observed future path, returns the smoothed forecast for all variables. Per-variable pins live in condPaths (set a cell, leave others NaN); the optional linConstraints pin a linear combination h'·y_t = value at quarter t (used for the fiscal primary-balance constraint revenue − primaryExp = target). The forecast is affine in the pinned values for a fixed pin mask — the property the GVAR solver exploits.

Convention invariant. An all-NaN condPaths reproduces baseline_levels.mean to ~1e-11. This is the canary that the engine and the shipped data agree.

⚠️ The returned variance is the FILTERED state covariance, not the DK-smoothed one (it is submatrix(filteredP[t], …), kalman.ts:405). The MEAN is smoothed; the variance is not — so the conditional band is overstated for quarters that precede a future pin (a later observation would tighten them). Known issue #4.

Called from useConditionalForecast.ts (single-bloc path), ChartGrid.tsx, gvarSolve.ts (baseline + every probe), and runGvarBandSolve.ts.

docs.title: affineBlocKernels
docs.text:
function affineBlocKernels(
  params: BVARParams,
  historicalData: Matrix,
  condPaths: Matrix,    // (H, n) probe BASELINE: pin mask + baseline values
  cells: number[]       // variable columns to bump (one kernel set per column)
): { Sc: Matrix; kernels: Matrix[][] }
// Sc = baseline smoothed mean (H, n)
// kernels[ci][tp] = (H, n) smoothed-mean response to condPaths[tp, cells[ci]] += 1

The exact perf path for the GVAR coupling build. Because conditionalForecast is affine in the pinned values for a fixed mask, the filter/smoother factors (PPred_t, gain PZF_t, smoother L_t, ZF_t) are value-independent. affineBlocKernels computes them once (one full filter+smoother pass on condPaths), then evaluates each unit-bump kernel as a cheap delta forward/backward recursion (pure matvecs). The arithmetic is byte-equal to running conditionalForecast once per (cell, tp) probe, at a large constant-factor win (~10–20× on the n=41, lags=3, H=20 endogenous US bloc). Used inside gvarSolve.ts's blocKernels.

docs.title: Numerical thresholds (kalman.ts)
docs.text:
Constant Value Site Purpose Measurement noise R 1e-12·I buildStateSpace (set(R,i,i,1e-12)) near-exact observation; keeps the filter conditioned Initial state cov P0 1e-7·I initState / dkSimulationDraw tight diffuse prior on the seeded last-p rows Cholesky innovation floor max(Σ_ii, 1e-12) dkSimulationDraw / bvarForecastDraw diagonal-sqrt fallback when the full cholesky(Σ) is not positive definite

These compose with matrix.ts's solve (singular pivot < 1e-15) and cholesky (PD diagonal ≤ 1e-14): a failed cholesky(Σ) in a simulation draw is caught and replaced by the diagonal square root above, so a marginally non-PD posterior Σ degrades gracefully rather than throwing.

docs.title: Worked example — single-bloc conditional forecast
docs.text:
Pin US revenue to a fixed 18% of GDP over the first four quarters and read the smoothed forecast of every variable (the rest free):

import { conditionalForecast } from '@/lib/compute/kalman'
import { mat } from '@/lib/compute/matrix'

// params: BVARParams parsed from companion_mean.json (n, lags, betahat, sigmahat)
// historical: (T_hist, n) Matrix in companion space (100·log levels / raw lin)
const H = 20
const n = params.n
const condPaths = mat(H, n)                 // mat() zero-fills; "free" cells must be NaN, so:
condPaths.data.fill(NaN)
const FREV = idx.revenue                    // column of FREV_GDP in this bloc
for (let t = 0; t < 4; t++) condPaths.data[t * n + FREV] = 18.0   // pin 18% of GDP, q0..q3

const { mean, variance } = conditionalForecast(params, historical, condPaths)
// mean: (H, n) smoothed forecast; mean.data[t*n + col] is variable `col` at horizon t
// variance: H entries, each an (n, n) Matrix (filtered/smoothed state cov at that horizon)

The all-NaN canary that proves the engine and the shipped data agree:

const free = mat(H, n); free.data.fill(NaN)
const { mean } = conditionalForecast(params, historical, free)
// mean ≈ baseline_levels.mean to ~1e-11, column by column

docs.title: Worked example — linear-combination pin (fiscal primary balance)
docs.text:
The fiscal "required primary balance" path is conditioned as revenue − primaryExp = pb_t, a linear combination across two columns at each quarter — exactly the linConstraints channel:

const h = new Float64Array(n)               // selector h'·y_t
h[idx.revenue] = 1; h[idx.primaryExp] = -1  // revenue − primaryExp
const linConstraints = pbPath.map((value, t) => ({ t, h, value }))   // value = required pb at q t

const { mean } = conditionalForecast(params, historical, free /* all-NaN */, linConstraints)

This is how useConditionalForecast.ts / fiscalAccounting.ts's requiredPrimaryBalance output is fed back into the BVAR (caller iterates 1–2 passes; see fiscalAccounting.ts below).

docs.title: gvarSolve.ts
docs.text:
Responsibility. The cross-country GVAR scenario solver. Each bloc is an independent single-country BVAR whose baseline is computed once; cross-country coupling enters only through the scenario delta, which propagates via three trade-weighted "RoW" star variables (plus, when the US is endogenous, the US global commons). Reuses kalman.ts unchanged.

The delta system is a linear fixed point x = M·x + d (x = stacked star pin-deltas, optionally augmented with US-global deltas). It is solved two ways that agree to ~1e-6:

  1. Gauss-Seidel sweeps (solveGVAR) — sums the Neumann series Σ Mᵏ d.
  2. Direct closed form (solveGVARLinear / solveGVARClosedForm) — (I−M)⁻¹d or the inverse-free Neumann fixed point, with M cached across scenarios that share a pin structure.

docs.title: Star variables and link convention
docs.text:
Each non-US (and endogenous-US) spec carries three block:'row' stars, all lin in the receiver:

Star Meaning Linked partner series ROWDEM (DEM) trade-weighted foreign GDP growth, % q/q ann partner GDP (log) ROWINF (INF) trade-weighted foreign inflation, % q/q ann partner deflator/CPI (log) ROWFIN (FIN) trade-weighted foreign short rate, % partner policy rate (lin)

gdp/price deltas are 4·Δ(level-gap) growth operators (t=0 anchored to the shared in-sample last level, whose delta is 0); rate deltas are the level gap. Rate-less blocs (CH, NO) drop out of FIN; hyperinflation blocs (AR) drop out of INF and FIN. (NOMINAL_GDP_BLOCS is empty since the 2026-06 CN-only re-estimate — CN now ships real GDP, so its DEM reads gdp directly rather than gdp − price.)

docs.title: Topology configuration
docs.text:
function configureGVARTopology(specs: Partial<Record<CountryCode, SpecJSON>>): CountryCode[]

(Re)configures the module from loaded spec.jsons + weights_24bloc.json. A country is an active RoW bloc iff its spec carries block:'row' stars and it has a weights row; the US is the closed anchor unless its own spec carries stars (then US_ENDOGENOUS = true). Mutates these live module exports in place (importers hold references):

  • ROW_COUNTRIES — every active bloc except US.
  • COUPLED_COUNTRIES — the blocs in the fixed point (ROW_COUNTRIES, US prepended when endogenous).
  • US_ENDOGENOUS — boolean.
  • IDX: Record<CountryCode, BlocIdx> — per-bloc column indices (gdp, price, rate, n, row, dem, glob), derived by deriveBlocIdx.
  • W — trade weights, renormalized per active panel so each row sums to 1 (zero diagonal).

deriveBlocIdx(code, spec) mirrors the estimator's column resolution: gdp = GDP_<CC> else GDPH; price = HICP_<CC> else CPI_<CC> else JGDP; rate = RATE_<CC> else POLRATE else YIELD10_<CC> (optional → -1). resetGVARTopologyToDefault() restores the shipped 6-bloc set (US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN) and runs at module load.

docs.title: Model container
docs.text:
function makeCountryModel(code, companionMean, historicalLevels, horizon): CountryModel
// CountryModel = { code, params, histMat, histLastLvl, baseLvl, H, n }
// baseLvl: (H, n) UNCONDITIONAL level forecast, computed ONCE via all-NaN conditionalForecast

buildParams(cm) parses a companion_mean.json into BVARParams.

docs.title: Solvers
docs.text:
Function Strategy Returns solveGVAR(models, scenarioPins, opts?) Gauss-Seidel; tol default 1e-4, maxSweeps default 12. US frozen unless endogenous. GVARResult (scenLvl, delta, sweepCount, residuals, rhoEmpirical) solveViewedScenLvl(models, pins, viewed, opts?) single-bloc shortcut: a US-only view (frozen US) needs one forecast, not the full sweep Matrix solveGVARLinear(models, pins, opts?) direct (I−M)⁻¹d or Smith/doubling; accepts cached coupling GVARLinearResult (adds M, d, rhoSpectral, method) solveGVARClosedForm(models, pins, modelSig?) production path: cached M, inverse-free Neumann; routes to the endogenous augmented form when US_ENDOGENOUS GVARResult solveGVARDirect(models, pins, modelSig) first-order one-hop preview (x = d), pure cached-kernel matvecs, sub-50 ms; falls back to the full solve when the kernel cache is cold GVARResult

ScenarioPins = Partial<Record<CountryCode, Matrix>>; each pin is (H, n), NaN = free.

docs.title: M build and coupling cache
docs.text:
M depends on the models and the conditioning structure (which cells are pinned) but not on the pinned values, so it is cached and reused across the dominant drag-a-pin UI flow. The relevant exports:

  • buildGVARCoupling / buildGVARCouplingEndo — assemble M, (I−M)⁻¹, ρ(M) (frozen / endogenous).
  • buildGVARCouplingM / buildGVARCouplingMEndo — assemble M and ρ without the inverse, for the export-time precompute that inverts (I−M) in MATLAB BLAS (precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs shells out to invert_coupling.m; invert_coupling.py is a NumPy fallback). The in-JS O(dim³) inverse was measured at ~46 min at the frozen-anchor dim≈1380, and is larger at the endogenous dim=2060, so it is never built live.
  • gvarPinStructureSignature(pins) — stable key over pinned cells; frozen-anchor topology excludes US pins, endogenous includes them.
  • seedGVARCouplingCache / hydrateGVARCoupling — load the pre-shipped artifact into the module cache.
  • prewarmGVARKernels / prewarmGVARKernelsChunk — warm the per-bloc kernel cache off the critical path (so the first US-pin solve re-probes only the US bloc; the RoW blocs hit the cache).
  • resetGVARCouplingCache / resetGVARKernelCache — test hooks.
interface GVARCouplingArtifact {
  modelSig: string; pinStructure: string; dim: number; rhoSpectral: number;
  M: number[][]; invImM: number[][]
}

This is the shape of app/public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json(.gz), produced by app/scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs and consumed by runGvarSolve.ts.

docs.title: US_ENDOGENOUS augmented state
docs.text:
When the US is a coupled bloc it both receives partner spillovers (its own ROW*_US stars) and drives every RoW bloc's global commons (oil / US 10Y / US BAA / US equity), which RoW blocs pin to own-base + US-global-delta. The compact state is therefore augmented:

x = [ star pin-deltas s_{c,v,t} for every coupled bloc c (US included) ]
    ++ [ US global-column deltas gΔ_{p,t} ]

with three coupling families assembled in buildLinearSystemEndo: (1) star ← star, (2) US-global ← US-star (definition row), (3) RoW-star ← US-global. solveGVARClosedFormEndo solves x = (I−M)⁻¹d (cached inverse) or the inverse-free Neumann fixed point (maxIt = 2000, tolerance 1e-10), then reconstructs each bloc with one final conditionalForecast.

⚠️ No ρ(M) < 1 divergence guard on the live solve (known issue #3). The Neumann iteration (gvarSolve.ts:1296) stops at maxIt/tolerance but never checks ρ(M) < 1. A refit producing ρ ≥ 1 would diverge silently and return a confident-wrong forecast. ρ is shipped in the artifact (currently 0.924) but not read as a guard. See Known issues #3.

docs.title: Spectral radius by panel size
docs.text:
spectralRadius(M) power-iterates M² (deterministic seed, 400 iters) and takes the sqrt (M is block-off-diagonal, so raw power iteration oscillates). Representative ρ(M):

Panel ρ(M) 6-bloc ~0.59 24-bloc, US frozen ~0.726 33-bloc, US endogenous (dim≈2060) 0.924041 (live coupling_default.json.gz; moves with each refit, stays < 1)

docs.title: Call sites
docs.text: runGvarSolve.ts (the orchestrator: seeds the artifact, prewarms kernels, routes to solveGVARDirect preview vs solveGVARClosedForm), gvarWorker.ts, runGvarBandSolve.ts (per-draw bands), and useGvarForecast.ts. The closed-form ≡ Gauss-Seidel equivalence and the shipped-artifact match are enforced by canary tests (gvar-convergence, gvar-coupling-artifact).

docs.title: Result shapes
docs.text:
interface GVARResult {                       // solveGVAR / solveGVARClosedForm / solveGVARDirect
  scenLvl: Record<CountryCode, Matrix>       // converged scenario LEVEL path per bloc, each (H, n)
  delta:   Record<CountryCode, Matrix>       // scenLvl − baseLvl per bloc, each (H, n)
  sweepCount: number                         // Gauss-Seidel sweeps used (closed form reports 1)
  residuals: number[]                        // per-sweep max |Δ change| over {EA, JP}
  rhoEmpirical: number                       // empirical ρ(M) (geometric decay of residuals)
}
interface GVARLinearResult extends Pick<GVARResult,'scenLvl'|'delta'> {
  M: Matrix; d: Float64Array; rhoSpectral: number; method: 'direct' | 'doubling'
}

ScenarioPins = Partial<Record<CountryCode, Matrix>>, each pin (H, n) with NaN = free.

docs.title: Numerical thresholds (gvarSolve.ts)
docs.text: Constant Value Site Meaning Gauss-Seidel tol 1e-4 solveGVAR default sweep stops when max level-change < tol Gauss-Seidel maxSweeps 12 solveGVAR default sweep cap (ρ(M) < 1 ⇒ a handful suffice) Closed-form ≡ iterative ~1e-6 canary gvar-convergence the two solve paths must agree Endogenous Neumann maxIt 2000 solveGVARClosedFormEndo inverse-free fixed-point cap Endogenous Neumann tol 1e-10·(1+‖x‖) solveGVARClosedFormEndo relative fixed-point convergence In-JS (I−M)⁻¹ cost ~46 min measured at frozen dim≈1380; larger at endogenous dim=2060 buildGVARCoupling why the inverse is precomputed, never built live

docs.title: Worked example — propagate a US rate shock through the panel
docs.text:
import { runGVARSolve } from '@/lib/compute/runGvarSolve'

// raw: GVARModelsRaw from loadGVARModels() (specs + companion_mean + levels + coupling artifact)
// Pin the US policy rate +100 bp over q0..q3 (companion space: a lin rate, so pin the LEVEL).
const usIdx = IDX['US']
const H = 20, n = usIdx.n
const usPin = mat(H, n); usPin.data.fill(NaN)
for (let t = 0; t < 4; t++) usPin.data[t * n + usIdx.rate] = baselineUSRate[t] + 1.0  // +100 bp

const { result } = runGVARSolve({ raw, scenarioPins: { US: usPin } })
// result.scenLvl['EA'], result.delta['JP'], ... : (H, n) coupled responses
// US frozen-anchor mode: US delta is exactly the pinned path; RoW deltas come via the stars.
// US endogenous mode (US_ENDOGENOUS): US ALSO receives RoW feedback + drives the global commons.

runGvarSolve.ts seeds the shipped coupling artifact (seedGVARCouplingCache), keys it by gvarSignature(raw) + gvarPinStructureSignature(pins), and routes to the sub-50 ms first-hop preview (solveGVARDirect) or the full solveGVARClosedForm. Prewarm the per-bloc kernels off the critical path first:

prewarmGVAR(raw)                 // warms RoW bloc kernels; first US-pin solve then re-probes only US

docs.title: fiscalAccounting.ts
docs.text: Responsibility. Derives the public-finance reporting variables (primary/overall balance, deficit, interest, debt-to-GDP, gross financing needs, snowball) from the BVAR-estimated fiscal drivers via the IMF FPP / CBO budget identity. Debt is derived, never estimated. See fiscal block methodology.

docs.title: Units (the main bug source)
docs.text: Quantity Unit revenue, primaryExp, sfa, amortization, debt-service-to-GDP % of GDP, annual ratio effRate % per annum (effective rate on the debt stock) gNomQ % per quarter (nominal GDP growth) d0, debt debt-to-GDP, %

docs.title: The recursion (per quarter, lagged debt d₋₁) — LIVE / displayed path
docs.text:
This is computeFiscalBlock (fiscalAccounting.ts:163), the only fiscal recursion wired to the displayed debt-to-GDP charts and bands (called from ChartGrid.tsx and runGvarBandSolve.ts). It is a passive accounting recursion — no debt-feedback term:

pb_t      = rev_t − primexp_t                             // primary balance (NO Bohn term)
i_eff_t   = effRate_t                                     // effective rate (NO risk premium)
int_t     = i_eff_t · d₋₁ / 100                           // interest / debt service, % GDP
deficit_t = int_t − pb_t
d_t       = d₋₁·(1 + i_eff_t/400)/(1 + gNomQ_t/100) − pb_t/4 + sfa_t/4
gfn_t     = deficit_t + amortization_t
