All four drivers (rev, primexp, effRate, sfa) are BVAR-forecast; debt/GDP is a derived accounting identity, never a BVAR variable. sfa_t in the deployed roll is the structural constant sfaDefault (not the per-quarter forecast SFA column — that gets dragged to extremes by the long-run anchors). There is no debt→primary-balance reaction and no debt→yield risk premium in anything the user sees.

⚠️ The γ/β (Bohn FRF + risk-premium) levers are DORMANT / test-only. A separate function runFiscalRecursion (fiscalAccounting.ts:66) adds the optional terms pb_t += γ·(d₋₁ − d*) (Bohn, default 0.03) and i_eff_t += β·max(0, d₋₁ − d*) (risk premium, default 0), gated by feedbackOn. runFiscalRecursion has ZERO production callers — it is invoked only by __tests__/compute/fiscalAccounting.test.ts and fiscal-backcast.test.ts. No UI control sets γ/β/feedbackOn. They are documented and validated but not in the live path; a possible future endogenous-debt-feedback extension, not current behaviour. See fiscal-block-methodology.md.

✅ CN inflation double-count — RESOLVED (2026-06). computeFiscalBlock forms gNomQ as Δ(level[idx.gdp]) + Δ(level[idx.deflator]). The CN block previously shipped a nominal GDP_CN, so the price term was double-counted; the surgical CN-only re-estimate replaced it with real GDP (idx.gdpIsNominal = false), so CN's roll-forward now matches US/EA/JP/UK/CA. CN-only graft; the other 32 blocs are byte-identical.

docs.title: Key exports
docs.text:
Function Input → output runFiscalRecursion(input: FiscalRecursionInput) driver arrays → FiscalRecursionResult. If dsvc (estimated debt-service-to-GDP) is supplied it is the interest and the effective rate is backed out; else the effective-rate form is used. computeFiscalBlock(input: FiscalBlockInput) reads drivers off a level-space (H × n) forecast via FiscalBlockIndices (gdp, deflator, revenue, primaryExp, effRate, optional debtService, optional sfa); rolls the same identity forward → FiscalRecursionResult. gQ = q/q nominal growth = Δ(real 100·log) + Δ(deflator 100·log). requiredPrimaryBalance(input: DebtInversionInput) inverts the recursion: the annual primary balance per quarter required to make debt follow targetDebt (IMF DSA "required primary balance"). pb = 4·[(d₋₁ + int/4)/(1+gQ) − d_t] + sfa. The caller conditions the BVAR on this path via the revenue − primExp linear constraint, iterating 1–2 passes.

FiscalRecursionResult carries primaryBalance, effectiveRate, interest, overallBalance, deficit, debt, dDebtGdp (= debt[t] − debt[t-1], derived), gfn, snowball — each a length-H array.

docs.title: Driver provenance — the stationary prior matters here
docs.text: The three estimated drivers computeFiscalBlock reads off the level path — revenue (FREV_GDP), primaryExp (FPEXP_GDP) and sfa (SFA) — are exactly the variables now carried with the stationary WN prior (§Fiscal stationary prior). So the level path this module differences and rolls forward now mean-reverts at the long end rather than drifting; the derived debt-to-GDP trajectory inherits that. effRate (EFFI) stays RW. The recursion code is unchanged — only the shape of the forecast it consumes changed.

docs.title: The two parametrisations (debt service vs effective rate)
docs.text:
Both runFiscalRecursion and computeFiscalBlock branch on whether an estimated debt-service-to-GDP column is supplied:

Supplied Interest intₜ (% of GDP) Effective rate (% p.a.) dsvc / idx.debtService set = dsvcₜ directly (the BVAR forecast) backed out: intₜ·100/d₋₁ not set (fallback) = effRateₜ · d₋₁ / 100 = effRateₜ (the estimated EFFI)

The quarterly roll-forward iQ = effRate/400 = intₜ/(4·d₋₁) is identical either way (this keeps the effRate-only callers byte-identical and fixes a ~100× over-statement of interest on the DSVC path).

docs.title: Worked example — derive the fiscal block from a GVAR scenario level path
docs.text:
import { computeFiscalBlock } from '@/lib/compute/fiscalAccounting'

// levelPaths: H×n (number[][]) US scenario LEVEL forecast (e.g. scenLvl['US'] unpacked row-major)
// prevLevelRow: the last historical level row (for the t=0 growth diff)
const result = computeFiscalBlock({
  levelPaths, prevLevelRow,
  idx: {                                  // FiscalBlockIndices — column positions in the level path
    gdp: usIdx.gdp, deflator: usIdx.price,
    revenue: cRev, primaryExp: cPexp, effRate: cEffi,
    sfa: cSfa,                            // ESTIMATED SFA column (WN-prior) → per-quarter, with bands
  },
  d0: 122.3,                              // initial debt/GDP (%) for the US
})
// result.debt[t]    : derived debt-to-GDP (%), budget-identity roll-forward
// result.deficit[t] : interest − primary balance (% of GDP, annual)
// result.snowball[t]: ((1+iQ)/(1+gQ) − 1)·d₋₁  (the interest-growth differential, pp)
//   where gQ = q/q nominal growth = Δ(real 100·log) + Δ(deflator 100·log), over 100

This is the exact call runGvarBandSolve.ts makes per draw (line ~163, req.fiscal.sfa.slice(0,Hf)) to band the US debt path, and the single-bloc useConditionalForecast.ts / ChartGrid.tsx make for the central path.

docs.title: Worked example — required-primary-balance debt inversion (DSA)
docs.text:
import { requiredPrimaryBalance } from '@/lib/compute/fiscalAccounting'

const pb = requiredPrimaryBalance({
  targetDebt,          // desired debt/GDP path, % (length H) — e.g. a flat 100% anchor
  effRate, gNomQ,      // read off the current forecast (% p.a. and % per QUARTER)
  d0: 122.3, sfa,
})
// pb[t] = annual primary balance (% of GDP) per quarter that makes debt follow targetDebt:
//   pb = 4·[(d₋₁ + int/4)/(1+gQ) − d_t] + sfa,  d₋₁ taken along the TARGET path.
// Feed pb back via the revenue − primaryExp linConstraints of conditionalForecast (see kalman.ts),
// then re-read effRate/gNomQ and re-invert — 1–2 passes converge (rate & growth respond via the BVAR).

Called from useConditionalForecast.ts and ChartGrid.tsx (single-bloc) and from runGvarBandSolve.ts (GVAR mode — the per-draw US fiscal block).

docs.title: taylor-rule.ts
docs.text:
Responsibility. Helpers for a rule-based mode: an implied policy-rate path from a 3-parameter Taylor rule, plus translation to the Macroeconomic Model Database (MMB) 33-coefficient rule format. No component currently imports this module (no callers in src/, no test) — it is a standalone, self-contained API kept for the rule-based path.

r_t = ρ·r_{t-1} + (1-ρ)·[r* + φ_π·(π_t − π*) + φ_y·y_t]

docs.title: Key exports
docs.text: Function Input → output computeTaylorRule(params: TaylorRuleParams, baselineRate, inflationDev, outputDev) applies the rule in deviations from baseline → { ratePath: Float64Array, rateDeviation: Float64Array }. params = { rho, phiPi, phiY, rStar, piStar } (rStar/piStar default 2.0). All deviation arrays length H. taylorToMMBCoefficients(params) → number[33]: coeffs[0] = ρ; coeffs[4..7] = φ_π·(1−ρ)/4 (annual inflation split over 4 quarters); coeffs[13] = φ_y·(1−ρ); coeffs[31]=1, coeffs[32]=0.25 (rate std).

docs.title: barnichon-mesters.ts
docs.text: Responsibility. Optimal monetary policy via the Barnichon & Mesters (2023 AER) sufficient- statistics approach: given DSGE IRFs to a policy shock, find the shock sequence minimizing the central bank's loss, as a regularized least-squares QP min (H·ε − b)ᵀ W (H·ε − b), ε* = (HᵀWH)⁻¹ HᵀWb.

docs.title: Key exports
docs.text:
Function Input → output optimizePolicy(irfs: DSGEIRFs, targetInflation, targetOutput, lambdaPi, lambdaY) irfs = { inflation, outputgap, interest } (number[] IRFs); targets are Float64Array(H) deviations. → BMOptimizationResult (optimalShocks, policyPath, impliedInflation, impliedOutput, loss). Builds Toeplitz convolution matrices via matrix.toeplitzLower; normal equations regularized with Tikhonov α = 1e-6 on the diagonal. computeCounterfactualDeltas(irfs: Record<string, number[]>, rateDeviation: Float64Array) back out the shock sequence from a desired rate deviation (forward substitution on the lower-triangular interest IRF), then convolve every IRF → Record<string, Float64Array> of per-variable deltas. Reads irfs['interest'] or irfs['interest_']; throws if neither is present or if the impact effect |interestIRF[0]| < 1e-6.

optimizePolicy is called from components/controls/ControlPanel.tsx. computeCounterfactualDeltas is currently only exercised by barnichon-mesters.test.ts.

docs.title: longRunTilt.ts
docs.text: Responsibility. After-estimation entropic tilting toward a "Prior for the Long Run" (PLR) restriction (Giannone–Lenza–Primiceri 2019). It does not re-estimate: it takes the existing posterior draws and computes per-draw importance weights w_i ∝ exp(γ'·g_i) so the weighted mean of each long-run moment hits a target, then the band loop applies them as weighted quantiles. Ported from pipeline/_verify/entropic_tilt.m.

docs.title: The long-run moment
docs.text: Default mode: 'forecast' — g = h'·E[y_{T+H}], the H-step mean forecast (default horizon = 40 ≈ 10y). The alternative mode: 'steadyState' (g = h'·(I−ΣB_l)⁻¹c) is opt-in and only valid for genuinely stationary combinations: on the I(1) 100·log-level real VARs (I−ΣB_l) is near-singular and the steady state diverges (verified −21103 vs an H≈40 forecast of +35), so forecast is the default.

docs.title: Key exports
docs.text:
Function Input → output computeLongRunMoments(draws, historicalData, restrictions, opts?) per draw, per restriction → gByDraw[r] = Float64Array(nDraws) solveEntropicTilt(gByDraw, restrictions, opts?) Newton dual with backtracking line search → LongRunTiltResult (weights, ess, tiltedMean, priorMean, gamma, converged, maxResidual). maxIter default 200, gradient tol default 1e-10. longRunTilt(...) convenience wrapper: moments + tilt computeLongRunTilt(draws, historicalData, anchors: AnchorSpec[], opts?) the function ChartGrid consumes: display-unit variable anchors → LongRunTiltDiagResult (weights, ess, nDraws, tiltedMean, priorMean, maxResidual, converged). Empty anchors → uniform weights / full ESS. computeAnchorMoments(draws, historicalData, anchors) one mean-forecast pass per draw; log anchor → 4·Δ annualized growth at H, lin anchor → level at H weightedQuantile(values, weights, q) weighted quantile (drops non-finite, renormalizes) weightedBands(drawPaths, weights, H, n, qLo=0.16, qHi=0.84) per-draw paths → { q16, q84 }, each (H × n) 
interface AnchorSpec { varIdx: number; isLog: boolean; target: number; horizon?: number }

PosteriorDraws is the flat draws layout beta[draw*k*nv + j*k + i], beta_shape = [k, nv, nDraws] — the same layout loadCompanionDraws produces. ESS = 1/Σwᵢ² ∈ [1, nDraws]; a joint multi-constraint tilt can degenerate (ESS → 1) at low draw counts, so computeLongRunTilt std-scales each anchor by its own sample standard deviation for conditioning.

⚠️ Non-finite long-run moments are NOT filtered before the Newton dual (known issue #2). solveEntropicTilt builds Gc straight from gByDraw and the softmax exp(Σ Gc·γ) will NaN if any draw's moment is non-finite (an unstable draw whose long-horizon forecast diverged), silently emptying the anchor band. The scaling pre-pass skips non-finite values, but the matrix fed to the dual does not. See Known issues #2.

docs.title: data/loader.ts
docs.text:
Responsibility. Per-country single-bloc JSON fetch for the standalone (non-GVAR) views, plus the DSGE catalog/IRF loaders. Fetch base is ${NEXT_PUBLIC_BASE_PATH || ''}/data.

Function Returns loadJSON<T>(path) fetch + parse; throws on non-OK loadBVARData(country) { spec, companion, baseline, historical, companionDraws: null, baselineLevels, historicalLevels, fiscalMeta }. baseline_levels / historical_levels / fiscal_meta are optional (404 → null). companion_draws is not fetched here (loaded lazily). loadCompanionDraws(country) the heavy draws file (US beta ~2M doubles). Converts beta/sigma from boxed number[] to Float64Array once (faster indexing + memcpy into the worker). 404 → null. loadDSGECatalog() / loadModelIRFs(modelName, ruleName) DSGE catalog + per-rule IRFs

The draws cache is a bounded LRU (_DRAWS_CACHE_MAX = 4): each companion_draws is ~7–26 MB, so browsing all ~50 economies would otherwise retain hundreds of MB; eviction is transparent (a miss re-fetches).

docs.title: data/gvarLoader.ts
docs.text:
Responsibility. Multi-bloc GVAR bundle fetch + self-configuring topology. Tries every candidate bloc in weights_24bloc.json (US first), tolerates 404s, and keeps a bloc iff its spec carries block:'row' stars (US always kept as anchor). The qualifying set re-configures the solver topology via configureGVARTopology.

Export Purpose GVAR_COUNTRIES active GVAR codes, US first; mutated in place by qualifyGVARRaw isGvarCountry(country) the single GVAR-membership predicate loadGVARModels() fetches the 4 engine files per bloc (companion_mean, historical_levels, baseline_levels, spec) — not the heavy companion_draws — plus the 404-tolerant coupling artifact → GVARModelsRaw qualifyGVARRaw(raw) configures topology + drops non-qualifying blocs; idempotent gvarSignature(raw) cheap stable signature (codes, n, lags, horizon, a few betahat/sigmahat entries) keying the model + coupling caches buildGVARCountryModels(raw) builds (and caches by signature) the CountryModels; each runs the unconditional smoother once

loadCouplingArtifact() tries the gzipped coupling_default.json.gz first (decompressed via the native DecompressionStream, no dependency — the plain JSON exceeds GitHub's 100 MB limit at the full panel), then falls back to plain JSON. GVARModelsRaw is plain JSON so it round-trips through zustand and the Web Worker.

docs.title: data/countries.ts
docs.text: Responsibility. The static country registry backing the dropdown (TopBar). COUNTRIES is a CountryEntry[] ({ code, name, group }); group ('core' | 'oecd' | 'em') is only a coarse geographic tag for sorting — GVAR membership is data-driven via isGvarCountry / GVAR_BLOC_CANDIDATES, not this field. Adding a country = add an entry here and drop its app/public/data/bvar/<code>/ files. Convenience filters: CORE_COUNTRIES, OECD_COUNTRIES, EM_COUNTRIES.

docs.title: conditionedDotY.ts — optimistic conditioned-point dot
docs.text:
Path. app/src/lib/conditionedDotY.ts (it lives directly under lib/, not under lib/compute/).

Responsibility. Convert between the clicked display value and the stored conditioning-path deviation, as an exact inverse pair, so a pinned marker (the "dot") snaps to the just-clicked value instantly — without waiting for the ~700 ms forecast recompute. The dot is sourced from the conditioning path the click writes (instant); the forecast line + uncertainty bands still come from the solve and reconcile a moment later. Two functions, exact inverses (conditionedDotDisplayY ∘ conditioningDeviationFromDisplayY = id):

Function Mapping conditioningDeviationFromDisplayY(displayY, baseMeanVal, baseLevelVal, specVarId) (conditionedDotY.ts:62) display → stored. Level-display vars (oil/FX/equity/house): dev = indexLevelToLog(displayY) − baseLevelVal. Other vars (growth-displayed log / lin): dev = displayY − baseMeanVal. conditionedDotDisplayY(dev, baseMeanVal, baseLevelVal, specVarId) (conditionedDotY.ts:34) stored → display (the inverse). Level vars: logToIndexLevel(baseLevelVal + dev). Other vars: baseMeanVal + dev.

isLevelDisplay(specVarId) (from displayUnits.ts) decides the branch. The forward map is the single source of truth for both handleDrag (drag a pin) and the right-click "plant a bullet at the cursor" activation — keeping them beside each other guarantees a conditioned point always round-trips to the cursor for every variable type (no transform double-applied). Used in ChartGrid.tsx: conditioningDeviationFromDisplayY at the drag/right-click write (lines ~1230, ~1300) and conditionedDotDisplayY at the dot render (line ~1656, where active+conditioned quarters override the scenario forecast y with the reconstructed click value). Pinned by conditioned-dot-optimistic.test.ts and right-click-plant-at-cursor.test.ts.

docs.title: compute/gvarDragState.ts — drag/compute signal bus
docs.text:
Responsibility. A tiny module-level signal bus shared between the chart drag handler (ChartGrid.tsx) and the GVAR solve hook (useGvarForecast.ts). It exists off the zustand store on purpose: the drag begin/end and in-flight flags are pure UI signals that must NOT enter the scenario/pins state — putting them in the store would re-render the whole grid on every pointer-down/up. useSyncExternalStore lets only the badge subscribe to the in-flight flag, while useGvarForecast reads the drag flag imperatively + subscribes to drag-END.

Export Purpose beginGvarDrag() / endGvarDrag() mark pointer-down / pointer-up; endGvarDrag notifies the drag-END subscribers so a held structure-change solve fires exactly once for the committed pin isGvarDragging() / getGvarDragging() imperative read of the drag flag onGvarDragEnd(fn) subscribe to pointer-up (returns an unsubscribe); the channel useGvarForecast uses to dispatch the deferred solve subscribeGvarDragging(fn) useSyncExternalStore subscribe to the drag FLAG (begin + end) — lets ChartGrid's effective memo reactively swap the viewed bloc's line between the frozen GVAR path (idle) and the cheap live condForecast (dragging) setGvarComputing(on) / subscribeGvarComputing(fn) / getGvarComputing() the "computing…" badge state (only the badge re-renders)

Why two listener sets. _dragFlagListeners fires on begin AND end (drives the line fallback); _dragEndListeners fires only on pointer-up (dispatches the held solve). Sharing one would either re-render on every begin or fail to dispatch on end. Tested by gvar-drag-scheduling.test.ts and gvar-drag-line-band.test.ts.

docs.title: useGvarForecast.ts — drag-aware two-tier GVAR solve hook
docs.text:
Responsibility. The React hook that turns the active bloc's own-pins into a propagated GVAR forecast, keeping the heavy solve off the live drag path. It owns a module Worker (main-thread fallback), a stale-response reqId guard, and two timers (a live debounce + a separate drag-release macrotask). The load-bearing perf fact (verified in the code, useGvarForecast.ts:279): on the live 50-bloc US-endogenous panel every mean solve — direct OR full — is ~4571 ms warm / ~13327 ms cold (the US-pinned structure misses the shipped empty-pinStructure coupling artifact and falls to the dim-2060 Neumann fixed point + one Kalman smoother per coupled bloc).

The drag boundary (useGvarForecast.ts:287):

  • While a drag is in progress (isGvarDragging()): ZERO solves fire — neither value-only nor structure-change. The hook marks pendingReleaseSolveRef = true and bails. The dragged bloc's own line still follows the cursor live because ChartGrid's effective falls back to the single-country condForecast (a ~1–5 ms DK smoother) while gvarFc is frozen at the last solve.
  • On pointer-up (onGvarDragEnd): exactly one direct solve is dispatched, deferred to a ~100 ms macrotask (coalesces back-to-back gestures + lands after the final store write flushes into dataRef). direct is used so the ~95 MB coupling artifact is stripped (withoutCoupling) from the worker post — solveGVARDirect rebuilds/caches M from the per-bloc kernel cache and never consumes the inverse.
  • Non-drag edits (numeric/programmatic pin entry, Reset, scenario switch) dispatch live: full solves immediately (explicit user action — solveFullToken bumped via Compute / "Solve full GVAR"), direct previews after a 120 ms debounce.

So the full cross-country fixed point (mode:'full', hydrated (I−M)⁻¹) fires only on the explicit Compute / "Solve full GVAR" token bump; everything else is the direct one-hop preview. Pinned by gvar-drag-scheduling.test.ts.

docs.title: Render memoization in ChartGrid (reconcileCharts + chartDeepEqual + React.memo)
docs.text:
Responsibility (Phase-2 render optimization, issue #68). A single conditioning edit changes scenarios, re-running the monolithic charts useMemo → every chart object gets a fresh identity → absent memoization, Plotly would repaint all ~35 panels. Two levers (in ChartGrid.tsx) make one edit repaint ≈1 panel:

Mechanism Where What it does chartDeepEqual(a, b) ChartGrid.tsx:173 tight structural equality over the chart objects (plain objects / arrays / primitives — no Maps/Sets/Dates/functions appear in a chart object) reconcileCharts(next, prev, onRebuilt?) ChartGrid.tsx:205 reuses the PRIOR render's object reference for any panel whose freshly-built content is deep-equal (keyed by chartVar.bvarIdx, so a panel maps to its prior self even if list order/length shifts on a country switch); returns the prior array when every element kept its reference (stable outer identity, no [charts] effect churn) MemoDraggableChart = memo(DraggableChart) ChartGrid.tsx:2523 so a stable chart reference + the grid's useCallback'd handler props mean an unchanged panel does not re-render / repaint

The math/builder is untouched: reconcileCharts only swaps a NEW object for an EQUAL old one, so the rendered data is byte-identical; it just preserves reference identity where the content did not move. Two test-only instrumentation counters (chartRebuildCounter, draggableChartRenderCounter, both exported, ~zero production cost) let chartgrid-render-memo.test.ts assert that one conditioning edit rebuilds ≈1 panel and re-renders only the touched panel(s), not all ~35.

docs.title: Empirical evaluation (companion repo — in progress)
docs.text:
A separate repository — em-bayesian-gvar-eval (sibling to this one) — turns the deployed engine + the MATLAB estimator into a publishable evaluation. It does not ship in the app; it is the empirical core of the paper. The full design is final in em-bayesian-gvar-eval/docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md; results are preliminary / forthcoming.

It races two proposed models against six benchmarks (model codes are the registry in eval/tables.py / eval/score.py):

Code Model Role emgvar_endo EM-Bayesian GVAR, US endogenous proposed (headline) emgvar_frozen EM-Bayesian GVAR, frozen US anchor proposed rw driftless Random Walk (+ sample-mean "climatology" anchor) universal reference arp AR(p), p by AIC over {1..4}, iterated and direct strong univariate sbvar single-country Bayesian VAR (stars dropped, same prior) isolates coupling cgvar_a textbook OLS Pesaran–Schuermann–Weiner / Dees GVAR (no COVID-as-missing) literature GVAR cgvar_b single-pass Bayesian GVAR (MAXIT=1, COVID-as-missing) isolates EM completion dfm Bayesian dynamic factor model non-GVAR global benchmark

Nesting map (stated in the paper): sbvar ⊂ cgvar_b ⊂ emgvar_*; RW/AR nested in every VAR.

Design (final). Recursive expanding window, origins 2010Q1–2024Q4 (drop the 2020 COVID origins from the headline); rolling as-of-t0 trade weights (leak-free — built from IMTS bilateral trade with endPeriod ≤ year(t0); the shipped 2021–2023 weights_full.json is forbidden in the OOS sweep, as it would inject future trade structure); targets {real GDP growth, inflation, short rate} for US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN; horizons h ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8} (each scored only where a ≤2025Q4 realization exists). COVID-as-missing reapplies in-sample at every origin where 2020Q2–Q4 ≤ t0.

Scoring. CRPS is the primary density score (ensemble estimator, robust at the modest draw counts); a parametric Gaussian/skew-t log-score corroborates; PIT / coverage for calibration. Significance: Giacomini–White is the headline test (valid under nesting and the recursive scheme), Clark–West for nested point/MSE comparisons, Diebold–Mariano (HAC, HLN-corrected) for non-nested pairs only; multiplicity handled with Romano–Wolf + the Model Confidence Set per (variable, horizon).

Part 2 — spillovers. Tier A (ships now, zero new estimator code): the conditional-scenario endogenous-vs-frozen US contrast, driven through the shipped gvarSolve.ts (x = Mx + d) — its driver is em-bayesian-gvar-eval/eval/tier_a_scenario.mjs, specified to be exercised from the main repo via a vitest harness (app/src/__tests__/eval/tierA_spillover.test.ts, planned). Tier B (new gvar_girf module, constructible from the exported betahat/Σ + coupling M): GIRF / generalized FEVD / Diebold–Yilmaz index. The protocol is explicit that gvarSolve.ts computes conditional-mean scenario deltas, not GIRFs/FEVD — the only spillover strings in this repo are UI labels.

Headline result (first full pass — results/tables.{txt,json}, 56 origins, 9 models). AR(p)/DFM have the lowest point CRPS, but the 90% Model Confidence Set is the publishable signal: emgvar_endo is in the inflation MCS at all horizons and the short-rate MCS at h=1,2,4 (indistinguishable from the best on the nominal block), out of the growth MCS at h=1 & h=8; emgvar_endo ⪰ emgvar_frozen across the MCS; and the EM-Bayesian treatment decisively beats the textbook OLS classical GVAR (cgvar_a is removed from most sets). The unconditional point leader for inflation h=1 is the univariate AR(p) (relRMSE 0.641); emgvar_endo (relRMSE 0.799) is the best among the EM/GVAR family, not the overall best. Honest caveats: the draw-based densities are over-confident (PIT cov90 ~0.73–0.83 vs 0.90), and the parametric log-score column is numerically broken (CRPS + MCS are the robust primaries). Tier-A (results/tierA/us_rate_100bp.json) shows non-trivial endogenous-only cross-country feedback the frozen anchor omits, but the magnitudes are large/sign-flipping and it is a multi-period conditional projection, NOT a causal IRF — needs a validation pass before being headlined. The design is final; see em-bayesian-gvar-eval/docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md.

docs.title: Known issues & limitations (flagged-for-fix, NOT yet fixed)
docs.text:
Documented honestly so a reader does not mistake a known bias for a verified result. Each is flagged-but-unfixed at the current deployment; cite this section, do not silently rely on the affected output.

  1. CN fiscal debt — inflation double-count (fiscalAccounting.ts). RESOLVED (2026-06). The CN debt-snowball roll-forward computes nominal growth as gQ = Δ(level[idx.gdp]) + Δ(level[idx.deflator]). CN previously shipped a nominal GDP_CN, so the price term was double-counted and the CN debt-to-GDP path was biased. The surgical CN-only re-estimate replaced GDP_CN with real GDP (spec.json var 0 = "CN Real GDP (quarterly, chain-linked SA volume index, base 2011Q1=100)", fiscal_meta.json idx.gdpIsNominal = false), so the snowball now reads the correct real-GDP + deflator nominal growth, matching US/EA/JP/UK/CA. The fix re-estimated CN only; the other 32 GVAR blocs are byte-identical to the prior working model.

  2. Entropic long-run tilt does not filter non-finite moments (longRunTilt.ts). solveEntropicTilt builds the centered/scaled constraint matrix Gc.push(col) directly from the per-draw moments gByDraw (longRunTilt.ts:~254) and never drops non-finite draws. The Newton dual then evaluates z[i] = Σ Gc[r][i]·γ_r and exp(z[i] − zmax) (:284–:289): a single non-finite Gc[r][i] (from an unstable draw whose long-horizon forecast diverged) makes z[i]=NaN, exp(NaN)=NaN, which poisons wsum and silently empties the anchor band (all weights NaN). The scaling pre-pass does skip non-finite values when computing each anchor's mean/std (:529–:532), but the matrix fed to the dual is not filtered. Fix: drop the non-finite draws (and renormalize) before the Newton iteration, as weightedQuantile already does at the band step.

  3. No ρ(M) < 1 divergence guard on the live closed-form solve (gvarSolve.ts). The endogenous Neumann fixed point x ← d + M·x (gvarSolve.ts:1296) iterates to maxIt = 2000 or a relative tolerance 1e-10·(1+‖x‖) — but there is no check that ρ(M) < 1. If a refit produced a M with ρ ≥ 1 (a non-stationary coupling), the iteration would diverge (or stop at maxIt far from a fixed point) and the solver would return a confident-wrong forecast with no warning. ρ is computed at export time and shipped in the artifact (and is currently 0.924), but the live solve does not read it as a guard. Fix: assert coupling.rhoSpectral < 1 (or estimate ρ and bail/flag) before trusting the iterate.

  4. Conditional band uses the FILTERED, not DK-smoothed, covariance (kalman.ts). conditionalForecast returns the smoothed MEAN but extracts the per-horizon variance from the filtered state covariance: variances.push(submatrix(filteredP[t], 0, 0, n, n)) (kalman.ts:405–:409, the comment says "Extract forecast variances from filtered covariances"). The DK smoother would tighten the covariance for quarters that precede a future pin (a later observation reduces earlier-quarter uncertainty); using the filtered covariance does not apply that reduction, so the conditional band is overstated for the quarters before a future pin. The mean is correct; only the band is conservative.

  5. The porter does not regenerate fiscal_meta.json — STALE-POSITIONAL-INDEX (port_gvar_exports.py). fiscal_meta.json's six idx values are positional column offsets, but pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py ports the file verbatim. Any variable-order change (the 33-bloc GVAR-EM refit inserted variables ahead of the fiscal block in every spec except US) silently re-points them — EA's effRate once landed on the equity index (~col 537) and debt/GDP exploded. Mitigation (mandatory after any re-port): run node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs, which re-resolves all six indices BY VARIABLE ID from the deployed spec.json (idempotent, preserves d0/sfaDefault). fiscal-meta-indices.test.ts is the guard that blocks a deploy with stale indices. See DATA_SCHEMA §9 and ARCHITECTURE §7.

docs.title: Build & test
docs.text:
From app/:

npx tsc --noEmit     # type check
npm test             # FAST suite (vitest run minus the heavy solver --exclude globs)
npm run test:heavy   # HEAVY solver/perf canaries (1 fork) — BOTH halves gate the deploy
npm run build        # static export

Compute-engine tests live in app/src/__tests__/compute/. The suite is split — npm test is vitest run minus seven heavy --exclude patterns; npm run test:heavy runs exactly those serialised to one fork; npm run test:all sweeps everything (see BUILD_AND_CI.md). The deploy-blocking canaries (all in the fast suite) are scenario-scale-convention, gvar-convergence (Gauss-Seidel ≡ closed-form), gvar-coupling-artifact (the shipped .gz matches a live build at the current modelSig), and fiscal-meta-indices (the STALE-POSITIONAL-INDEX guard above).

docs.title: Data & Estimation Pipeline
docs.text:
This page documents the offline pipeline that turns raw public data into the static JSON the web app serves: build → estimate → check → port → couple → test → deploy. Everything here runs on the researcher's machine (MATLAB + Python + Node); the deployed browser app only ever reads the resulting JSON.

Scope / where to look next. This page is the mechanism reference — the script inventory, the raw-data precedence chain, the GVAR-EM loop internals, the prior/coherence tagging sweeps, and the port/coupling steps. For the decision tree ("what kind of update is this, and what does it re-estimate?") and the operational runbooks (exact copy-paste sequences with the green gate) see MAINTAINER_OPERATIONS.md, REESTIMATING_MODELS.md and ADDING_A_COUNTRY.md; for the live compute architecture see ARCHITECTURE_FOR_AI.md; for the fiscal block see fiscal-block-methodology.md. The per-economy variable table (transformation · prior · financial-tag, all 1048 ported variable rows audited coherent) lives in the user manual (docs/user_manual.tex, sec:varcoherence). The out-of-sample forecast horse-race + endogenous-US spillover evaluation lives in a separate repo — see §10 and its docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md. This page does not repeat those — it complements them.

The deployed platform is ~50 economies: 33 coupled GVAR blocs (the US dominant unit + 32 partners, with the euro area as one aggregate EA bloc) + 17 euro-area FSAP standalones (the euro members, estimated in separation). Data is harmonised to a common 2025Q4 vintage from FRED, Eurostat, BIS, ECB, OECD and IMF sources.

docs.title: 1. Flow at a glance
docs.text:
┌──────────────┐
  FRED/Eurostat │ build_*.py   │  pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json
  BIS/ECB/OECD  │ (data build) │  pipeline/fiscal_raw/* , pipeline/fiscal_meta/<CC>.json
  IMF IMTS      └──────┬───────┘  app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_*.json
                       │
                       ▼
                ┌──────────────────────────┐
                │ export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m │  GVAR-EM loop (parfor, COVID-as-missing)
   (estimate)   │ export_bvar_euro_fsap.m   │  17 euro standalones
                │ single-country exporters  │  ──►  /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/*.json
                └──────────┬───────────────┘
                           │
                           ▼  (check: console GDP1q, star-change < TOL, /tmp/gvar24_workspace.mat)
                ┌────────────────────┐
   (port)       │ port_gvar_exports  │  6-sig-fig compaction
                │ .py                │  ──►  app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/*.json
                └─────────┬──────────┘
                          │   + cp pipeline/fiscal_meta/<CC>.json → app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/
                          ▼
                ┌──────────────────────────────┐
   (couple)     │ precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs │  (I−M)⁻¹ via MATLAB BLAS
                │ + invert_coupling.{m,py}     │  ──►  coupling_default.json.gz
                └─────────┬────────────────────┘
                          ▼
   (test)        cd app; npx tsc --noEmit; npm test; npm run test:heavy; BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario npm run build
                          ▼
   (deploy)      git push bvarscenario main  →  GitHub Action (next build)  →  copy app/out/* into starter-hugo-academic static/bvar-scenario/  →  Hugo rebuilds & serves on GitHub Pages

docs.title: 2. Dependencies
docs.text: Tool Used for Notes MATLAB + Parallel Computing Toolbox All .m estimators The GVAR-EM loop uses parfor; it calls parpool('local') (a no-op if absent — it then runs serially). Bundled MFBVAR / GLP Gibbs sampler + Minnesota prior Vendored in-repo under BVAR/ — the loop adds BVAR/MFBVAR, BVAR/AdditionalFunctions, BVAR/BGLreplication to the path. The COVID-as-missing sampler is BVAR/MFBVAR/cgl_bvarV5.m; the GLP (Giannone–Lenza–Primiceri 2015) marginal-likelihood machinery is BVAR/MFBVAR/bvarGLP.m. No external MATLAB downloads. Python 3 (standard library) Almost all build_*.py data builders Pure stdlib (urllib, json, csv, math) — no numpy/pandas/requests needed. The only exception is build_gvar_2025q4.py, which import requests for the IMF WEO pull. Node (project's pinned toolchain) Coupling precompute, tests, build Run scripts via npx vite-node / npx tsx; the app builds with next build (output: 'export' → static site). vitest runs the compute test suite. FRED API key FRED data pulls Read from FRED_API_KEY; a hardcoded fallback key is present in the scripts, but always pass your own: FRED_API_KEY=<key> python3 pipeline/.... Never commit a key.

docs.title: 3.1 Data builders (pipeline/build_*.py and friends)
docs.text:
All builders write the per-bloc raw contract DataRaw_<CC>.json (schema: { name, dates, variables:[{id,name,transformation('log'|'lin'),isFinancial,block}], data }) into pipeline/country_rawdata/, or edit a fiscal column / weights file in place. Fiscal-source builders write pipeline/fiscal_raw/.

Script Purpose Inputs Outputs build_country_data.py FRED-based core economies (EA/UK/JP/CA) — assemble quarterly macro+financial block FRED API (COUNTRIES dict) country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>.json build_us_auto.py Hands-off US builder — pulls all US series from FRED + builds the 3 fiscal drivers, COVID handled FRED API /tmp/us_dataraw_aug.csv, /tmp/us_spec.csv, /tmp/us_fiscal_meta.json (consumed by export_bvar_us_fiscal.m) build_oecd_eurostat.py Current (2025Q4–2026Q1) EU/EFTA OECD economies from Eurostat (live source FRED mirrors) + shared US block from FRED Eurostat SDMX + FRED country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>.json build_oecd_batch.py Batch single-country OECD builds from probe_oecd_fred.py's resolved IDs (FRED-mirror vintage ~2023Q3) oecd_fred_probe.json + FRED country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>.json build_oecd_merge.py Merge swarm-sourced oecd_swarm/DataRaw_<CC>_dom.json domestic data with the shared US block oecd_swarm/* + FRED country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>.json probe_oecd_fred.py Discovery only — probe FRED for quarterly coverage across OECD members, no estimation FRED API oecd_fred_probe.json build_gvar_2025q4.py Extend every bloc's domestic series to a common 2025Q4 endpoint via IMF WEO annual + proportional-Denton disaggregation (balanced panel) DataRaw_<CC>.json + IMF WEO (requests) country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_2025q4.json build_globals_new_blocs.py Augment a standalone economy with the 4 US-determined global commons (GLB_OIL/GLB_USLR/GLB_USBAA/GLB_USEQ), drop the legacy _US block → make it a GVAR RoW bloc DataRaw_<CC>_2025q4.json (or .json) country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json build_gvar_data.py Build the 3 RoW star blocks for the legacy 6-bloc GVAR (trade-weighted foreign demand/inflation/rate) DataRaw_<CC>.json + weights_6bloc.json edits DataRaw_<CC>.json build_euro_polrate_hpi.py Add POLRATE_<CC> (ECB DFR) + HPI_<CC> (real house-price index) to euro members ECB / Eurostat edits DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json + app spec.json build_euro_financial_block.py Add the shared euro corporate-risk yield EACORP (ECB YC G_N_C curve) + financials to euro models ECB Data Portal SDMX edits DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json build_polrate_hp_group.py Add POLRATE_<CC> (BIS CBPOL) + HP_<CC> to the SE/PL/DK/CZ/HU/NO/IS/CH/IL/TR blocs BIS CBPOL edits DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json build_polrate_hp_emg.py Same, for the emerging-market BVARs BIS CBPOL edits DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json build_trade_weights_full.py GVAR trade weights for the full ~50-economy panel from IMF IMTS (goods exports FOB + imports CIF, 2021–23 avg) IMF IMTS SDMX weights_full.json build_trade_weights.py Legacy 24-bloc trade weights from IMF IMTS IMF IMTS SDMX weights_24bloc.json build_us_weights_row.py Add a US own-reporter trade-weights row (USA → each partner) so the US can be endogenous IMF IMTS (USA reporter) edits weights_full.json (mirrors weights_24bloc.json) validate_and_promote_weights.py Sanity-check the full weights (rows sum to 1, no NaN/negatives, US has no row) and promote weights_full.json → weights_24bloc.json weights_full.json weights_24bloc.json (--check-only to validate only) build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py (us/ea/jp/uk/ca/cn) The 4-variable fiscal-block builder — write the {FREV_GDP, FPEXP_GDP, EFFI, SFA} columns with the recursion-consistent exact-residual SFA + COVID-NaN (build_fiscal_us_4var.py only overwrites the SFA column of an already-built US glob; the others rebuild the full 4-column block) fiscal_raw/fiscal_<CC>.json (US: fiscal_raw/us_grid.json) + DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json edits DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json in place + rewrites fiscal_meta/<CC>.json build_fiscal_drivers.py Legacy 3-driver fiscal builder (revenue/GDP, primary-exp/GDP, debt-service) — superseded by the *_4var.py builders fiscal_raw/* + glob edits DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json build_fiscal_cn.py / build_fiscal_jp.py Raw fiscal-source assemblers (national-currency quarterly revenue/expenditure/interest/gross-debt/NGDP) feeding the 4-var builders national sources / OECD fiscal_raw/fiscal_CN.json, fiscal_raw/fiscal_JP.json build_fiscal_walkthrough_xlsx.py Reviewer-facing Excel walkthrough of US/UK fiscal accounting built fiscal data docs/fiscal_accounting_walkthrough_US_UK.xlsx tag_fiscal_wn.py Stationary-prior tagger — set prior:"WN" on FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/SFA (and prior:"RW" on every other var, incl. EFFI) in the 6 fiscal globs, so the fiscal GDP-ratios mean-revert (see §3.3) DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json (US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN) edits the same files in place (format-preserving, idempotent) tag_global_financial.py Global-commons coherence tagger — flip isFinancial:1 on GLB_USEQ + GLB_OIL in every DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json, so the two US-determined market-price globals stay observed through the COVID window (see §3.4) every DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json edits in place (value-only splice, idempotent) build_ca_ip_level.py CA IP level rebuild — reconstruct IP_CA as a log-LEVEL index (chain the discontinued FRED level CANPROINDMISMEI forward with q/q growth PRINTO01CAQ657S), flip its transform to log (see §3.5) FRED CSV (no key) + DataRaw_CA_glob.json edits DataRaw_CA_glob.json in place

Two kinds of "fiscal" builder. build_fiscal_cn.py / build_fiscal_jp.py write the raw national-currency source (fiscal_raw/fiscal_<CC>.json); build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py then consume that source (plus the macro glob) to produce the four estimated columns and the exact-residual SFA. Run the raw builder first when the source data changes.

docs.title: 3.2 MATLAB estimators (pipeline/*.m)
docs.text:
Estimator Purpose Inputs Outputs export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m Canonical GVAR-EM loop — the 33-bloc coupled set (US endogenous, EA aggregate), COVID-as-missing per bloc, parfor Jacobi sweeps to convergence country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json, weights_full.json/weights_24bloc.json /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/*.json (8-file contract), /tmp/gvar24_workspace.mat export_bvar_euro_fsap.m The 17 euro standalones — FSAP-style two-country BVARs (domestic + exogenous US block + 4 globals), one VAR jointly estimated over all columns via the COVID-as-missing cgl_bvarV5 (which wraps the GLP bvarGLPmf machinery; exogeneity enforced by the block tags the app reads, not by the estimator) DataRaw_<CODE>.json (11 domestic + 9-var US block, prepared block tags) /tmp/euro_fsap_export/<CODE>/*.json export_bvar_us_covid.m Isolated US-only single-country export (COVID-as-missing cgl_bvarV5) DataRaw_US*.json /tmp/us_export/*.json export_bvar_oecd_covid.m Single-country OECD exporter (COVID-as-missing) DataRaw_<CC>.json /tmp/<CC>_export/*.json export_bvar_country.m Generic single-country / domestic+US-block exporter DataRaw_<CC>.json per-bloc export dir export_bvar_us_fiscal.m US export with the fiscal drivers appended build_us_auto.py outputs US export dir

The deployed macro models come from export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m (the 33 coupled blocs) and export_bvar_euro_fsap.m (the 17 euro members). The single-country exporters are templates / isolated re-runs.

docs.title: 3.3 The fiscal stationary ("WN") prior — tag_fiscal_wn.py
docs.text:
The GLP/Minnesota prior in BVAR/MFBVAR/GLPreplicationWeb/bvarGLPmf.m builds the prior coefficient matrix b with a unit own-first-lag mean by default (a random walk: diagb=ones(n,1)). Any column listed in the pos vector gets its own-first-lag prior mean zeroed, turning that variable into a mean-reverting white-noise (WN) process under the prior:

% BVAR/MFBVAR/GLPreplicationWeb/bvarGLPmf.m, lines 192–195
b=zeros(k,n);
diagb=ones(n,1);
diagb(pos)=0;          % zero the own-first-lag prior mean for the 'pos' columns -> stationary/WN
b(2:n+1,:)=diag(diagb);

pos is the 3rd positional argument of the COVID-as-missing sampler cgl_bvarV5 (which renames it pos=stationary; and forwards it as 'pos' to bvarGLPmf):

% BVAR/MFBVAR/cgl_bvarV5.m
function [Xcond,storeP,BETA,SU,X_interp0,Dforecast] = cgl_bvarV5(x,p,stationary,nDraws,...)
pos=stationary;
... r = bvarGLPmf(..., 'pos', pos);

Why this matters. Before this change every call passed [] for stationary, so every variable in every bloc was a prior random walk. The fiscal GDP ratios — FREV_GDP (revenue/GDP), FPEXP_GDP (primary expenditure/GDP), and SFA (the stock-flow adjustment) — are economically stationary: they fluctuate around a long-run share, they do not drift. Under the RW prior the projected ratios stayed pinned near their last observation at the long horizon (US revenue stuck near ~19%). The WN prior lets them mean-revert to their in-sample means — US FREV_GDP → ~17.9% (sample mean 17.91, last obs 18.77; commit e38673a) instead of drifting. The effective interest rate EFFI is genuinely persistent and stays RW.

How the tag is consumed. Each estimator reads the per-variable prior field out of the raw JSON via a priorsfrom helper and builds the WN column-index vector, passed as cgl_bvarV5's 3rd arg:

% export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m — on the kept domestic block, indices into x=[dom | stars]
statCols = find(Spec.Prior(keep)=="WN")';   % empty for the 44 non-fiscal blocs
[Xcond,~,B,S,~,Dfc] = cgl_bvarV5(x, lags, Dc.statCols, nDraws, nBurn, ...);

% export_bvar_euro_fsap.m
statCols = find(Prior=="WN")';
[~,storeP,BETA,SU,~,Dforecast] = cgl_bvarV5(x, lags, statCols, nDraws, nBurn, ...);

Because the stars (ROW*) are appended after the domestic columns, the WN indices (which point into the domestic block) are unchanged by the star append — and they remain on the domestic ratios only; stars are always RW. statCols is empty for the 44 non-fiscal blocs, where pos=zeros(1,0) behaves exactly like the old [] — so the WN change is a byte-identical no-op for every bloc that carries no fiscal ratio.

priorsfrom and the cell-array trap. tag_fiscal_wn.py writes prior on every variable object (not just the 3 WN ones) so the field set stays homogeneous: MATLAB's jsondecode returns J.variables as a struct array only when all objects share the same fields; a partial prior tag makes it a cell array and breaks the estimators' arrayfun(@(v)v.id, J.variables) calls. priorsfrom (present in both estimators) is written to be robust to either shape. The Python tagger is format-preserving (a targeted text splice — only the inserted/updated prior bytes change; the data array is untouched) and idempotent (re-running once tags are present is a no-op). It edits the globals-augmented file the GVAR loop actually reads, preferring _glob → _2025q4 → plain.

python3 pipeline/tag_fiscal_wn.py
# DataRaw_US_glob.json: 0 field(s) changed this run; WN = ['FPEXP_GDP', 'FREV_GDP', 'SFA']; RW count = 35
# ... (idempotent: 0 changes once tagged)

The exported spec.json carries the real prior per variable (WN/RW), so the contract and the per-economy coherence table reflect it. Committed in 6f0161a (plumbing) and 22250be/e38673a (the refit). See the fiscal recursion in fiscal-block-methodology.md.

docs.title: 3.4 Global-commons financial coherence — tag_global_financial.py
docs.text:
The COVID-as-missing masking in the estimator NaN's the 2020Q2–Q4 window only for the slow national-accounts macro variables (isFinancial:0), so the Durbin–Koopman smoother imputes them; fast-moving market prices (isFinancial:1) are kept observed through COVID and condition that imputation (§5.1, step 3). An audit found two of the four US-determined global commons mis-tagged: GLB_USEQ (US equity index) was isFinancial:false in every non-US bloc, and GLB_OIL (oil price) was isFinancial:false in every bloc — so they were being NaN-imputed across COVID like a GDP series, which is incoherent for a daily-quoted market price. (GLB_USLR long rate and GLB_USBAA BAA spread were already financial.)

tag_global_financial.py flips both to isFinancial:1 in every DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json so they match WTI_US/PZTEXP and stay observed through COVID. It is a value-only splice (the isFinancial field already exists on every object, so it never inserts a field — avoiding the same cell-array trap as §3.3), format-preserving, and idempotent. The source-of-truth fix is in build_globals_new_blocs.py, which now forces all four globals (GLB_FINANCIAL = {GLB_OIL, GLB_USLR, GLB_USBAA, GLB_USEQ}, line 37) financial when a new bloc is built, so future rebuilds stay coherent; the tagger is the one-time sweep over already-built globs.

python3 pipeline/tag_global_financial.py
# DataRaw_CA_glob.json: 0 field(s) changed this run; {'GLB_USEQ': 1, 'GLB_OIL': 1}

Committed in 22250be/e38673a.

docs.title: 3.5 Canada IP as a log-level — build_ca_ip_level.py
docs.text:
The CA glob's IP_CA column had been stored as a y/y growth rate (transformation:"lin"), incoherent with the US/UK IP blocs (stored as levels, transformation:"log"). build_ca_ip_level.py rebuilds it as a quarterly level index using the same growth-chaining convention as build_gvar_2025q4.py (level_t = level_{t-1}·(1+g_t)):

  1. average the discontinued monthly FRED level index CANPROINDMISMEI (Index 2015=100, ends 2024-02) to complete quarters (last complete quarter ≈ 2023-Q4);
  2. chain forward from there with the q/q growth series PRINTO01CAQ657S (to 2025-Q4): level_t = level_{t-1}·(1 + g_t/100);
  3. align onto the CA glob's exact quarterly grid (1998-Q1 … 2025-Q4), replace the IP_CA column, and flip the spec to transformation:"log", isFinancial:0 (prior RW unchanged).

It reads FRED via the public fredgraph.csv endpoint (no API key), caching CSVs under pipeline/country_raw/. It asserts the reconstructed level is positive/finite/smooth (no q/q spike beyond ~±20%) and ends near the observed ~106–107 range.

python3 pipeline/build_ca_ip_level.py
# WROTE .../DataRaw_CA_glob.json (IP_CA now LEVEL, transformation=log, isFinancial=0)

Committed in 9839a46 (the CA-only targeted re-estimate, §10.1) and folded into the full refit e38673a. After this rebuild, CA's IP propagates correctly through partners' ROW* stars (the earlier CA-IP approximation is removed).

Coherence audit. All 1048 ported variable rows (across the 50 blocs in app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/spec.json) were audited for transformation·prior·isFinancial coherence; the per-economy table is in the user manual (sec:varcoherence, commit fa104cd).

docs.title: 4. The DataRaw precedence chain
docs.text:
Each bloc has up to three raw-input layers; the GVAR-EM loop picks the first that exists (export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m, the per-bloc load loop):

raw = DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json        % globals-augmented (domestic + 4 GLB_* commons; _US dropped)
if missing → DataRaw_<CC>_2025q4.json   % common 2025Q4 endpoint (WEO-Denton extended)
if missing → DataRaw_<CC>.json          % the original FRED/Eurostat pull
Layer Built by Contents DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json build_globals_new_blocs.py (+ fiscal/financial editors) domestic block (legacy _US removed) + 4 global commons; the fiscal columns live here DataRaw_<CC>_2025q4.json build_gvar_2025q4.py domestic series extended to a common 2025Q4 endpoint (balanced panel) DataRaw_<CC>.json build_country_data.py / build_oecd_*.py the original pull (domestic + US block)

The _glob file is the production input: it carries the 4 globals the GVAR estimator expects, and it is the file the 4-variable fiscal builders edit in place (the SFA column). The 3 RoW stars are not stored in the glob — the loop computes them at solve time from partners' completed panels and appends them only at export.

Important. This precedence is offline only — it governs which raw input the MATLAB estimator reads. The browser app never sees DataRaw_*; it reads the ported app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/ JSON.

docs.title: 5. The GVAR-EM loop (export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m)
docs.text: The loop is an EM-style fixed-point over the cross-country foreign components. It interleaves a Gibbs/DK imputation step (estimate each bloc, fill the COVID + foreign-star holes) with a trade-weighting step (rebuild every bloc's RoW stars from partners' completed panels), and iterates until the foreign components stop moving.

docs.title: 5.1 Per-iteration internals
docs.text:
1. Bootstrap the US (closed anchor). Estimate the US once with no stars (cgl_bvarV5(xUS, …)) over the COVID-masked domestic block, take the posterior-mean DK-completed series XcUS = mean(Xcond,3) and form its [realGDP, price, rate] panel. This seeds iteration-1's stars. With US_ENDOGENOUS=1 the US is then folded into the loop and re-estimated with its own ROW*_US stars (the no-stars fit is discarded — a warm start, not exported).
  2. Build stars (buildstars). For each bloc, ROWDEM/ROWINF/ROWFIN = trade-weighted averages of partners' real-GDP growth / inflation / short rate, renormalised over the partners present each quarter (unbalanced panel). Stars are 4·Δ(100·log) (annualised %), not 400·. Per-component availability: a rate-less partner (CH/NO) still feeds DEM/INF; hyperinflation partners (AR) are dropped from INF/FIN but kept in DEM. Leading NaN is back-filled; internal COVID NaN is left for the smoother.
  3. Estimate each bloc (parfor). x = [domestic | ROWDEM ROWINF ROWFIN]; over 2020Q2–Q4 NaN only the macro columns (domestic isFinancial==false + ROWDEM + ROWINF); keep all financial columns + ROWFIN observed. The financial set kept observed now includes all four global commons GLB_OIL/GLB_USLR/GLB_USBAA/GLB_USEQ (after the §3.4 coherence fix), matching the domestic market prices (WTI_US/PZTEXP). The WN/stationary fiscal columns enter as the 3rd positional statCols arg (§3.3). cgl_bvarV5(..., statCols, ..., 'sur',0,'noc',1,'Ndraws',1000, 'Ndrawsdiscard',500) then imputes the macro holes via the Durbin–Koopman smoother conditioned on the observed financials. The completed panel is the posterior mean mean(Xcond,3).
  4. Converge. Compute max|Δstar| across blocs (excluding the COVID-NaN'd macro-star cells, which are MC noise; ROWFIN stays in the metric). Stop when it>1 && max|Δstar| < TOL.

This is a Jacobi sweep: all stars are built from the frozen start-of-iteration state, then all non-US blocs (or all blocs when US_ENDOGENOUS) are estimated independently in parallel — same fixed point as Gauss–Seidel, ~Ncores faster.

Algorithm (EM-style fixed point over the foreign components).

Input:  per-bloc panels {X_c}, trade weights W, COVID window 2020Q2..Q4, TOL, MAXIT
Output: per-bloc completed panels {X̃_c} + posterior draws (the 8-file contract)

  ── E-step seed ───────────────────────────────────────────────
  Estimate US with NO stars (cgl_bvarV5, COVID-macro-masked) → X̃_US⁰ = mean(Xcond,3)
  Seed each bloc's foreign stars S_c⁰ from the iteration-0 completed panels.

  ── EM loop (Jacobi) ──────────────────────────────────────────
  for it = 1 .. MAXIT:
    M-step / star build:   for every bloc c:               # frozen start-of-iter state
        S_c = buildstars(W, {X̃_p}_{p≠c})                   # ROWDEM/ROWINF/ROWFIN, 4·Δ(100·log),
                                                            #   per-quarter renormalised over present partners
    E-step / re-estimate:  parfor every bloc c:
        x_c = [domestic_c | S_c]                            # WN cols = statCols (3rd arg)
        NaN the 2020Q2..Q4 MACRO cells (isFinancial==false + ROWDEM + ROWINF) only;
            keep all financials + ROWFIN (incl. GLB_*) observed
        [Xcond, β_c, Σ_c, ...] = cgl_bvarV5(x_c, lags=3, statCols, nDraws, nBurn, 1, H, ...)
        X̃_c = mean(Xcond,3)                                 # DK-smoother posterior-mean completion
    convergence:  Δ = max_c |S_c^{it} − S_c^{it−1}|         # exclude COVID-NaN'd macro-star cells
    if it>1 and Δ < TOL: break                              # MC noise floor ≈ 0.13

  save /tmp/gvar24_workspace.mat   then   exportbloc(c) → /tmp/gvar24_export/<c>/ (8 files)

The E-step is the Durbin–Koopman data-augmentation completion of the foreign blocs under COVID-macro-masked / financials-observed missingness; the M-step is the Bayesian (GLP) re-estimation plus the trade-weighted star rebuild. Iterating to a fixed point in the completed stars is what couples the blocs at estimation time (distinct from the solve-time coupling x=Mx+d in §7).

docs.title: 5.2 Defaults & env knobs
docs.text:
In-script defaults (lags=3; H=20; nDraws=1000; nBurn=500; MAXIT=12; TOL=0.03;):

Env var Default Effect US_ENDOGENOUS unset (anchor) 1 ⇒ the US joins the loop with its own ROW*_US stars (requires a weights.US row). Set it for production. BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS all blocs in the weights file space-separated subset, US first (for fast validation) NDRAWS 1000 (burn auto = NDRAWS/2) MCMC draws per bloc MAXIT 12 max convergence sweeps; MAXIT=1 ⇒ single-country / separated (one-shot historical stars, no coupling) TOL 0.03 convergence tolerance on max|Δstar| (Monte-Carlo noise floor ≈ 0.13) GVAR_WEIGHTS_FILE weights_full.json, else weights_24bloc.json which weights/bloc list to use; the file's blocs field is authoritative (US first) 
# Production: full 33-bloc endogenous-US run (hours, not minutes)
US_ENDOGENOUS=1 matlab -batch "run('pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"

# Fast smoke test: 3 blocs, few draws, 1 sweep (separated, no coupling)
BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US EA JP" NDRAWS=200 MAXIT=1 US_ENDOGENOUS=1 \
  matlab -batch "run('pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"

Outputs. The full estimation state is saved to /tmp/gvar24_workspace.mat before export (so an export-only bug is a cheap re-export, not a multi-hour re-estimation), then each bloc's 8-file contract goes to /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/.

docs.title: 5.3 The 8-file per-bloc contract
docs.text:
Each export dir contains:

File Contents spec.json { n, variables:[{id, name, transformation('log'\|'lin'), isFinancial, prior, block}] } — block ∈ domestic/external/us/global/row/fiscal; prior ∈ RW (own-first-lag mean 1, default) / WN (own-first-lag mean 0, stationary — the 3 fiscal ratios FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/SFA, §3.3) companion_mean.json { betahat, sigmahat, n, lags } companion_draws.json { beta, beta_shape, sigma, sigma_shape, n_draws, n, lags } (posterior draws → Full uncertainty bands) baseline.json unconditional forecast in growth/display space (mean, q05, q16, q84, q95, horizon) baseline_levels.json same forecast in level space historical.json / historical_levels.json last ~40 quarters (growth / level) — observed data, not the DK counterfactual transformations.json per-variable 'log'/'lin'

Plus, where applicable: fiscal_meta.json { d0, sfaDefault, idx:{gdp,deflator,revenue,primaryExp,effRate,sfa} } (copied from pipeline/fiscal_meta/<CC>.json) and the GVAR weights_*.json.

docs.title: 6. Port (port_gvar_exports.py) — 6 significant figures
docs.text:
The deployed convention is 6 significant figures, compact JSON. port_gvar_exports.py copies the 8 contract files from /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/ to app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/, rounding every float to f"{x:.6g}" (ints / strings / 0 / NaN / ±inf pass through) and writing with separators=(",",":").

# Port every bloc present in the export dir
python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py

# Restrict to specific blocs, or dry-run (report sizes, no write)
GVAR_PORT_BLOCS="US EA JP" python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py
python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py --dry-run

# Then refresh the per-country fiscal meta (the app reads these directly)
for cc in US EA JP UK CA CN; do
  cp "pipeline/fiscal_meta/$cc.json" "app/public/data/bvar/$cc/fiscal_meta.json"
done

# MANDATORY: re-resolve the positional fiscal indices against the CURRENT specs.
node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs

Override the source dir with GVAR_EXPORT_DIR. The script prints per-bloc file count and the MB → MB size reduction.

🔴 port_gvar_exports.py does NOT regenerate fiscal_meta.json's positional indices. The 33-bloc refit inserts variables ahead of the fiscal block in every spec except US, so a verbatim port leaves the six idx offsets pointing at the wrong columns — the EA-effRate-lands-on-equity (~col 537) debt explosion. node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs re-resolves all six BY VARIABLE ID (FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/EFFI/SFA; gdp=0; deflator=first HICP|CPI|JGDP|PGDP|DEFL) from the deployed spec.json, preserves d0/sfaDefault, and is idempotent. The fiscal-meta-indices.test.ts canary blocks a deploy whose indices are stale (every fiscal bloc's derived debt path must stay bounded).

docs.title: 7. Coupling (precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs) — the pre-shipped inverse
docs.text:
The browser solves the cross-country fixed point x = (I−M)⁻¹ d from a pre-shipped coupling artifact. The in-browser M build (DK-smoother probes) is ~19 min at 24 blocs and worse at the augmented endogenous dimension, so pre-shipping is mandatory, not an optimisation.

app/scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs (run via npx vite-node):

  1. assembles the deployed bloc bundle exactly like the loader (a non-US bloc qualifies iff its spec.json has block:'row' vars; US is always included), self-configures the topology, and computes modelSig;
  2. builds M in JS (buildGVARCouplingM / buildGVARCouplingMEndo when US_ENDOGENOUS);
  3. inverts (I−M) via MATLAB BLAS (app/scripts/invert_coupling.m; the NumPy invert_coupling.py is the no-MATLAB fallback);
  4. writes the gzipped app/public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz ({ modelSig, pinStructure:'', dim, rhoSpectral, M, invImM }, 6-sig-fig). The plain .json is removed (and the loader still falls back to a plain .json if no .gz exists). At the current 33-bloc dim 2060 the artifact is ~31 MB gzip (the live file is 32,166,056 B) vs ~91 MB raw — already most of GitHub's 100 MB per-file limit, and the script projects ~200 MB raw at the full ~50-bloc panel, so gzip is mandatory. The loader decompresses it in-browser via the native DecompressionStream.
cd app
npx vite-node scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs

Keyed to the data by modelSig. The artifact MUST be regenerated whenever any deployed bloc changes — a stale modelSig is rejected and the app falls back to the slow path.

The augmented operator's spectral radius grows with the bloc count (it must stay < 1 for a stable fixed point):

Topology dim ρ(M) 6-bloc — 0.59 24-bloc (frozen anchor) — 0.726 33-bloc (endogenous US) 2060 0.924041 (live coupling_default.json.gz)

The exact live rhoSpectral moves slightly with each refit (the shipped artifact reads 0.924041 at dim 2060; earlier artifacts read ~0.89). What matters is that it stays comfortably < 1. The artifact's modelSig (per-bloc n_lags_H_<betahat-fingerprint>) keys it to the exact data; US shows 41 confirming the endogenous-US augmentation.

docs.title: 8. Test & deploy
docs.text:
cd app
npx tsc --noEmit                       # type-check
npm test                               # FAST compute suite (excludes the heavy solver canaries)
npm run test:heavy                     # HEAVY solver/perf canaries (1 fork) — BOTH halves gate deploy
BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario npm run build # static export (next build, output:'export')

Tests live in app/src/__tests__/compute/. The suite is split: npm test is vitest run minus seven heavy --exclude globs (gvar-us-endogenous, gvar-direct-channel, gvar-band-solve, gvar-closed-form-*, gvar-band-closedform-*, affine-blockernels-parity, gvar-kernel-affine-independence); npm run test:heavy runs exactly those, serialised to one fork; npm run test:all sweeps everything (see BUILD_AND_CI.md). Canary tests block deploy (all in the fast suite):

  • scenario-scale-convention — the scale conventions (100·log, 4·Δ, q/q annualised).
  • gvar-convergence — Gauss–Seidel ≡ closed-form fixed point.
  • gvar-coupling-artifact — the shipped .gz matches a live rebuild (and its modelSig).
  • fiscal-meta-indices — the STALE-POSITIONAL-INDEX guard (run node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs after every re-port; §6).

Feature tests cover fiscal*, gvar-us-endogenous, longRunTilt, conditioning-*, the drag-perf (gvar-drag-*), the render memoization (chartgrid-render-memo), and the optimistic dot (conditioned-dot-optimistic, right-click-plant-at-cursor).

Deploy is a push to the bvarscenario remote's main (the BVARScenarioTool repo, which holds the workflow .github/workflows/deploy-to-website.yml):

git push bvarscenario main   # ⚠️ this IS the deploy — only after a green gate, and with confirmation

The Action triggers on a push to main that touches app/** or netlify.toml (so a data-only change under app/public/data/** still deploys). It runs npm ci && npm run build with BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario, checks out the matyasfarkas/starter-hugo-academic website repo (token WEBSITE_DEPLOY_TOKEN), then rm -rf website/static/bvar-scenario/* and cp -r app/out/* website/static/bvar-scenario/, and commits/pushes the website repo. The starter-hugo-academic site is a Hugo Academic site published to GitHub Pages (note the netlify.toml trigger), which rebuilds and serves it at the /bvar-scenario path. This workflow has no Hugo step; any Hugo version pin (0.122.0 — 0.123+ removed getCSV and broke the theme) lives in the starter-hugo-academic repo, not in this workflow.

Never push without explicit per-action confirmation (deploy = irreversible public change). See REESTIMATING_MODELS.md §5 for the full deploy gate.

docs.title: 9. End-to-end (copy-paste skeleton)
docs.text:
# from the repo root
# 1. (optional) refresh raw data
FRED_API_KEY=<key> python3 pipeline/build_us_auto.py
python3 pipeline/build_fiscal_us_4var.py            # exact-residual SFA, COVID-NaN (per fiscal bloc)

# 1b. (re)apply the coherence/prior tags — all three are idempotent no-ops if already done
python3 pipeline/tag_fiscal_wn.py                   # WN on FREV_GDP/FPEXP_GDP/SFA, RW elsewhere (§3.3)
python3 pipeline/tag_global_financial.py            # isFinancial:1 on GLB_USEQ + GLB_OIL (§3.4)
python3 pipeline/build_ca_ip_level.py               # CA only: IP_CA -> log level (§3.5)

# 2. estimate the 33-bloc coupled GVAR (hours — validate on a subset first)
US_ENDOGENOUS=1 matlab -batch "run('pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"

# 3. port (6 sig-figs) + refresh fiscal meta
python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py
for cc in US EA JP UK CA CN; do cp "pipeline/fiscal_meta/$cc.json" "app/public/data/bvar/$cc/fiscal_meta.json"; done
node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs               # MANDATORY: porter does NOT regen positional indices (§6/§10)

# 4. regenerate the coupling artifact
cd app && npx vite-node scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs

# 5. green gate (BOTH halves)
npx tsc --noEmit && npm test && npm run test:heavy && BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario npm run build
cd ..

# 6. deploy (ONLY after the gate is green AND confirmed)
git add app/public/data/bvar pipeline/fiscal_meta pipeline/country_rawdata
git commit -m "Re-estimate GVAR + fiscal block (data through <quarter>)"
git push bvarscenario main

For the fully-annotated runbook with the verification gate, see REESTIMATING_MODELS.md.

docs.title: 10. Targeted single-bloc re-estimation
docs.text:
Two distinct "re-estimate one bloc" patterns exist; picking the wrong one is the most common maintenance mistake. The rule is set by whether the bloc is in the coupled GVAR. See the decision tree in MAINTAINER_OPERATIONS.md §0; this is the mechanism behind it.

The bloc is… Pattern Re-estimates Coupling regen? a euro-area FSAP standalone (AT BE DE EE ES FI FR GR IE IT LT LU LV NL PT SI SK) truly single-bloc — it is not in the coupled system (no weights row, no block:'row' vars) only that bloc no a coupled GVAR bloc (US/EA/any RoW) full GVAR re-estimate — its stars feed every other bloc, so the fixed point moves for everyone all 33 yes

docs.title: 10.1 The euro standalone — genuinely isolated
docs.text:
A euro member is a self-contained FSAP two-country BVAR (domestic + exogenous US block + 4 globals), estimated by export_bvar_euro_fsap.m. It carries no coupling, so re-estimating it touches nothing else:

# build/edit its DataRaw, then estimate just that member (subset via BVAR_EURO_MEMBERS)
BVAR_EURO_MEMBERS="DE" matlab -batch "run('pipeline/export_bvar_euro_fsap.m')"   # → /tmp/euro_fsap_export/DE/
GVAR_EXPORT_DIR=/tmp/euro_fsap_export GVAR_PORT_BLOCS="DE" python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py
# NO coupling regen (not in the GVAR); then the green gate (§8)

docs.title: 10.2 The coupled bloc — there is no "just one bloc" path
docs.text:
Because each coupled bloc's ROW* stars are trade-weighted aggregates of every partner's completed panel, changing one coupled bloc's data perturbs the EM fixed point for all of them. The honest path is the full loop. But you can iterate fast on a subset using BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS (US first), which is the validate-then-commit pattern: confirm a data/coherence fix is correct on a 2–3-bloc smoke run before launching the multi-hour full refit. This is exactly how the §3.5 CA-IP fix and the §3.3/§3.4 tags were landed:

# (a) FAST validation subset (separated, no coupling) — confirm the fix is sane
BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US CA" NDRAWS=200 MAXIT=1 US_ENDOGENOUS=1 \
  matlab -batch "run('pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"
GVAR_PORT_BLOCS="US CA" python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py   # inspect /tmp/gvar24_export first

# (b) once happy, the FULL coupled refit (the only deploy-grade path for a coupled change)
US_ENDOGENOUS=1 matlab -batch "run('pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"
python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py
cd app && npx vite-node scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs    # MANDATORY: stars changed for everyone

The intermediate commits 6f0161a (WN plumbing) → 9839a46 (CA-only targeted re-estimate, the validate step) → 22250be → e38673a (the self-consistent full 33-bloc refit folding in all three fixes) are this pattern in the git history. A MAXIT=1 subset run is not deploy-grade for a coupled bloc — it freezes the stars at their historical (uncoupled) values; only the full MAXIT=12 loop reaches the cross-country fixed point. After any coupled change you must regenerate coupling_default.json.gz (§7) — a stale modelSig is rejected and the app silently falls back to the slow path. See MAINTAINER_OPERATIONS.md §C–§D for the per-bloc checks (n == spec == companion, convergence vs the MC floor, fiscal closure) and §E for the green gate.

docs.title: 11. Empirical evaluation (out-of-sample horse-race + spillover)
docs.text:
A separate repo — em-bayesian-gvar-eval (docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md) — evaluates the platform's forecasts against benchmarks. It consumes the same estimator and conventions documented here; this section is the cross-link, not a duplicate.

Design (final). Two cores:

  1. Out-of-sample forecast horse-race. Recursive expanding-window, origins 2010Q1–2024Q4 (drop the 2020 COVID origins for the headline), targets {real GDP growth, inflation, short rate} for US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN (full-panel = all 33 blocs in the appendix), horizons h ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8}.
  2. Proposed: the EM-Bayesian GVAR — endogenous-US (emgvar_endo, US_ENDOGENOUS=1) and the legacy frozen-anchor (emgvar_frozen).
  3. Benchmarks: Random Walk (driftless + a sample-mean "climatology" anchor), AR(p) (AIC-selected, iterated + direct), single-country Bayesian VAR (the coupling ablation = strip the ROW* columns, MAXIT=1), textbook OLS Pesaran–Schuermann–Weiner / Dees GVAR (cgvar_a), single-pass Bayesian GVAR (cgvar_b, MAXIT=1 — the EM ablation), and a Bayesian DFM.
  4. Leak-free by construction. Panels truncated ≤ t0 before transformData and before any star is built; rolling as-of-t0 trade weights (IMTS bilateral trade with endPeriod ≤ year(t0)) — the shipped 2021–2023 weights_full.json is forbidden in the sweep (it would inject future trade structure). COVID-as-missing is reapplied in-sample at every origin where 2020Q2–Q4 ≤ t0.

  5. Scoring: CRPS (primary density metric) + parametric log-score + PIT/coverage, scored from the per-draw predictive cloud Dforecast(1:8,:,:). Significance via Giacomini–White (headline — valid under nesting and correct for the expanding scheme), Clark–West (nested point), Diebold–Mariano (non-nested), with Romano–Wolf + the Model Confidence Set for multiplicity.

  6. Endogenous-US spillover analysis. The contribution the endogenous-US GVAR enables and the frozen-anchor cannot: a US shock that propagates abroad and feeds back into the US. Tier A (directly solver-computable conditional scenario propagation via solveGVARClosedFormEndo / solveGVARLinear in app/src/lib/compute/gvarSolve.ts) ships first; Tier B (GIRF/FEVD/connectedness) is a new module built from the exported companion_mean.json (betahat=Γ, sigmahat=Σ) + the coupling M — not a call into the current solver (which computes conditional-mean deltas, not GIRFs).

Headline result (first full pass — results/tables.{txt,json}, 56 origins, 9 models; DESIGN final). AR(p)/DFM have the lowest point CRPS, but the 90% Model Confidence Set carries the value-add: emgvar_endo is in the inflation MCS at all horizons and the short-rate MCS at h=1,2,4 (out of the growth MCS at h=1 & h=8), emgvar_endo ⪰ emgvar_frozen, and it decisively beats the textbook OLS classical GVAR (cgvar_a removed from most sets). Best inflation h=1 = emgvar_endo (relRMSE 0.799). Honest caveats: the draw-based densities are over-confident (PIT cov90 ~0.73–0.83 vs 0.90) and the parametric log-score column is numerically broken (CRPS + MCS are the robust primaries). The Tier-A US +100 bp result shows non-trivial endogenous-only feedback the frozen anchor omits, but the magnitudes are large/sign-flipping and it is a multi-period conditional projection, NOT a causal IRF — validate before headlining. Do not cite the log-score numbers.

See em-bayesian-gvar-eval/docs/EMPIRICAL_PROTOCOL.md for the full leak-free driver design (oos_driver.m), the per-origin forecast JSON schema, the compute plan, and the look-ahead invariants.

docs.title: How to update / re-estimate the models
docs.text:
This is the operational runbook for re-estimating the GVAR macro blocs and rebuilding the fiscal block, then porting, verifying, and deploying. It documents the current state of the platform: an endogenous-US, 33-bloc GVAR estimated by an iterative GVAR-EM loop, plus a 4-variable fiscal block (with an exact-residual SFA) for the six fiscal economies.

Scope. This covers re-estimation and data refresh of the existing blocs. To add a brand-new country or RoW bloc, see ADDING_A_COUNTRY.md (this runbook reuses the same port / verify / deploy gate). For the live methodology, see ARCHITECTURE_FOR_AI.md and fiscal-block-methodology.md.

The deployed tool is live at the /bvar-scenario path of the academic website, hosted on GitHub Pages (the matyasfarkas/starter-hugo-academic Hugo Academic site).

Golden rules. Exact MATLAB replication when porting (no shortcuts). Use real observed data, never synthetic placeholders. Never deploy without a green gate. Do not hand-edit the GVAR topology (IDX/W/ROW_COUNTRIES in gvarSolve.ts) — it self-configures from the deployed specs + weights.

docs.title: 0. The pieces, at a glance
docs.text:
Layer Estimator / builder Raw input Output GVAR macro (33 blocs, US endogenous) pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m (GVAR-EM, COVID-as-missing) pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/*.json Fiscal block (US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN) pipeline/build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json + pipeline/fiscal_raw/* + pipeline/fiscal_meta/<CC>.json edits the glob's SFA column + fiscal_meta/<CC>.json Port to app pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py (6 sig-figs) /tmp/gvar24_export/ app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/*.json GVAR coupling artifact app/scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs (+ invert_coupling.py/.m) deployed app/public/data/bvar/** app/public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz Fiscal meta (app) copied from pipeline/fiscal_meta/<CC>.json — app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/fiscal_meta.json

Bloc set is data-driven from app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_24bloc.json (blocs field, US first; the 24 in the filename is historical — it currently lists 33 blocs). The US is the first entry and has a weights.US row, which is what makes it endogenous.

docs.title: 1. Re-estimate the GVAR macro blocs (GVAR-EM loop)
1.1 What the loop does
docs.text:
pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m is the canonical GVAR-EM estimator:

  1. For each non-US bloc, build its 3 RoW "star" vars (ROWDEM/ROWINF/ROWFIN) = trade-weighted averages of partners' real-GDP growth / inflation / short rate, renormalised over the partners present each quarter (unbalanced panel). COVID + no-partner quarters start NaN.
  2. Estimate each bloc by cgl_bvarV5([domestic | 3 stars]) — the COVID-as-missing Gibbs sampler; the DK smoother fills the missing star + COVID quarters → a completed matrix per bloc.
  3. Loop (Jacobi): recompute every bloc's stars from partners' completed data, re-estimate all blocs in parallel (parfor), until the foreign components converge (max |Δstar| < TOL).

Key facts encoded in the script (do not change without re-validating):

  • US_ENDOGENOUS=1 (env). With a weights.US row present, the US is folded into the convergence loop and re-estimated with its own 3 RoW stars (n=41: domestic + 3 ROW*_US). It drives RoW and receives RoW→US feedback. A no-stars bootstrap still runs first only to seed partners' iter-1 stars (it is discarded, not exported).
  • COVID window is the argmin of ΔGDP restricted to 2019Q4–2021Q1 (a global argmin misfires, e.g. KR's 2008Q4 GFC drop), then ± quarters around it are NaN'd → DK-imputed.
  • MAXIT=12, TOL=0.03 (MC noise floor ≈0.13). MAXIT=1 ⇒ single-country (separated): one-shot historical stars, no coupling iteration.
  • Scale traps (already correct in-script, listed so you don't "fix" them): stars are 4·Δ(100·log) not 400·; the completed panel is mean(Xcond,3); CN GDP is real (since the 2026-06 CN-only re-estimate; NOMINAL_GDP_BLOCS empty) → read directly, not deflated by subtraction; leading star NaN is back-filled; the _US column is dropped (no-op on _glob inputs); the short rate is optional (CH/NO have none → excluded from partners' ROWFIN); AR hyperinflation excluded from partners' INF/FIN (kept in DEM).

docs.title: 1.2 Run it
docs.text:
From the repo root (MATLAB with the Parallel Computing Toolbox; the loop uses parfor):

# Full 33-bloc endogenous-US run (the production estimate). Hours, not minutes.
US_ENDOGENOUS=1 matlab -batch "run('pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"

Useful env overrides (for fast subset validation before committing the multi-hour full run):

# Fast smoke: 3 blocs, few draws, 1 iteration (single-country, no coupling)
BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS="US EA JP" NDRAWS=200 MAXIT=1 US_ENDOGENOUS=1 \
  matlab -batch "run('pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"
Env Default Meaning US_ENDOGENOUS unset (anchor) 1 ⇒ US is a coupled bloc (requires weights.US). Set it for production. BVAR_GVAR_BLOCS all blocs in weights file space-separated subset, US first NDRAWS 1000 (burn = 500) MCMC draws per bloc MAXIT 12 max convergence sweeps (1 = separated) TOL 0.03 convergence tol on max |Δstar| GVAR_WEIGHTS_FILE weights_full.json else weights_24bloc.json which weights/bloc list to use

Outputs. Each bloc's full deployed JSON contract to /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/ (spec, companion_mean, companion_draws, historical, historical_levels, baseline, baseline_levels, transformations). The full estimation state is also saved to /tmp/gvar24_workspace.mat before export, so an export-only bug is a cheap re-export, not a re-estimation.

Watch the console. Each iteration prints max star change vs tol; convergence prints CONVERGED. Each export prints n, draws, and the 1-quarter GDP growth (GDP1q) — confirm it is a sane number, not NaN or a collapsed flat line.

If a bloc returns a singular / NaN SIGMA, the data is the cause (a leading NaN in a discontinued series, or near-collinear national-accounts identities). Fix the raw DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json, not the estimator. With COVID imputed the empirical-Bayes lambda optimum is well-behaved (~0.24–0.56); a fixed lamfix is not needed (and pinning one with noc/sur both fixed makes lambda degenerate to NaN).

docs.title: 2. Rebuild the fiscal block (4-variable, exact-residual SFA)
docs.text:
The fiscal block carries 4 estimated BVAR variables for US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN:

ID Meaning Units FREV_GDP revenue / GDP × 100 % of GDP (annual) FPEXP_GDP primary expenditure / GDP × 100 % of GDP (annual) EFFI effective interest rate = net interest / lagged gross debt × 100, annualised % p.a. SFA stock-flow adjustment % of GDP (annual)

SFA is the exact residual of the app's debt recursion. The build script rearranges the exact recursion in app/src/lib/compute/fiscalAccounting.ts (computeFiscalBlock, ~line 169) onto the observed gross-debt/GDP path so that the BVAR's SFA column closes the recursion to machine precision (it captures the federal-flows-vs-gross-debt perimeter seam / intragovernmental holdings — the deficit–debt discrepancy):

d_t = d_{t-1}·(1 + EFFI/400)/(1 + gNomQ/100) − pb/4 + sfa/4        (% of GDP)
pb     = FREV_GDP − FPEXP_GDP
gNomQ  = 100·log(realGDP_t/realGDP_{t-1}) + 100·log(deflator_t/deflator_{t-1})   (% per quarter)
