⇒ SFA_t = 4·(d_obs_t − d_{t-1} − snowball_t + pb_t/4)

The crucial subtlety: gNomQ is read off the same glob columns the app reads (idx.gdp/idx.deflator, stored as 100·log), as a log growth — not a simple nominal SAAR growth. Building it the app's way is what drives the closure error to < 1e-6 (an interpolated COVID value can never equal the residual, so COVID quarters are set NaN for the Gibbs to impute).

fiscal_meta/<CC>.json carries idx = {gdp, deflator, revenue, primaryExp, effRate, sfa} (note: there is no debtService — DSVC was reverted to EFFI), d0, and sfaDefault. The debt/GDP projection now carries the forecast SFA forward (idx.sfa set ⇒ the recursion reads the estimated SFA level path with its own dynamics + bands, instead of defaulting SFA to 0).

docs.title: 2.1 Run the builds
docs.text:
One script per fiscal economy. They are data-build only (no estimation, no network): each edits only the SFA column of DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json in place (everything else byte-identical), sets the COVID window to NaN, runs the closure check, and writes sfaDefault (the non-COVID, ex-t0 SFA mean) into fiscal_meta/<CC>.json.

cd "<repo root>/pipeline"
python3 build_fiscal_us_4var.py
python3 build_fiscal_ea_4var.py
python3 build_fiscal_jp_4var.py
python3 build_fiscal_uk_4var.py
python3 build_fiscal_ca_4var.py
python3 build_fiscal_cn_4var.py

The gate is automatic and hard: each script asserts the one-step closure |d_obs_t − recon_t| < 1e-6 for every non-COVID quarter t≥1 and aborts on failure. It prints backcastMaxErrPP (must be ~1e-13), the SFA range, and the chosen sfaDefault.

Current sfaDefault (≈ non-COVID SFA mean, % of GDP) — sanity benchmarks after a rebuild:

US EA JP UK CA CN sfaDefault 1.75 0.33 2.23 3.15 3.19 4.02

Order: rebuild the fiscal SFA before re-estimating the GVAR if the fiscal data changed — the SFA column lives in the same DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json the GVAR-EM loop reads. If only the macro data changed, the fiscal SFA still must be rebuilt so the residual stays exact against the new gdp/deflator levels.

docs.title: 2.2 (Optional) refresh the human-checkable walkthrough
docs.text:
A reviewer-facing Excel walkthrough of the US/UK fiscal accounting lives at docs/fiscal_accounting_walkthrough_US_UK.xlsx (the user's source is docs/Fiscal_Accounting_US_UK_NSA.xlsx). Regenerate it after a fiscal rebuild:

python3 pipeline/build_fiscal_walkthrough_xlsx.py

docs.title: 3. Port the exports into the app (33 blocs, 6 sig-figs)
docs.text:
# Ports EVERY bloc present in the export dir, compacting to 6 significant figures + compact JSON.
python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py

# Restrict to specific blocs, or dry-run to report sizes:
GVAR_PORT_BLOCS="US EA JP" python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py
python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py --dry-run

This copies /tmp/gvar24_export/<CC>/*.json → app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/*.json at the deployed 6-significant-figure precision.

docs.title: 3.1 Refresh the app fiscal_meta
docs.text:
The app reads fiscal_meta per country at app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/fiscal_meta.json. Copy the freshly built pipeline metas over:

for cc in US EA JP UK CA CN; do
  cp "pipeline/fiscal_meta/$cc.json" "app/public/data/bvar/$cc/fiscal_meta.json"
done

RULE — re-index the fiscal meta after porting (MANDATORY on any variable-order change). Run node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs. fiscal_meta.json indexes the fiscal drivers by column POSITION; any change to a bloc's variable order/count (the GVAR-EM refit inserts variables ahead of the fiscal block in every spec except US) silently re-points those indices at the WRONG columns — the cause of the recent EA/UK debt explosion (EA's effRate landed on the equity index ~col 537). The porter does not regenerate the meta. fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs re-resolves the indices by variable id from the deployed spec.json and rewrites all six fiscal metas, preserving d0/sfaDefault; it is idempotent (prints ok when nothing moved). Run it after every port that touches a fiscal bloc — a stale index can pass tsc/build and still ship a wrong debt path.

node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs   # re-resolves idx{gdp,deflator,revenue,primaryExp,effRate,sfa} by id

docs.title: 3.2 Regenerate the GVAR coupling artifact (MANDATORY after any bloc data change)
docs.text:
The browser solves the cross-country fixed point x = (I−M)⁻¹ d from a pre-shipped coupling artifact. At 33 blocs the in-browser inverse is ~19 min — pre-shipping is mandatory, not an optimisation. With the endogenous US the artifact is the augmented (stars ++ US-global) operator: dim = 2060, ρ(M) ≈ 0.924 (< 1, stationary/solvable; verified rhoSpectral = 0.924041 in the shipped coupling_default.json.gz), keyed to the data by modelSig.

From app/ (the script builds M in JS, then inverts (I−M) via MATLAB BLAS — the invert_coupling.py NumPy path is the no-MATLAB fallback):

cd app
npx vite-node scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs

It writes the gzipped app/public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz directly (the plain .json is removed — at this bloc count it exceeds GitHub's 100 MB per-file limit; gzip ≈ 27–32 MB). The loader decompresses it via the browser's native DecompressionStream. The artifact is keyed to the data by modelSig, so it MUST be regenerated whenever any deployed bloc changes — a stale artifact (modelSig mismatch) is rejected and the app falls back to the slow path.

docs.title: 4. Verify (the green gate — do not skip)
4.1 Fast GVAR coupling / fixed-point gate
docs.text:
cd app
NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 npx tsx scripts/verify_gvar24.mjs

Uses the shipped artifact (no live rebuild). Checks: (1) artifact modelSig matches the deployed blocs (else STALE — re-run §3.2); (2) the shipped inverse really is the inverse (max|(I−M)·invImM − I| small); (3) ρ(M) < 1 and matches the stored value; (4) Gauss-Seidel ≡ artifact-seeded closed form for representative shocks.

docs.title: 4.2 Type-check, tests, build
docs.text:
cd app
npx tsc --noEmit
npx vitest run          # ALL must pass — watch the canaries
BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario npm run build

Canary / regression tests to watch (in app/src/__tests__/compute/):

  • Fiscal: fiscalAccounting.test.ts, fiscal-backcast.test.ts, fiscal-conditioning-integration.test.ts — the debt back-cast and the SFA-forward projection.
  • GVAR: gvar-us-endogenous.test.ts, gvar-coupling-artifact.test.ts (artifact ≡ live), gvar-closed-form-routing.test.ts, gvar-derived-topology.test.ts, gvar-direct-coupling-strip-equivalence.test.ts, gvar-convergence.test.ts, gvar-band-solve.test.ts.
  • Scale canary: scenario-scale-convention.test.ts — if n changed, its fixture may be pinned to the old layout; regenerate via pipeline/_verify/gt.m (resolves columns by variable id, so it is layout-robust) and re-run.

docs.title: 4.3 Back-cast / ground-truth canaries (MATLAB)
docs.text:
pipeline/_verify/gt.m recomputes the canonical conditional-forecast ground truth directly from the deployed app/public/data/bvar/US/*.json via conforekf.m; compare against the app's conditional output to confirm the scale conventions still hold after re-estimation.

matlab -batch "run('pipeline/_verify/gt.m')"

Done = tsc clean, all vitest green, build succeeds, verify_gvar24 all PASS.

docs.title: 5. Deploy
docs.text:
The deploy target is the bvarscenario remote (matyasfarkas/BVARScenarioTool). A push to its main triggers a GitHub Action (.github/workflows/deploy-to-website.yml, on push to main touching app/**) that runs npm ci && npm run build (with BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario), then copies app/out/* into the matyasfarkas/starter-hugo-academic website repo's static/bvar-scenario/. That repo is a Hugo Academic site published to GitHub Pages (note the netlify.toml trigger), which rebuilds and serves it at the /bvar-scenario path. (This workflow has no Hugo step itself; it only copies app/out.)

Do not push without explicit confirmation. Pushing to bvarscenario main is a deploy. Confirm the gate is green first.

cd "<repo root>"
git add app/public/data/bvar pipeline/fiscal_meta pipeline/country_rawdata \
        app/public/data/bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz
git commit -m "Re-estimate GVAR + fiscal block (data through <quarter>)"
git push bvarscenario main          # Action copies app/out → starter-hugo-academic → Hugo rebuilds & serves on GitHub Pages

Hugo is pinned to 0.122.0 in the starter-hugo-academic repo (0.123+ removed getCSV and broke the theme — do not bump it); that pin lives in the website repo, not in this workflow.

Verify live. Open the /bvar-scenario path of the academic site (GitHub Pages) and confirm a US baseline, a quick conditioning scenario, and the fiscal debt/GDP projection all look sane.

docs.title: 6. End-to-end (copy-paste skeleton)
docs.text:
# from <repo root>
# 1. GVAR-EM re-estimation (hours) — validate on a subset first
US_ENDOGENOUS=1 matlab -batch "run('pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m')"

# 2. Fiscal SFA = exact residual (auto closure gate < 1e-6 each)
cd pipeline
for cc in us ea jp uk ca cn; do python3 "build_fiscal_${cc}_4var.py"; done
cd ..

# 3. Port (6 sig-figs) + refresh fiscal_meta + RE-INDEX fiscal meta + regen coupling
python3 pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py
for cc in US EA JP UK CA CN; do cp "pipeline/fiscal_meta/$cc.json" "app/public/data/bvar/$cc/fiscal_meta.json"; done
node pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs   # MANDATORY: re-resolve fiscal idx by id (stale-index guard)
cd app && npx vite-node scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs

# 4. Gate
NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 npx tsx scripts/verify_gvar24.mjs
npx tsc --noEmit && npx vitest run && BASE_PATH=/bvar-scenario npm run build
cd ..

# 5. Deploy (ONLY after the gate is green and confirmed)
git add app/public/data/bvar pipeline/fiscal_meta pipeline/country_rawdata
git commit -m "Re-estimate GVAR + fiscal block (data through <quarter>)"
git push bvarscenario main

docs.title: Reference — what re-estimation touches
docs.text: Concern File(s) GVAR-EM estimator pipeline/export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m (US_ENDOGENOUS, MAXIT, TOL, COVID-as-missing via cgl_bvarV5) GVAR raw inputs pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>_glob.json (domestic + 4 GLB_* globals) Trade weights / bloc list app/public/data/bvar/gvar/weights_24bloc.json (33 blocs, US first; name historical), pipeline/build_trade_weights.py Fiscal builds pipeline/build_fiscal_<cc>_4var.py (us/ea/jp/uk/ca/cn) Fiscal meta (pipeline) pipeline/fiscal_meta/<CC>.json (idx{gdp,deflator,revenue,primaryExp,effRate,sfa}, d0, sfaDefault) Fiscal raw / grids pipeline/fiscal_raw/* (observed debt/GDP grids) Debt recursion (app) app/src/lib/compute/fiscalAccounting.ts (computeFiscalBlock) — the SFA closure target Port pipeline/port_gvar_exports.py → app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/*.json Fiscal meta (app) app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/fiscal_meta.json Fiscal meta re-index (RULE) pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs — re-resolve idx by variable id; run after ANY port/variable-order change (§3.1) Coupling regen app/scripts/precompute_gvar_coupling.mjs + app/scripts/invert_coupling.{py,m} → coupling_default.json.gz (dim 2060, ρ≈0.924) Topology (auto, no edit) app/src/lib/compute/gvarSolve.ts (self-configures from spec + weights; US_ENDOGENOUS derived) Verification app/scripts/verify_gvar24.mjs, app/src/__tests__/compute/ (fiscal + GVAR canaries), pipeline/_verify/gt.m Deploy git push bvarscenario main → .github/workflows/deploy-to-website.yml copies app/out → starter-hugo-academic → Hugo rebuilds & serves on GitHub Pages

docs.title: Monetary-Financial Model — Quick Start
docs.text:
This tool shows you quarterly forecasts for about fifty economies — growth, inflation, interest rates, public finances, and more — and lets you bend them. Click on a chart where you think inflation will be, and every other chart updates so the whole forecast stays consistent with the estimated economics. Everything runs in your browser; nothing to install.

This page is the five-minute walk-through. The complete manual is the User Manual (PDF): Part I, "Using the tool", covers every feature in the same style as this page — what you do, how you do it, what you find — and Part II has the full methodology. When this page and the manual disagree, trust the manual.

docs.title: Three rules that govern everything you see
docs.text:
1. Data are data. Solid history is observed. Where a country's latest quarter has not been published yet, the model fills the gap from everything already observed elsewhere — those filled-in points are always drawn as an amber dashed tail, never disguised as data.
  2. The model is estimated once, then frozen. New data releases shift the starting point of the forecast within the year but never silently change the model. Re-estimation happens once a year, on a complete final year of data. The Monitor page (top bar) shows what each data release did to the forecasts.
  3. Judgment is visible. If you move a forecast away from what the model says — a scenario, a long-run target, a policy counterfactual — the chart says so.

docs.title: Your first scenario in five minutes
docs.text:
1. Open the tool and pick a country. The Country dropdown in the top bar reads United States by default. The GVAR group in that dropdown lists the economies linked into the cross-country system; everything under Other countries moves on its own. Leave Units on q/q ann. and Uncertainty on None.

2. Read one chart before touching anything. On the Inflation chart: history sits on a lightly shaded background (solid line = observed data); the forecast is the dotted line labelled Baseline, wrapped in the model's own faint grey fan. The first forecast quarter is labelled NC — short for nowcast, the model's estimate of the current, not-yet-published quarter. If a series' latest quarters are amber and dashed, the model filled them in — they are flagged, not data.

3. Bend the policy rate. On the Policy Rate chart, click the small condition button in its header — it turns blue and reads CONDITIONED. Press the mouse down on the forecast part of the chart about four quarters in, drag half a percentage point above the dotted baseline, release. (Shortcut: right-clicking an unconditioned chart conditions it and plants the point where you clicked, in one go.) A dot marks the quarter you pinned; only that quarter is imposed — the model fills in the most plausible route through your point.

4. Watch the rest of the economy respond. Scroll the grid: every chart has updated, not just the one you touched. With a tighter rate path, growth typically cools and inflation eases with a lag. Read it as "what usually accompanies a higher rate," not "what the rate causes" — for true cause-and-effect, use the policy counterfactual (manual Part I, §7). Other countries have not moved: cross-border spillovers are off until you switch them on (§5).

5. Put uncertainty bands around it. In the top bar set Uncertainty to Quick and press Compute. A shaded band in the scenario's colour wraps the solid line on every chart — the middle two-thirds of plausible outcomes. It pinches shut at the quarter you dragged: you imposed that value, so there is no uncertainty about it there. Full does the same with 200 draws.

6. Clean up. In the left control panel, click Reset at the top of the Scenario section (clears the active scenario for the current country only), and set Uncertainty back to None. You are back at the model's own view.

docs.title: Where to go next — manual Part I
docs.text:
Section What it covers §2 Quick start The walk-through above, in full detail §3 A tour of the screen Every control, top bar to bottom bar §4 Building scenarios Several variables and quarters at once, per-quarter editing, cookbook recipes, save/load §5 Cross-country spillovers Switch on transmission and let a US scenario reach the euro area, Japan, and China §6 Long-run targets Set where a variable settles in the long run (per-variable vs joint solve) §7 Policy counterfactuals The true policy experiment: what a different rate path would have caused §8 Public finances and external accounts Revenue, spending, deficits, debt, and the NFA satellite §9 Uncertainty bands What the bands mean, Quick vs Full, what they leave out §10 The data behind the charts Sources, vintages, the amber nowcast tails, the Monitor page, exports

Part II of the same PDF documents the machinery — BVAR estimation, the state space and Kalman smoother, the GVAR coupling, the fiscal accounting, long-run targets, data, and reproducibility. You do not need it to use the tool.

docs.title: 2026Q1 Data-Refresh Feasibility (no re-estimation)
docs.text: Date of check: 2026-06-22 · Branch: improve-live · Mode: READ-ONLY availability probe (no data/code edited, nothing committed).

docs.title: Goal recap
docs.text: Extend the OBSERVED panel by one quarter (2025Q4 → 2026Q1) and let the forecast re-anchor from the new last-observed state. The model companion (companion_mean / companion_draws) stays fixed — same post-estimation philosophy as the δc long-run sliders. This note only answers: is 2026Q1 published yet, source-by-source, economy-by-economy?

docs.title: What the current panel looks like
docs.text:
• Estimated/shipped BVAR panels (app/public/data/bvar/<C>/historical.json) end at 2025Q4 (n_periods=40, US). Dates aren't stored in the shipped matrices; anchor comes from build_gvar_2025q4.py (common 2025Q4 endpoint).
  • Committed raw JSONs (pipeline/country_rawdata/DataRaw_<CC>.json) are already ragged: CA & JP at 2026Q1, EA/UK/BR/MX/ZA at 2025Q4, CN & IN only at 2025Q2 (these EMs lag and are back-filled to 2025Q4 via WEO-annual + Denton disaggregation in build_gvar_2025q4.py — i.e. already partly synthetic at quarterly freq).

docs.title: Sources & access (verified live)
docs.text:
• FRED — key 8ce324fcfb4663bfef63d3d44fbdbc73 (hardcoded in pipeline/build_country_data.py:19, build_fiscal_us_gg.py, build_credit_*.py, etc.). All US series + EA HICP + several EM real-GDP series resolve here.
  • Eurostat SDMX 2.1 (build_oecd_eurostat.py): namq_10_gdp real GDP, prc_hicp_midx HICP, etc. — used for EU/EFTA OECD members' real GDP.
  • Note: EA-aggregate HICP is sourced from FRED (CP0000EZ19M086NEST), not the Eurostat HICP endpoint (build_ea_eurostat.py:27).

docs.title: Live query results (latest available obs as of 2026-06-22)
docs.text:
US (FRED) — 2026Q1 fully published: | Series | FRED ID | Latest | 2026Q1? | |---|---|---|---| | Real GDP | GDPC1 | 2026Q1 (upd 2026-05-28) | ✅ | | GDP deflator | GDPDEF | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | PCE price index | PCEPI | 2026Q1 (2026Q2 still .) | ✅ | | Fed funds | FEDFUNDS | 2026-05 (monthly) | ✅ | | Unemployment | UNRATE | 2026-05 (monthly) | ✅ | | Fiscal revenue | W066RC1Q027SBEA | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | Fiscal expenditure | W068RCQ027SBEA | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | Fiscal interest | A180RC1Q027SBEA | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | Debt %GDP | GFDEGDQ188S | 2026Q1 | ✅ |

Real GDP, other economies: | Economy | Source | Latest | 2026Q1? | |---|---|---|---| | EA20 / DE / FR / IT / ES / NL | Eurostat namq_10_gdp | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | UK | FRED NGDPRSAXDCGBQ | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | JP | FRED JPNRGDPEXP | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | CA | FRED NGDPRSAXDCCAQ | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | BR | FRED NGDPRSAXDCBRQ | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | MX | FRED NGDPRSAXDCMXQ | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | ZA | FRED NGDPRSAXDCZAQ | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | CN | mixed (nominal GDP/IP, BIS-on-FRED) | raw json ends 2025Q2 | ⚠️ lags | | IN | mixed (OECD/FRED real GDP) | raw json ends 2025Q2 | ⚠️ lags |

Prices / rates (timely): | Series | Source | Latest | 2026Q1? | |---|---|---|---| | EA HICP | FRED CP0000EZ19M086NEST | 2026-05 (upd 2026-06-17) | ✅ | | EA HICP (Eurostat direct) | prc_hicp_midx / prc_hicp_manr | 2025-12 (API not refreshed) | ❌ for this endpoint | | US PCE / deflator | FRED | 2026Q1 | ✅ | | Policy/short rates | FRED, ECB | through 2026-05 | ✅ |

docs.title: Readiness matrix (source × economy)
docs.text:
Legend: ✅ 2026Q1 ready · ⚠️ partial/lags · ❌ not available today

Economy Real GDP Prices (CPI/HICP/defl) Rates Fiscal Refresh to 2026Q1? US ✅ FRED ✅ FRED ✅ FRED ✅ FRED YES EA ✅ Eurostat ✅ FRED (HICP) ✅ n/a here YES DE / FR / IT / ES / NL ✅ Eurostat via FRED EA-HICP mirror ✅ ✅ — YES (GDP), verify member-CPI source per build UK ✅ FRED per UK build (ONS/FRED) — CPI timely ✅ ✅ YES (likely) — confirm UK CPI build series JP ✅ FRED per JP build — CPI timely ✅ ✅ YES (likely) CA ✅ FRED per CA build — CPI timely ✅ ✅ YES (likely) BR / MX / ZA ✅ FRED per EM build — CPI usually timely ✅ — YES (GDP) CN ⚠️ raw 2025Q2 ⚠️ ⚠️ — NO clean 2026Q1 (lags; WEO back-fill only) IN ⚠️ raw 2025Q2 ⚠️ ⚠️ — NO clean 2026Q1 (lags; WEO back-fill only)

docs.title: Caveats found
docs.text:
1. Eurostat HICP endpoint is stale in the direct API (ends 2025-12) — but it's a non-issue: EA HICP is pulled from FRED (CP0000EZ19M086NEST, current to 2026-05). For euro-member CPI inputs, confirm each member build's price source isn't the stale Eurostat HICP path.
  2. Discontinued OECD-on-FRED CPI mirrors: GBRCPIALLMINMEI, CPALTT01CAM659N both end 2025-03 (discontinued) — these are NOT the pipeline's UK/CA price sources, but a refresh script must avoid them.
  3. CN / IN are the structural laggards: real source data ends 2025Q2; the panel only reaches 2025Q4 via WEO-annual disaggregation. A genuine 2026Q1 observed point is not available today; refreshing them would mean another round of back-fill, not real data.
  4. FREV / FPEXP are internal variable names, not FRED IDs — the real FRED fiscal IDs are W066…/W068…/A180…/GFDEGDQ188S (all ✅).
  5. Rates/CPI are monthly and already run into 2026-04/05; quarterly aggregation for 2026Q1 (Jan–Mar) is complete.

docs.title: Bottom line — realistic 'as-of' for a refresh TODAY
docs.text:
A clean, no-re-estimation refresh to 2026Q1 is feasible for the advanced economies — US (incl. all fiscal), the euro area + EU/EFTA members (real GDP from Eurostat, HICP from FRED), UK, JP, CA, and the FRED-sourced EMs (BR, MX, ZA). For these, 2026Q1 macro is published and the forecast can simply re-anchor from the new last-observed state.

The binding constraint is the EM laggards (CN, IN) whose real source data still ends ~2025Q2 and which only reach 2025Q4 through WEO-annual disaggregation — no genuine 2026Q1 observation exists for them today.

Recommended as-of quarter: 2026Q1 for the AE + FRED-EM blocs (the bulk of the system), while CN/IN remain at their back-filled 2025Q4 (or are extended again by the same WEO mechanism, flagged as synthetic). If a single common balanced endpoint is required across all blocs, the honest common observed endpoint is still 2025Q4; 2026Q1 is achievable as a deliberately ragged-edge refresh with CN/IN flagged.

docs.title: Part 2 — Refresh MECHANICS & Automation (no re-estimation)
docs.text: Complements Part 1 (data availability). This part answers: given the data, exactly HOW does a no-re-estimation refresh work — what re-projects vs what's a static artifact, which JSONs must be regenerated, whether δc/conditioning/plotting follow, and a one-command automation sketch.

docs.title: (1) How the displayed BASELINE is produced — split brain (precomputed and re-projected)
docs.text:
There are two distinct baseline objects, and the refresh story depends on which:

A. The SCENARIO / DEVIATION engine RE-PROJECTS in-browser (no JSON dependence). makeCountryModel (gvarSolve.ts:344-363) computes baseLvl at load time via conditionalForecast(params, histMat, allNaN) — the DK smoother projecting the fixed companion (companion_mean.betahat/sigmahat) forward from historical_levels.data. It does not read baseline_levels.json. The single-country path is identical: useConditionalForecast.ts:199-203 re-runs the unconditional smoother and explicitly uses "SMOOTHER's unconditional as reference (not stored baseline)" so deviation=0 ⇒ delta=0. ⇒ Every conditioning result, GVAR cross-country solve, δc overlay, and scenario band already re-anchors from historical_levels + companion_mean the instant the history changes — no MATLAB, no JSON regen.

B. The displayed BASELINE LINE + always-on BANDS are PRECOMPUTED MATLAB artifacts. The grey baseline central line and its fan are read directly from static JSON: q/q view from baseline.json .mean/.q16/.q84 (ChartGrid.tsx:1396,1491-1492); level view from baseline_levels.json (ChartGrid.tsx:1399-1402,1539-1540). These come from export_bvar_gvar24_loop.m:218,233-234: meanLvl = VARcf_DKcks([Xc;nan(H,n)],lags,Gam,sm,0) (deterministic DK mean of the fixed posterior-mean companion — byte-identical to the browser's conditionalForecast(allNaN), memory invariant "~1e-11"); bands = quantile(R.Dfc,…) where R.Dfc is the predictive density cgl_bvarV5 produced during estimation from the old last-observed state.

Settled: the engine re-projects (JSON-independent); the displayed baseline line+band is a precomputed artifact. A refresh that re-anchors only the engine but leaves baseline*.json stale shows the scenario on the new vintage while the grey baseline fan stays on the old anchor — visibly inconsistent. So baseline*.json MUST be regenerated.

docs.title: (2) Minimal refresh — exact regen list (per bloc)
docs.text:
File Refresh? How / why historical_levels.json REGEN (append 1 row) observed 100·log levels; engine state anchor historical.json REGEN (append 1 row) q/q-ann display history = gh(levels) (4·Δlog) baseline.json REGEN (mean+bands) mean = re-project fixed companion (deterministic); bands = re-project companion_draws β,Σ from the new state baseline_levels.json REGEN (mean+bands) same, in level space fiscal_meta.json REGEN d0 only (US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN) d0 = last-observed debt level (US=122.568, 100·log) = debt-recursion seed; sfaDefault/idx unchanged companion_mean.json UNCHANGED estimated model — frozen companion_draws.json(.gz) UNCHANGED posterior β,Σ — frozen (re-used to re-project bands) spec.json, transformations.json UNCHANGED frozen bvar/gvar/coupling_default.json.gz, weights_*.json UNCHANGED (I−M)⁻¹ depends on betahats+trade weights, not data vintage → no 19-min rebuild, no 100MB issue DSGE catalog/IRFs UNCHANGED independent of BVAR vintage

App recomputes for itself (no regen): all conditioning/deviation paths, the GVAR solve, scenario bands (runGvarBandSolve.ts re-runs each companion_draws draw through the solver from the refreshed history), and the δc drift cache.

Bands — the one real "need MATLAB?" question → NO. Today's baseline bands come from cgl_bvarV5's R.Dfc. But companion_draws.json ships the full per-draw (β,Σ), and the browser already re-projects bands from them via conditionalForecast per draw (runGvarBandSolve.ts:64-70, drawCompanion). So the baseline band can be regenerated in pure JS/Python by looping the shipped draws through the same DK projector from the new state. (The app's band is parameter-only by documented design — runGvarBandSolve.ts:11-14 — so reproducing it parameter-only is the intended, consistent choice; R.Dfc additionally carried DK simulation shocks, which the live app does not display anyway.)

docs.title: (3) δc overlay + conditioning on the refreshed baseline — YES, both still work
docs.text:
• δc drift: the drift cache is built from betahat/n/lags (fixed companion) and re-anchors off baseline.mean's terminal (ChartGrid.tsx:1062-1087). Post-baseline by construction — feed it the refreshed baseline.json and it re-anchors automatically. No code change.
  • Conditioning: the engine reference is the recomputed unconditional smoother (§1A), so pins/scenarios re-anchor with zero changes; bands re-run draws from the new history. Fiscal conditioning seeds the debt recursion from fiscal_meta.d0 — hence d0 is in the regen list.

docs.title: (4) Plotting / dates — ONE hardcoded constant blocks the auto-extend
docs.text:
X-axis is numeric offsets (x=−1 last hist, x=0 first forecast) mapped to calendar quarters by lastHistOf(country) (dates.ts). Forecast-start is not derived from historical.json length — it hangs off one constant:

dates.ts:38   export const DEFAULT_LAST_HIST_DATE = '2025-10-01'   // 2025 Q4 — common panel end

Every dated label (offsetLabel/offsetYQ → ChartGrid.tsx:2328,2338,2345, ScenarioBar.tsx, CSV export ChartGrid.tsx:24) flows from this. Left at 2025-10-01, an appended 2026Q1 obs would be mis-dated (new point labelled as a forecast quarter; whole fan slides one quarter into the past). Fix: bump DEFAULT_LAST_HIST_DATE → '2026-01-01'; advance the per-bloc LAST_HIST_DATE overrides only for blocs that actually got a new quarter (CN/IN, if held at 2025Q4, get an override). Horizon stays 20 (H = min(horizon, baseline.mean.length)) — not auto-extended, so the forecast END recedes one quarter (2031Q1), which is the desired re-anchor. No other hardcoded dates/horizons found in app/src (grep 2025Q4/2025-12/2025-10/forecastStart/lastObserved). Stale comments to fix (not load-bearing): ScenarioBar.tsx:10, ChartGrid.tsx:2327, dates.ts:31-37.

docs.title: (5) Automation — one command, NO MATLAB
docs.text:
All four projected artifacts reduce to append + DK-project the shipped companion, a computation the codebase already implements in pure TS (kalman.ts:conditionalForecast, validated to 1e-11 vs MATLAB). A Node refresher reuses the production engine verbatim; MATLAB is needed only to re-estimate β,Σ (out of scope).

refresh-quarter.mjs — for each bloc C in weights_full.json:
 1. PULL 2026Q1 for C from the SAME sources build_*.py use (FRED hardcoded key, Eurostat keys,
    WEO/raw JSON); reuse each build's series map, fetch only the latest obs.
 2. APPEND → rebuild the 100·log level row exactly as the build script does (same transforms,
    COVID-as-missing, window policy); push onto historical_levels.data; historical.json = gh(levels).
 3. RE-PROJECT (pure JS, FIXED companion — import kalman.ts/conditionalForecast):
      params  = companion_mean.json  (UNCHANGED)
      histMat = refreshed historical_levels
      baseline_levels.mean = conditionalForecast(params, histMat, allNaN).mean   # = VARcf_DKcks(…,0)
      baseline.mean        = gh(baseline_levels.mean)
      bands: for each draw (β_d,Σ_d) in companion_draws:
               Lfc_d = conditionalForecast({β_d,Σ_d}, histMat, allNaN).mean
             baseline_levels.q16/84/05/95 = quantile_d(level);  baseline.q16/… = quantile_d(gh(Lfc_d))
 4. FISCAL blocs: fiscal_meta.d0 = refreshed last-observed debt level.
 5. WRITE historical.json, historical_levels.json, baseline.json, baseline_levels.json, (fiscal_meta).
 6. ONE-TIME: dates.ts DEFAULT_LAST_HIST_DATE → '2026-01-01' (+ per-bloc overrides for laggards).
  • Cost: band re-projection = nDraws × conditionalForecast per bloc (US: ~500 × one 20q smoother over 41×41) — exactly what runGvarBandSolve does in the worker; offline it's seconds/bloc. Companion + coupling untouched → no (I−M)⁻¹ rebuild, no GitHub 100MB issue.
  • MATLAB-free: YES for a pure data refresh; required ONLY for re-estimation.
  • Validate before shipping: match each build_*.py source map + the COVID-as-missing / WEO-extension / window rules so the appended row reproduces the harmonised panel byte-for-byte (the panel was built to a common endpoint with held-last/truncate — Part 1; the refresher must apply the same window policy, and treat CN/IN per Part 1's ragged-edge recommendation).

docs.title: Bottom line (mechanics)
docs.text:
• Baseline source: split — engine re-projects from companion_mean+historical_levels (JSON-independent); the displayed line+band is precomputed baseline*.json.
  • Regen exactly: historical.json, historical_levels.json, baseline.json, baseline_levels.json (+ fiscal_meta.d0 for fiscal blocs). UNCHANGED: companion_mean/draws, spec, transformations, coupling, weights.
  • δc + conditioning: unaffected (post-baseline, auto re-anchor).
  • Plotting: one constant dates.ts:DEFAULT_LAST_HIST_DATE 2025-10-01→2026-01-01; rest is offset-based. Horizon stays 20 (forecast end recedes one quarter — desired).
  • Automation: single Node script (append → DK-project the shipped companion for mean AND per-draw bands → write 4 JSONs → bump the date constant) refreshes the whole panel without MATLAB.

docs.title: Part 3 — Synthesis: verdict, risks, and the build plan
docs.text: Consolidates Parts 1–2 into the six deliverables. Parts 1–2 stand as the evidence; this part is the decision-ready answer plus the actual implementation plan for later review on improve-live.

docs.title: (1) FEASIBILITY VERDICT
docs.text:
YES — a no-re-estimation refresh to 2026Q1 is feasible today, for the bulk of the system, and it is exactly the post-estimation "re-anchor the fixed model from a new last-observed state" operation the δc sliders already embody. The companion (companion_mean/companion_draws) never changes; only the observed panel extends by one quarter and the forecast re-projects forward from it.

Realistic as-of quarter: - 2026Q1 for the advanced economies + FRED-sourced EMs — US (incl. all fiscal), EA + EU/EFTA members (real GDP Eurostat, HICP FRED), UK, JP, CA, BR, MX, ZA. 2026Q1 macro is published; monthly rates/CPI already run to 2026-04/05 so the quarterly aggregate is complete. - 2025Q4 (held / WEO-synthetic) for CN and IN — their real source data still ends ~2025Q2 and only reaches 2025Q4 via WEO-annual disaggregation; no genuine 2026Q1 observation exists today. - ⇒ Deliberately ragged-edge refresh: ship 2026Q1 for the AE+FRED-EM blocs, hold CN/IN at back-filled 2025Q4 (flagged synthetic). The honest common balanced endpoint across all blocs remains 2025Q4; 2026Q1 is a ragged edge, which the panel already tolerates (raw JSONs are ragged today: CA/JP at 2026Q1, EA/UK at 2025Q4, CN/IN at 2025Q2).

docs.title: (2) AUTOMATION DESIGN (one command, MATLAB-free) — recap
docs.text: refresh-quarter.mjs: pull → append → re-project the FIXED companion → regen JSONs → bump one date constant. Mean = conditionalForecast(companion_mean, refreshedHist, allNaN) (the production TS DK smoother, validated 1e-11 vs VARcf_DKcks(…,0)); bands = the same projector looped over the shipped per-draw companion_draws (β,Σ) — identical to what runGvarBandSolve already does in the worker. Companion + coupling untouched ⇒ no (I−M)⁻¹ rebuild, no MATLAB, seconds/bloc. Full sketch in Part 2 §5.

docs.title: (3) JSON REGEN LIST + who re-projects
docs.text:
Object Regen by script? App auto-reprojects? historical_levels.json, historical.json YES (append 1 row) — (these ARE the new state) baseline.json, baseline_levels.json (mean + bands) YES engine ignores them, but the displayed grey line+fan reads them → must regen fiscal_meta.d0 (US/EA/JP/UK/CA/CN) YES (debt-recursion seed only) — companion_mean/draws, spec, transformations, coupling_default, weights_*, DSGE NO — frozen — Conditioning / deviation / GVAR solve / scenario bands / δc cache NO YES — re-anchors automatically from historical_levels+companion_mean at load

The split-brain rule (Part 2 §1): the engine re-projects itself JSON-independently the instant history changes; the static baseline line+band does NOT. So regen baseline*.json or the grey fan sits on the stale anchor while scenarios move on the new vintage.

docs.title: (4) PLOTTING CHANGES
docs.text:
• One load-bearing constant: dates.ts:38 DEFAULT_LAST_HIST_DATE = '2025-10-01' → '2026-01-01'. Everything dated flows from it via numeric offsets + lastHistOf(); left stale, the appended 2026Q1 point is mis-labelled and the whole fan slides one quarter into the past.
  • Per-bloc overrides in LAST_HIST_DATE only for blocs that did NOT advance (add a 2025-10-01 override for CN/IN so they stay correctly dated at 2025Q4).
  • Horizon unchanged (H = min(horizon, baseline.mean.length), =20): forecast END recedes one quarter (→2031Q1) — the intended re-anchor.
  • No other hardcoded 2025Q4/2025-12/forecastStart in app/src. Stale comments to tidy (cosmetic): ScenarioBar.tsx:10, ChartGrid.tsx:2327, dates.ts:31-37.

docs.title: (5) RISKS + WHAT TO VERIFY
docs.text: # Risk Mitigation / verification R1 Panel-build mismatch — appended row not built byte-identically to the harmonised panel (transforms, COVID-as-missing, WEO-extension, window/truncate policy) ⇒ a level discontinuity at the seam. Replay each build_*.py source map + transform exactly; diff the re-derived 2025Q4 row against the shipped historical_levels last row → must match to ~1e-9 before trusting the appended 2026Q1 row. R2 Stale-anchor inconsistency — regen the engine but not baseline*.json ⇒ grey fan on old anchor, scenarios on new. Always regen all four JSONs together; visual check that grey baseline central line == unconditional smoother at deviation 0. R3 Date misalignment — forget the DEFAULT_LAST_HIST_DATE bump ⇒ fan slides one quarter. After bump, eyeball the x-axis: last hist tick = 2026Q1, first forecast = 2026Q2 (AE blocs), CN/IN still 2025Q4. R4 Band reproduction drift — JS per-draw projection ≠ original cgl_bvarV5 R.Dfc band. Expected & acceptable: the live app's band is parameter-only by design (runGvarBandSolve.ts:11-14); regen the same parameter-only way → consistent with what users already see. Sanity: new bands ⊇ central path, widen with horizon, no NaN. R5 CN/IN ragged edge — accidentally extending them with fake 2026Q1. Explicitly exclude CN/IN from the 2026Q1 pull; hold at 2025Q4; flag as synthetic in UI/notes. R6 FRED ID traps — discontinued OECD-on-FRED CPI mirrors (GBRCPIALLMINMEI, CPALTT01CAM659N end 2025-03), FREV/FPEXP are internal names not FRED IDs, stale Eurostat HICP endpoint (ends 2025-12). Use only the build scripts' actual series IDs; pull EA/member HICP from FRED, never the Eurostat HICP path. R7 δc / conditioning regression — sliders or pins anchor to the wrong terminal after refresh. Post-refresh smoke test: δc=0 ⇒ overlay == baseline; a single pin re-solves; fiscal pin uses refreshed d0 (debt recursion continuous). R8 Vintage-drift sanity — a refreshed bloc looks crazy (units, sign, 4·log scale). Per-bloc: 2026Q1 q/q-ann within plausible range vs 2025Q4; level continuous; debt %GDP continuous; spot-check 2–3 blocs by hand against the source print.

docs.title: (6) BUILD PLAN (later, on improve-live, for user review)
docs.text:
Read-only today; this is the implementation sequence to propose:

  • Phase 0 — Scaffold & lock sources. Inventory each bloc's exact source map from build_*.py (series IDs, transforms, COVID/WEO/window rules). Define the bloc set: AE+FRED-EM → 2026Q1; CN/IN → hold.
  • Phase 1 — Replay/validate the LAST row (gate). For each bloc, re-derive the existing 2025Q4 row from live sources and diff against shipped historical_levels (~1e-9). Gate: must match before any append — proves the refresher reproduces the panel's transform/window policy.
  • Phase 2 — Append 2026Q1 + re-project (core). Pull 2026Q1, append the 100·log row, run conditionalForecast(companion_mean,…) for the mean and loop companion_draws for bands; write historical*.json, baseline*.json, and fiscal_meta.d0. Reuse kalman.ts/gh verbatim (no re-implementation of the projector).
  • Phase 3 — Plotting. Bump DEFAULT_LAST_HIST_DATE → '2026-01-01'; add CN/IN per-bloc overrides; tidy the stale comments.
  • Phase 4 — Verify (the gate to ship). Run R1–R8 checks: seam continuity, grey-fan vs smoother, axis dates, band sanity, δc=0==baseline, single-pin + fiscal-pin re-solve, 2–3 hand spot-checks. Green-gate + diff review before any commit.
  • Phase 5 — Ship as ragged edge. Commit the regen'd JSONs + the one date change; CN/IN flagged. Document the as-of (2026Q1 ragged; common balanced 2025Q4). Re-runnable each quarter (the script is the quarterly-refresh tool, not a one-off).

One-line cost: nDraws × conditionalForecast per bloc (seconds), companion/coupling frozen — the whole refresh is minutes, MATLAB-free.

docs.title: Data Refresh 2026Q1 — Rollout Runbook (no re-estimation)
docs.text:
Status: US pilot LANDED on improve-live. Full rollout is HELD for green-light (this doc).

This is the operator runbook for extending the rest of the panel to 2026 Q1 with the same no-re-estimation refresh that shipped for US. It complements the design in docs/data-refresh-2026q1-feasibility.md (read that first for the why); this file is the how to roll out and the what to check before merge.

The mechanism is fixed: app/scripts/refresh-quarter.mjs extends the OBSERVED panel by one quarter and lets the forecast RE-ANCHOR from the FIXED companion. No estimation runs. cgl_bvarV5.m is never touched. companion_mean / companion_draws(.gz) / spec / transformations / coupling / weights stay frozen. Per bloc the script rewrites only: historical.json, historical_levels.json, baseline.json, baseline_levels.json, and (fiscal blocs only) fiscal_meta.json d0 — plus the single per-bloc date in app/src/lib/dates.ts.

docs.title: 0. Hard prerequisite — the source registry (this is what gates the rollout)
docs.text:
refresh-quarter.mjs only knows how to refresh a bloc that has a SOURCES[<CC>] entry (live source IDs + transform + window, honouring the R6 ID traps). Today only US is mapped. Every other bloc is fail-closed: running the script against it exits 4 ("NO SOURCE MAP — refusing to touch it") and writes nothing. CN / IN are fail-closed by design (exit 3, structural laggards — see R5).

So the rollout is NOT a single command yet. It is: author + gate the registry for each target bloc, one at a time, then run the refresh. The "one command" below is the per-bloc command; the rollout is that command run down the target list once each registry is in.

docs.title: Target set (advanced economies + FRED-sourced EMs)
docs.text:
EA  (+ euro members)   UK   JP   CA   BR   MX   ZA

HELD at 2025 Q4 (never fabricated, R5): CN, IN — real source data still ends ~2025 Q2; they keep the 2025-10-01 override already in dates.ts. Do not attempt to advance them.

Israel (IL) stays at its existing 2024-10-01 override unless/until its quarterly source advances — out of scope for the 2026Q1 rollout.

docs.title: 1. The one command (per bloc)
docs.text:
Gate-then-write (the rollout command), run from app/:

FRED_API_KEY=8ce324fcfb4663bfef63d3d44fbdbc73 \
  npx vite-node scripts/refresh-quarter.mjs --bloc <CC> --asof 2026Q1

Useful variants (use these while authoring a new registry):

# gate ONLY — proves transform/window/source fidelity, never writes:
… scripts/refresh-quarter.mjs --bloc <CC> --asof 2026Q1 --gate-only
# gate + project + report, still no write (eyeball the seam/bands first):
… scripts/refresh-quarter.mjs --bloc <CC> --asof 2026Q1 --dry-run
# tighten the gate against a full-precision DataRaw_*_glob.json panel:
… --gate-tol 1e-9     # default is 5e-3 vs the 6-sig-fig shipped historical_levels

docs.title: Exit codes (what a non-zero means)
docs.text:
code meaning action 0 gate passed, wrote (or, with --gate-only/--dry-run, validated) proceed to verify 1 GATE FAILED — transform/window/source fidelity not proven, or fatal fix the registry; nothing was written 2 bad args (missing --bloc/--asof) fix invocation 3 bloc is a HELD laggard (CN/IN) expected — leave held 4 no source map for this bloc author SOURCES[<CC>] first, then re-run 5 new quarter not fully published yet for this bloc wait for the data; do not partial-write 6 fiscal: debt/GDP series missing at as-of fix the fiscal source id

The gate (R1) is the binding safety check. Before appending the new quarter it re-derives the existing last shipped row (2025 Q4) from the same live sources the same way and diffs it against the shipped panel. STABLE columns must be byte-exact (max abs diff 0); revision-prone columns (debt/IP/ cumulative) are allowed a documented revision tolerance. On any STABLE-column breach it writes nothing. A passing gate is the precondition for the write — there is no override.

docs.title: 2. Rollout sequence
docs.text:
For each bloc in the target set, in order, do NOT batch — one bloc fully verified before the next:

  1. Author SOURCES[<CC>] in refresh-quarter.mjs (live FRED/Eurostat IDs + transform + window). Honour the R6 traps: discontinued OECD-on-FRED CPI mirrors; FREV/FPEXP are internal names, not FRED IDs; stale Eurostat HICP → use CP0000EZ19M086NEST for EA HICP. EA members get the shared-EA exogenous rate/corp handling already baked into the model spec (do not re-source those endogenously).
  2. Gate with --gate-only until it passes (max abs diff 0 on STABLE cols). If it can't pass, the registry is wrong — fix the IDs/transform/window, never loosen the tolerance to force it.
  3. Dry-run (--dry-run) and eyeball the reported seam + bands (see §3 R8 note — a continuous level with a noisy annualized q/q step is fine; a true level discontinuity is not).
  4. Write (no flag) once gate+dry-run are clean.
  5. Set the date in dates.ts: when the whole target panel has advanced, the default is already 2026-01-01; a bloc that advances to 2026Q1 needs no override (it inherits the default). Only add/keep an override for a bloc that does NOT advance (CN/IN held; IL held).
  6. Verify (§3) for that bloc, then move on.

When all target blocs are in: re-run the GVAR star aggregates if the build owner wants the ROW*_X star columns recomputed from the refreshed cross-section (the US pilot held them last for a single-bloc refresh). For a panel-wide 2026Q1 advance this should be revisited so the star aggregates reflect the new quarter rather than held-last.

docs.title: 3. Verify checklist (per bloc, before merge)
docs.text:
Run all of these green before considering a bloc done. (The US pilot passed every item.)

  • [ ] tsc clean: cd app && npx tsc --noEmit → exit 0.
  • [ ] cgl_bvarV5.m 0-diff: git diff --stat BVAR/MFBVAR/cgl_bvarV5.m empty. Production estimator untouched.
  • [ ] Frozen artifacts unchanged: git status --porcelain app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/ shows ONLY historical.json, historical_levels.json, baseline.json, baseline_levels.json, and (fiscal) fiscal_meta.json modified. companion_mean / companion_draws(.gz) / spec / transformations MUST NOT appear.
  • [ ] fiscal_meta = d0 only (fiscal blocs): git diff …/fiscal_meta.json changes only d0; sfaDefault and idx frozen.
  • [ ] History extends one quarter: historical*.json row count +1, last row dated 2026 Q1.
  • [ ] R1 gate PASS: the run's gate report shows STABLE max abs diff 0 (revision-prone within tol).
  • [ ] Bands sane: baseline_levels.json mean+bands all finite, q05 ≤ q16 ≤ q84 ≤ q95 everywhere.
  • [ ] Re-anchor (split-brain healed): the engine's re-projected baseline (from the refreshed history) equals baseline_levels.json mean to ~0. The grey fan and the engine sit on the SAME vintage.
  • [ ] δc = 0 == baseline (R7): an all-unconditional solve reproduces the baseline (no spurious shift).
  • [ ] Dates plot (R… ragged edge): lastHistOf('<CC>') = 2026-01-01; x=−1 is 2026Q1, x=0 is the first forecast (2026Q2); held blocs still date at 2025Q4. No off-by-one on the x-axis.
  • [ ] Seam continuity (R1, the GATE visual): the level across the 2025Q4→2026Q1 join is continuous. A large annualized q/q number on a genuinely volatile quarter (e.g. WTI) is annualization, not a discontinuity — confirm against the underlying level before flagging.
  • [ ] No CN/IN fabrication (R5): CN/IN untouched, still held at 2025Q4.

docs.title: 4. Guardrails (do not violate)
docs.text:
• improve-live ONLY. No deploy, no push to main, no production release as part of the rollout.
  • No re-estimation. Reuse kalman.ts / bvarForecastDraw verbatim; never touch the companion.
  • Never git add -A. Stage only the touched bloc's data dir + the script + dates.ts + this doc.
  • Fail-closed is a feature. If a bloc exits 4/5/6, the data isn't ready or the registry is wrong — do not force a write. Honour CN/IN as held.
  • Never loosen the gate to make it pass. A failing gate means the source/transform/window is wrong.

docs.title: 5. Files
docs.text:
• app/scripts/refresh-quarter.mjs — the refresher (gate + re-project + bands writer).
  • app/src/lib/dates.ts — the single per-bloc last-historical-date constant (default + overrides).
  • app/public/data/bvar/<CC>/{historical,historical_levels,baseline,baseline_levels,fiscal_meta}.json — the regenerated per-bloc display/state files.
  • docs/data-refresh-2026q1-feasibility.md — the design and the R1–R8 risk register.

docs.title: Euro-area cross-border spillover & harmonization
docs.text:
This page explains how the tool links the euro-area aggregate (EA) and its individual members (DE, FR, IT, ES, …) so that a scenario built on one bloc reaches the others in an economically consistent way — and how the shared building blocks of a monetary union (the ECB policy rate, the risk-free curve, sovereign spreads) are kept coherent across all of them.

If you just want the "how do I use it" version, jump to Using it, step by step.

docs.title: The setup: why members need special treatment
docs.text:
Each member (DE, FR, IT, …) is estimated as its own standalone BVAR. The EA aggregate is a full bloc of the coupled GVAR (it trades and co-moves with the US, China, etc.). The members are not in the GVAR coupling — they hang off the EA.

That design is deliberate (a member's domestic detail — its fiscal block, its house prices, its sovereign spread — lives in the member model, while cross-border transmission is carried by the aggregate). But it creates three gaps that this feature set closes:

  1. A US or EA scenario you build would never reach a member, because the member isn't in the coupled solve.
  2. The ECB policy rate — one common instrument — was being forecast separately by each member model, so it came out different across members.
  3. There was no direct handle on the euro-area risk-free curve or a country's sovereign spread in the member view.

docs.title: 1. Cross-border spillover: a US/EA scenario reaches the members
docs.text:
When you condition the US or the EA aggregate and press ▶ Solve full GVAR, the tool solves the complete coupled system (including the EA bloc's response). It then reads the EA aggregate's solved deviation and maps it onto each member's foreign/rest-of-area inputs, so the member's own model transmits the area-wide shock into its domestic GDP, inflation, and rates.

  • The member you are viewing shows a small amber note — "Cross-border spillover from the US/EA scenario not yet computed" — with a ▶ Solve full GVAR button, until you run the solve. This is deliberate: the full coupled solve takes a few seconds, so it runs only when you ask for it (pressing the button, or Compute), never automatically on every click.
  • The transmission uses the deviation of the EA path from the member's own baseline (not the raw EA level), so it never injects the gap between the EA and the member's baseline — at a zero scenario it is an exact no-op.

docs.title: 2. The ECB policy rate is common across EA and members
docs.text:
The problem. The euro area has one policy rate, set by the ECB. But because every member was a separate BVAR forecasting its own POLRATE column, the common rate came out different across members — and sometimes implausibly so:

bloc old terminal ECB-rate forecast EA 2.25% DE 0.71% FR 2.55% IT 3.02%

(This was not the long-run "consensus drift" target — the policy rate has no long-run anchor in any bloc. It was purely the separate-model artefact.)

The fix. Every member's policy rate is now pinned to the EA aggregate's POLRATE_EA path — the single common ECB rate — in both the baseline and every scenario. Concretely, POLRATE_<member> is treated as exogenous and driven by the EA rate; the member's own model then re-solves its domestic block in response to the common rate. So:

  • The policy-rate chart is identical across EA and all members.
  • Each member's domestic GDP/inflation baseline responds to the common rate — e.g. Germany, whose rate rises from the old 0.7% to the ~2.25% area path, now shows the correspondingly tighter activity; Italy (rate falling 3.0→2.25%) the opposite.
  • Scenario deviations are unchanged where you don't touch the rate: because the same common-rate pin sits under both the baseline and the scenario, it cancels in (scenario − baseline). Only the level baseline shifts.

One consequence worth knowing

Because the ECB rate is now exogenous and common, conditioning a member variable that would otherwise induce an endogenous domestic rate response (e.g. a large GDP shock) leaves the policy rate on the common path rather than letting the member's own rate drift. That is the correct behaviour for a single area-wide policy rate — the ECB does not set a Germany-specific rate.

docs.title: 3. Conditioning the ECB rate on a member = an area-wide shock
docs.text:
The ECB Policy Rate now appears as a draggable chart in the member's first row. Because it is the common rate, conditioning it on any euro bloc is an area-wide monetary shock:

  • Drag the ECB-rate path on, say, Germany, then press ▶ Solve full GVAR.
  • Your pin is mirrored onto the EA aggregate's POLRATE_EA, the coupled system re-solves, and the new rate propagates to the EA aggregate and every other member.
  • It is one shared rate — editing it on DE then FR edits the same underlying ECB path (last edit wins).

docs.title: 4. The euro risk-free curve (Bund) and the country spread
docs.text:
Members carry their long rate as Bund + spread: a common risk-free benchmark (the German 10-year, YIELD10_DE) plus a country-specific sovereign spread (SPREAD_<member>). Both are now surfaced as draggable charts:

  • EA Long Yield (Bund). Conditioning the benchmark is a common risk-free curve shock: it maps one-for-one onto the area long yield and propagates to every member's risk-free reference.
  • Sovereign Spread (vs Bund). Conditioning a member's spread is a country-specific stress, and it is contagious: it feeds the euro-area long yield in proportion to that country's share of euro-area GDP. Formally, Δ YIELD10_EA = w_member · Δ spread_member, which is exactly the mechanical identity for a GDP-weighted area yield (YIELD10_EA ≈ Bund + Σᵢ wᵢ · spreadᵢ). So a widening Italian spread (weight ≈ 14%) transmits far more to the area than an Estonian one (≈ 0.3%).

The GDP weights are real Eurostat data (current-price GDP, 4-quarter-trailing share of the EA20, app/public/data/bvar/gvar/ea_gdp_weights.json) — not assumed. If both a Bund pin and a spread pin are set, their effects add on the area yield.

docs.title: Using it, step by step
docs.text:
1. Build the scenario where it's natural.
    • Area-wide monetary or curve shock → condition ECB Policy Rate or EA Long Yield (Bund) on any euro bloc.
    • Country-specific sovereign stress → condition Sovereign Spread (vs Bund) on that member.
    • A global/US shock → condition the US or EA aggregate.
  2. Press ▶ Solve full GVAR (or Compute). The coupled system solves and the shock propagates to the EA aggregate and every member. On a member, the amber prompt tells you when a solve is pending.
  3. Switch blocs to read the transmission. View the EA aggregate and the other members to see the propagated paths; the policy-rate chart will read the same everywhere.

docs.title: Honest caveats
docs.text:
• The common rate is identical to within a sub-basis-point (~0.005 pp) numerical residual — the in-browser smoother differs infinitesimally from the stored baseline. It is invisible on the charts; a two-decimal tooltip can very occasionally show e.g. 2.24 vs 2.25.
  • Spread contagion is a modelling choice, not an estimated equation: a country's spread feeds the area yield by its GDP weight. It is a transparent, data-grounded pass-through, deliberately simple.
  • Germany is the risk-free anchor: its YIELD10_DE is the Bund, so DE carries a Bund chart but no sovereign-spread chart (its spread is zero by construction).
  • Everything here is forecast conditioning (the Durbin–Koopman smoother), layered on top of the frozen estimated models — no re-estimation is involved.

Implementation: app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx (the member→EA pin mirror, the rate-driven baseline, the Bund/spread channels), app/src/lib/compute/eaMemberSpillover.ts and useEaMemberSpillover.ts (the EA→member mapping), app/public/data/bvar/gvar/ea_gdp_weights.json (the GDP weights). See also the Fiscal Block and External Block methodology pages.

docs.title: External Block — the balance-of-payments satellite (Trade Balance + Current Account + NFA/GDP)
docs.text:
The external block estimates the external drivers inside the BVAR (trade flows and the exchange rate) and derives the reporting variables — trade balance, current account, and the net international investment position (net foreign assets, NFA) as a share of GDP — by accounting identity. This is the exact same division of labour as the fiscal block: a reduced-form model is never allowed to freely forecast a stock (there, public debt; here, NFA). Instead the BVAR forecasts the flows and the FX index, and a separate, side-effect-free accounting module rolls the stock forward by the balance-of-payments identity, seeded from a real, sourced anchor.

Together the fiscal block, this external block, and the monetary core complete the IMF FPP monetary–fiscal–external triad of sectoral consistency: fiscal rolls the debt stock from a budget identity, external rolls the NFA stock from the BoP identity, and both share the same two-layer pattern (BVAR flow generator + identity recursion) and the same %-of-GDP annual-ratio convention.

docs.title: The shared backbone (identical to the fiscal block)
docs.text:
Both satellites separate "forecast the macro + sector flows in the BVAR" from "derive the sector stock by accounting." Neither lets the reduced form forecast the stock directly.

  • Fiscal: BVAR forecasts revenue/GDP, primary-expenditure/GDP, the effective rate and the SFA → app/src/lib/compute/fiscalAccounting.ts rolls debt/GDP forward by the FPP/DSA debt-dynamics identity, seeded by d0 in fiscal_meta.json.
  • External: BVAR forecasts exports, imports, GDP and the FX index → app/src/lib/compute/externalAccounting.ts rolls NFA/GDP forward by the BoP identity, seeded by nfa0 (real net-IIP / nominal GDP) in external_meta.json.

The authoritative implementation is computeExternalBlock in app/src/lib/compute/externalAccounting.ts. It is read-only and side-effect-free: it consumes a level-space forecast path and returns per-quarter arrays of length H. It is wired into app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx exactly the way the fiscal block is — a derived useMemo, a per-draw band loop, and synthetic chart traces — and is fully gated on the presence of a chartable external_meta.json (a bloc without one renders nothing, with zero band work).

docs.title: Element-by-element mapping
docs.text:
Concept IMF BoP / FPP external sector This tool Exports / imports (drivers) goods+services trade flows estimated BVAR level vars (US XH/MH; EA EXP_EA/IMP_EA) GDP (denominator) nominal GDP estimated BVAR level var (GDPH / GDP_EA) Exchange rate (driver) NEER / REER estimated BVAR log var (US FXTWM; EA NEER_EA) Trade balance exports − imports, % GDP derived TB = 100·(X−M)/gdp$ Primary income on NFA investment income on the net IIP derived income = iNFA·NFA₋₁ (imputed return) Current account trade balance + primary income (+ transfers) derived CA = TB + income FX valuation effect revaluation of FX-denominated net assets derived val = −fxShare·NFA₋₁·ΔFX NFA / net IIP path NFAₜ = NFA₋₁ + CAₜ + valuationₜ derived recursion (the BoP stock identity) NFA seed observed net IIP / GDP sourced nfa0 in external_meta.json (real, per bloc)

The NFA recursion is the standard BoP stock-flow identity — the net international investment position changes by the current-account flow plus valuation (revaluation) effects, the same decomposition the IMF/ECB external statistics and the Lane–Milesi-Ferretti External Wealth of Nations dataset use.

docs.title: The recursion, precisely
docs.text:
Read the units block at the top of externalAccounting.ts before editing anything here. Per forecast quarter t = 0 … H−1, read off the level-space forecast path (100·log for log vars; level for lin):

  • exports / imports / GDP — log vars; the quantity is exp(level/100). Trade balance is a % of GDP, so the numerator (exports − imports) and the denominator gdp$ must be in a common nominal scale. For US (XH/MH/GDPH all real chained Bil USD) and EA (EXP_EA/IMP_EA/GDP_EA all real, common units) they are, so the ratio is real-trade-balance / real-GDP and validates against the published BoP figure.
  • the FX index (idx.fx, log var: US FXTWM, EA NEER_EA, CN REER_CN) — "up = home currency appreciates." A rise lowers the domestic-currency value of FX-denominated net assets, hence the negative valuation sign below.
  • iNFA (idx-level iNFA, % p.a.) — the imputed return on the NFA stock, a modeling default (~3%), flagged in external_meta.json (_flags).
  • fxShare (fxShare, fraction in [0,1]) — the FX-exposed share of the NFA stock, a modeling default (~0.4), flagged.

The seed nfa0 (initial NFA/GDP, %) is the real, sourced net-IIP/GDP anchor from external_meta.json.

docs.title: Per-quarter recursion (using lagged NFA NFA₋₁ so the loop stays explicit)
docs.text:
With NFA₋₁ = nfa0 at t=0 and NFA₋₁ = nfa[t−1] thereafter, and prev the previous level row (prevLevelRow at t=0, for the FX diff seam):

  1. Trade balance (% of GDP, annual ratio): TBₜ = 100·(exp(levelₜ[exports]/100) − exp(levelₜ[imports]/100)) / exp(levelₜ[gdp]/100) (externalAccounting.ts:104).
  2. Primary income on the NFA stock: incomeₜ = iNFA · NFA₋₁ / 100 (externalAccounting.ts:106). This is the imputed-return channel (modeling default), the only place iNFA enters.
  3. Current account (% of GDP, annual): CAₜ = TBₜ + incomeₜ (externalAccounting.ts:107).
  4. FX valuation effect this quarter: with dFXₜ = levelₜ[fx] − prev[fx] (Δ of 100·log FX), valₜ = −fxShare · NFA₋₁ · dFXₜ / 100 (externalAccounting.ts:109–110). A home appreciation (dFX>0) cuts the home value of FX-denominated net assets, so for a net creditor it reduces NFA and for a net debtor (nfa0<0) it raises NFA (FX-denominated net liabilities shrink).
  5. Roll the stock forward (the BoP stock identity), quarterly: NFAₜ = NFA₋₁ + CAₜ/4 + valₜ (externalAccounting.ts:112). The current account is an annual ratio so it enters as a quarter-share (/4); the valuation effect is already this quarter's.
  6. Derived reporting variable: Δ(NFA/GDP)ₜ = NFAₜ − NFA₋₁ (externalAccounting.ts:119).

The recursion returns { tb, income, ca, valuation, nfa, dNfa }, all length H.

docs.title: Anchors and which blocs have an external block
docs.text:
The seed nfa0 is never invented; it is a real, sourced net-IIP/GDP figure (per the absolute no-synthetic rule). A bloc whose IIP cannot be verified, or whose trade flows and GDP are not in a common scale, ships without a chartable external_meta.json and renders no external charts.

Bloc External block? nfa0 (% GDP) Source TB anchor (h0) US Yes (renders) −66.75 FRED IIPUSNETIQ (US Net IIP, −21,270,237 Mil USD, 2026-01-01) / FRED GDP (nominal SAAR, 31,865.721 Bil USD, 2026Q1). US = large net debtor. −3.80% (deficit; published −3 to −4% range) EA Yes (renders) +11.0 ECB official release "Euro area quarterly balance of payments and international investment position: Q4 2025" — net assets EUR 1.76 tn = 11.0% of EA GDP. EA ≈ small net creditor. +3.59% (modest surplus, net exporter) CN Yes (renders, via unit reconciliation) +20.35 SAFE "China's IIP at end-2025" — net external assets USD 4,071.3 bn; NBS 2025 nominal GDP CNY 140.1879 tn = USD 20.01 tn (implied CNY/USD 7.006) ⇒ +20.35% GDP. Cross-check BoFiT ~20%. CN = net creditor. +4.46% (goods-trade surplus; published ~4–5% range) JP, UK, CA No (no external_meta.json) — IIP not yet verified for these blocs (flagged) —

So US, EA and CN are the blocs that render the external block. CN required a units reconciliation (see caveat 5) because its trade flows and GDP are in incompatible native units; with the real, sourced conversion factors it now ships externalChartable:true. JP / UK / CA ship no external_meta.json at all (loader's Promise.allSettled resolves 404 → null → nothing renders, no band work).

The iNFA (~3% p.a.) and fxShare (~0.4) parameters are modeling defaults shipped clearly flagged (the _flags key in each external_meta.json), exactly like the structural-constant SFA in the fiscal block — they are not estimated or sourced.

docs.title: Honest caveats (read before relying on the output)
docs.text: These are documented honestly because the tool is a live research instrument.

docs.title: 1. The trade balance is a real-VOLUME balance, not the nominal customs CA (US, EA)
docs.text: For US and EA the trade flows and GDP are in a common real scale (US real chained Bil USD; EA real, common units), so the ratio is a real-trade-volume / real-GDP balance. It validates against the published nominal BoP trade balance to within a relative-price wedge (US ≈ −3.8%, EA ≈ +3.5%), but it is not the nominal customs/BoP current account to the decimal. Terms-of-trade swings between export and import prices are not captured because the BVAR carries real volumes, not separate export/import deflators. Treat the levels as economically faithful, the high-frequency wiggles as volume-driven.

docs.title: 2. Primary income is IMPUTED, not estimated (iNFA)
docs.text: The primary-income leg of the current account is income = iNFA·NFA₋₁ with a modeling-default imputed return iNFA ≈ 3% p.a. — it is not an estimated BVAR variable and not sourced. It is flagged in external_meta.json (_flags), like the structural SFA. Because income compounds on the lagged NFA stock, a more-negative NFA path mechanically produces a more-negative income drag (and vice versa); this is a deliberate, transparent channel, but it is a parameterized assumption, not data.

docs.title: 3. FX valuation uses an external parameter (fxShare)
docs.text: The valuation (revaluation) effect val = −fxShare·NFA₋₁·ΔFX uses a modeling-default FX-exposed share fxShare ≈ 0.4 — the fraction of the net IIP denominated in foreign currency. It is not sourced and is flagged like iNFA. The sign convention (home appreciation cuts the home value of FX net assets) means a fiscal expansion that appreciates the currency can, for a net debtor (nfa0<0, e.g. US), produce a positive valuation gain (FX-denominated net liabilities shrink) — an economically correct J-curve/valuation channel, kept ON in the live charts. The unconfounded twin-deficit signal is therefore read off the trade/CA flow in the medium-term window, not the long-horizon NFA stock, whose dynamics are valuation-rich.

docs.title: 4. nfa0 is the EXTERNAL seed — real, sourced, per bloc
docs.text: The NFA recursion is only as anchored as its seed. nfa0 is a real net-IIP/GDP figure sourced per bloc (US: FRED IIPUSNETIQ/GDP; EA: ECB Q4-2025 IIP release; CN: SAFE end-2025 IIP). It is never invented. If a bloc's IIP cannot be verified it ships without an external block (JP/UK/CA today). After any re-estimation that changes a bloc's variable order, re-verify the positional idx in external_meta.json (exports/imports/gdp/deflator/fx) — the indices are positional and not auto-regenerated, the same staleness hazard the fiscal block flags.

docs.title: 5. CN units reconciliation — incompatible native units brought to a common nominal-USD ratio
docs.text:
CN is the one bloc whose trade flows and GDP are not in a common native scale, so the raw level paths cannot be ratioed directly (the un-converted TB/GDP is off by ~10⁵). The mismatch is purely one of units and frequency, not of missing data, so it is resolved by a small set of real, sourced conversion factors (units.* in external_meta.json) — no FX rate or flow is fabricated.

Updated (surgical CN-only real-GDP re-estimate, 2026-06). GDP_CN is now a real chain-linked volume index (not nominal CNY), so the GDP denominator is reconstructed in nominal USD via the gdpKind:'realIndexDeflate' branch: gdp$ = exp(gdp/100)·exp(deflator/100)·usdBase, with a single calibrated usdBase = 158760958.8679 scalar (CN's external block carries tradeFreqMult: 3 and gdpKind:'realIndexDeflate'). This pins the CN goods-trade balance to the published +4.46% of GDP at the historical anchor. The legacy nominal-CNY path below (gdpScale/fxToUsd) is retained for historical reference only and is no longer the active conversion for CN. This was a CN-only graft; the other 32 GVAR blocs are byte-identical to the prior working model.

The legacy native units, verified from the last historical level row (exp(level/100)):

Series Native unit exp(level/100) (last row) Conversion GDP_CN (legacy nominal) nominal CNY, millions, quarterly 35,984,541 (≈ 36 tn CNY/quarter) gdpScale=1e6 (millions → ones), then ÷ fxToUsd (CNY → USD) EXP_CN nominal USD, monthly-average flow 314,673,049,926 (≈ $315 bn/month) × tradeFreqMult=3 (monthly → quarterly) IMP_CN nominal USD, monthly-average flow 239,543,292,627 (≈ $240 bn/month) × tradeFreqMult=3 (monthly → quarterly)

The factors:

  • usdBase = 158760958.8679 (active) — realIndexDeflate scalar that maps the real chain-linked exp(gdp/100)·exp(deflator/100) product into nominal USD, calibrated so the CN trade balance lands at the published +4.46% of GDP at the anchor.
  • gdpScale = 1e6 (legacy nominal path) — when GDP_CN was in millions of CNY; multiplying exp(gdp/100) by 1e6 put the denominator in ones (CNY), matching the trade flows in ones (USD).
  • fxToUsd = 6.7686 (legacy nominal path) — the real CNY/USD spot rate; GDP-in-CNY ÷ this = GDP-in-USD. Sourced from FRED series DEXCHUS, latest non-missing observation 2026-06-18. Cross-confirmed against its primary upstream source — the Federal Reserve H.10 Foreign Exchange Rates release (federalreserve.gov/releases/h10/current, release dated 2026-06-22, China spot for 2026-06-18), a byte-exact match. Not synthetic.
  • tradeFreqMult = 3 — EXP_CN/IMP_CN are monthly-average USD flows while GDP is quarterly; ×3 quarterizes the flows so numerator and denominator share a frequency.

Applied to the recursion (externalAccounting.ts):

gdp$  = exp(gdp/100) · gdpScale / fxToUsd          // CNY-millions → USD
exp$  = exp(exports/100) · tradeFreqMult           // monthly-USD → quarterly-USD
imp$  = exp(imports/100) · tradeFreqMult
TB    = 100 · (exp$ − imp$) / gdp$                 // % of GDP, common nominal-USD ratio

which gives, at the forecast anchor, TB ≈ +4.46% of GDP — squarely in China's published goods-trade surplus range (~4–5% of GDP). CN therefore now ships externalChartable:true and renders the external block on this common-USD ratio.

These factors are pure unit/frequency conversions, not data. Each native series keeps its own real sourced value; the factors only restate them in a single nominal-USD, quarterly scale. The default for any bloc that ships no units.* (US, EA) is gdpScale=fxToUsd=tradeFreqMult=1, so the US/EA paths are byte-identical to before this change (guarded by the byte-identity test). The CN factors are load-bearing — a test asserts that without them the ratio is absurd (>10³) — so a future editor must never drop them back to the raw level paths. The CN units behaviour is pinned by app/src/__tests__/compute/externalAccounting.test.ts.

docs.title: 6. The displayed NFA path is a PASSIVE accounting recursion (no debt/NFA feedback)
docs.text: Like the fiscal block's deployed computeFiscalBlock, the external recursion has no endogenous feedback from the NFA stock back onto the BVAR drivers (no NFA→FX risk premium, no NFA→trade adjustment). The trade flows and FX are debt-agnostic BVAR baselines; the income and valuation legs layer on the lagged stock with no double-counting. This is a transparent passive recursion, not a sustainability model — a possible future extension.

docs.title: Bands
docs.text: The external bands (TB / CA / NFA at q16/q84) are a faithful clone of the fiscal default-mode band: they are computed per-draw via computeExternalBlock on the unconditional level path, parallel-shifted by (scenarioMean − baselineMean), and injected into the three chart traces. They are cleared everywhere the fiscal bands are. The synthetic chart bvarIdx is 9100+si (distinct from the fiscal 9000+) with draggable:null, so the j-indexed conditioning / band / forecast loops skip it — the external path is strictly additive and the δc=0 + external no-op stays byte-exact with the fiscal no-op.

docs.title: Placement in one line
docs.text: A compact, interactive realization of the IMF FPP external sector: the BVAR forecasts the trade flows and the exchange rate, and a passive balance-of-payments stock-flow recursion rolls NFA/GDP forward from a real, sourced net-IIP seed (trade balance + imputed primary income + FX valuation). It is the external leg of the monetary–fiscal–external triad, built on the identical two-layer pattern as the fiscal block, shipped for US, EA and CN (CN via a real, sourced nominal-USD units reconciliation; JP/UK/CA pending verified IIP).

docs.title: Key references
docs.text: IMF, Balance of Payments and International Investment Position Manual (BPM6, the CA/IIP identity and valuation decomposition) · IMF Financial Programming (FPP, four-sector consistency) · ECB, Euro area balance of payments and international investment position (quarterly statistical releases) · Lane & Milesi-Ferretti, The External Wealth of Nations (net-IIP construction, valuation effects) · Obstfeld & Rogoff, Foundations of International Macroeconomics (the intertemporal current account and NFA accumulation) · Gourinchas & Rey, International Financial Adjustment (valuation channel of external adjustment).

docs.title: Fiscal Block — relation to the CBO and IMF FPP frameworks
docs.text: The fiscal block estimates the fiscal drivers inside the BVAR and derives the reporting variables (deficit, debt service, debt-to-GDP, gross financing needs) by accounting identity. This is the same division of labour used by CBO and by IMF Financial Programming (FPP) / debt sustainability — not a coincidence; the design is built on their shared backbone.

docs.title: The shared backbone
docs.text:
Both frameworks separate "forecast the macro + fiscal flows" from "derive debt and the balances by accounting." Neither lets a reduced-form model freely forecast the debt stock.

  • CBO runs a conditional BVAR (Minnesota/GLP shrinkage, hierarchical tightness — the same prior family this tool already uses; Conditional Forecasting With a Bayesian VAR, 2023) purely as a macro scenario generator; those paths feed a separate, simplified budget module that produces revenues, outlays, net interest, deficit, and debt. Budget accounting lives outside the BVAR.
  • IMF FPP is the four-sector (real / fiscal / monetary / external) consistency framework. The fiscal block forecasts revenue, expenditure, and financing, then imposes the government budget constraint and debt-dynamics identity to get debt, with a stock-flow-adjustment (SFA) residual closing it. The modern quarterly incarnation is the IMF QMFF (A Novel Quarterly Macroeconomic Forecasting Framework, 2026): a QPM core + FPP sectoral accounting, linkable to the IMF debt tools (DDT / DDT_GFN).

This tool is that division of labour: the BVAR (extended to carry the fiscal flow ratios) is the scenario generator; app/src/lib/compute/fiscalAccounting.ts is the budget/accounting module. We fuse CBO's "macro BVAR" and "budget module" into one interactive tool, with the accounting module implemented as the IMF FPP/DSA debt-dynamics recursion.

docs.title: Element-by-element mapping
docs.text:
Concept CBO IMF FPP / DSA This tool Macro determinants (g, π, rates) macro model + conditional BVAR real + monetary sectors existing BVAR vars (GDPH, JGDP, 2Y, +new 10Y) Revenue revenue module, elasticities to wages/profits/GDP revenue (% GDP) estimated BVAR flow revenue/GDP Primary spending mandatory + discretionary outlays primary expenditure (% GDP) estimated BVAR flow primexp/GDP Effective interest rate weighted-avg rate on outstanding debt (lags market) effective rate on debt estimated BVAR var EFFI Net interest / debt service eff. rate × debt held by public i_eff · d₋₁ derived int = i_eff·d₋₁ Primary balance deficit excl. net interest rev − non-interest exp derived pb = rev − primexp Overall deficit outlays − revenues −(pb − int) derived deficit = int − pb Debt path Debtₜ = Debt₋₁ + deficit + other means of financing dₜ = d₋₁(1+i)/(1+gⁿ) − pb + sfa derived recursion (the FPP/DSA equation) Snowball / (i−g) interest-vs-growth in long-run outlook Fiscal Monitor decomposition derived snowball decomposition Gross financing needs issuance to fund operations primary deficit + interest + amortization derived gfn SFA / "other means of financing" OMF line stock-flow adjustment the sfa soft-identity closure Conditioning on official/policy paths current-law & alternative scenarios WEO-consistent soft conditions the tool's scenario conditioning (drag driver paths)

The debt recursion is the IMF DSA / DDT–DDT_GFN equation — same (1+i)/(1+gⁿ) snowball, same SFA term, same primary-vs-overall-balance distinction and sign convention (primary balance positive = surplus). Both frameworks require debt service to use the effective average rate on the stock, not the policy/market rate — hence we estimate EFFI rather than reusing the 2Y.

docs.title: The debt recursion, precisely
docs.text:
This section pins the exact arithmetic the accounting module runs, so the manual and the code never drift. The authoritative implementation is app/src/lib/compute/fiscalAccounting.ts, which exposes two recursions:

  • computeFiscalBlock (fiscalAccounting.ts:158) — the live forecast/band path (called per draw from runGvarBandSolve.ts). It reads the drivers off the level-space path and rolls the debt identity forward. It does not apply the Bohn / risk-premium feedback (pb = revenue − primexp exactly; no γ/β term).
  • runFiscalRecursion (fiscalAccounting.ts:66) — the array-level core that adds the optional active debt-feedback levers (the Bohn fiscal-reaction-function γ and the risk-premium β).

⚠️ runFiscalRecursion has NO live UI/production caller. As of this writing it is invoked only from the test suite (__tests__/compute/fiscalAccounting.test.ts, __tests__/compute/fiscal-backcast.test.ts) — the γ/β-lever path is test-covered only, not wired into the production forecast flow (the band/forecast path goes exclusively through computeFiscalBlock). The feedback levers are documented and validated but currently dormant.

The roll-forward identity below is identical in both; the feedback terms (step 1's γ, step 2's β) apply only in runFiscalRecursion. Read the units block at the top of the file (fiscalAccounting.ts:7) before editing anything here.

docs.title: Inputs (the BVAR-estimated drivers)
docs.text:
Per forecast quarter t = 0 … H−1, read off the level-space forecast path:

  • revenue / GDP and primary expenditure / GDP — % of GDP, expressed as an annual ratio (the 4-quarter-sum over 4-quarter-nominal-GDP ratio the BVAR carries as a lin variable).
  • the effective rate EFFI (idx.effRate, % p.a., the average rate on the outstanding stock): the deployed form for all six fiscal blocs (US, EA, JP, UK, CA, CN), where interest = EFFI · d₋₁ / 100. The recursion also supports an estimated debt-service / GDP column (idx.debtService, % of GDP) — when present that is the endogenous interest and the effective rate is backed out of it — but idx.debtService is not in the deployed fiscal_meta.json of any bloc, so the live path takes the effective-rate branch.
  • stock-flow adjustment SFA (idx.sfa, % of GDP annual ratio) — read per-quarter off the level path when an SFA column is estimated, so the debt projection carries the deficit–debt discrepancy forward with its own dynamics and bands; otherwise a constant sfaDefault (or zeros).
  • nominal GDP growth gⁿ — built from the level path as gNomQ = Δ(real-GDP 100·log) + Δ(deflator 100·log) (% per quarter), i.e. ((levelPaths[t][idx.gdp] − prev[idx.gdp]) + (levelPaths[t][idx.deflator] − prev[idx.deflator]))/100 (fiscalAccounting.ts:169). The growth and rate are quarterized internally while the flow reporting variables stay annual.
