The seed d₀ (initial debt/GDP, %) and the feedback anchor d* (default d₀) come from fiscal_meta.json (d0).

docs.title: Per-quarter recursion (using lagged debt d₋₁ so the loop stays explicit)
docs.text:
With dₚᵣₑᵥ = d₀ at t=0 and dₚᵣₑᵥ = debt[t−1] thereafter (fiscalAccounting.ts:170):

  1. Primary balance pb_t = rev_t − primexp_t (live path, fiscalAccounting.ts:171), plus the optional Bohn fiscal-reaction-function + γ·(d₋₁ − d*) in runFiscalRecursion (fiscalAccounting.ts:98; γ default 0.03 ⇒ consolidation as debt rises above d*).
  2. Interest / debt service (% of GDP): int_t = EFFI_t · d₋₁ / 100 (deployed effective-rate branch, fiscalAccounting.ts:177), or int_t = dsvc_t directly when a debt-service column is estimated; runFiscalRecursion additionally adds the debt-level risk premium β·max(0, d₋₁ − d*)·d₋₁/100 (fiscalAccounting.ts:94–95, β default 0). The effective rate is backed out for the roll-forward and for display: i_eff_t = int_t · 100 / d₋₁ (fiscalAccounting.ts:178, 96).
  3. Roll the stock forward (the IMF DSA / FPP debt-dynamics identity), quarterly: d_t = d₋₁ · (1 + i_eff_t/400) / (1 + gⁿ_t/100) − pb_t/4 + sfa_t/4  (fiscalAccounting.ts:185). Here i_eff_t/400 is the quarterized effective rate (so d₋₁·(1+iQ) = d₋₁ + int_t/4), the /(1+gⁿ/100) term is the denominator that shrinks the ratio when nominal GDP grows, and the primary balance and SFA enter as quarter-shares (/4, since they are annual ratios).
  4. Derived reporting variables, all falling straight out of the same identity:
  5. overall deficit deficit_t = int_t − pb_t (fiscalAccounting.ts:188);
  6. implied change in the ratio Δ(debt/GDP)_t = d_t − d₋₁ (fiscalAccounting.ts:186, the chart's third-row derived variable);
  7. the snowball (automatic debt dynamics) snowball_t = [(1+iQ)/(1+gQ) − 1]·d₋₁ — the interest-minus-growth (i−g) contribution the IMF Fiscal Monitor decomposition isolates (fiscalAccounting.ts:190);
  8. gross financing needs gfn_t = deficit_t + amortization_t (fiscalAccounting.ts:191).

docs.title: SFA closure and the inverse map
docs.text:
The SFA is the soft-identity residual that closes the deficit-vs-debt gap. It is estimated as its own BVAR column (the idx.sfa path) and forecast with its own dynamics and bands; the deployed debt roll-forward, however, uses the structural constant sfaDefault (the long-run SFA mean), not the per-quarter forecast SFA column (computeFiscalBlock lines 192–200). The forecast column is dragged to spurious extremes by the δc long-run/consensus anchors' cross-response (e.g. UK −3.2→−8.2), which would collapse the projected debt path; the structural constant is the economically-correct long-run residual. The recursion is also invertible: requiredPrimaryBalance (fiscalAccounting.ts:217) solves the same equation for the primary balance that pins debt to a target path, pb_t = 4·[(d₋₁ + int_t/4)/(1 + gⁿ_t/100) − d_target,t] + sfa_t, which the tool feeds back as a conditioning constraint (revenue − primexp). Because effective rate and growth respond to the primary balance through the BVAR, the caller iterates invert → condition → re-read i,g → re-invert (1–2 passes converge).

The exact quarterly convention here is pinned by the historical back-cast test (reconstructed debt/GDP vs observed; fixture app/src/__tests__/fixtures/us_fiscal_backcast.json) — the identity error is ≈ 0 except for the modelled SFA, exactly the consistency check both CBO and FPP demand.

docs.title: Where we follow faithfully
docs.text:
• The two-layer separation (BVAR/flows + identity recursion) — the core of both frameworks.
  • Conditional forecasting itself: the tool's existing scenario engine (condition on a few driver paths → consistent forecast of the rest) is CBO's conditional-BVAR method and FPP's "official paths as soft information." The fiscal block just adds the accounting layer on top.
  • SFA soft-identity closure, primary/overall balance definitions, effective-rate debt service.

docs.title: Where we extend beyond the simplified versions
docs.text:
• Endogenous, jointly-estimated fiscal flows. CBO's budget module is largely elasticity-based; we let the BVAR's reduced form capture revenue/expenditure cyclicality jointly with the macro block — closer to the ECB mixed-frequency fiscal BVAR and the IMF QMFF than to CBO's simplest module.
  • Debt-feedback channels (documented; DORMANT — not in the live path) — a risk-premium channel i_eff += β·(d₋₁−d*) and a Bohn fiscal-reaction-function pb += γ·(d₋₁−d*) (γ≈0.03 default) are implemented only in runFiscalRecursion, which has zero production callers (test-suite only, see the ⚠️ note above). The deployed computeFiscalBlock path — the one wired to every displayed debt chart and band — runs with β=γ=0 and there is no UI control for them. So the live tool has no endogenous debt→primary-balance reaction (Bohn) and no debt→yield risk premium; the debt path is a passive accounting recursion. CBO's simplified debt-service tool likewise does not endogenize macro feedback; FPP/DSA usually handle it via scenarios. These channels are a possible future extension toward IMF SRDSF "snowball realism" and the Bohn / fiscal-fatigue sustainability literature; because debt is not a BVAR variable, î_eff/p̂b are debt-agnostic baselines, so the +β/+γ terms would layer on with no double-counting (and, using lagged debt, the recursion stays explicit) were they ever activated.

docs.title: Where we deliberately simplify
docs.text:
• No line-item decomposition — CBO splits revenue (individual income / payroll / corporate / excise) and outlays (Social Security / Medicare / Medicaid / discretionary). We estimate aggregate general-government revenue and primary expenditure; the BVAR subsumes the elasticities but not the components.
  • Not the full four-sector FPP — only the fiscal block plus its macro linkages through the BVAR's correlations (no monetary-survey or BoP identities). A fiscal-block extension of a macro BVAR, not a complete financial program.
  • SFA is a closure/scenario term, not a modelled valuation/FX/contingent-liability block (Phase 2/3).
  • No amortization/maturity profile for GFN, and no accrual↔cash bridge / mixed-frequency in v1.

docs.title: Known issues and limitations (flagged, not yet fixed)
docs.text: These are documented honestly because the tool is a live research instrument. They are flagged for fix, not resolved — read this section before relying on the affected output.

docs.title: 1. China debt: nominal-GDP inflation double-count (material)
docs.text:
The debt-snowball roll-forward shrinks the debt ratio by nominal GDP growth, built as gNomQ = Δ(real-GDP) + Δ(deflator) in 100·log space (fiscalAccounting.ts:169). For the five advanced fiscal blocs idx.gdp correctly points at a real GDP series, so — unlike China below — the growth term does not double-count prices. The deflator term, however, is a true GDP deflator only for the US (idx.gdp = GDPH, idx.deflator = JGDP, idx 7). The other four advanced blocs deflate with a consumer price index, not a GDP deflator (verified against each bloc's spec.json + fiscal_meta.json): EA GDP_EA real + HICP_EA (idx 6), JP GDP_JP real + CPI_JP (idx 7), UK GDP_UK real + CPI_UK (idx 8), CA GDP_CA real + CPI_CA (idx 8). Because real GDP already strips out prices, adding a CPI (rather than the matching GDP deflator) is a CPI-vs-deflator wedge in the nominal-growth term for EA/JP/UK/CA, not the double-price-counting error CN suffers.

For China this was previously wrong but is now fixed (surgical CN-only re-estimate, 2026-06). The CN model historically shipped a single nominal GDP series (spec.json name "CN Nominal GDP (quarterly)"), so the growth term became gNomQ = Δ(nominal GDP) + Δ(CPI) and added the price level twice — biasing the CN debt-to-GDP path. The CN block has since been re-estimated against real GDP: spec.json var 0 is now "CN Real GDP (quarterly, chain-linked SA volume index, base 2011Q1=100)", CN/fiscal_meta.json sets idx.gdpIsNominal = false, and the growth term is now the correct real-GDP + deflator nominal growth used by the advanced blocs. This was a CN-only graft — the surgical fix re-estimated and replaced only the CN bloc; the other 32 GVAR blocs are byte-identical to the prior working model (the earlier full 33-bloc re-run degraded them and was reverted). The CN debt-to-GDP path is now quantitative on the same footing as US/EA/JP/UK/CA.

docs.title: 2. Positional fiscal indices can go stale (fiscal_meta.json)
docs.text: computeFiscalBlock resolves every driver by integer column index from fiscal_meta.json (idx.gdp / deflator / revenue / primaryExp / effRate / sfa). Those indices are positional, and the MATLAB porters (export_bvar_country.m, export_bvar_gvar*.m, export_bvar_euro_fsap.m) do not regenerate fiscal_meta.json — it is maintained by separate scripts (build_fiscal_*.py, pipeline/fix_fiscal_meta_indices.mjs). So any change to a bloc's variable order silently desynchronizes the fiscal indices, and the recursion then reads the wrong columns. This was the cause of a recent EA/UK debt explosion (fixed in commit a42a2db, "Fix explosive fiscal debt (stale fiscal_meta indices)"). After any re-estimation that touches variable order, regenerate / re-verify fiscal_meta.json for every fiscal bloc before trusting the debt path.

docs.title: 3. Long-run anchor tilt does not filter non-finite draws
docs.text: The entropic long-run-anchor tilt (app/src/lib/compute/longRunTilt.ts) can silently empty the anchor band. computeLongRunMoments writes one moment per posterior draw with no finiteness check; a singular / near-unit-root draw returns a NaN steady state (longRunTilt.ts:145), and a single NaN moment poisons the soft-max weight sum in solveEntropicTilt → all weights become NaN → the weighted-quantile band collapses to empty with no warning. The fix is to drop non-finite draws before the dual solve. (This is the tilt machinery the fiscal block can be conditioned through, hence its relevance here.)

docs.title: 4. Conditional band uses the filtered (not smoothed) covariance
docs.text:
The conditional-forecast mean is the Durbin–Koopman smoothed path, but the band variance is read from the filtered covariance filteredP[t] (app/src/lib/compute/kalman.ts:404–407). The filtered covariance at quarter t does not yet incorporate information from a future pin, so for quarters preceding a forward conditioning point the band is overstated (wider than the true smoothed band). This applies to any conditioned fiscal driver pinned at a future date.

For the empirical out-of-sample density caveats (the GVAR predictive intervals are over-confident at short horizons — a separate, opposite issue from the conditioning band above), see the Empirical Evaluation page.

docs.title: Placement in one line
docs.text: A compact, interactive realization of CBO's "conditional-BVAR → budget-accounting" division of labour, with the accounting layer implemented as the IMF FPP/DSA debt-dynamics recursion (snowball + SFA). The displayed debt path is a passive accounting recursion; the active-debt-feedback and Bohn-reaction-function channels are documented and validated but dormant (test-only, no live caller) — a possible future extension. It sits methodologically between CBO's simplified budget module and the IMF's QMFF / DDT debt tools. The Step-1 historical back-cast (reconstructed debt/GDP vs observed, identity error ≈ 0 except modelled SFA) is exactly the consistency check both frameworks demand.

docs.title: Key references
docs.text: CBO, The Budget and Economic Outlook (current-law baseline, budget mechanics) · CBO, Conditional Forecasting With a Bayesian VAR (2023) · IMF, Fiscal Monitor (debt decomposition, snowball) · IMF, A Novel Quarterly Macroeconomic Forecasting Framework (QMFF, 2026) · IMF, DDT / DDT_GFN debt tools · IMF MAC SRDSF (realism, fan charts) · Bohn, The Behavior of U.S. Public Debt and Deficits (fiscal reaction function) · Favero & Giavazzi, Debt and the Effects of Fiscal Policy (debt feedback in fiscal VARs).

docs.title: Fiscal Debt/GDP Trajectory Audit — Big-6 Economies
docs.text:
Date: 2026-06-25 · Branch: feature/trend-cycle-longrun-targets Recursion source of truth: app/src/lib/compute/fiscalAccounting.ts — computeFiscalBlock is the single recursion wired to the displayed debt-to-GDP charts and bands (baseline, scenario, counterfactual, and band paths all go through it). It is a passive accounting recursion: no Bohn primary-balance reaction and no debt-driven risk premium (those live only in the test-only runFiscalRecursion, which has no production caller). There is no separate fiscalProjection.ts file. Law of motion (per quarter, % of GDP): d_t = d_{t-1}·(1+i_q)/(1+g_q) − pb_t/4 + sfa_t/4, with i_q = EFFI/400, g_q = nominal q/q GDP growth (real Δ + deflator Δ), pb = FREV − FPEXP, and the projection using the structural sfaDefault constant (not the noisy forecast SFA column). Snowball = d·(r−g)/(1+g). Debt RISES iff snowball − pb + sfa > 0 (primary deficit, and/or r>g, and/or positive SFA).

All numbers below were reconstructed from the shipped baseline_levels.json + historical_levels.json + fiscal_meta.json for each bloc, replaying the exact computeFiscalBlock recursion — not assumed. Default view ships with consensus anchors OFF (raw model), so the paths below are what users see today.

docs.title: 1. One-line verdict per economy
docs.text: Bloc d0 (2025Q4) Current path (5y / 20q) Root cause Fix US 122.57 FLAT (→121.77, −0.8pp) Snowball −4.4%/yr (g 7.4% ≫ r 3.7%) cancels a too-small −2.5% deficit Cut nominal g to ~3.9%, raise EFFI→5.0, widen deficit to −4.0% → rises to ~143–159 EA 87.85 RISING, too gentle (→93.21, +5.4pp) Favorable snowball −1.4%/yr masks the −2.2% deficit Raise EFFI→2.9, trim g→3.0–3.3 → steeper rise to ~98–100 JP 192.87 FLAT (→196.08, +3.2pp, humped) r≪g snowball −2.4%/yr + late primary surplus anchor Flip pb to −1.5% deficit, raise EFFI→2.0 → rises to ~211 UK 96.24 FALLING (→92.13, −4.1pp) Spurious primary surplus +2.7% (no FREV/FPEXP anchors) Add FREV 39 / FPEXP 41.5 (pb −2.5%), EFFI→4.0 → rises to ~108–112 CA 100.16 FALLING (→90.60, −9.6pp) Spurious surplus +3.1% + oversized SFA +3.19 Add FREV 40.5 / FPEXP 41.5 (pb −1.0%), EFFI→4.0, cut SFA→~0.5 → rises to ~105–108 CN 94.33 RISING, overshoots (→136.52, +42pp) FREV drifts below floor → −8.9% deficit; correct SFA +4.0 Raise FREV target → 25.5 (pb ~−6.5%) → rises to ~120–125 (IMF-augmented-consistent)

docs.title: 2.2 Annual decomposition (% of GDP per year)
docs.text:
Term Value Sign Note Snowball d·(r−g)/(1+g) −4.40 debt-reducing (DOMINANT) r−g = −3.69pp Primary balance pb = FREV−FPEXP −2.49 debt-increasing deficit, but too small vs CBO Structural SFA (sfaDefault) +1.75 debt-increasing held constant Net Δd/yr ≈ −0.16 flat snowball ≈ −(deficit+SFA)

Avg effective rate EFFI = 3.70%/yr; avg nominal growth g = 7.39%/yr; avg pb −2.49%.

docs.title: 2.3 Diagnosis
docs.text:
The engine keeps US debt flat because the snowball is implausibly favorable. Nominal g averages 7.39%/yr (RW/WN-prior-elevated inflation persistence; recent history is 5.4–5.9%) while EFFI only drifts from ~3.2% to ~4.1% (avg 3.70%), locking r−g = −3.69pp. This single term (−4.4%/yr) almost exactly cancels the deficit + SFA. Secondary error: the WN stationary priors revert revenue UP (~17.6% last-hist → ~19% mean) and primary expenditure to ~21.8%, so pb is only −2.5% — CBO is −3 to −4%.

This violates the CBO r→g convergence the most of any bloc: CBO has the effective rate climbing toward nominal g (r−g closing to ~0/slightly positive) as low-coupon debt rolls over; the model locks in r−g = −3.7pp. CBO 2025 Long-Term Budget Outlook has gross debt/GDP RISING ~100%→107% over 5y and to ~120%+ over 10y, with a primary deficit widening toward −4%+.

The current consensus preset in default_anchors.json is also wrong: US.FPEXP_GDP = 17.88 implies a 1.9% primary surplus (US primary spending is ~21–22% of GDP), and FREV_GDP is missing entirely.

docs.title: 2.4 Recommended anchors (terminal-display δc targets at H=20)
docs.text:
Direction is owned by r−g (driftSetter caveat 3), so a CBO-rising path needs BOTH the snowball and the deficit fixed — widening the deficit alone only lifts debt to ~128 because the −4.4%/yr snowball swamps it.

Anchor (US) Set to Units Effect GDPH (real growth) 1.9 % q/q ann nominal g ↓ to ~3.9–4.8% JGDP (deflator) 2.0 % q/q ann (with GDPH) EFFI 5.0 % p.a. r−g closes to ~0/+1pp (CBO convergence) FPEXP_GDP 21.5 % GDP with FREV → pb = −4.0% (CBO-sized) FREV_GDP 17.5 % GDP ADD (currently missing)

Resulting rising path (verified by recursion): with anchors approached over the horizon via the δc forward-pass (partial application, gMult~0.65 on g), debt/GDP rises monotonically 122.6 → ~125 → 128 → 132 → 137 → ~143 (+~20pp/5y). Drivers then read snowball ~−1 to 0%/yr (neutral), primary deficit −4%/yr, SFA +1.75/yr. Held at full steady-state values from t=0 the recursion reaches ~159; the realized path lands between, ~143. Keep SFA at sfaDefault = 1.752.

Milder, debt-held-by-public-style slope: EFFI:4.0, pb −3.0% (FPEXP 21.0 / FREV 18.0), g held ~4.5% → +12–15pp over 5y.

docs.title: 3. EA / JP / UK / CA / CN — compact table
docs.text: Bloc d0 Direction now (5y) Recommended treatment Target pb (%GDP) EA 87.85 Rising +5.4 (too gentle) EFFI* ↑ 2.16→2.9–3.0 (cheap ECB debt rolls over); g ↓ to ~3.0–3.3 (real 1.0 + infl 2.0); hold FREV ~45.8 / FPEXP ~48 → r−g→~0, rises to ~98–100* −2.0 (hold) JP 192.87 Flat +3.2 (humped) FLIP late surplus: FREV_GDP 35→33.5, FPEXP_GDP 33.3→35.0 (pb −1.5%); EFFI* ~1.0→2.0 (BoJ normalization, matches YIELD10_JP=2); keep g ~2.7%, keep SFA +2.23 → rises to ~211 −1.5 UK 96.24 Falling −4.1 KILL spurious surplus: ADD FREV_GDP=39.0, FPEXP_GDP=41.5 (pb −2.5%); EFFI 3.5→4.0 so r−g→+0.7pp; keep/trim SFA (3.15→~1.5) → rises to ~108–112 −2.5 CA 100.16 Falling −9.6 ADD FREV_GDP=40.5, FPEXP_GDP=41.5 (pb −1.0%); ADD EFFI=4.0; cut sfaDefault 3.19→~0.5 (oversized) → rises to ~105–108 −1.0 CN 94.33 Rising +42 (overshoots) RAISE FREV target ~22.3→25.5 (back to hist floor); keep FPEXP_GDP ~32.0–32.5; keep sfaDefault=4.017 (correct LGFV/augmented channel); keep EFFI ~1.4 / g ~5.5 → r−g ~−4pp → moderates to ~120–125 −6.5

docs.title: 4. How to apply it in the tool
docs.text:
Two mechanisms drive long-run targets, both feeding the δc forward-pass drift setter (driftSetter.ts): - app/public/data/default_anchors.json — the shipped consensus presets per bloc (keyed by variable name, e.g. FREV_GDP, FPEXP_GDP, EFFI, GDPH, JGDP, GDP_<C>, CPI_<C>/HICP_<C>). - The "long-run target" sliders in the UI — the same δc targets, settable interactively (terminal-display value at H=20; lin vars = level, e.g. % of GDP; log vars = % q/q annualized, e.g. growth/inflation/rate).

To put every big-6 debt/GDP on a realistic increasing trajectory, edit default_anchors.json as follows. (SFA changes are in each bloc's fiscal_meta.json sfaDefault, NOT in default_anchors.json.)

"US": { "GDPH": 1.9, "JGDP": 2.0, "UI": 2.3, "LR": 4.2,
        "EFFI": 5.0, "FPEXP_GDP": 21.5, "FREV_GDP": 17.5 },   // FIX FPEXP 17.88→21.5, ADD FREV
"EA": { /* + */ "EFFI": 2.9, "GDP_EA": 1.0 /* trims nominal g */ },
"JP": { "GDP_JP": 0.7, "CPI_JP": 2, "LR_JP": 2.5, "YIELD10_JP": 2,
        "FPEXP_GDP": 35.0, "FREV_GDP": 33.5, "EFFI": 2.0 },    // FLIP 33.3/35 → 35/33.5, ADD EFFI
"UK": { "GDP_UK": 1.3, "CPI_UK": 2, "POLRATE_UK": 2.75, "LR_UK": 4.5, "YIELD10_UK": 3.75,
        "EFFI": 4.0, "FREV_GDP": 39.0, "FPEXP_GDP": 41.5 },    // EFFI 3.5→4.0, ADD FREV+FPEXP
"CA": { "GDP_CA": 1.7, "CPI_CA": 2, "LR_CA": 6,
        "EFFI": 4.0, "FREV_GDP": 40.5, "FPEXP_GDP": 41.5 },    // ADD EFFI+FREV+FPEXP
"CN": { "GDP_CN": 4, "POLRATE_CN": 3, "FPEXP_GDP": 32.0, "FREV_GDP": 25.5 }  // ADD FREV, FPEXP 29.1→32.0
Plus fiscal_meta.json SFA edit: CA sfaDefault: 3.191 → ~0.5. Keep US 1.752, EA 0.325, JP 2.233, UK 3.149 (or trim to ~1.5), CN 4.017.

Why these levers: the debt direction is governed by r−g plus the primary balance. For US/JP the binding fix is raising EFFI toward g (collapsing the free-lunch snowball) and widening/flipping the deficit; for UK/CA it is adding the missing FREV/FPEXP anchors to kill the spurious WN-prior surplus (the single highest-leverage fix); for EA it is steepening an already-rising path; for CN it is moderating an over-steep but correctly-signed path by anchoring revenue.

docs.title: 5. Honesty flag — where rising debt would be UNrealistic
docs.text:
• JP (level). The model's d0 ≈ 193% sits on the GG-gross perimeter, below the ~250% headline. The recommended treatment makes the direction realistic (gently rising, IMF Art-IV-consistent: deficit worsening, interest costs doubling by 2030). But do not read the level as the headline 250% — match direction, not level. Forcing a steeper rise than ~+3.7pp/yr would overstate vs IMF.
  • CN (level/perimeter). d0 = 94.3 and pb = −8.9% sit on the augmented-leaning side of the two IMF perimeters (GG ~88%→110% by 2029 vs augmented ~124%→150%+). The current +42pp overshoots even the augmented GG path; the recommendation moderates it to ~120–125 (between GG and full augmented). A larger rise here would be unrealistic — the SFA already encodes the LGFV channel, so do not also widen the deficit.
  • EA aggregate masks dispersion. The +5.4pp rise is the EA-aggregate; it hides IT/FR (~135/115% rising) vs DE (~63% falling). The 4-var block does not model the SGP/debt-brake consolidation channel — treat the EA rise as an unconstrained aggregate, not a rules-consistent forecast.
  • General caveat. "Realistic rising" means matching the benchmark sign and slope (CBO for US, IMF Fiscal Monitor / WEO for the rest), NOT mechanically forcing every bloc up. EA, JP, CN already rise (CN strongly); only US/UK/CA require flipping a flat/falling artifact, and those flips are anchor-driven corrections of over-optimistic WN-prior means, not fabrications.

docs.title: Live Smell Test — Deployed BVAR Scenario Tool (51 economies)
docs.text:
Branch: improve-live @ 89c4501 (LIVE deployed) · Model: RW-BVAR, no target-reversion, no Taylor reaction · Horizon: h20 (terminal display) · Read-only diagnostic.

This document grades every deployed economy on the central (terminal) forecast against economic-plausibility bands and confirms whether the UK/CA pathologies flagged by the user are systemic across the model set. All values are terminal-h forecasts. log columns are q/q-ann %, lin columns are levels/rates. Snowball = r − g where r = EFFI if a fiscal block exists, else the policy rate.

Legend: ✅ = within plausibility band · ⚠️ = smell (drift / non-anchoring / no-reaction artifact). Smell thresholds: inflation >3.5% (advanced) / off-target (EM) = not-anchoring · real rate < −0.5 = no-Taylor · snowball < −1.5 = debt-falls-implausibly.

docs.title: 2. Systemic Findings
docs.text: The UK/CA pattern flagged in the brief is not idiosyncratic — it is the dominant failure mode of the deployed model. Counts over the 51 economies:

docs.title: Inflation not anchoring (terminal >3.5% advanced / clearly off-target EM)
docs.text: 25 of 51 (49%) smell on inflation: AU(3.07, above 3% band), CA, CL, CN(deflation −1.21), CO, CZ, EA, EE, HU, IE, IS, IT(too-low 0.69), LT, LU, LV, MX, NZ, PL, RU, SI, SK, TR, UK, US, plus CR/ID/FI on the low side (excluded from the hard count). Of these, the inflation drifts UP and stays high (the RW non-reversion signature) in ~20; a smaller cluster (CN, IT, AT, FI) drifts implausibly low/deflationary — confirming the drift runs in both directions, not just upward.

docs.title: Negative real rate (i − π < −0.5, no Taylor reaction)
docs.text: 21 of 51 (41%): AT(−1.02), CA(−0.93), CH(−1.02), DE(−2.19), DK(−2.00), EA(−1.07), EE(−4.73), IL(−0.66), JP(−1.62), KR(−0.74), LT(−10.09), LU(−4.09), LV(−1.31), NO(−0.60), NZ(−1.87), PT(−0.50), SE(−0.50), SI(−1.90), SK(−2.85), UK(−1.74). (US escapes — its policy rate happens to stay above inflation.) Where a credible nominal-rate path exists it is by luck of the frozen terminal level, not policy logic.

docs.title: Debt snowball << −1.5 (debt falls implausibly)
docs.text: 30 of 51 (59%) — the single most pervasive smell: AU, CA, CH, CL, CN, CZ, DE, DK, EA, EE, FR, ID, IE, IL, IN, JP, KR, LT, LU, LV, MY, NL, NO, NZ, PT, SA, SE, SI, SK, TW, UK, US (32 by the strict −1.5 cut; ~30 are the implausible-direction subset after netting EM growth-driven cases). For high-debt sovereigns where consensus is rising (FR d0~116, JP, EA, UK, US, NL, PT) a sustained r−g << −1.5 is squarely implausible. Three small EA members (EE −8.26, SI −8.69, LT −9.97) show outright debt collapse.

docs.title: Cross-cutting
docs.text:
• 17 economies carry all three core nominal smells (non-anchored inflation and negative real rate and snowball << −1.5) or the EA-common-rate equivalent: CA, CH, DE-adjacent, EA, EE, LT, LU, LV, NZ, SI, SK, UK among them. These are the textbook RW-no-reversion cluster.
  • Real-side drift (no mean reversion in growth/unemployment) is the secondary failure: growth flatlines near 0 (AR, AT, GR −0.94, IT −0.22, HU −0.46) or runs implausibly hot (SA ~8, MY ~6.9, TW ~4.9, US 3.45, IN 7.86); unemployment drifts to absurd levels (SA → 0.1, FI 11.6, GR 12.4 drifting up, CH 6.2 vs true ~2-3).
  • The model is internally honest only where the data's terminal level happens to be sensible (BE, ES, TH, ZA, BR, MX, ID-rates) — i.e. the "clean" blocs pass by coincidence of the frozen RW endpoint, not by any anchoring mechanism.

Verdict: the UK/CA observation is systemic. ~half the set fails inflation-anchoring, ~40% show negative real rates, and ~60% show implausibly favorable debt dynamics — all traceable to the same two missing mechanisms.

docs.title: 3. Worst Offenders
docs.text:
Ranked by number and severity of smells:

  1. EE (Estonia) — worst in set. Inflation 4.68 (volatile −3.81→+4.68, no anchor), real rate −4.73, snowball −8.26 → debt collapses from d0~24. Every band fails except unemployment.
  2. SI (Slovenia) — inflation drifts back UP to 5.71 (no glide to 2%), growth 5.2 >> potential, real rate −1.9, snowball −8.69 → debt collapse.
  3. LT (Lithuania) — inflation 10.27 oscillating (−13 to +14), real rate −10.09, snowball −9.97, nominal growth 12.4 absurd. Pure RW artifact contaminating everything.
  4. SK (Slovakia) — worst-anchored EA inflation 7.32, real rate −2.85, snowball −6.87.
  5. CA (Canada) — the brief's textbook case. Inflation 3.69 sticks, policy 2.77 < π → real rate −0.93, snowball −1.97 → debt falls vs CA consensus.
  6. UK (United Kingdom) — canonical pathology. Inflation 4.35 never glides to 2% BoE target, real rate −1.74, EFFI 3.32 << nominal growth 6.39 → snowball −3.07, debt falls vs consensus-rising.
  7. US (United States) — inflation 4.13 drifts up, growth 3.45 above potential, nominal growth 7.58 >> EFFI 4.16 → snowball −3.41; debt-fall masked by SFA drift to +14pp.
  8. EA (Euro Area aggregate) — inflation 3.32 RISES through horizon, real rate −1.07, snowball −3.12.
  9. NZ (New Zealand) — inflation RISES monotonically 2.83→5.45, real rate −1.87, snowball −3.87.
  10. CH / DK / DE — the negative-rate cluster: frozen near historical negative-rate regimes (CH −0.43, DK −0.51, DE 0.71 vs 2.9 π), real rates −1.0 to −2.2, snowballs −2.8 to −4.0.

Opposite-direction offenders (drift to a bad equilibrium): GR (growth −0.94, unemployment drifts 8.4→12.4, snowball +1.29 = debt RISING vs consensus-falling), IT (inflation 0.69, growth −0.22, nominal growth ~0), SA (unemployment → 0.1). These prove the drift is not a one-sided upward-inflation bias but a generic absence of any attractor.

docs.title: Root cause
docs.text:
The deployed model is a random-walk-in-levels BVAR with a Minnesota/RW prior whose constant row acts as a drift, not a steady-state intercept. Two specific absences produce every smell above:

  1. No target-reversion for inflation. With a unit-root companion and no long-run mean, the terminal forecast is whatever the recent sample drifted to. Nothing pulls π toward 2% (advanced) or the EM target → inflation sticks high (UK, CA, NZ, EA…) or drifts low (CN deflation, IT) with equal ease.
  2. No monetary reaction function. The policy rate is a free RW series, not a function of the inflation/output gap. When π drifts up, the rate does not follow → systematically negative real rates (21 economies).

These compound in the debt block: high un-anchored nominal growth − a frozen-low EFFI/policy rate → snowball << −1.5 → debt falls implausibly (30 economies). The real-side analog (no reversion in growth/unemployment) gives the flatline/hot-growth/unemployment-drift smells.

docs.title: Lightweight LIVE-version fix options
docs.text:
(Not the full trend-cycle rebuild — that is parked on feature/trend-cycle-longrun-targets.) These are bolt-ons to the deployed RW-BVAR, ordered by effort:

  • (A) Per-country long-run prior tilt (cheapest, post-estimation). Entropic/PLR tilt of the existing posterior draws toward a per-country terminal target vector (π = target, i = π + neutral real rate, g = potential, u = NAIRU). Lives entirely in the projection layer; no re-estimation. Risk per MEMORY/PLR-vs-tilt: joint multi-constraint tilt degenerates (ESS→1) at 500 draws — apply per-constraint* (inflation-only, then rate) rather than a joint 13-moment tilt.
  • (B) Taylor-rule overlay on the rate path (targeted). Replace the free RW rate forecast with a reaction rule i_t = r* + π_t + φ_π(π_t − π*) + φ_y·ỹ_t evaluated on the model's own π/output draws. Fixes the 21 negative-real-rate blocs and feeds a sane r into the snowball, without touching the macro block. Cheapest fix for the debt-falls-implausibly smell on rate-proxy economies.
  • (C) Target-reverting inflation prior (medium, in-prior). Add a soft long-run mean (a "sum-of-coefficients"-style or explicit mean-reversion dummy) so π glides to π* over the horizon. This is the most economically honest of the bolt-ons but requires a (cheap) re-estimation per country.
  • (D) Combination A+B (tilt the macro terminal targets + Taylor-overlay the rate) addresses inflation-anchoring, negative-real-rate, and debt-snowball simultaneously with no macro-block re-estimation — the recommended interim while the trend-cycle rebuild matures.

Recommendation (original, superseded — see the status update below): ship (B) Taylor overlay first (mechanical, no re-estimation, kills the negative-real-rate + a large share of the snowball smells), then layer (A) per-constraint inflation tilt for anchoring. Reserve (C) for the next re-estimation cycle. Defer to the parked trend-cycle VAR for the structural cure.

Status update (implemented). Option (A) shipped as a deterministic δc drift overlay, not the entropic tilt — per-variable "Long-run targets" sliders that shift the companion drift so a variable's terminal forecast hits a user target (instant, exact, band-coherent; no re-estimation, no draw reweighting). It replaces the tilt (which degenerated, ESS→1) and sidesteps the per-constraint workaround entirely. The policy rate carries its own long-run-target slider like every other variable — set its terminal value directly through the drift setter. Option (B) Taylor overlay was not retained: an early taylorOverlay policy-rate-row toggle was shipped briefly and then removed (commit 9142265) — it was confusing and inert next to the rate's own slider; no taylorOverlay/setTaylorOverlay symbol remains. The perverse/near-singular policy-rate sliders (BE, CA, CZ) are instead disabled + labelled (the |sens| < 0.3 guard), not routed through any overlay. See MAINTAINER_OPERATIONS.md §G4 and USER_GUIDE.md §14. Option (C) (in-prior reversion) and the structural trend-cycle rebuild remain the next-cycle work.

docs.title: MP Committee — Live Demo Script (15 min, laptop only, no slides)
docs.text: A timed, click-by-click runbook for the live tool demo. Each segment gives the exact clicks, the one-line econometric point to say out loud, and the expected on-screen result. Language rule: every conditional path is "the most likely projection given the conditioning assumptions" (a Waggoner–Zha / DK conditional forecast). Never say "entropic tilting."

docs.title: Pre-flight checklist (do this before the room)
docs.text:
• [ ] Tool open in the browser, live build loaded (production bvarscenario/main).
  • [ ] Country = United States (top-right Country dropdown, GVAR optgroup; US is the default on load).
  • [ ] Units toggle = "q/q ann." — NOT "% vs baseline" (that view is read-only and disables dragging).
  • [ ] Anchors confirmed ON: left ControlPanel → "Long-run targets" header reads "(7 set)". If the debt path looks flat, anchors haven't seeded yet (async fetch of default_anchors.json) — refresh and wait a beat.
  • [ ] Clean scenario: ControlPanel header → "Reset all" → "Yes, reset all" once, so no stray pins and spillover switches are OFF.
  • [ ] Know where the financial charts live: 10Y (GLB_USLR), Baa (GLB_USBAA), oil (GLB_OIL), equity (GLB_USEQ) are in the "Global commons" section near the bottom. Aaa corporate (FAAA) and domestic WTI (PZTEXP) are in the "Country-specific variables" section. Scroll down once now so you can find them fast.
  • [ ] Between cases use "Reset" (clears this scenario's pins, keeps anchors). Only use "Reset all" when you want spillover switches OFF too.

docs.title: (0:00–0:30) — What it is
docs.text: "This is a multi-country Bayesian VAR forecasting tool with a fiscal satellite and a GVAR cross-country layer. Everything you'll see is live: I open the central projection, then build scenarios by conditioning, and export the whole thing to Excel."

docs.title: Monetary block (0:30–2:00)
docs.text:
Clicks: The grid is already on US. Row 1 (macro triad) holds Inflation (source JGDP, "GDP Chain Price Index"), Policy Rate (POLRATE, "Effective Federal Funds Rate"), and GDP Growth (GDPH).

Say (one-liner — rates vs levels): "Price indices like JGDP are modeled as 100·log price levels with a Minnesota / Litterman random-walk prior; what you see on the Inflation chart is a display transform — 4·Δ of that log level, i.e. q/q annualized. The policy rate and yields, by contrast, are modeled directly as levels in percent. So inflation is a transform of a modeled price level; the policy rate is a modeled level itself."

Also say (prior + COVID, one line each): "Estimation is GLP — Giannone–Lenza–Primiceri — Minnesota prior shrinking toward a random walk. And the 2020 COVID quarters are treated as missing data, imputed inside the Gibbs sampler, so the pandemic outliers don't contaminate the dynamics."

Expected on screen: Inflation glides to ~2%, policy rate toward neutral. Frame it: "the central projection with long-run targets anchored to consensus" — the consensus long-run anchors (the δc forward-pass: inflation→2%, policy→neutral, fiscal→OBR/IMF/CBO) are ON by default and are a deterministic drift, separate from any scenario conditioning. (If asked to show the raw BVAR: in a panel click "reset all to estimate" — inflation/debt revert to the drifting RW path; re-apply with "Apply consensus anchors.")

docs.title: Fiscal satellite (2:00–3:15)
docs.text:
Clicks: Scroll to Row 2 — the 4 estimated BVAR fiscal variables, each its own chart: Revenue (FREV_GDP), Primary Expenditure (FPEXP_GDP), Effective Interest Rate (EFFI), Stock-Flow Adjustment (SFA). Directly under them: the header "Fiscal block — derived by accounting identity" with 3 derived charts: Primary Balance, Δ Debt-to-GDP, Debt-to-GDP.

Say (one-liner — the satellite): "Government debt is not a BVAR variable. The BVAR estimates only these four — revenue, primary spending, the effective interest rate on the stock, and the stock-flow-adjustment residual. Debt is derived by rolling them through the budget identity: dₜ = dₜ₋₁·(1+i)/(1+g) − pb/4 + sfa/4, seeded at d₀ = 122.6% of GDP."

Say (the snowball, one line): "(1+i)/(1+g) is the snowball — when the effective rate exceeds nominal growth, debt rises even with a balanced primary budget; pb = revenue − primary spending is the discretionary effort; SFA is the deficit-debt residual — below-the-line and valuation items the headline deficit misses."

Expected on screen: Debt-to-GDP rises realistically (not a flat degenerate path) — because the consensus anchors hold inflation at ~2%, the effective rate near neutral (EFFI 5.0), and revenue/spending at CBO ratios. The four WN-prior fiscal ratios mean-revert (revenue ~17.5%) rather than drift, which is what keeps the path defensible.

docs.title: Excel export (3:15–4:00)
docs.text:
Clicks: First set Uncertainty = Quick (TopBar) and press Compute so the bands populate. Then on the bottom ScenarioBar click the Excel button.

Say: "It downloads forecast_US_<name>_<timestamp>.xlsx — one sheet per variable, columns Quarter | Date | Uncond. Q16 | Baseline (median) | Uncond. Q84 | scenario Q16 | scenario median | scenario Q84 | Deviation, plus a Summary sheet and a Notes sheet documenting each source variable and unit."

Expected on screen: File downloads; every monetary and fiscal variable (including derived Debt-to-GDP) has its own tab. (Scenario columns are blank until a scenario is computed — that comes next.)

docs.title: (4:00–8:00) — CASE 1: GFC corporate-yield shock (Waggoner–Zha)
docs.text: Story: pull the corporate yield up. First without pinning the govt 10Y (flight-to-quality), then with it pinned unchanged (clean credit-spread shock).

docs.title: Step 1 — corporate up, 10Y FREE (4:00–6:00)
docs.text:
Clicks: Scroll to "Country-specific variables" → Moody's Seasoned Aaa Corporate Bond Yield (FAAA). Click the panel's "condition" button (turns blue → "CONDITIONED"). Drag the line UP ~+150–200 bp in the early quarters (t+1..t+4), or right-click a quarter to plant the bullet exactly at the cursor value. Do not touch the 10Y. Press Compute (Quick).

Say (one-liner): "Only the corporate yield is pinned, so the Treasury 10Y is free. This is the most likely projection given that one conditioning assumption — and the model pulls the risk-free 10Y down: flight-to-quality, with output and inflation softening. A recession read."

Expected on screen: Corporate yield up where you pinned it; the 10Y (GLB_USLR, Global commons) drifts down; growth/inflation soften.

docs.title: Step 2 — ADD 10Y held UNCHANGED (6:00–8:00)
docs.text:
Clicks: Scroll to "Global commons" → 10-Year Treasury Note Yield (GLB_USLR; conditionable because Country==US). Click its "condition" button but do not move the line — a fresh condition starts at the baseline (zero-deviation), which is "held unchanged." Press Compute again.

Say (one-liner): "Now both yields are pinned — corporate up, risk-free curve held flat. That isolates a pure credit-spread / financial-conditions shock with no flight-to-quality offset. The clean scenario."

Expected on screen: Corporate up, 10Y flat at baseline; the spread blowout is now the whole story. (Tip: per-quarter sliders in the left ControlPanel "Conditioning" section let you pin only the early impact window and leave the tail free; "All" toggles all 20 quarters.)

docs.title: (8:00–11:30) — CASE 2: EA-yield shock → US & global spillovers (GVAR)
docs.text:
"Reset, switch to the Euro Area, shock the same yield there, and watch it reach the US through the cross-country coupling."

Clicks: 1. ControlPanel header → "Reset all" → "Yes, reset all" (clean, not stacked on the GFC; also returns spillover switches to OFF — we turn them back on deliberately). 2. Top-bar Country dropdown → Euro Area (GVAR-linked section). 3. Shock the same yield you used for the US: EA 10Y Gov Bond Yield (YIELD10_EA) for the govt version, or EA IG Corporate Bond Yield (EACORP) for the corporate version. Right-click the chart at the target quarter/level (right-click conditions AND plants the bullet in one step), then drag up ~+100–150 bp across the first few quarters. 4. Left ControlPanel → "Cross-border spillovers" → set "Scenario spillovers (GVAR solve)" to On. 5. Click "▶ Solve full GVAR" for the complete coupled fixed point (all feedback loops, RoW→US).

Say (one-liner): "The GVAR links blocs through three trade-weighted RoW star variables per country — foreign demand, foreign inflation, foreign short rate. The EA shock raises EA's contribution to every partner's stars, and the solver iterates the coupled fixed point x = Mx + d. A single-country EA BVAR would hold the US fixed; the GVAR lets EA tightening pull on US financial conditions and global activity. This is where the cross-country coupling shines."

Expected on screen (two read-outs): - Stay on EA and watch the in-model Global commons charts move: US 10Y (GLB_USLR), US BAA (GLB_USBAA), US equity / S&P 500 (GLB_USEQ), WTI oil (GLB_OIL). - Switch Country → United States — the EA pins persist across the switch (separate store slice), so the full US bloc (growth, inflation, policy rate, yields) shows the EA→US spillover even though the US has no own pins.

Gotcha to remember: without turning the spillover switch On (or pressing Solve full GVAR), EA moves alone and the US/global charts won't budge — this is the #1 thing to not forget. A full 50-bloc solve is ~4.5s warm; press once and let it land.

docs.title: (11:30–14:30) — CASE 3: US oil/energy — demand vs supply
docs.text:
"Reset first — this is NOT on top of the GFC. Condition only on higher oil → demand dominates. Then add short-term Cholesky-type sign restrictions to flip it to an adverse supply shock."

Setup clicks: Country back to United States. ControlPanel header → "Reset" (clears this scenario's pins; keeps the consensus anchors — exactly what we want). Confirm all "condition" buttons are grey.

docs.title: Step 1 — oil UP alone → DEMAND-dominant (11:30–13:00)
docs.text:
Clicks: Use the US-domestic oil series Spot Oil Price: West Texas Intermediate (PZTEXP, "Country-specific variables"). Click "condition", then drag the line UP to a higher level (e.g. ~\(70 → ~\)100). It's a level-display var, so you're setting a $/bbl level, not a growth rate — drag to the actual target price. Press Compute (Quick).

Say (one-liner): "With only oil pinned, the conditional solve reads the historical oil–output covariance as demand-driven — so US output rises with the oil price. The demand-dominant response."

Expected on screen: Oil at the pinned level; US output up.

docs.title: Step 2 — add sign restrictions → ADVERSE SUPPLY (13:00–14:30)
docs.text:
Clicks: Keep oil conditioned up. Now also condition GDP Growth (GDPH) and drag it DOWN at t+1..t+2; condition Inflation (JGDP) and drag it UP at t+1..t+2. Use the per-quarter sliders / "All"-off-then-enable-early so only t+1..t+2 are active and the tail stays free. Press Compute.

Say (one-liner): "The 'Cholesky-type' move here is multi-variable Waggoner–Zha conditioning that pins the early signs: output down, inflation up in the impact window. That forces the supply-shock identification — a stagflationary response — instead of the demand read. The later quarters stay free, so the model fills in the dynamics."

Expected on screen: Same oil rise, but now output down and inflation up early — stagflation. The contrast with Step 1 is the whole point.

docs.title: (14:30–15:00) — Close + Excel
docs.text:
Clicks: After the final Compute, hit the ScenarioBar Excel button once more.

Say: "Every case exports the same way — one sheet per variable, organized by date, with the unconditional baseline and ±1-s.d. band sitting next to the conditional path, band and deviation — including the satellite's derived Debt-to-GDP. The Summary tab lines all variables up. That's the full forecasting system: a central projection anchored to consensus, scenario conditioning on top, cross-country spillovers via the GVAR, and a one-click Excel hand-off."

docs.title: "If asked" — FAQ
docs.text:
• The prior? "Minnesota / Litterman, GLP (Giannone–Lenza–Primiceri) implementation, shrinking toward a random walk on the macro and financial variables. The three fiscal ratios — revenue, primary spending, SFA — instead carry a white-noise prior so they revert to a long-run mean rather than drift; that's what keeps revenue at ~17.5% and stops the debt path running away."
  • Weak exogeneity of the RoW stars? "Each bloc's three trade-weighted foreign aggregates (ROWDEM/ROWINF/ROWFIN) enter as weakly exogenous regressors in the country VAR — standard Pesaran-type GVAR identification. At solve time the blocs are coupled and the foreign aggregates become endogenous to the system; we solve the fixed point x = Mx + d (closed form (I−M)⁻¹d), so consistency holds across the coupled solution. The US is endogenous in the live system with its own RoW stars."
  • COVID treatment? "2020 quarters are treated as missing data, imputed inside the Gibbs sampler — data augmentation in cgl_bvarV5, not Lenza–Primiceri volatility scaling. The outliers never enter the likelihood for the dynamics or the prior."
  • Anchors on vs raw? "The consensus long-run anchors are a deterministic δc forward-pass drift to publicly-defensible targets (inflation→2%, policy→neutral, fiscal→OBR/IMF/CBO). They're on by default, separate from the Waggoner–Zha conditioning, and toggle off via the 'Long-run targets' panel ('reset all to estimate') to show the raw drifting RW-BVAR."
  • Does the BVAR forecast debt? "No — it forecasts the four fiscal drivers; the satellite accumulates debt by the budget identity. The debt/PB bands come from running the recursion per-draw."

docs.title: Refined boss-reply (4 sentences)
docs.text: The 15-minute script is written to docs/mp-presentation-prep.md and follows your exact flow: (0:30–4:00) the unconditional US central projection — monetary block (inflation as a 4·Δ display transform of a 100·log price level vs the policy rate modeled directly as a level, Minnesota/GLP prior, COVID-as-missing) and the fiscal satellite (4 BVAR drivers → snowball/primary-balance/SFA debt recursion, now rising realistically with consensus anchors on by default), plus the per-variable Excel export. Case 1 is the GFC corporate-yield Waggoner–Zha scenario — FAAA up with the 10Y free (flight-to-quality), then GLB_USLR pinned unchanged (clean credit-spread shock); Case 2 is the EA-yield shock (YIELD10_EA/EACORP) with the GVAR spillover switch and "Solve full GVAR" showing the US/global response via the trade-weighted RoW stars; Case 3 resets and runs US oil (PZTEXP) demand-dominant alone, then early-quarter output↓/inflation↑ sign restrictions to flip it to an adverse supply shock. Throughout it's framed as "the most likely projection given the conditioning assumptions" — never entropic tilting — with the anchors kept separate from the scenario layer, and it ends on the per-variable/per-date Excel hand-off. A pre-flight checklist (tool open, anchors-on confirmed, clean reset between cases) and an "if asked" FAQ (prior, weak exogeneity, COVID, anchors-on vs raw) are appended.

docs.title: Pre-ship smoke test — default δc long-run anchors (CONSOLIDATED, all economies)
docs.text:
What this is. The earlier docs/live-smell-test.md found the LIVE MFM (BVAR Scenario Tool, branch improve-live) is systemically implausible out of the box: the RW-prior baselines have a constant row that is a pure drift term, so the model has no steady state — the H=20 (5-year) terminal forecast is just "wherever the drift carried the level path." That produced inflation not anchoring (~49% of cases), negative terminal real rates (~41%), and a falling debt-snowball (~59%).

This file does the per-country × per-variable version and asks the operational question: does a single, simple, publicly-known, common-sense long-run anchor (one δc per variable) make the forecast sensible? If yes, that anchor is a recommended ship-with default the tool can present as a one-click preset. Method is the verbatim app/src/lib/compute/driftSetter.ts algebra (companion T from betahat, S_h=Σ_{k<h}Tᵏ, sens_j=4·(S_H−S_{H-1})[j,j] for log / S_H[j,j] for lin, δc_j=(anchor−terminal)/sens_j; glided terminal = anchor by construction). Replicated and the identity holds. READ-ONLY — no app/src or data files were modified.

The consolidated synthesis below covers all 46 graded blocs; the per-alphabet-band sections that follow (D–G, H–K, L–N, S–Z, A–C, plus PL/PT/RU folded in) are the full supporting detail.

docs.title: 1. Coverage scorecard (all 46 blocs, 227 graded variables)
docs.text:
metric value Blocs graded 46 (every bvar/<C> bloc except gvar; EA/EE/IE/IT not separately graded) Key variables graded 227 Resolved by one simple, public δc 219 / 227 = 96.5% Not resolved by a δc (structural / regime) 8 Additional ill-conditioned-but-finite sliders to flag (resolve, do not auto-ship) US POLRATE, JP POLRATE, SK EFFI, PT LENDR

A simple common-sense δc resolves essentially the entire smell test. Every flagged inflation-non-anchoring, every negative terminal real rate that lives on a well-behaved rate slider, every above/below-potential growth drift, and every snowball-from-π/g-drift is fixed by a single publicly-defensible long-run shift. The exceptions below are not drift — they are accounting identities or regimes a long-run intercept cannot move.

docs.title: The 8 that do NOT resolve via a δc — and why
docs.text:
# bloc:var category reason a long-run δc cannot fix it 1 UK debt direction primary-balance identity Debt falls because the terminal primary balance is a +3.1% surplus (rev 43.1 > pexp 40.0). The snowball does resolve via the π δc; the direction is an accounting fact the rev/pexp sliders are no-ops against. 2 SK primary deficit primary-balance identity Structural −6.0% primary deficit (pexp 50.4 ≫ rev 44.3) drives debt regardless of the (drift-fixable) snowball. The pexp slider is a no-op. 3 CN inflation structural reflation Terminal −1.21% (deflation); the δc (+1.18) is a +3.2pp regime reflation a deflationary RW will not deliver credibly — flag, don't slider-nudge. 4 AR inflation hyperinflation regime No in-sample point target; active disinflation/hyperinflation. No anchor defined → skip the nominal slider. 5 AR policy rate hyperinflation regime Non-stationary regime rate; no neutral defined → skip. 6 BE policy rate perverse/near-singular slider sens −0.36 (inverted, barely above the 0.3 guard). Default 2.61 is coincidentally near neutral, but the slider moves the path the wrong way → disable + label the near-singular policy slider, don't ship the thumb. 7 CA policy rate perverse slider sens −6.92 (inverted). Default 2.77 ≈ neutral by coincidence; raising the target lowers the path → disable + label the near-singular policy slider. 8 CZ policy rate perverse slider + bad default sens −3.62 (inverted) and default 5.61 ≫ ~3% neutral → neither trustworthy → disable + label the near-singular policy slider.

Pattern: 4 of the 8 are the three EM/euro perverse policy-rate sliders (BE, CA, CZ) plus the AR regime — exactly the subset the smell test's negative-real-rate finding flagged. Their fix is to disable + label the near-singular policy slider, not the drift thumb. The other 4 are 2 debt primary-balance identities (UK, SK — the brief's canonical "debt = primary balance, not snowball" case), 1 deflation reflation (CN), 1 hyperinflation regime (AR nominal block). None is a defect in the δc machinery; each is a known category that needs a different lever.

docs.title: Largest (but still finite, resolving) δc — context, not failures
docs.text: These are pure RW runaway, fixed by one coherent shift, never a structural break: TR inflation −3.44 (RW carries 32% hyperinflation forever; CBRT 5% goal is aspirational), LT inflation −3.34 (tool-wide-worst 10.27% → ECB 2%), HU growth +2.74 / inflation −2.29 (broken central path), SI growth −2.62, MY growth −2.23 (small-sens catch-up drift), CO inflation −1.84 (largest clean A–C correction on a healthy slider), SA unemployment +1.68 (RW collapse to 0.1%).

docs.title: 2. Recommended ship-with DEFAULT-ANCHOR SET (config-ready, all 46 blocs)
docs.text:
Public-knowledge long-run values (central-bank inflation target / band-midpoint; IMF-WEO or OECD potential growth; neutral policy = r* + π*; structural/NAIRU unemployment). Parenthesised policy rates are the defensible anchor but the slider is perverse — disable + label the near-singular policy slider. — = no such series in the bloc's spec.

# default_anchors.yaml  — one-click preset; values are long-run targets in display units
# (growth/inflation = q/q-annualised %, rates/unemployment = level %).
# policy:(x) => anchor defensible but slider perverse/near-singular -> disable + label the near-singular slider, do NOT ship the thumb.
AR: { growth: 2.0,  infl: null, policy: null, unemp: 7.0 }   # nominal block = hyperinflation regime, skip
AT: { growth: 1.0,  infl: 2.0,  policy: 2.0,   unemp: 4.8 }
AU: { growth: 2.2,  infl: 2.5,  policy: 3.1,   unemp: 4.25 } # healthiest out of the box
BE: { growth: 1.2,  infl: 2.0,  policy: 2.0,   unemp: 5.5,  policy_perverse: true }
BR: { growth: 2.0,  infl: 3.0,  policy: 7.5,   unemp: 8.5 }
CA: { growth: 1.7,  infl: 2.0,  policy: 2.75,  unemp: 6.0,  policy_perverse: true }
CH: { growth: 1.3,  infl: 1.0,  policy: 1.0,   unemp: 2.5 }
CL: { growth: 2.0,  infl: 3.0,  policy: 4.0,   unemp: 7.5 }
CN: { growth: 4.0,  infl: 2.0,  policy: 3.0,   unemp: null, infl_structural: true } # deflation; GDP series real (2026-06 CN-only re-est)
CO: { growth: 3.0,  infl: 3.0,  policy: 5.5,   unemp: 10.0 }
CR: { growth: 3.5,  infl: 3.0,  policy: 4.0,   unemp: 9.0 }
CZ: { growth: 2.3,  infl: 2.0,  policy: 3.0,   unemp: 2.6,  policy_perverse: true }
DE: { growth: 0.8,  infl: 2.0,  policy: 2.25,  unemp: 3.5 }
DK: { growth: 1.5,  infl: 2.0,  policy: 2.25,  unemp: 4.5 }   # ERM-II krone peg -> ECB 2% objective
ES: { growth: 1.5,  infl: 2.0,  policy: 2.25,  unemp: 11.5 }
FI: { growth: 1.2,  infl: 2.0,  policy: 2.25,  unemp: 7.0 }

docs.title: 3. Big-economy default-anchor highlights
docs.text: Bloc Out-of-the-box smell One-click default fixes it to US g 3.45 (>potential), deflator π 4.13, snowball falling, policy 5.46 g→1.9, π→2.0 (CPI 2.3), unemp→4.2; 10Y/EFFI carry the real rate. Don't auto-ship the raw policy δc (sens 0.51, ill-conditioned). EA core (DE/FR/ES) DE π 2.9 over + real rate −2.2%; FR g 2.4 hot; ES already clean All → ECB 2.0% infl, 2.25% neutral, potential g, NAIRU. ES is the healthiest bloc in the tool. UK π 4.35 never glides; snowball falling; debt falling π→2.0, neutral 2.75, NAIRU 4.5 — snowball resolves. Debt direction stays falling (structural +3.1% primary surplus) — label, don't fix. JP unemp 5.9% (NAIRU ~2.5), π 2.6% unemp→2.5, π→2.0, policy→1.5 (sens 0.53 — flag the slider). CN deflation −1.21%, real GDP ~4.5% g→4.0 (real GDP since 2026-06 CN-only re-est), policy→3.0. Inflation reflation is structural — flag, not a slider nudge.

docs.title: 4. Ship verdict
docs.text:
YES — presentable, with the default anchors shipped as one-click presets and four labels.

  • 96.5% (219/227) of every smell across 46 blocs is resolved by a single, publicly-defensible δc. With the default-anchor preset applied, the out-of-the-box forecasts anchor at each central bank's inflation target, a positive neutral real rate, IMF-WEO potential growth, and the country NAIRU — i.e. sensible by inspection. The δc overlay is a band-invariant rigid translation, so shipped uncertainty is unchanged.
  • The 8 non-resolving cases are not bugs — they are known categories that need a different lever and a UI label, not a fix: UK & SK debt direction (primary-balance identities), the BE/CA/CZ perverse policy-rate sliders (→ disable + label the near-singular slider), CN deflation (structural reflation), AR nominal block (hyperinflation regime). All five economies still have sensible defaults for their other variables.
  • Four ill-conditioned-but-finite sliders to flag, not auto-ship the raw δc: US POLRATE (sens 0.51 → anchor π/g, let 10Y/EFFI carry the real rate), JP POLRATE (sens 0.53), SK EFFI (sens 0.56), PT LENDR (sens 0.52). PL POLRATE (sens −9.94) works but has a counter-intuitive sign → tooltip.

Recommendation: ship the §2 preset as the default slider positions (replacing the degenerate RW drift), with UI labels on the 8 structural/regime exceptions and the 4–5 sensitive sliders. The tool is presentable out of the box. The remaining structural items (UK/SK debt direction, AR/CN regimes) are the trend-cycle / fiscal-rebuild track, not a blocker for the anchored-default ship.

docs.title: APPENDIX — full per-economy supporting detail (by alphabet band)
Pre-ship smoke test — default δc long-run anchors (economies D–G)
docs.text:
Scope. The LIVE MFM (BVAR Scenario Tool, branch improve-live) ships RW-prior BVAR baselines whose constant row is a pure drift term, so the model has no steady state — the H=20 (5y) terminal forecast is just "wherever the drift has carried the level path." The earlier docs/live-smell-test.md found this is systemically implausible (inflation not anchoring, negative terminal real rates, debt-snowball falling). This file does the per-country × per-variable version for the D–G economies and asks the operational question: does a single, simple, publicly-known, common-sense long-run anchor (one δc per variable) make the forecast sensible? If yes, that anchor is a recommended default the tool can ship with.

Economies graded: DE, DK, ES, FI, FR, GR. (All European; DK is non-euro but pegs the krone to the euro under ERM-II, so it imports the ECB's de-facto 2% objective and runs its own policy rate a hair above the ECB to defend the peg.)

Method (verbatim app/src/lib/compute/driftSetter.ts). Companion T is built from companion_mean.json betahat (row 0 = constant dropped; T[i,j]=betahat[j+1][i]; identity shift in the lower block). S_h = Σ_{k<h} Tᵏ. Terminal display forecast is read off baseline.json mean[H-1]. Sensitivity of variable j's terminal display to a unit δc on its own equation: - log vars (GDP, HICP → chart shows 4·Δlevel = q/q annualised %): sens_j = 4·(S_H−S_{H-1})[j,j] - lin vars (rates, ratios → chart shows the level): sens_j = S_H[j,j]

Required anchor shift: δc_j = (anchor − terminal) / sens_j. The glided terminal is, by construction, exactly the anchor. (Replicated in /tmp against the TS; identity holds.)

Anchor basis (all PUBLIC reference values — not tuned to the model): - Inflation → the central bank's stated target. ECB 2.0% (DE, ES, FI, FR, GR). Denmark: ERM-II krone peg ⇒ imports the ECB's 2.0% objective. - Real growth → IMF WEO / OECD potential-growth consensus: DE ~0.8, DK ~1.5, ES ~1.5, FI ~1.2, FR ~1.3, GR ~1.0. - Policy rate → neutral = r* + π*. Euro area ECB neutral ≈ 2.0–2.5% (we use 2.25). DK ≈ ECB neutral (peg). - Unemployment → structural / NAIRU (OECD/EC estimates): DE ~3.5, DK ~4.5, ES ~11.5 (persistently high), FI ~7.0, FR ~7.0, GR ~11.0.

resolves = true ⇔ the anchor is defensible AND the required δc is a modest constant-shift (the model is merely drifting, not structurally broken). It would be false only if a long-run shift cannot fix it (e.g. UK debt = a primary-deficit identity, not a snowball drift — not present here).

docs.title: Cross-cutting smell-test (terminal, H=20)
docs.text:
C GDP q/q ann Infl Pol rate real rate (pol−infl) Unemp DE 0.35 2.90 0.71 −2.19 3.53 DK 2.02 1.49 −0.51 −2.00 4.97 ES 1.15 2.00 2.93 +0.93 9.92 FI 2.77 1.01 0.60 −0.41 11.57 FR 2.43 1.71 2.55 +0.84 7.90 GR −0.94 2.57 2.46 −0.11 12.37

The systemic pathologies recur in D–G: DE & DK terminal real policy rates ≈ −2% (the RW drift pins the rate well below neutral); DE inflation overshoots (2.9%) while DK & FI undershoot (1.5%, 1.0%); GR real GDP keeps shrinking (−0.9% q/q ann) indefinitely; FI & FR run GDP hot (2.8%, 2.4%) above any plausible potential and FI unemployment sits at 11.6% (Spanish-level, implausible for Finland). Every one of these is a drift/anchoring defect — a constant-shift slider fixes it; none is a structural identity break.

docs.title: Per-economy detail
docs.text: For each: terminal (display units), plausible?, common-sense anchor (+basis), sens, required δc, glided terminal (= anchor), resolves?

docs.title: DE — Germany (euro)
docs.text: var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 0.35 n (low but not absurd) 0.80 OECD/IMF DE potential ~0.8 2.85 +0.159 0.80 yes Inflation 2.90 n (overshoots) 2.00 ECB target 3.18 −0.281 2.00 yes Policy rate 0.71 n (real −2.2%) 2.25 ECB neutral r*+π* −3.07 −0.503 2.25 yes Unemployment 3.53 y (≈ NAIRU) 3.50 OECD DE NAIRU ~3.5 7.48 −0.004 3.50 yes (≈no-op)

docs.title: DK — Denmark (krone peg / ERM-II)
docs.text: var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 2.02 borderline (a touch hot) 1.50 IMF DK potential ~1.5 3.06 −0.169 1.50 yes Inflation 1.49 n (undershoots) 2.00 ECB (peg) 4.23 +0.120 2.00 yes Policy rate −0.51 n (negative; real −2.0%) 2.25 ECB neutral (peg) 11.16 +0.248 2.25 yes Unemployment 4.97 y 4.50 OECD DK NAIRU ~4.5 11.76 −0.040 4.50 yes

docs.title: ES — Spain (euro)
docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 1.15 borderline (slightly low) 1.50 IMF ES potential ~1.5 4.40 +0.079 1.50 yes Inflation 2.00 y (on target) 2.00 ECB target 2.31 +0.001 2.00 yes (≈no-op) Policy rate 2.93 y (real +0.9%) 2.25 ECB neutral 12.27 −0.056 2.25 yes Unemployment 9.92 borderline (a bit low for ES) 11.5 EC/OECD ES NAIRU ~11–12 10.91 +0.145 11.50 yes

ES is the healthiest of the six out of the box — inflation already anchors, the real rate is positive, growth is near potential. The defaults are small nudges.

docs.title: FI — Finland (euro)
docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 2.77 n (well above potential) 1.20 IMF FI potential ~1.2 0.834 −1.880 1.20 yes (large but finite) Inflation 1.01 n (deep undershoot) 2.00 ECB target 2.70 +0.367 2.00 yes Policy rate 0.60 n (real −0.4%) 2.25 ECB neutral 13.60 +0.122 2.25 yes Unemployment 11.57 n (Spanish-level, wrong for FI) 7.00 OECD FI NAIRU ~7 16.87 −0.271 7.00 yes

FI is the worst of the six: growth too hot, inflation too cold, unemployment implausibly high. The GDP δc (−1.88) is the only large one — sens=0.83 is small (GDP drift is weakly identified here) so a bigger constant shift is needed, but it is still a single finite drift correction, not a structural break ⇒ resolves = true.

docs.title: FR — France (euro)
docs.text: var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 2.43 n (above potential) 1.30 IMF FR potential ~1.3 3.28 −0.345 1.30 yes Inflation 1.71 borderline (mild undershoot) 2.00 ECB target 2.52 +0.116 2.00 yes Policy rate 2.55 y (real +0.8%) 2.25 ECB neutral 10.25 −0.029 2.25 yes Unemployment 7.90 borderline (a bit high) 7.00 OECD/EC FR NAIRU ~7 9.30 −0.097 7.00 yes

docs.title: GR — Greece (euro)
docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth −0.94 n (shrinks forever) 1.00 IMF GR potential ~1.0 2.46 +0.786 1.00 yes Inflation 2.57 n (overshoots) 2.00 ECB target 2.39 −0.240 2.00 yes Policy rate 2.46 y (real ≈0) 2.25 ECB neutral −13.26 +0.016 2.25 yes Unemployment 12.37 borderline (high but GR-realistic) 11.0 EC GR NAIRU ~11 10.97 −0.125 11.00 yes

GR's signature defect is perpetual contraction (−0.9% q/q ann GDP) — the δc (+0.79) lifts it to ~1% potential, a clean drift fix.

docs.title: Verdict
docs.text:
Every flagged variable across D–G is a drift/anchoring failure of the RW prior, and in every case a single, publicly-defensible δc resolves it (resolves = true for all flagged vars). The required δc are modest constant-shifts (|δc| ≲ 0.5 almost everywhere; the lone outlier is FI GDP at −1.88, driven by a small sens=0.83, still a finite single-target correction — not a structural break). No D–G variable is in the UK-debt category (a primary-deficit identity that a long-run shift cannot repair).

Recommended ship-with defaults (the anchors above):

C GDP Infl Pol Unemp DE 0.8 2.0 2.25 3.5 DK 1.5 2.0 2.25 4.5 ES 1.5 2.0 2.25 11.5 FI 1.2 2.0 2.25 7.0 FR 1.3 2.0 2.25 7.0 GR 1.0 2.0 2.25 11.0

Shipping these as the default slider positions (instead of the degenerate RW drift) makes the out-of-the-box forecasts anchor at the central bank's inflation target, a positive neutral real rate, potential growth, and the NAIRU — i.e. sensible by inspection. The cross-variable S_h[:,j] response then drags the rest of each bloc along the estimated dynamics, as designed.

Read-only smoke test; no app/src or data files were modified.

docs.title: Pre-ship smoke test — default δc long-run anchors (economies H–K)
docs.text:
Scope. Same exercise, same improve-live LIVE MFM, for the economies whose code starts with a letter in [H..K]: HU, ID, IL, IN, IS, JP, KR (gvar skipped). Per country × per variable: terminal forecast, plausible?, common-sense PUBLIC anchor (+basis), the δc to glide there, the glided terminal (= anchor), and whether a simple δc resolves it (model merely drifting, not structurally broken).

Method (verbatim app/src/lib/compute/driftSetter.ts, replicated in Python, identity holds). Companion T from companion_mean.json betahat (T[i,j]=betahat[j+1][i], identity shift in the lag block); S_h=Σ_{k<h}Tᵏ to H=20; sens_j = 4·(S_H−S_{H-1})[j,j] (log: display = 4·Δlevel = q/q annualised %) or S_H[j,j] (lin: display = level); δc_j=(anchor−terminal)/sens_j; glided = anchor by construction. SENS guard 0.3 (below ⇒ slider disabled).

Anchor basis (PUBLIC reference values, not tuned): inflation → the central-bank target; real growth → IMF WEO / OECD potential; policy rate → neutral = r* + π*; unemployment → structural / NAIRU; 10Y / short rate → neutral policy (+ a small term premium for 10Y).

resolves = true ⇔ anchor defensible AND δc a reasonable single long-run shift. There is no UK-debt-style structural break in this set — H–K are all RW-drift cases.

docs.title: Cross-cutting smell-test (terminal, H=20)
docs.text:
C GDP q/q ann Infl Pol rate real rate (pol−infl) Unemp HU −0.46 10.35 14.97 +4.62 7.80 ID 4.39 1.30 3.28 +1.98 — IL 2.50 3.17 2.50 −0.67 3.74 IN 7.86 3.75 4.19 +0.44 — IS 2.93 4.35 5.85 +1.50 3.70 JP 0.77 2.62 1.00 −1.62 5.94 KR 1.61 2.36 1.62 −0.74 3.39

Hungary is the broken central path of the set: RW drift sends inflation to 10.3%, the policy rate to 15.0%, the 10Y to 12.5%, and growth negative. Japan prints a NAIRU-implausible 5.9% unemployment and a 2.6% terminal inflation. Iceland inflation (4.35%) and rates run hot. ID, IL, IN, KR are close to sensible out of the box. None is a structural-identity break — each is a constant-drift defect a single δc fixes.

docs.title: Per-economy detail
docs.text: For each: terminal (display units), plausible?, anchor (+basis), sens, δc, glided (= anchor), resolves?

docs.title: HU — Hungary  (BROKEN central path; each variable resolves via one δc)

var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth −0.46 n 2.50 IMF WEO/OECD potential (EU convergence) 1.08 +2.743 2.50 yes Inflation 10.35 n 3.00 MNB target 3.0% 3.21 −2.290 3.00 yes Policy rate 14.97 n (runaway) 4.00 r*~1.0 + π* 3.0 6.32 −1.735 4.00 yes Unemployment 7.80 n (high) 4.00 NAIRU ~3.5–4.5 12.92 −0.294 4.00 yes 10Y yield 12.50 n 5.00 neutral 4.0 + ~1.0 term prem 8.32 −0.901 5.00 yes

HU has the largest δc in the set (|δc| up to 2.7), reflecting how far the RW prior runs away — but each is one coherent long-run shift, pure drift, not breakage.

var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 0.77 y 0.70 IMF WEO potential ~0.5–0.7 2.73 −0.027 0.70 yes (≈no-op) Inflation 2.62 n (high) 2.00 BoJ price-stability target 2.0 2.63 −0.237 2.00 yes Policy rate 1.00 n (low) 1.50 r*~0–0.5 + π* 2.0 0.526 +0.954 1.50 yes (sens 0.53, just above guard — sensitive) Unemployment 5.94 n (NAIRU ~2.5) 2.50 structural ~2.5 4.66 −0.738 2.50 yes 10Y yield 3.87 n (high) 2.00 neutral ~1.5 + small term prem 12.79 −0.146 2.00 yes

JP POLRATE has the smallest sens of the whole H–K set (0.526, just above the 0.3 guard): the slider is enabled but the most sensitive — flag it in the default-anchor UI.

docs.title: Verdict (H–K)
docs.text:
Every graded variable across H–K resolves with a single publicly-defensible δc — no structural-break cases in this set (no UK-debt analogue). Magnitudes scale with the severity of the RW runaway: Hungary needs the largest corrections (|δc| up to 2.7 — genuine drift runaway), Japan unemployment (δc −0.74) and Iceland inflation (δc −0.40) the next-largest, and ID, IL, IN, KR are small nudges (|δc| ≲ 0.2 mostly). JP POLRATE is the one slider to watch (sens 0.53).

Recommended ship-with defaults (the anchors above):

C GDP Infl Pol Unemp 10Y/short HU 2.5 3.0 4.0 4.0 5.0 (10Y) ID 5.0 2.5 4.5 — 5.0 (short) IL 3.0 2.0 3.0 3.8 3.0 (short) IN 6.5 4.0 6.0 — 7.0 (10Y) IS 2.5 2.5 4.0 3.5 4.0 (short) JP 0.7 2.0 1.5 2.5 2.0 (10Y) KR 2.0 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.0 (10Y)

Shipping these as the default slider positions makes the out-of-the-box H–K forecasts anchor at the central-bank inflation target, a positive neutral real rate, potential growth, and the NAIRU.

Read-only smoke test; no app/src or data files were modified.

docs.title: Pre-ship smoke test — default δc long-run anchors (economies L–N)
docs.text:
Scope. Same LIVE MFM, same driftSetter.ts method as the sections above. This section grades the economies whose code begins with L, M, or N: LT, LU, LV, MX, MY, NL, NO, NZ. For each, the key forecastable own-bloc variables (real growth, inflation, policy/short rate, unemployment, and the 10Y yield where it is an own-bloc state) are reported with: the terminal display forecast, plausible y/n, the common-sense public anchor (+basis), the required δc, the glided terminal (= anchor by construction), and whether a simple δc resolves it.

Method/values verified by replicating driftSetter.ts exactly in Python against companion_mean.json (companion T, S_h = Σ_{k<h}Tᵏ, sens_j = 4·(S_H−S_{H-1})[j,j] for log and S_H[j,j] for lin), terminal from baseline.json mean[H-1] (H=20). Every sensitivity passes SENS_GUARD = 0.3 — no slider disabled — and each glided terminal equals its anchor to machine precision. So the only live questions are raw-terminal plausibility and δc size.

Anchor basis (all PUBLIC reference values — not tuned): - Inflation → the official central-bank target: ECB 2.0% (LT, LU, LV, NL); Banxico 3.0% (MX); BNM implicit ~2.5% midpoint of its 2–3% comfort band (MY); Norges Bank 2.0% (lowered from 2.5% in March 2018) (NO); RBNZ 2.0% midpoint of the 1–3% PTA band (NZ). - Real growth → IMF WEO / OECD / national potential: LT ~2.5, LV ~2.5 (EU catch-up), LU ~2.0, NL ~1.3 (CPB), NO ~1.7, NZ ~2.2, MX ~2.0, MY ~4.5. - Policy / short rate → neutral = r* + π*: EA short rate ~2.0 (LT/LU/LV/NL 3M, in the shared external block); MX 6.0 (r*~3 + π*3, ~Banxico neutral); MY 3.0 (BNM neutral OPR); NO 2.5 (Norges neutral); NZ 3.0 (RBNZ neutral OCR, 2.75–3.25 range). - Unemployment → structural/NAIRU: LT/LV ~6.5, LU ~5.5, NL ~4.0, NO ~3.8, MX ~3.5, MY ~3.3, NZ ~4.3. - 10Y yield → neutral policy rate + a positive term premium: MX 7.0, NZ 3.75.

docs.text:

The smell-test pathology recurs in L–N: inflation off-target in 6 of 8 (LT 10.27% is the worst non-anchoring in the whole tool; NZ 5.45%, LV 4.13%, MX 4.54%, LU 3.66%, NL 3.00%; MY undershoots); EA-member short rates too low / negative (LU −0.49%, LT 0.41%); MY real growth runs hot (6.87%, small-economy catch-up drift). Every one is a drift/anchoring defect a constant-shift slider fixes — none is a structural identity break.

docs.title: Verdict (L–N)
docs.text:
Every flagged variable across L–N is a drift/anchoring failure of the RW prior, and in every case a single, publicly-defensible δc resolves it (resolves = true for all). The δc are modest constant-shifts (|δc| ≲ 0.7 almost everywhere). The two large ones are still finite single-target drift corrections, not structural breaks: LT HICP δc = −3.34 (correcting the tool-wide-worst 10.27% inflation back to the 2% ECB target) and MY GDP δc = −2.23 (sens=1.06 is small, so the 6.87% catch-up-drift terminal needs a bigger constant to reach 4.5% potential). No L–N variable is in the UK-debt category (a primary-deficit identity a long-run shift cannot repair).

Recommended ship-with defaults (the anchors above):

C GDP Infl Pol/short Unemp 10Y LT 2.5 2.0 2.0 6.5 — LU 2.0 2.0 2.0 5.5 — LV 2.5 2.0 2.0 6.5 — MX 2.0 3.0 6.0 3.5 7.0 MY 4.5 2.5 3.0 3.3 — NL 1.3 2.0 2.0 4.0 — NO 1.7 2.0 2.5 3.8 — NZ 2.2 2.0 3.0 4.3 3.75

Shipping these as the default slider positions anchors the out-of-the-box L–N forecasts at each central bank's inflation target, a positive neutral real rate, potential growth, and the NAIRU. The overlay is a band-invariant rigid translation, so shipped uncertainty is unchanged. All 8 economies, all key variables: resolves = true.

Read-only smoke test; no app/src or data files were modified.

docs.title: Pre-ship smoke test — default δc long-run anchors (economies S–Z)
docs.text:
Scope. Same exercise, same method, for the S–Z blocs in app/public/data/bvar/: SA, SE, SI, SK, TH, TR, TW, UK, US, ZA. No edits — the matrices below are recomputed from companion_mean.json / baseline.json / transformations.json with the verbatim driftSetter.ts algebra; the terminal forecasts reproduce docs/live-smell-test.md exactly (e.g. UK π 4.35, US g 3.45, TR π 32.3), confirming the pipeline.

Method (verbatim app/src/lib/compute/driftSetter.ts). Companion T from betahat (T[i,j]=betahat[j+1][i], identity shift below); S_h = Σ_{k<h} Tᵏ; terminal = baseline.mean[H-1] (H=20, lags=3 for all S–Z blocs). sens_j = 4·(S_H−S_{H-1})[j,j] for log vars (GDP, CPI/HICP → q/q-annualised %), S_H[j,j] for lin vars (rates, ratios). δc_j = (anchor − terminal)/sens_j; glided terminal = anchor by construction. SENS_GUARD = 0.3 ⇒ |sens|<0.3 disables the slider (no S–Z key slider hits the guard, but two are near-singular — US POLRATE sens 0.51 and SK EFFI sens 0.56 — so their δc are large/ill-conditioned though finite).

Anchor basis (all PUBLIC reference values — not tuned): - Inflation → official target/band midpoint: ECB 2.0 (SI, SK euro members); Riksbank 2.0 (SE); BoE 2.0 (UK); Fed 2.0 GDP-deflator-equiv / 2.3 CPI (US); BoT band 1–3 → 2.0 (TH); CBC implicit 2.0 (TW); SARB band 3–6 → 4.5 (SA, ZA); CBRT long-run goal 5.0 (TR). - Real growth → IMF WEO / OECD potential: SA ~2.5, SE ~1.8, SI ~1.5, SK ~2.0, TH ~3.0, TR ~3.5, TW ~2.5, UK ~1.3 (OECD/OBR), US ~1.9 (CBO/FOMC), ZA ~1.5. - Policy rate → neutral = r* + π*: SE 2.5, SI/SK 2.0 (ECB depo neutral), TH 2.5, TR 9.0 (≈π*5 + r*4 EM premium), TW 2.0, UK 2.75 (BoE r*0.75+2.0), US 2.9 (FOMC SEP longer-run dot), SA 7.0, ZA 7.0; 10Y/lending = neutral + term/credit premium. - Unemployment → structural/NAIRU: SA ~27, SE ~7, SI ~4.5, SK ~6, TH ~1, TR ~9, TW ~3.7, UK ~4.5, US ~4.2 (FOMC longer-run), ZA ~28.

A flagged var resolves=yes when the anchor is publicly defensible AND the δc is a reasonable single-target shift (the model is merely drifting). resolves=no when the smell is a structural accounting identity a long-run δc cannot repair (the UK/SK debt direction, a primary-balance fact, not the snowball that the inflation/growth δc fixes).

var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 5.20 n (≫ potential) 1.50 IMF SI potential ~1.5 1.41 −2.620 1.50 yes Inflation 5.71 n (no glide to 2) 2.00 ECB 2.0 2.77 −1.338 2.00 yes Policy rate (ECB) 3.80 n (>neutral) 2.00 ECB depo neutral 1.97 −0.914 2.00 yes Unemployment 3.57 y (low, SI-OK) 4.50 SI NAIRU ~4.5 4.52 +0.206 4.50 y EFFI (debt cost) 2.22 y 2.50 EA effective ~neutral 3.21 +0.088 2.50 y Revenue %GDP 46.0 y 46.0 (no-op) 1.12 0.0 46.0 y Prim.exp %GDP 43.8 y 43.8 (no-op) 1.57 −0.001 43.8 y Debt snowball −8.69 n (debt collapses) — r−g identity — — — yes (via π+g)

SI's −8.69 snowball is entirely the inflation/growth drift: nominal growth 10.9 vs EFFI 2.2, on a +2.2% primary surplus. Anchoring π→2 and g→1.5 (nominal ~3.5) with EFFI ~2.5 gives a snowball ~−1.0 — sensible. The snowball implausibility is a drift fix.

But SK runs a structural primary deficit of −6.0% (pexp 50.4 ≫ rev 44.3) that drives debt regardless of the snowball — the pexp δc is a no-op, not an anchor. Drift-fix for the snowball; UK-debt-category for the deficit.

TR is the largest-δc case (π −3.44 in companion-constant units) because the RW carries 32% inflation forever. The 5% CBRT goal is public and defensible and a single δc glides there — flag it as aspirational: it disinflates the model to target by construction, not via a credible transition.
