docs.title: UK — United Kingdom (BoE, fiscal block) — the brief's canonical case
docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 2.04 borderline (>potential) 1.30 OECD/OBR UK potential ~1.3 3.72 −0.199 1.30 yes Inflation 4.35 n (never glides to 2) 2.00 BoE 2.0 2.96 −0.792 2.00 yes Policy rate 2.60 n (real <0) 2.75 BoE r*0.75+2.0 12.49 +0.012 2.75 yes Unemployment 6.08 borderline (>NAIRU) 4.50 UK NAIRU ~4.5 14.14 −0.112 4.50 yes EFFI (debt cost) 3.32 y 3.50 UK avg debt cost ~3.5 1.12 +0.162 3.50 y 10Y gilt 3.63 y 3.75 neutral+term prem 7.98 +0.016 3.75 y Revenue %GDP 43.1 y 43.1 (no-op) 1.54 +0.019 43.1 y Prim.exp %GDP 39.98 y 39.98 (no-op) 2.18 −0.002 39.98 y Debt snowball −3.07 n — r−g identity — — — yes (snowball) Debt direction falling n (consensus rising) — primary-balance identity — — — no (structural)

UK is the textbook pathology. The −3.07 snowball is the inflation drift (nominal 6.39 vs EFFI 3.32); anchoring π→2 brings nominal to ~3.3 vs EFFI ~3.5 ⇒ snowball ~+0.2, neutral — so the snowball smell is a drift fix. But debt then falls because the model's terminal primary balance is a +3.1% surplus (rev 43.1 vs pexp 40.0) — an accounting fact a long-run δc cannot move (the rev/pexp δc are no-ops). This is exactly the brief's distinction: UK debt = primary balance, not snowball ⇒ resolves=no for the debt direction.

docs.title: US — United States (Fed, fiscal block)
docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 3.45 n (>potential) 1.90 CBO/FOMC potential ~1.9 3.40 −0.456 1.90 yes Inflation (deflator) 4.13 n (drifts up) 2.00 Fed 2.0 (deflator-equiv) 4.44 −0.478 2.00 yes CPI 3.36 n (>target-equiv) 2.30 Fed 2% PCE ≈ 2.3 CPI 4.57 −0.232 2.30 yes Policy rate 5.46 n (>neutral) 2.90 FOMC SEP longer-run dot 0.51 −5.012 2.90 yes but ill-cond Unemployment 5.97 borderline (>NAIRU) 4.20 FOMC longer-run 4.2 8.33 −0.212 4.20 yes EFFI (debt cost) 4.16 y 3.50 US avg debt cost ~3.5 15.89 −0.042 3.50 y 10Y yield 5.90 n (>neutral) 3.75 neutral 2.9+term prem 8.47 −0.254 3.75 yes Revenue %GDP 19.8 y 19.8 (no-op) 1.70 0.0 19.8 y Prim.exp %GDP 21.8 y 21.8 (no-op) 1.58 +0.004 21.8 y Debt snowball −3.41 n — r−g identity — — — yes (snowball) Debt direction falling* n (SFA-masked) — primary-deficit identity — — — partial

US smells (g 3.45, π 4.13, snowball −3.41) are all drift and resolve with modest δc. Caveat: US POLRATE sensitivity is 0.51 — just above the 0.3 guard but near-singular — so the −5.0 δc to reach the 2.9% dot is ill-conditioned (slider barely effective, cross-response large). Prefer anchoring inflation+growth and letting EFFI/10Y carry the real-rate story; do not ship the raw policy-rate δc as a default. Debt: anchoring π→2, g→1.9 gives nominal ~3.9 vs EFFI ~3.5 ⇒ snowball ~−0.4; with the −2.0% primary deficit debt then rises modestly — the sensible US direction (the display "falling" is the SFA-drift artifact noted in the live smell test).

docs.title: ZA — South Africa (SARB; ISO-3166 block)
docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 0.85 n (≪ potential) 1.50 IMF ZA potential ~1.5 3.36 +0.194 1.50 yes Inflation 3.95 y (in 3–6 band) 4.50 SARB band midpoint 3.55 +0.154 4.50 y Policy rate 8.07 y 7.00 r*2.5+π*4.5 14.17 −0.075 7.00 y Unemployment 30.58 y (ZA-realistic) 28.00 ZA structural ~28 9.09 −0.284 28.00 y 10Y yield 9.74 y 9.00 neutral+term prem 7.42 −0.100 9.00 y House price −2.63 n (real −2.6) 2.00 real HP ~ growth 4.99 +0.927 2.00 yes Credit/GDP 33.5 y 33.5 (no-op) 16.15 −0.002 33.5 y Lending rate 11.58 y 11.00 policy+spread 16.60 −0.035 11.00 y

The two SARB blocs in this set are SA (the OECD-style real-component block, code SA) and ZA (the ISO-3166 South-Africa block, code ZA) — different model blocs in the data dir, both graded here; the SARB band-midpoint inflation anchor (4.5) and ~7% neutral apply to both.

ZA is among the cleanest in the set: inflation already in band, real rate positive, unemployment ZA-realistic. Only GDP (cold) and real house prices need a small lift.

docs.title: Verdict (S–Z)
docs.text:
Across the 10 S–Z blocs, every flagged nominal/real variable is a drift/anchoring failure that a single publicly-defensible δc resolves. The required shifts are modest constant-shifts almost everywhere (|δc| < 1 in companion-constant units), with three understandable larger ones — SA unemployment +1.68 (RW collapse to 0.1%), SI growth −2.62, TR inflation −3.44 (RW carries 32% hyperinflation forever). The two genuine non-resolvable cases are exactly the brief's primary-balance category: - UK debt direction — falls because of a structural +3.1% primary surplus, not the snowball (which the inflation δc fixes). resolves=no. - SK primary deficit (−6.0%) — drives debt regardless of the (drift-fixable) snowball. resolves=no for the deficit.

Two ill-conditioned sliders (above the 0.3 guard but near-singular): US POLRATE sens 0.51 (δc −5.0 to reach the 2.9% dot — ship inflation/growth anchors instead) and SK EFFI sens 0.56. SK POLRATE has a negative sens (−6.88, the ECB common-rate cross-loading) so its δc sign is counter-intuitive though finite.

Recommended ship-with defaults (S–Z):

C GDP Infl Pol Unemp Notes SA 2.5 4.5 7.0 27.0 SARB band-mid; unemp RW-collapse fix (+1.68) SE 1.8 2.0 2.5 7.0 clean bloc; lift real rate only SI 1.5 2.0 2.0 4.5 snowball is the π/g drift SK 2.0 2.0 2.0 6.0 snowball drift-fix; primary deficit structural TH 3.0 2.0 2.5 1.0 too-cold equilibrium → lift TR 3.5 5.0 9.0 9.0 CBRT goal; π δc −3.44 aspirational TW 2.5 2.0 2.0 3.7 only GDP runs hot UK 1.3 2.0 2.75 4.5 snowball drift-fix; debt direction = primary surplus (no-fix) US 1.9 2.0 2.9 4.2 POLRATE δc ill-conditioned (sens 0.51) — anchor π/g instead ZA 1.5 4.5 7.0 28.0 cleanest EM bloc

Shipping these as the default slider thumbs anchors each bloc at its central bank's inflation target/band-midpoint, a positive neutral real rate, IMF-WEO potential growth, and the country NAIRU — sensible out of the box. The two structural exceptions (UK/SK debt) should be labelled in the UI, not "fixed" by a long-run slider.

Read-only smoke test; recomputed from shipped data with the verbatim driftSetter.ts algebra; no app/src or data files were modified.

docs.title: Pre-ship smoke test — default δc long-run anchors (economies A–C)
docs.text:
Scope. Same exercise, same improve-live LIVE MFM, for the economies whose code starts with a letter in [A..C]: AR, AT, AU, BE, BR, CA, CH, CL, CN, CO, CR, CZ (gvar skipped). The LIVE RW-prior BVAR baselines have a constant row that is a pure drift, so the model has no steady state — the H=20 (5y) terminal forecast is just "wherever the drift carried the level path." The earlier docs/live-smell-test.md found this systemically implausible. Per country × per variable: terminal forecast, plausible?, common-sense PUBLIC anchor (+basis), the δc to glide there, the glided terminal (= anchor), and whether a simple δc resolves it (model merely drifting, not structurally broken).

Method (verbatim app/src/lib/compute/driftSetter.ts, replicated in Python, identity holds). Companion T from companion_mean.json betahat (row 0 = constant dropped; T[i,j]=betahat[j+1][i]; identity shift in the lag block); S_h = Σ_{k<h} Tᵏ to H=20; terminal display read off baseline.json mean[H-1]. Sensitivity of variable j's terminal display to a unit δc on its own equation: log vars (GDP, CPI/HICP → chart shows 4·Δlevel = q/q annualised %) sens_j = 4·(S_H−S_{H-1})[j,j]; lin vars (rates, ratios → chart shows the level) sens_j = S_H[j,j]. Required shift δc_j = (anchor − terminal)/sens_j; glided terminal = anchor by construction. SENS_GUARD = 0.3: a slider whose |sens| is below it is near-singular and disabled by the UI.

Anchor basis (all PUBLIC reference values — not tuned to the model): - Inflation → the central bank's stated target: ECB 2.0 (AT, BE); RBA 2.5 midpoint (AU); BoC 2.0 (CA); SNB de-facto ~1.0 (CH); CNB 2.0 (CZ); EM official targets BCB 3.0 (BR), BCCh 3.0 (CL), BanRep 3.0 (CO), BCCR 3.0 (CR), PBoC de-facto ~2.0 (CN). AR has no in-sample point target (an active-disinflation/hyperinflation regime) → skipped. - Real growth → IMF WEO / OECD potential: AR ~2.0, AT ~1.0, AU ~2.2, BE ~1.2, BR ~2.0, CA ~1.7, CH ~1.3, CL ~2.0, CN ~4.0, CO ~3.0, CR ~3.5, CZ ~2.3. - Policy rate → neutral = r* + π*: euro-area ECB ~2.0 (AT, BE); advanced AU ~3.1, CA ~2.75 (BoC nominal-neutral midpoint), CH ~1.0, CZ ~3.0; EM higher per its target + a positive real neutral: BR ~7.5, CL ~4.0, CN ~3.0, CO ~5.5, CR ~4.0. AR rate skipped (non-stationary regime). - Unemployment → structural / NAIRU: AR ~7.0, AT ~4.8, AU ~4.25, BE ~5.5, BR ~8.5, CA ~6.0, CH ~2.5, CL ~7.5, CO ~10.0, CR ~9.0, CZ ~2.6. CN has no unemployment series in its spec.

resolves = true ⇔ the anchor is defensible AND the δc is a reasonable single long-run shift on a well-behaved slider (positive sens comfortably above the 0.3 guard — model merely drifting). resolves = false where a long-run intercept move cannot fix it: a perverse / near-singular policy-rate slider (negative or near-guard sens, so the thumb moves the path the wrong way), a structural reflation (CN deflation), or a regime case (AR hyperinflation).

docs.title: Cross-cutting smell-test (terminal, H=20)
docs.text:
C GDP q/q ann Infl Pol rate real rate (pol−infl) Unemp AR −0.03 20.11 30.94 +10.83 7.58 AT 0.11 1.05 0.03 −1.02 5.39 AU 2.18 3.07 3.71 +0.64 4.12 BE 0.86 2.02 2.61 +0.59 8.18 BR 1.12 4.00 12.11 +8.10 10.05 CA 2.03 3.69 2.77 −0.93 6.31 CH 1.77 0.58 −0.43 −1.02 6.20 CL 2.78 4.30 4.42 +0.12 8.98 CN 5.18* −1.21 1.63 +2.85 — CO 2.31 6.92 10.45 +3.53 9.14 CR 3.51 1.42 4.04 +2.61 10.24 CZ 2.64 4.53 5.61 +1.08 4.86

* CN GDP series is real (since the 2026-06 CN-only re-estimate); the 4% anchor is a real number, now on the same footing as the other blocs.

The smell-test pathologies recur: inflation off-target both ways (AU/CL/CO/CZ too hot; CH/CR/CN too cold); negative terminal real rates (AT −1.0, CA −0.9, CH −1.0); growth flat-lining (AR, AT, BE) or above potential (CL, CR); unemployment drift (CH 6.2 vs ~2.5, BE 8.2 vs ~5.5). All are drift/anchoring defects except the AR hyperinflation regime and the CN deflation case.

docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves Inflation 2.02 y (on target) 2.00 ECB 2% HICP target 3.43 −0.006 2.00 yes (≈no-op) GDP growth 0.86 n (low) 1.20 OECD/IMF BE potential ~1.2 3.78 +0.090 1.20 yes Policy rate 2.61 y (default OK) 2.00 ECB neutral ~2.0 −0.36 +1.691 2.00 no (sens −0.36, near-singular & inverted) Unemployment 8.18 n (high) 5.50 BE NAIRU ~5.5 (OECD) 9.81 −0.273 5.50 yes

BE's terminal policy rate (2.61) is coincidentally near neutral so the default is fine, but the slider sens is −0.36 (negative, barely above the 0.3 guard): a "raise the long-run rate" move lowers it and the δc to hit 2.0 is a huge +1.69 ⇒ do not advertise a BE policy-rate target slider — disable + label the near-singular policy slider (it falls inside the |sens| < 0.3 guard band) rather than shipping the thumb.

docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves Inflation 3.69 n (sticks high) 2.00 BoC 2% CPI target (1–3 band) 2.28 −0.742 2.00 yes GDP growth 2.03 y 1.70 BoC/IMF CA potential ~1.7 3.58 −0.092 1.70 yes Policy rate 2.77 y (default OK) 2.75 BoC nominal-neutral 2.25–3.25 midpt ~2.75 −6.92 +0.002 2.75 no (sens −6.92, inverted) Unemployment 6.31 y 6.00 CA NAIRU ~6 (BoC/OECD) 3.18 −0.097 6.00 yes

CA is the smell-test's textbook case: inflation 3.69 never glides to 2% (δc −0.74 fixes it) but the real-rate problem (policy 2.77 < π) is not repairable via the policy-rate thumb — sens is −6.92 (inverted). Default (2.77) is near neutral, but raising the target lowers the path ⇒ flag, disable + label the near-singular policy slider.

GDP series is nominal, so the 4% real anchor glide is approximate.

docs.title: Verdict (A–C)
docs.text:
40 of the 45 graded key variables resolve with a single, publicly-defensible δc — modest, well-behaved constant shifts on positive sliders comfortably above the 0.3 guard (|δc| ≲ 0.55 almost everywhere; the largest clean correction is CO inflation at −1.84 on a healthy sens). Inflation glides to the central-bank target, growth to potential, unemployment to NAIRU, most policy rates to a positive neutral real rate — exactly the anchoring the smell test called for.

Five exceptions do NOT resolve via a simple δc (carry as known limitations): 1. BE policy rate — sens −0.36 (near-singular, inverted); default coincidentally OK, slider unsafe. 2. CA policy rate — sens −6.92 (inverted); default ≈ neutral, slider moves the wrong way. 3. CZ policy rate — sens −3.62 (inverted) and default 5.61 too high; neither trustworthy. 4. CN inflation — terminal −1.21 (deflation); δc +1.18 is a structural reflation, not a drift nudge. 5. AR nominal block (CPI, policy rate) — a hyperinflation/disinflation regime, not a drift; skip.

The three perverse policy-rate sliders (BE, CA, CZ) are precisely the subset the smell test's negative-real-rate finding flags — the fix there is to disable + label the near-singular policy slider, not the drift slider. Debt-direction is out of scope for a single macro δc (per the driftSetter.ts caveat the real-growth slider owns the debt direction but the snowball with a frozen-low EFFI is a fiscal-block issue) — it stays parked on the trend-cycle / fiscal rebuild, as in live-smell-test.md for UK.

Recommended ship-with defaults (the anchors above):

C GDP Infl Pol Unemp note AR 2.0 — — 7.0 nominal block (CPI/policy) skipped — regime AT 1.0 2.0 2.0 4.8 AU 2.2 2.5 3.1 4.25 healthiest out of the box BE 1.2 2.0 (2.0) 5.5 policy slider perverse → disable + label near-singular slider BR 2.0 3.0 7.5 8.5 CA 1.7 2.0 (2.75) 6.0 policy slider perverse → disable + label near-singular slider CH 1.3 1.0 1.0 2.5 CL 2.0 3.0 4.0 7.5 CN 4.0 (2.0) 3.0 — inflation structural (deflation); GDP real since 2026-06 CN-only re-est CO 3.0 3.0 5.5 10.0 inflation needs the largest clean δc (−1.84) CR 3.5 3.0 4.0 9.0 CZ 2.3 2.0 (3.0) 2.6 policy slider perverse → disable + label near-singular slider

Parenthesised policy rates are the defensible anchor but the slider is perverse — disable + label the near-singular policy slider rather than shipping the drift thumb.

Read-only smoke test; no app/src or data files were modified.

docs.title: Pre-ship smoke test — default δc long-run anchors (economies O–R)
docs.text:
Scope. Same improve-live LIVE MFM, same verbatim driftSetter.ts method, for the O–R blocs in app/public/data/bvar/: PL, PT, RU (no graded O/Q blocs). Per country × per variable: terminal forecast, plausible?, common-sense PUBLIC anchor (+basis), the δc to glide there, the glided terminal (= anchor), and whether a simple δc resolves it.

Anchor basis (PUBLIC reference values, not tuned): inflation → the central-bank target (NBP 2.5%, ECB 2.0%, CBR 4.0%); real growth → IMF WEO / OECD potential (PL ~2.5, PT ~1.2, RU ~1.5 sanctions-constrained); policy rate → neutral = r* + π*; unemployment → structural / NAIRU; 10Y / lending → neutral + term/credit premium.

docs.title: PL — Poland (NBP, non-euro; fiscal/credit block)
docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 4.60 n (>potential) 2.50 IMF/OECD PL potential ~2.0–2.7 — −1.238 2.50 yes Inflation (HICP) 4.30 n (no glide to 2.5; ~9% mid-horizon overshoot first) 2.50 NBP target 2.5 (±1) — −0.448 2.50 yes Policy rate 7.62 n (drifts up, no neutral) 4.00 NBP target 2.5 + r* ~1.5 −9.94 +0.365 4.00 yes (sens<0: +δc lowers terminal — counter-intuitive sign, slider still works) 10Y yield 7.70 n (drifts up) 4.50 neutral nominal ~4–5 — −0.523 4.50 yes NFC lending rate 9.19 n (drifts up) 5.50 neutral policy ~4 + spread ~1.5 17.9 −0.206 5.50 yes Unemployment 4.11 borderline (a touch <NAIRU) 5.00 PL structural/NAIRU ~5 — +0.080 5.00 yes

PL is the textbook drift signature: inflation overshoots to ~9% mid-horizon then bleeds back to 4.3% (not the 2.5% target), and policy/10Y/lending all drift up (the model extrapolates the recent high-rate regime instead of reverting to neutral). All implausibility is drift; every key variable resolves via one public anchor. UI flag: POLRATE_PL has a negative own-equation sens (−9.94), so the slider moves the terminal counter-intuitively — functional (|sens| ≫ 0.3 guard) and the default δc is small, but it warrants a tooltip note.

docs.title: PT — Portugal (euro member; ECB-anchored exogenous-rate block, fiscal block)
docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 1.05 y (on potential) 1.20 IMF/OECD PT potential ~1.0–1.5 — +0.050 1.20 yes (≈no-op) Inflation (HICP) 2.92 n (mild overshoot) 2.00 ECB target 2.0 — −0.341 2.00 yes Unemployment 7.48 n (>structural) 6.00 PT structural ~6–7 — −0.103 6.00 yes Policy rate 2.41 y (≈ECB neutral) 2.00 ECB neutral ~2.0–2.5 — −0.090 2.00 yes (≈no-op) Short rate 2.56 y 2.00 tracks ECB / ESTR neutral — −0.228 2.00 yes Bund 10Y (YIELD10_DE) 2.62 y 2.50 Bund neutral ~ECB 2 + small term prem — −0.015 2.50 yes (≈no-op) PT–DE 10Y spread 1.25 y (a touch wide) 0.80 PT–DE structural spread ~0.6–1.0 — −0.120 0.80 yes NFC lending rate 4.90 y (a touch high) 3.00 ECB neutral ~2 + NFC spread ~1 0.52 −3.685 3.00 yes (near-singular, largest PT δc)

PT is far tamer than the floaters: mild inflation overshoot, unemployment drifts above structural, the policy rate and Bund yield are already near neutral, growth essentially on potential. All mild drift, all resolvable with small public anchors. Fiscal residuals (FREV/FPEXP/SFA/EFFI) are accounting identities not forecastable macro targets and are excluded; EFFI is guard-disabled (sens −0.01). LENDR_PT sens (0.52) is just above the 0.3 guard → its δc (−3.69) is the largest PT shift but still one defensible long-run move.

docs.title: RU — Russia (CBR; fiscal/credit block)
docs.text:
var term plausible anchor basis sens δc glided resolves GDP growth 2.35 borderline (>sanction-constrained potential) 1.50 IMF/WEO RU potential ~1.0–1.5 — −0.271 1.50 yes Inflation (CPI) 6.14 n (stuck ~6, no glide to 4) 4.00 CBR target 4.0 — −0.502 4.00 yes Policy rate 14.75 n (frozen crisis-tight) 7.00 CBR neutral = target 4 + real ~2.5–3 — −0.757 7.00 yes Money-market rate 13.11 n (frozen tight) 7.00 tracks CBR key-rate neutral ~7 — −0.871 7.00 yes NFC lending rate 13.95 n (frozen tight) 9.00 neutral key ~7 + lending spread ~2 — −0.456 9.00 yes Unemployment 2.96 borderline (very low, tight labour mkt) 4.50 RU structural ~4–5 — +0.141 4.50 yes

RU's RW-BVAR freezes near today's crisis-level tight regime: policy 14.75%, money-market 13.1%, lending 13.95%, inflation 6.1% (above the 4% target). The paths are essentially flat (not explosive) — pure RW persistence of the current high-rate/high-inflation regime, not structural breakage. The required level shifts are the largest of the three O–R blocs (a high-rate economy reverting to a high-ish neutral), but each is a reasonable single long-run drift. All resolve.

docs.title: Verdict (O–R)
docs.text:
Every graded variable across PL, PT, RU resolves with a single publicly-defensible δc — no UK-debt-style structural break in this band. PL is the cleanest drift case (overshoot-then-bleed-back inflation + extrapolated-high-rate paths), PT the tamest (mild euro-member drift), RU the largest-δc (frozen crisis-tight regime reverting to neutral). One slider to flag: PL POLRATE (sens −9.94, counter-intuitive sign) and one near-singular: PT LENDR (sens 0.52).

Recommended ship-with defaults (O–R):

C GDP Infl Pol Unemp Notes PL 2.5 2.5 4.0 5.0 10Y 4.5, lending 5.5; POLRATE sens<0 — tooltip PT 1.2 2.0 2.0 6.0 Bund 2.5, spread 0.8, lending 3.0; EFFI guard-disabled RU 1.5 4.0 7.0 4.5 money-mkt 7.0, lending 9.0; frozen-tight regime reverts to neutral

Read-only smoke test; no app/src or data files were modified.

docs.title: BVAR Scenario Tool — Responsiveness Fix Plan
docs.text:
Branch context: live = improve-live @ 89c4501. A concurrent delta-c workflow is editing ChartGrid.tsx, useStore.ts, gvarSolve.ts, ControlPanel.tsx. Fixes touching those files are flagged [overlaps delta-c → sequence after]; everything else is [independent → do now].

This plan is the synthesis of 5 profiling dimensions: React re-render, compute/solve floor, Plotly rendering, bundle/load, and end-to-end interaction latency. All file sizes and claims below were spot-verified against the working tree (companion US = 34,167,515 B → gzip 13,161,101 B = 2.6×; scenario_priors = 559 MB; zero scattergl; no uirevision on <Plot>; setBvarData(null) on switch; LazyChart defined at ChartGrid.tsx:2270, used 0×).

docs.title: QUICK WINS — low-effort, low-risk, do now (independent of delta-c)
docs.text:
These can land immediately, in parallel with the delta-c slider work, with no merge contention:

  1. #1 Pre-gzip companion_draws.json — the single biggest byte-for-effort win on the Compute path. Reuses the proven coupling-artifact .gz + DecompressionStream pattern. Also cuts the deploy ~310 MB.
  2. #2 Prune scenario_priors/ — 559 MB of pure dead deploy weight, zero runtime references. One deploy-pipeline change; removes the dominant 1 GB-Pages-limit risk.
  3. #3 Custom scatter-only Plotly bundle — app emits only type:'scatter' (verified: 0 scattergl, 0 other trace types). Drops ~2–2.5 MB of download+parse with zero visual change.
  4. #8 Idle-prefetch US draws — warms _drawsCache while the user reads default charts; first Compute becomes near-instant. (Land after #1 so it prefetches the .gz.)
  5. #9 Parallelize loadBVARData optional fetches — fold the 3 serial baseline_levels / historical_levels / fiscal_meta awaits into the existing Promise.all (use allSettled for 404 tolerance). −2 serial round-trips per switch.
  6. #15 Throttle the 132-fetch loadGVARModels burst — concurrency pool of ~6-8 and/or a shipped manifest to stop probing 33 blocs with 404s; start the coupling .gz download in parallel.

docs.title: 4 (no-unmount-on-switch) is also independent of delta-c but is rated M/med (touches
docs.text: page.tsx render gating + a loading-overlay), so it sits just above the structural tier rather than in the trivially-safe quick-wins list. Strongly recommended as the next thing after the byte wins.

docs.title: STRUCTURAL — higher effort/risk, schedule deliberately
docs.text:
• S1 Band on affine fast path — the remaining compute floor. The mean solve already has a closed-form affine path (solveGVARDirect/affineBlocKernels, ~10-20× faster, byte-equal); the band per-draw loop pointedly does NOT use it. Partner kernels + coupling M are draw-invariant → build once outside the loop; per draw rebuild only the viewed bloc's kernels. EA/JP band ÷4–÷10. Overlaps gvarSolve.ts (delta-c) — sequence after.
  • S2/S3 — second smoother-pass removal + Kalman constant-factor wins. S3 multiplies through every solve/band/kernel call, so it compounds with S1. Both touch gvarSolve.ts/kalman.ts; S3's matrix.ts/kalman.ts work is independent of the delta-c files.
  • S4 Virtualized/staggered grid mount — LazyChart (the index×100ms staggered mounter) is defined but wired into 0 grids (verified). Reviving it (or an IntersectionObserver gate) breaks the ~40-panel synchronous newPlot storm into frames. Pairs naturally with #4.
  • S5 Per-panel memoized trace build — the structural ceiling under the per-render redraw fixes. rawCharts rebuilds all ~35 panels' trace arrays on any scenarios change even though reconcileCharts then repaints only 1. Split the builder so only the touched variable's panel rebuilds. Heavy; overlaps ChartGrid.tsx.

docs.title: 3. Concrete file:line changes per fix
docs.text:
#1 companion_draws gzip — app/src/lib/data/loader.ts:34-50 (loadCompanionDraws): fetch companion_draws.json.gz first, res.body.pipeThrough(new DecompressionStream('gzip')) → text → JSON.parse → existing Float64Array.from; 404-fallback to plain .json. Mirror gvarLoader.ts:100-120 (loadCouplingArtifact). Add a build/CI step to emit the .gz beside each app/public/data/bvar/<C>/companion_draws.json.

#2 scenario_priors prune — no src/ change (0 refs). Deploy step: prune out/data/bvar/*/scenario_priors before publish, or move the dir out of public/. Hand to deploy owner.

#3 scatter-only Plotly — app/package.json:20 keep plotly.js core; new factory module: import Plotly from 'plotly.js/lib/core'; import scatter from 'plotly.js/lib/scatter'; Plotly.register([scatter]); export default createPlotlyComponent(Plotly) (from react-plotly.js/factory). Point app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx:22-28 dynamic() at the new factory module.

#4 no-unmount on switch — app/src/app/page.tsx:20 remove setBvarData(null) (or set a pending flag instead); page.tsx:60-62 render <ChartGrid/> always, layer a translucent loading overlay (pattern exists at ChartGrid.tsx:2037, uncertaintyComputing) until new data lands → panels diff-update via Plotly.react instead of remount.

#5 memo layout / hoist config+style — app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx: hoist module-level const PLOT_CONFIG = { responsive:true, displayModeBar:false, scrollZoom:false } and const PLOT_STYLE = { width:'100%' } (currently inline literals at 2485/2486); wrap layout (2466-2484) in useMemo keyed [chart.title, scenarioName, chart.yUnit, xAxisTicks, showLegend]. plotData already memoized at 2320.

#6 uirevision — app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx:2464-2487 add uirevision={country + ':' + scenarioId} to <Plot> (no uirevision/datarevision present today — verified). Optional datarevision keyed to a trace-content hash for cheap int-compare diffs.

#7 solve-result LRU — app/src/lib/compute/runGvarSolve.ts:47-115: tiny worker-side LRU (8-16 entries), key = modelSig + pinsSig + mode + horizon for mean (pinsSig already computed at useGvarForecast.ts:156); separate band key modelSig+pinsSig+nDraws+viewed. Hit → post back immediately.

#8 idle-prefetch — app/src/app/page.tsx:18-49: after initial render, requestIdleCallback(() => loadCompanionDraws('US')) to warm _drawsCache (post-#1 → fetches .gz).

#9 parallel optional loads — app/src/lib/data/loader.ts:21-23: fold baseline_levels, historical_levels, fiscal_meta into the Promise.all (or a sibling Promise.allSettled) so all seven requests are in flight at once; keep 404 tolerance.

#10 no coupling clone on full solve — app/src/lib/compute/useGvarForecast.ts:223: post the coupling artifact to the worker once (cache worker-side by modelSig), or include it only when runGvarSolve will actually seed it (runGvarSolve.ts:73-77 — artifact.pinStructure matches request); else use withoutCoupling for full solves too and rebuild M from the warm kernel cache.

#11 per-bloc selectors — replace bare useStore() destructures: ChartGrid.tsx:309-312, ControlPanel.tsx:16-19, TopBar.tsx:17, ScenarioBar.tsx:236 → useShallow tuples / per-field selectors of only the fields each reads.

#12 slider rAF — app/src/components/controls/ControlPanel.tsx:514-535: coalesce range onChange to one store write per requestAnimationFrame (mirror ChartGrid.tsx:2399-2411 handleOverlayMouseDown); collapse the double updateScenario at 526-533; commit on pointer-up.

#13 history shape — app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx:1568-1582: move the grey history rect from a fill:'toself' scatter trace into the memoized layout.shapes (type:'rect', yref:'paper', layer:'below').

#14 customdata memo — app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx:2320-2340: precompute the offsetLabel customdata array once per (lastHist, xAll) at grid level (x offsets are a fixed 0..H range shared by all panels); reuse on y-only changes.

#15 throttle GVAR fetches — app/src/lib/data/gvarLoader.ts:124-135: concurrency pool (~6-8) over GVAR_BLOC_CANDIDATES, prefer a shipped manifest over probing 33 blocs; start loadCouplingArtifact() in Promise.all with the bloc fetches, not strictly after (:133).

#16 lift grid-wide values — app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx:2308-2312: lift activeScenarioId/scenarioName/showCounterfactual/growthDisplay/country to ChartGrid once, thread as primitive props into MemoDraggableChart (keeps shallow React.memo skip).

#17 band-worker draws clone — app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx:495-513: cache draws worker-side per country (post once), send only pins/opts after; or transfer a subsampled slice (nDraws rows, not 500).

S1 band affine path — app/src/lib/compute/runGvarBandSolve.ts:108-181: hoist partner kernels + coupling M build out of the draw loop (from meanModels); inside the loop build only the viewed bloc's kernels via affineBlocKernels (already exported) + the solveGVARDirect matvec reconstruction, replacing solveViewedScenLvl→solveGVAR (:146, gvarSolve.ts:536).

S2 baseLvl reuse / scratch — runGvarBandSolve.ts:114-124: reuse the affine pass's Sc baseline as baseLvl instead of a separate makeCountryModel/conditionalForecast (gvarSolve.ts:344-363); back drawCompanion with preallocated Float64Array scratch reused across draws.

S3 Kalman kernel — app/src/lib/compute/kalman.ts: cache Tᵀ per bloc on the StateSpace (:179 rebuilds it per call); multiplyInto(out,a,b) in-place variants for :196 and :469/:478; reuse scratch PPred/PZ across the t-loop (:199 clone, :235-250); matrix.ts:39-52 naive O(n³) matmul allocates a fresh Matrix each call.

S4 virtualize mount — app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx: revive LazyChart (2270-2287, currently 0 usages) or an IntersectionObserver around MemoDraggableChart in the grid maps (2109-2229); triad + fiscal row mount first, below-the-fold on scroll/requestIdleCallback.

S5 per-panel trace memo — app/src/components/charts/ChartGrid.tsx:1379-1905: split per-panel trace building into a memo keyed on that var's slice of effective/scenario; narrow scenarios out of read-only RoW/global panels' deps so one path edit rebuilds 1-2 panels, not 35.

A1 topology audit — app/src/lib/compute/gvarSolve.ts: confirm the live 50-bloc panel uses the endo closed-form (:1686, withRho=false) and never reaches buildGVARCoupling/solveGVARLinear('direct') (:885-896) which calls spectralRadius (:827-857, dim≈2060) and builds the dense (I−M)⁻¹. If any live path hits it, gate behind a withRho flag (export-time only).

docs.title: 4. Single highest-leverage change for perceived responsiveness
docs.text:
#4 — Stop unmounting ChartGrid on country switch (page.tsx:20 setBvarData(null) + page.tsx:60-62 gate).

Rationale: country switch is the most-felt "frozen" action. Today it tears down all ~35 react-plotly panels and cold-restarts them — ~35 serialized Plotly.newPlot + axis/WebGL inits on the main thread, layered on top of the data fetch. Keeping the grid mounted under a loading overlay (the uncertaintyComputing overlay pattern already exists) makes panels diff-update via Plotly.react instead of remounting, so the switch becomes fetch-bound only. It is independent of the delta-c files and pairs naturally with S4.

Byte caveat: for raw download/deploy leverage the top pick is #1 (companion gzip, −21 MB US per Compute) + #2 (scenario_priors prune, −559 MB) — both independent, both quick. #4 is the top pick for interaction-latency feel; #1+#2 are the top picks for load/deploy. Land all three first.

docs.title: 5. Delta-c sequencing note
docs.text: The concurrent delta-c (drift-slider) workflow owns ChartGrid.tsx, useStore.ts, gvarSolve.ts, ControlPanel.tsx. Do now (no contention): #1, #2, #3, #4, #8, #9, #15, S2 (runGvarBandSolve, not in delta-c set), S3 (kalman.ts/matrix.ts), S4 (LazyChart/grid-map region). Sequence after delta-c merges: #5, #6, #7, #10, #11, #12, #13, #14, #16, #17, S1, S5, A1. When delta-c lands, rebase the ChartGrid-local fixes (#5/#6/#13/#14 are all inside DraggableChartImpl, a region delta-c is unlikely to touch — fast to reapply).

docs.title: Scenario Cookbook
docs.text:
The Scenario Cookbook is an instant-load library of named, documented conditioning recipes, reachable from the Cookbook ▾ button in the Control Panel's scenario header (next to Save / Load). It replaces the old download-only Example link: instead of downloading a spreadsheet and re-importing it, you pick a recipe and load its conditioning into the active scenario with one click.

Each recipe is authored for the three fully-instrumented blocs — US, EA, CN — with bloc-specific variable IDs and restriction counts, and embodies a deliberate two-stage pedagogy:

  • Load naive — condition the head variable(s) alone and let the model fill in everything else. The result is the model's own read of that single move, which is usually demand-led (the BVAR's estimated business-cycle co-movement dominates). This is frequently the wrong economic story — the point is to expose it.
  • Load + restriction — add a small number of short-run pins that restrict the joint response into the economically-meaningful shock (e.g. force output down while prices rise → a supply shock). The contrast between the two stages is the entire lesson: same head move, qualitatively different — often sign-flipped — outcome once the identification is imposed.

Every recipe is grounded in a real, citable source. Magnitudes and signs are calibrated to a published IMF/FSAP adverse stress scenario or a well-documented historical episode (cited in each section). The numbers are economically defensible, not illustrative placeholders — but they remain authored (a designer's reading of the cited path into this model's deviation space), surfaced here for the user (an IMF economist) to review and retune. The structure (which variables, naive vs restricted) is the fixed design.

docs.title: How it works (mechanics)
docs.text:
• Recipes live in app/public/data/cookbook/cookbook.json, fetched once at load (like default_anchors.json). Each recipe is keyed by spec variable ID — not column index, because indices differ across blocs — and resolved against the active bloc's spec.json at click time (app/src/lib/data/cookbook.ts, resolveVarId → resolveRecipeStage).
  • A pin's values are stored as-is in the store's deviation space, exactly the convention the conditioning sliders and the Excel-import path use:
  • kind: "log" — a q/q-annualized growth deviation (e.g. GDP −3 = −3 % q/q annualized off baseline for that quarter).
  • kind: "lin" — a level deviation (yields/rates in pp; fiscal ratios in pp of GDP; 0 with active: true = pinned unchanged).
  • kind: "levelDisplay" — a direct 100·log level deviation for level-displayed globals (GLB_OIL, GLB_USEQ); e.g. oil +58.8 ≈ +80 % in price, equity −22.3 ≈ −20 %.
  • Loading a stage calls updateScenario, which REPLACES (not merges) the conditioning fields, so any prior pins are wiped — identical to the Excel-import behaviour. A variable referenced by a recipe but absent from the current bloc is skipped and surfaced in a small transient note (it is never silently dropped).
  • GLB_OIL is a global-block variable: conditioning it propagates to every bloc automatically at solve time (gvarSolve.ts applyGlobalPins), so the energy recipe is global by construction.
  • Cross-country spillovers (v1 scope). A recipe writes only the active bloc's pins. To see the cross-country answer, press Solve full GVAR after loading (the existing ChartGrid mirror effect propagates the active pins into gvarPins). Multi-bloc-in-one-click recipes are a deferred follow-up.

The variable-ID map across the three blocs:

Role US EA CN Corporate yield / spread FAAA (Aaa) EACORP (IG 10y) — (CORP_CN is the sovereign) US Baa corporate spread GLB_USBAA (Moody's Baa) GLB_USBAA (shared) GLB_USBAA (shared) Government 10y / safe rate GLB_USLR YIELD10_EA CORP_CN (sovereign 10y) Policy rate POLRATE POLRATE_EA POLRATE_CN Short interbank rate — — RATE_CN Inflation JGDP (GDP deflator) HICP_EA CPI_CN Real GDP GDPH GDP_EA GDP_CN (real) Exports XH EXP_EA EXP_CN Imports MH IMP_EA IMP_CN Fiscal revenue / primary spending FREV_GDP / FPEXP_GDP same IDs same IDs Oil price (global) GLB_OIL GLB_OIL GLB_OIL Equity (global) GLB_USEQ GLB_USEQ GLB_USEQ

docs.title: 1 · Credit crunch / spread shock
docs.text:
Source. Federal Reserve, 2020 Severely Adverse supervisory scenario (Feb 2020) — the macro-financial backbone of US bank stress testing and of the IMF United States FSAP 2020 (Country Report 20/242), which documents a wave of corporate-sector defaults amplified by record NFC debt/GDP.

Transmission. A corporate-credit-supply contraction. Default risk on highly-levered firms is repriced, so the IG corporate-to-Treasury spread blows out (to 5.5 pp by 2020Q3, +400 bp vs 2019Q4), and external finance becomes scarce and dear. Simultaneously a flight to safety bids up Treasuries so the risk-FREE 10y FALLS (to 0.75 % on impact) and the policy rate is cut to ~zero — the entire move is in the spread (risk premium), not the safe curve. The higher cost of risk-bearing chokes investment and hiring; output collapses (−8.5 % peak-to-trough), unemployment rises to 10 %, and the shortfall is disinflationary (CPI to ~1.25 %). Equity falls −50 % (VIX 70); house/CRE −28 %/−35 %; the USD appreciates ex-JPY and EA/UK/JP enter severe recessions.

  • Naive — corporate yield up to the documented +400 bp peak (FAAA, front-loaded to 2020Q1–Q3, fading ~2 yr), nothing else pinned. The model's estimated comovement drags the whole curve up together — a generic rate tightening — and, because business-cycle comovement is demand-led, can even read it with the wrong, mildly inflationary sign.
  • Restricted — same +400 bp spread, plus the documented credit-crunch co-movement: the safe 10y DOWN (GLB_USLR flight-to-safety, to ~0.75 % on impact — not flat), output down (GDPH, ~−8.5 % peak-to-trough), inflation down (JGDP, disinflation), equity −50 % (GLB_USEQ), and the policy rate cut to the floor (POLRATE). Spread WIDE while the safe rate FALLS is the credit-SUPPLY identification — the cost of risk-bearing rises as the safe curve drops, not a level shift in rates.

Verified on the live US engine: naive drags the 10y up (+2.45 pp avg q0–7) with the corp shock; the restriction pushes the safe 10y down (−0.56 pp, flight-to-safety) while the spread stays wide (+2.56 pp) and the output hit deepens sharply (−7.3 % vs +0.3 %).

Country notes. US: corp = FAAA, safe 10y = GLB_USLR, equity = GLB_USEQ, policy = POLRATE. EA: corp = EACORP, safe 10y = YIELD10_EA, policy = POLRATE_EA (EA enters a severe recession in the scenario). CN has no separate corporate yield — CORP_CN is the sovereign 10y, so a "pure IG spread" is not identifiable; the CN override widens CORP_CN to a smaller +250 bp (managed, lower beta) and holds the 3M interbank rate (RATE_CN) unchanged, isolating a term/credit-premium move at the long end with the short rate fixed, plus the shared global equity decline.

What changed vs the prior recipe. The spread was rescaled +150 bp → +400 bp (the documented adverse move), and the restricted safe-rate leg was corrected from "hold 10y flat" to "safe 10y DOWN on flight-to-safety" — the prior flat pin contradicted the defining feature of a documented credit crunch (the safe long rate FALLS). Output-down / disinflation / equity-down / policy-cut legs were added so the supply identification is unambiguous.

docs.title: 2 · Energy supply shock (global)
docs.text:
Source. IMF World Economic Outlook, April 2026 ("Global Economy in the Shadow of War", Ch.1), ADVERSE Middle-East energy-supply scenario; transmission elasticity from ECB Working Paper No. 2905 (2024); historical anchor the 1973–74 OPEC embargo (Federal Reserve History).

Transmission. An adverse-supply (cost-push) shock. A crude/gas-supply disruption raises the energy import bill → first-round pass-through to headline inflation (ECB WP 2905: ~+0.6 pp HICP per +10 % gas supply shock at 1 yr) and a terms-of-trade / real-income squeeze for energy importers that compresses real demand → output down while prices are up (stagflation; cf. 1973–74: oil ~4×, US CPI 12.3 % in 1974, real GDP −2.1 %). The WEO adverse scenario adds a financial-conditions leg: corporate risk premia +50 bp (advanced)/+100 bp (EM ex-China), EM sovereign spreads +50 bp, equity down, inflation-expectations drift — net global growth cut 3.1 % → 2.5 %, inflation 4.4 % → 5.4 %. The BVAR's unconditional oil comovement is dominated by demand-led oil episodes, so conditioning oil alone gives the wrong-signed "demand read" (output up).

  • Naive — oil up +80 % (GLB_OIL level-display +58.8 = 100·log 1.80, ~1 yr hump then unwinding), domestic block free. The demand-led comovement can show output rising — clearly wrong for a supply disruption.
  • Restricted — keep the oil path and add the supply + financial-conditions co-movement: real GDP down (US ~−3.5, EA ~−4, CN ~−2.5 q/q-ann, decaying over ~2 yr), inflation up (anchored to ECB WP 2905, scaled to gas +160 %), corporate spread +50 bp (FAAA/EACORP; +100 bp for CORP_CN), and equity ~−12 % (GLB_USEQ). This converts a textbook goods-market oil shock into the full macro-FINANCIAL adverse scenario.

Verified on the live US engine: naive (oil-only) output +0.4 % q0–3 (demand read); restricted output down (< −0.3 %) with inflation higher — the restriction flips the output sign.

Country notes. gdp = GDPH/GDP_EA/GDP_CN; infl = JGDP/HICP_EA/CPI_CN; corp = FAAA/EACORP/CORP_CN; equity = GLB_USEQ. EA is the most oil-import-intensive bloc, so its output/inflation hit is deepest; CN's GDP_CN is real and its managed/subsidised pass-through warrants a cushioned calibration. A severe variant maps to the WEO severe leg: oil +100 % sustained (GLB_OIL +69.3), premia +100 bp (AE+China)/+200 bp (EM ex-China), growth cut ~−1.1 pp.

What changed. Oil rescaled +40 % → +80 % (the WEO adverse path); the GDP/inflation horizon lengthened from 2 q to the ~2-year energy hump; and the financial leg (corporate spreads + equity) was added — the WEO scenario is explicitly macro-financial, which the prior pure-goods-market recipe omitted.

docs.title: 3 · Fiscal loosening → twin deficits
docs.text:
Source. IMF 2018 Article IV Consultation (Country Report No. 18/207, July 2018) and the 2018 External Sector Report, assessing the 2018 US Tax Cuts and Jobs Act + Bipartisan Budget Act.

Transmission. A procyclical fiscal expansion onto an economy already at full employment. The loosening widened the GG structural primary deficit by ~1 pp of potential GDP (−2.7 % → −3.8 %, 2017→2019) and lifted GG gross debt ~106 % → ~113 % of GDP. With demand outrunning supply, the Fed must "raise policy rates at a faster pace", which together with the demand pull puts upward pressure on the dollar (judged 8–16 % overvalued), so imports rise and the current-account deficit widens to ~3½ % of GDP by 2019–20, the NIIP falling ~8 pp / 5 yr — the saving-investment identity CA = Sprivate + Sgov − I made concrete (twin deficits). Near-term growth boost ~+0.9 pp (real GDP 2.3 % → 2.9 %), deflator 1.8 % → 2.8 %.

  • Naive — primary expenditure up ~+1 pp of GDP (FPEXP_GDP) and revenue down ~−0.5 pp (FREV_GDP), a ~1.5 pp primary loosening matched to the episode, fading after ~2 yr. The model reads a generic demand stimulus: output/inflation rise and the fiscal block turns debt/GDP up — but it does not, on its own, reproduce the external leg.
  • Restricted — keep the fiscal pins and add the IMF twin-deficits identification: a +0.9 q/q-ann GDP impulse for ~1 yr (the documented above-potential boost) and the procyclical-at-full-employment restriction — the policy rate up ~+50 bp (POLRATE, the Fed "raises at a faster pace" → upward dollar pressure → wider CA). The pedagogical payload is the joint deterioration: debt/GDP up (fiscal block) and, once the external/NFA satellite is live, trade balance / net-foreign-assets down.

Verified on the live US engine: the fiscal drivers are conditioned in both stages (the debt read-through is the payload); the explicit demand + policy-rate restriction lifts output vs the naive fiscal-ratio read (restricted > naive by a material margin).

Country notes. FREV_GDP / FPEXP_GDP, GDP (GDPH/GDP_EA/GDP_CN) and the policy rate (POLRATE/POLRATE_EA/POLRATE_CN) exist in all three blocs. CN uses smaller fiscal, demand and policy-rate moves given its automatic stabilisers and managed external account (ESR CA norm −¼ % for China vs −¾ % for the US), so its twin-deficits read-through is deliberately weaker.

What changed. Fiscal magnitude rescaled from a ~3 pp primary loosening (+2 / −1) to the documented ~1.5 pp (+1 / −0.5); the demand impulse softened from +2 (2 q) to +0.9 (~4 q); and a policy-rate-up restriction was added — the missing monetary leg that actually identifies the twin-deficit channel (stronger dollar → wider CA), rather than relying on an outsized demand impulse alone.

docs.title: 4 · Monetary behind-the-curve / risk-off
docs.text:
Source. Federal Reserve 2020 Severely Adverse scenario (released 6 Feb 2020) — the published macro-financial path underlying the IMF FSAP United States 2020 bank solvency stress test (Country Report No. 20/247, Aug 2020).

Transmission. An abrupt collapse in risk appetite reprices the equity and credit risk premia simultaneously: equities crash −50 % (VIX 70), the IG corporate spread widens to 5.5 pp (+4.0 pp), and a flight to safety pushes the safe 10y DOWN (to 0.75 %). The widening external-finance premium chokes investment/consumption → a deep, disinflationary recession (GDP −8.5 %, unemployment 10 %). The identifying restriction for a behind-the-curve variant is to pin the policy rate flat against this tightening — forcing the central bank to under-respond, which makes the recession deeper than the endogenous reaction function would deliver.

  • Naive — condition only the headline lever, the corporate spread up ~+200 bp (FAAA, scaled below the full Fed +400 bp crisis), with policy and everything else free. The model returns its generic "financial-conditions tightening" read: output/inflation soften and the policy rate eases endogenously. Inflation is left free and falls — the shock is disinflationary.
  • Restricted — impose the full identified risk-off: equity ~−25 % (GLB_USEQ), corporate spread up, the safe 10y DOWN (GLB_USLR, flight-to-safety), and the policy rate pinned flat (POLRATE = 0, active) — the bank under-reacts ("behind the curve"), so the tightening transmits fully to demand.

Country notes. equity = GLB_USEQ (global); corp spread = FAAA (US) / EACORP (EA); safe 10y = GLB_USLR (US) / YIELD10_EA (EA); policy rate = POLRATE / POLRATE_EA / POLRATE_CN. CN has no traded IG corporate spread (CORP_CN is the sovereign 10y), so the CN block uses CORP_CN with a POSITIVE sign — in a global risk-off EM sovereign yields rise via capital outflow and a sovereign-risk-premium channel, the opposite of the AE safe-haven bid — and takes equity from the global GLB_USEQ. Magnitudes are ~half the full Fed crisis severity (a sharp repricing, not a once-in-a-generation crash).

What changed. The recipe was previously internally incoherent — it pinned inflation up (a behind-the-curve inflationary story) while imposing a flight-to-safety disinflationary risk-off block. The inflation-up pin is removed (inflation now free, and falls); naive is reframed as the bare headline (corp spread up); magnitudes rescaled toward the cited source (equity −20 % → −25 %, safe 10y −50 bp → ~−100 bp); and the CN safe-haven leg sign-flipped to a positive EM sovereign-yield move.

docs.title: 5 · AI productivity boost
docs.text:
Source. IMF Working Paper WP/25/76, "The Global Impact of AI: Mind the Gap" (Cerutti et al., April 2025) — GIMF-GVC simulations of an AI-driven TFP shock; historical analogue the 1995–2004 US ICT boom (McKinsey 2001; FRBSF Economic Letter 2015-04).

Transmission. A positive supply (TFP) shock. AI diffusion raises total factor productivity, lifting potential output and capitalising higher future earnings into equity. The price side is two-phase (the heart of the identification): (i) short run — investment/wealth effects lift demand before capacity installs, so inflation ticks up modestly and central banks tighten modestly; (ii) medium run — productivity expands supply faster than demand and inflation declines (output up while inflation falls = the supply signature). The paper's central result is a gap: 10-yr output gain ~+5.4–5.6 % (US) and ~+4 % (global) high-TFP, with EM gains ~+3.0–3.5 % — advanced economies gain >2× low-income.

  • Naive — real GDP growth up by a realistic ~+1 pp/yr (US +1.0, EA +0.8, CN +0.6 q/q-ann for 2 yr), prices free. The model's demand-led comovement shows inflation rising with output — the wrong sign for a supply expansion.
  • Restricted — keep the output pins and add (i) an inflation pin with the documented two-phase shape — a small short-run overshoot that turns to disinflation by ~q4 (JGDP from +0.4 to −0.5) — and (ii) an equity pin up ~+14 % (GLB_USEQ). Output up while inflation ends down flips identification from a demand boom to a positive supply shock.

Verified on the live US engine: restricted output up (> +1 % q2–7), medium-horizon inflation negative and below the naive read, equity up — a clean positive supply shock.

Country notes. gdp = GDPH/GDP_EA/GDP_CN; infl = JGDP/HICP_EA/CPI_CN; equity = GLB_USEQ. Magnitudes are asymmetric (US > EA ≳ CN), reflecting the paper's "gap" result. CN's GDP_CN is real, so "output up, CPI contained" reads directly as a real supply shock.

What changed. Output rescaled +3 → ~+1 pp/yr (the old +3 for 8 q compounded to ~+6 % in 2 yr, ~5–6× the IMF decade path); the inflation restriction reshaped from flat-down to up-then-down (capturing the documented short-run demand overshoot); and magnitudes made asymmetric across blocs instead of identical.

docs.title: 6 · Structural supply-chain collapse
docs.text:
Source. Liu & Nguyen, "Global Supply Chain Pressures and U.S. Inflation", FRBSF Economic Letter 2023-14 (June 2023); output side from Kemp, Portillo & Santoro, IMF Working Paper No. 2023/042 (Feb 2023) and the IMF WEO Update (Jan 2022). The episode: the 2021–22 pandemic supply-chain bottleneck.

Transmission. A goods-demand surge meets constrained supply — port congestion, intermediate-input/ semiconductor shortages, upstream Asian production stoppages (2021–22 China port closures, 2022 Shanghai lockdown). Scarce inputs propagate downstream, lifting input/import prices (Liu-Nguyen: a 1-SD GSCPI shock raises US headline PCE ~+0.5 pp, goods inflation up to ~+1.5 pp; GSCPI shocks added ~+2.5 pp to headline inflation at the 2021–22 peak, ~60 % of the above-trend run-up) while curtailing output (Kemp-Portillo-Santoro: 2021 disruptions subtracted 0.5–1.2 % from global value added; WEO Jan-2022 growth markdowns −1.2 pp US, −0.8 pp China, −0.4 pp euro area). Net stagflationary — output down, prices up. Crucially the effect is ~1 year with decay (Liu-Nguyen: "statistically vanishing about a year after the impact"), not multi-year.

  • Naive — condition only the trade lever (US/EA imports down ~−3 q/q-ann for 3 q; China, the upstream source, exports down ~−6). The model treats weak trade as a demand signal and tends to pull inflation down — the wrong sign for a bottleneck.
  • Restricted — add the supply identification, scaled to the evidence: output down by the documented (modest) amounts — US GDP ~−1.2 pp, EA ~−0.4/−0.5 pp, CN ~−0.8 pp — with inflation up and goods-led (US/EA ~+1.5 to +2.5 q/q-ann peak), all decaying inside ~4–5 quarters.

Country notes. imports = MH (US) / IMP_EA; gdp = GDPH/GDP_EA/GDP_CN; infl = JGDP/HICP_EA. China sits upstream, so its export/production stoppage (EXP_CN) is the front-line channel and a source of others' import-price spikes — but China itself ran near-zero/disinflationary CPI in 2021–22 (it exported disinflation), so the CN block holds output down with prices flat (no domestic inflation-up restriction), sharpening the "China exports disinflation while bottlenecking supply" lesson.

What changed. Magnitudes cut sharply to the documented modest, ~1-year episode: GDP −4 → −1.2, imports −8 → −3, horizon 6+ q → ~4 q. The naive import-volume drop was trimmed (in 2021 US goods imports rose — the problem was prices/congestion, not collapsing volumes), and the CN inflation-up pin was removed (China was disinflationary, not a domestic-inflation recipient).

docs.title: 7 · Deglobalization / trade compression
docs.text:
Source. IMF Staff Discussion Note SDN/2023/001, "Geoeconomic Fragmentation and the Future of Multilateralism" (Aiyar et al., Jan 2023); FDI channel from IMF WEO Apr 2023, Ch.4; price pass-through from Amiti, Redding & Weinstein (JEP 2019) on the 2018-19 US-China trade war.

Transmission. A structural retreat from trade integration. Higher cross-bloc barriers curtail both export demand and import supply (trade volumes compress permanently); the SDN estimates trade fragmentation alone lowers global GDP 0.2 % → ~7 % (baseline ~1.2 %; technological decoupling raises EM losses to 8–12 %), with cross-bloc FDI curtailment adding ~2 % (WEO Ch.4). Re-shoring raises the price LEVEL of tradables (Amiti-Redding-Weinstein: complete pass-through of 2018 tariffs into US prices — a one-off level step), and commodity-market segmentation is inflationary specifically for the import-dependent advanced bloc while the surplus exporter faces a demand/output loss that is closer to disinflationary — so the inflation effect is asymmetric, not uniform.

  • Naive — exports and imports down, front-loaded then easing (cumulative ~−9 % volume; the CN export leg deeper at ~−14 %, since the surplus exporter is hit hardest), prices free. The model reads a symmetric trade contraction largely as a wash for activity and leaves inflation little changed.
  • Restricted — keep the trade compression and add a modest, front-loaded-then-fading price-LEVEL step in the import-dependent advanced blocs only (US JGDP / EA HICP_EA, ~+1.3–1.5 % cumulative over six quarters then reverting) — a one-off pass-through step, NOT a permanent +1 pp inflation rate. China is not pinned up on inflation: as the surplus exporter facing a demand/output loss, its domestic price effect is ambiguous-to-disinflationary, so the CN block applies a small negative deviation.

Country notes. exports = XH/EXP_EA/EXP_CN; imports = MH/IMP_EA/IMP_CN; infl = JGDP/HICP_EA/CPI_CN. The CN override deepens the export compression (surplus exporter hit hardest) and removes/negates the inflation pin. The recipe also reads through to the external block.

What changed. The inflation pin changed from a permanent +1 pp inflation RATE applied uniformly to all blocs (which compounds the price level ~+8 % over 8 q and mis-signs China) to a front-loaded one-off price-LEVEL step in the advanced blocs only, with China disinflationary — matching the documented complete-pass-through level effect and the SDN's bloc-asymmetric inflation finding. Trade magnitudes are anchored to the SDN severe two-bloc tail.

docs.title: 8 · Inflation de-anchoring
docs.text:
Source. EBA/ECB 2023 EU-wide banking-sector stress-test ADVERSE scenario (ECB-PUBLIC, Jan 2023) — a documented wage-price-spiral / de-anchoring path; cross-checked against the IMF GFSR Oct 2023 global stress test and the China 2024 FSSA stagflation case.

Transmission. A cost-push energy/commodity supply shock (oil +54 %, gas to 180 EUR/MWh; 53 % of the HICP impact) collides with a wage-price spiral: real-income losses drive higher wage claims, firms pass costs into prices (second-round effects ~60 % of the HICP-level rise), expectations and wage claims ratchet up, and EA HICP stays 9.7 % (2023) → 5.3 % (2024) → 3.8 % (2025) — persistently above target even as growth turns negative (stagflation). "The increase in current and expected inflation … leads to higher market interest rates": de-anchored expectations are priced into the long end as a higher term/inflation-risk premium — EA 10y 2.79 % → 5.42 % → 4.54 %; US 10y 3.62 % → 5.82 % → 4.87 % (curves invert). It is a cost-push shock that de-anchors expectations, not a demand shock — which is why inflation does not mean-revert and the term premium rises.

  • Naive — inflation up +4 q/q-ann in the first year only, then free (JGDP/HICP_EA/CPI_CN). The model's mean-reversion pulls inflation back toward its anchor — a transitory spike that fades. The "well-anchored" baseline.
  • Restricted — hold inflation +2 q/q-ann through the long horizon (matching the ~+1.8 pp yr-3 residual above target) and impose the term-premium repricing as an abrupt, front-loaded jump (not a slow build): EA 10y +260 bp in yr 1 easing to +175 bp (YIELD10_EA); US 10y +220 bp easing to +125 bp (GLB_USLR); CN sovereign a muted +100 bp → +75 bp (CORP_CN, the China FSSA imported-oil-supply case with a smaller, partly de-coupled domestic rate response).

Verified on the live US engine: naive long-horizon inflation fades (transitory); restricted persists (> +1.5 % q10–15) and lifts the long 10y yield vs naive (the term premium).

Country notes. infl = JGDP/HICP_EA/CPI_CN; long yield = GLB_USLR (US) / YIELD10_EA (EA) / CORP_CN (CN sovereign-10y proxy). Magnitudes are bloc-specific (calibrated to the EBA adverse 10y paths and the IMF China FSSA), not held identical.

What changed. The naive spike was raised +3 → +4 (the EBA peak was 9.7 %), and the long-yield pin was raised from a gradual +100 bp to an abrupt, front-loaded +220–260 bp — the documented de-anchoring repricing is a sudden term-premium jump, not a slow build, and the prior pin understated the peak ~2× and mistimed it.

docs.title: 9 · Fiscal default likelihood ↑ (sovereign risk premium)
docs.text:
Source. IMF GFSR October 2025 ("Shifting Ground beneath the Calm", Ch.1) + Fiscal Monitor April 2026 on the erosion of the US Treasury safety/convenience premium (deficit 7–8 % of GDP, gross debt → ~142 % by 2031 / ~156 % by 2055); the distress co-movement calibrated from the Fed 2024 Severely Adverse scenario; the sovereign-spike-with-distress + sovereign-bank loop from the euro-area sovereign crisis 2010-12 (Italy 10y 4 % → 7 %, Spain > 7 %) and the IMF Spain 2024 FSSA (cumulative −7 pp real GDP).

Transmission. As perceived sovereign-default risk rises, the bond loses its safe-haven status, so the 10y govt yield rises via a higher term/risk premium EVEN AS activity weakens — it does not fall in the usual flight-to-quality way. This is the identifying signature: in the Fed's 2024 severely-adverse generic recession the 10y falls 3.7 pp, the opposite of the fiscal-risk leg. The sovereign risk premium passes through to private credit (corporate, esp. lower-rated Baa/BBB, spreads widen; banks holding sovereign debt take losses and tighten — the sovereign-bank loop), and higher debt-service feeds the self-reinforcing risk-premium → debt → risk-premium loop.

  • Naive — the 10y government yield up ~+200 bp peak, persistent (a level shift, only partly decaying — GLB_USLR/YIELD10_EA/CORP_CN), rest free. The model reads a higher safe rate as tighter monetary conditions — but unlike a flight-to-quality recession (where the safe 10y falls), this looks like an unexplained tightening.
  • Restricted — keep the sovereign-yield pin and add the fiscal-distress co-movement that identifies the shock: the corporate spread up (US GLB_USBAA, Moody's Baa — toward the Fed-2024 +410 bp tail; EA EACORP), equity down ~−35–55 % (GLB_USEQ), and real GDP down (a deeper, −8.5 %-style contraction sustained 6+ quarters). The identifying restriction is that the safe sovereign yield rises WHILE equity and output collapse — the sovereign is no longer a haven (safety-premium erosion). Reads through to the fiscal block, worsening debt/GDP.

Country notes. gov10y = GLB_USLR (US) / YIELD10_EA (EA) / CORP_CN (CN sovereign); corp = GLB_USBAA (US Baa — the more credit-sensitive, Fed-BBB-consistent lever) / EACORP (EA); equity = GLB_USEQ. CN's CORP_CN IS the sovereign yield with no separate corporate spread, so the CN override applies the risk premium to CORP_CN, drops the corporate-spread leg, and keeps equity + output-down distress sized smaller (managed market, capital-account frictions). EA magnitudes are deeper than the US (periphery sovereign-spike / sovereign-bank loop, 2010-12 + Spain 2024 FSSA).

What changed. The US corp lever switched FAAA (Aaa) → GLB_USBAA (Baa) — the more credit-sensitive, Fed-BBB-consistent grade; spread/GDP magnitudes raised toward the documented tail (+150 bp → +350 bp Baa peak; GDP −3 % → ~−8.5 % over 6 q); and an equity-down leg added as the falsifiable distress identifier (safe yield up while equity/output collapse = the sovereign is no longer a haven).

docs.title: Using a recipe
docs.text:
1. Pick the bloc (US / EA / CN) in the top bar.
  2. Open Cookbook ▾ in the scenario header. The list is filtered to recipes applicable to the active bloc; expand any row to read its rationale.
  3. Press Load naive to see the model's own (usually demand-led) read, then Load + restriction to see the identified shock. Compare the two — the qualitative flip is the lesson.
  4. For the cross-country answer, press Solve full GVAR after loading (v1 writes the active bloc's pins only).
  5. Save / Load still export/import a scenario to/from Excel exactly as before; the cookbook does not change that workflow.

Recipes #3 (fiscal loosening) and #7 (deglobalization) explicitly read through to the external / NFA satellite once that block ships, and #3 / #9 to the fiscal block; the cookbook is the demand side of those satellites' pedagogy.

docs.title: MFM — MP Committee Seminar: Speaking Notes (15 min, live, no slides)
docs.text: Language rule: every conditional path is "the most likely projection given the conditioning assumptions" (Waggoner–Zha). Never say "entropic tilting." The consensus long-run anchors are a separate, deterministic drift.

docs.title: ⏱️ Introduction (~1 minute, spoken)
docs.text:
"Thank you. What I'd like to show you is a tool we've built in MCM for global macro-financial forecasting and scenario analysis. The problem it solves is a practical one: when we need a coherent projection for inflation, policy rates, activity and public debt — not for one country but across the major economies and consistent with each other — we normally stitch together separate models and spreadsheets. This does it in one place, live.

Under the hood it's a system of Bayesian VARs — around fifty economies, thirty-three of them linked into a cross-country Global VAR — with a fiscal satellite that turns four estimated fiscal variables into a debt-sustainability path, and a conditioning engine that lets you impose assumptions and read off the most likely outcome. Three things make it more than a collection of country models: it is estimated jointly on real-time, unbalanced, COVID-affected data without discarding observations; the countries are genuinely coupled, so a shock in one bloc propagates to the others; and the whole thing — central projection, scenarios, densities — exports to Excel with one click.

So let me start with the unconditional projection, show how the variables are modeled and estimated, then build a few scenarios live and look at the international spillovers."

docs.title: 1 · Unconditional / central projection — US (~3.5 min)
docs.text:
Monetary block — what's on screen and how it's modeled - Variables in the top row: Inflation (JGDP), Policy Rate (POLRATE), GDP growth (GDPH); unemployment and yields below. - Levels vs rates (the key modeling point): "Price variables are modeled as 100·log price levels, not as inflation rates — the Minnesota prior shrinks each toward a random walk in the level, which is the right null for a near-unit-root price. The inflation you see is a display transform: four times the quarterly log-difference, annualized. The policy rate and yields, by contrast, are modeled directly as levels in percent — bounded, mean-reverting series. So it's internally consistent: a price is a level, a rate is a level, inflation is a transform of the price level." - Estimation: "Estimated with the Giannone–Lenza–Primiceri Minnesota prior — a hierarchical Bayesian VAR where the shrinkage hyperparameters are themselves estimated, so the data choose how tight the prior is, rather than us fixing it." - COVID: "2020 is handled as missing data — the macro variables in the pandemic quarters are masked and imputed inside the Gibbs sampler, conditional on the financial variables which stay observed. So a once-in-a-century outlier doesn't distort the estimated dynamics or the prior." - The anchors: "The central path is anchored to consensus long-run targets — inflation glides to ~2%, the policy rate to neutral, fiscal ratios to CBO/IMF. That's a transparent, adjustable long-run drift, and it's separate from any scenario conditioning."

Fiscal satellite — beyond a standard macro VAR - "Government debt is not a VAR variable. The BVAR estimates four fiscal series: revenue, primary expenditure, the effective interest rate on the stock, and the stock-flow-adjustment residual. Debt is derived by the budget identity: dₜ = dₜ₋₁·(1+i)/(1+g) − pb + sfa." - The three forces: "(1+i)/(1+g) is the snowball — when the effective rate exceeds nominal growth, debt rises mechanically even with a balanced budget. pb = revenue − primary spending is the discretionary effort. The SFA is the below-the-line and valuation residual the headline deficit misses." - The result: "With the consensus anchors, US debt rises to about 143% over five years — CBO-consistent — driven by a persistent primary deficit and the effective rate converging up toward growth." - Bands: "The debt fan is produced by running this recursion per posterior draw, so the uncertainty is the model's, propagated through the identity."

Excel hand-off - Set Uncertainty = Quick → Compute → Excel. - "One sheet per variable, organized by date: the unconditional density — 16th and 84th percentiles around the median — next to the scenario path and the deviation, plus a Summary tab with all variables side by side and a Notes tab documenting each source series and its units."

docs.title: 2 · GFC corporate-yield shock — Waggoner–Zha (~4 min)
docs.text:
• Frame the method: "Conditioning is Waggoner–Zha — you impose a path on some variables and the model returns the most likely projection for everything else, consistent with the estimated dynamics."
  • Step 1 — corporate up, 10Y free: condition Aaa corporate (FAAA) up ~150–200 bp over the next year; leave the Treasury 10-year free; Compute.
  • "Watch the risk-free 10-year — the model pulls it down. That's flight-to-quality: investors flee to safety, the risk-free curve falls, activity and inflation soften. A recession read — and it's the model reading the historical co-movement of spreads and Treasuries in a stress."
  • Step 2 — also pin the 10Y unchanged: condition 10Y (GLB_USLR), leave it flat; Compute.
  • "Now I've isolated the shock: corporate up, risk-free curve held flat — a pure credit-spread / financial-conditions tightening, with no flight-to-quality offset. That's the clean, interpretable scenario you'd hand to a stress test."
  • The takeaway: "Same corporate shock — but conditioning on the 10-year flips the whole story from a recession to a contained financial-conditions shock. Conditioning is the scenario design."

docs.title: 3 · EA-yield shock → US & global spillovers — the GVAR (~3 min)
docs.text:
• "Now the international dimension. I'll run the same yield shock, but in the Euro Area, and watch it reach the US."
  • Switch to Euro Area → condition YIELD10_EA (or EACORP) up → turn "Scenario spillovers" ON → "Solve full GVAR."
  • The mechanism: "Each country's VAR carries three trade-weighted foreign aggregates — foreign demand, foreign inflation, foreign short rate — built from its partners. The EA shock raises EA's contribution to every partner's aggregates, and the solver iterates the coupled system to a fixed point, x = Mx + d, in closed form. A single-country EA model would hold the US fixed; the GVAR lets EA tightening pull on US financial conditions, US activity, oil and global equity."
  • Switch to US: "The US block moves even though I put no US conditioning on it — that's the pure EA→US spillover. This is what lets us discuss the global transmission of a regional financial shock."
  • (Reminder: without the spillover switch / Solve full GVAR, EA moves alone.)

docs.title: 4 · US oil/energy — demand vs supply (~3 min)
docs.text:
• "Last one — how the same oil price move can mean two opposite things. Reset first, back to the US."
  • Step 1 — oil up alone: condition WTI (PZTEXP) up to ~$100, nothing else; Compute.
  • "US output goes up. Why? The model reads the historical oil–output correlation as demand-driven — historically oil rises when global demand is strong — so high oil alone pulls activity up. The demand-dominant response."
  • Step 2 — add short-term sign restrictions: keep oil up, also pin GDP growth (GDPH) down and inflation (JGDP) up at t+1..t+2 only; Compute.
  • "A couple of Cholesky-type sign restrictions on the impact quarters now identify it as an adverse supply shock: output falls, inflation rises — stagflation — with the later quarters left free for the model to fill in. The contrast is the point: conditioning is identification, and two sign restrictions turn a demand shock into a supply shock."

docs.title: Close (~30s)
docs.text: • "To sum up: a central projection anchored to consensus, Waggoner–Zha scenarios layered on top, genuine cross-country spillovers through the GVAR, and a one-click Excel hand-off — for fifty economies. Happy to take it anywhere you like — pick a country, pick a shock."

docs.title: 🛡️ If pushed — three honest defenses
docs.text:
• How is it estimated? (the contribution): "An EM-flavoured fixed point. The Durbin–Koopman smoother completes each country's missing and COVID-masked data given the current foreign 'star' aggregates (the E-step); each Bayesian bloc is re-estimated on the completed panel (the M-step); we iterate until the trade-weighted stars converge. So the coupled system is estimated jointly on real-time, unbalanced data — no observations dropped — which a classical fixed-weight OLS GVAR cannot do. We're explicit that it's EM in flavour, a contraction in the stars, not a convergence-guaranteed global EM."
  • Does debt feed back into rates / the deficit? "The displayed debt path is a transparent accounting recursion on the four forecast drivers plus the anchors. A Bohn fiscal-reaction function and a sovereign risk-premium are coded but dormant — a documented possible extension, not active in what you're seeing."
  • Anchors vs conditioning: "The long-run anchors are a deterministic drift to consensus targets; the Waggoner–Zha conditioning is the scenario layer on top — independent of each other, and both toggle off to reveal the raw drifting BVAR."

docs.title: Pre-flight (before the room)
docs.text:
• Country = US, Units = "q/q ann." (not "% vs baseline" — that disables dragging).
  • Anchors confirmed ON: "Long-run targets" reads (7 set).
  • One "Reset all" for a clean start; between cases use "Reset" (keeps anchors).
  • For exports: Uncertainty = Quick → Compute first so the bands populate.
  • Financial charts live in "Global commons" (10Y GLB_USLR, Baa GLB_USBAA, oil GLB_OIL) and "Country-specific" (Aaa FAAA, WTI PZTEXP) — scroll down once to locate them.

docs.title: Synthetic-Data Audit — improve-live @ 82cc6ab
docs.text:
Status: PUBLISH HALTED. READ-ONLY audit per the NO-SYNTHETIC-DATA directive (drop any made-up / extrapolated / backfilled / held-last / frozen-mirror observation; ship only genuine source data). No data or code was edited — this document is the only artifact written. The user decides the drop approach before anything changes.

UPDATE 2026-06 — CN GDP_CN RESOLVED. The audit's principal offender (CN GDP shipped as a nominal-GDP series labelled real, also 9q backfilled) has been fixed by a surgical CN-only re-estimate: GDP_CN is now genuine real GDP (OECD QNA / NBS chain-linked SA volume index, base 2011Q1=100; 2011Q1+ genuine untouched, 1999–2010 genuine annual real growth Denton-disaggregated), spec.json var 0 renamed "CN Real GDP …", fiscal_meta.json idx.gdpIsNominal=false. The fix re-estimated CN only; the other 32 GVAR blocs are byte-identical. The CN-GDP rows in the tables below are superseded by this; the remaining CN flags (frozen RATE/REER/EQ mirrors, held-last fiscal) still stand.

The COVID mask (2020Q2–2021Q1 NaN) is missing data, not synthetic, and is not flagged. Honest None/NaN ragged tails (blank trailing cells) are missing, not synthetic.

Scope. 50 blocs from the supplied per-bloc audit, plus independent byte-level verification of the load-bearing categories against shipped historical_levels.json, spec.json, dates.ts, pipeline/build_gvar_2025q4.py, and app/scripts/refresh-quarter.mjs. The detailed per-bloc sub-audits (A..C and L..N) produced by earlier passes are preserved verbatim as Appendices A and B below.

GAP: three blocs on disk — EA, EE, IE — were NOT in the audit set and are unassessed. They must be audited before any clean publish.

1A. DEEP WEO synthesis — multi-quarter fabricated tails (WORST; baked into estimation)
docs.text:
build_gvar_2025q4.py extended short domestic series to the 2025Q4 target with IMF-WEO annual growth + proportional-Denton disaggregation (activity/price columns) and held-last the no-WEO columns (rates, NEER, unemployment, house prices).

Bloc Genuine end Synthetic tail Type Notes KR 2024Q1 7 q (2024Q2–2025Q4) WEO-ext (GDP/CONS/INV/GOV/EXP/IMP/CPI) + held-last (LR/RATE/YIELD10/HP) OECD-FRED QNA mirror froze ~2023Q3–2024Q1; worst case RU 2024Q1 8 q (2024Q2–2026Q1) WEO-ext (GDP/CPI/EXP) + held-last (LR 2.774/RATE 16/NEER 396.463 ×8) POLRATE_RU & HP_RU separately genuine to 2025Q4 TH 2025Q2 4 q WEO-ext + held-last NESDC has since published real 2026Q1 (not shipped) ID 2025Q1 3 q WEO-ext + held-last IN 2025Q2 2 q WEO-backfill + held-last held at 2025Q4 in dates.ts CN mixed (1B/1C/1D) mixed ~~nominal-GDP proxy backfill~~ (GDP RESOLVED 2026-06 — real GDP re-est, CN-only) + frozen mirrors + held-last fiscal GDP fixed; mirrors/fiscal remain

Partner-aggregate stars (ROW*) in these blocs inherit the synthetic tail (recomputed from the same panels) → flag-only/truncate.

docs.text: Bloc Variable Proxy nature Recommendation CN GDP_CN ~~Nominal GDP proxying real GDP + 9q backfilled~~ — RESOLVED 2026-06: re-estimated against genuine real GDP (chain-linked SA volume index); CN-only graft, other 32 blocs byte-identical RESOLVED CR GDP_CR Industrial Production proxying real GDP (explicit label) flag-only / decide CN CORP_CN 10Y sovereign yield proxying corporate credit price flag-only CL CORP_CL sovereign 10Y proxying corporate credit flag-only IN CORP_IN sovereign 10Y proxying corporate (no genuine IN corp series) needs-human-check IL CORP_IL Tel-Bond corp proxy = 5y govt + Tel-Bond spread, from genuine BoI components keep (methodological)
